Abstract: In remote sensing object detection, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) excel at capturing local details while Vision Transformers (ViTs) are better at global context modeling. However, existing detectors typically rely on a single fixed backbone or a manually designed hybrid architecture, and thus fail to adaptively exploit these complementary strengths across inputs of diverse complexity. To address this limitation, we propose Backbone Module Composition via Reinforcement Learning (BMCR). BMCR dynamically assembles input-adaptive inference paths from reusable modules decomposed from off-the-shelf CNN and ViT backbones. To enable such cross-family composition, we first construct an extensible module toolbox. Specifically, we decompose representative CNN and ViT backbones into reusable functional modules and encapsulate each module with explicit structural, semantic, and computational metadata for compatibility-aware assembly. To bridge the gap between grid-based CNN features and token-based ViT representations, we design a lightweight Optimal Transport (OT) based transition interface that ensures distribution-aware alignment while respecting spatial consistency. The backbone composition process is then formulated as a sequential decision problem, in which a policy network progressively selects task-relevant modules according to intermediate multi-scale observations. To stabilize the joint optimization of reusable modules and the routing policy, we further develop an Adaptive Module Cooperative Optimization (AMCO) strategy that coordinates module updating, routing exploration, and reward assignment during training. On DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5 and DIOR-R, BMCR achieves 79.31\%, 73.41\% and 71.86\% mAP, respectively, surpassing strong static and dynamic baselines by up to 2.5 points while maintaining competitive efficiency.

</details>


### [27] [HDST-GNN: Heterogeneous Dynamic Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Networks for Multi-Object Tracking in UAV Aerial Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05587)
*Phillip Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A heterogeneous dynamic spatiotemporal GNN for UAV MOT (HDST-GNN) with altitude-aware edges, typed node representations (detections, tracklets, lost targets), occlusion-aware aggregation, and differentiable Sinkhorn assignment; achieves state-of-the-art on VisDrone2019-MOT with both oracle and real detections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MOT for UAV imagery must handle varying camera altitude, tiny densely packed objects, and occlusions. Existing graph trackers assume fixed spatial context and treat detections uniformly, neglecting heterogeneous lifecycle states of detections, active tracklets, and lost targets.

Method: Altitude-Adaptive Edge Construction to adjust graph connectivity based on an altitude proxy; Heterogeneous Node Representation with distinct D/T/L node types and typed edges; Occlusion-Gated Temporal Aggregation to gate attention by occlusion confidence; End-to-end training with a differentiable Sinkhorn head using joint cross-entropy and triplet loss for association.

Result: With oracle VisDrone2019-MOT detections: MOTA 94.51%, IDF1 97.24%; +5.0 MOTA points over SORT; 81% fewer identity switches. With real YOLOv8n detections: 49% reduction in identity switches vs SORT. Ablation studies show each component contributes independently.

Conclusion: The proposed HDST-GNN successfully models altitude variability, heterogeneous detection/tracklet states, and occlusion, delivering substantial gains for MOT in UAV contexts when trained end-to-end with differentiable assignment.

Abstract: Multi-object tracking (MOT) from UAV imagery presents unique challenges: altitude varies across sequences, objects are small and densely packed, and frequent occlusion causes identity switches. Existing graph-based trackers assume fixed spatial context and treat all objects uniformly, ignoring the heterogeneous lifecycle states of detections, active tracklets, and lost targets. We propose HDST-GNN, a Heterogeneous Dynamic Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Network with three novel contributions. First, Altitude-Adaptive Edge Construction estimates a camera-altitude proxy from mean object area and adjusts the graph connectivity radius accordingly. Second, Heterogeneous Node Representation models detections (Type-D), confirmed tracklets (Type-T), and lost tracklets (Type-L) as distinct node types with dedicated projections and typed edge relations. Third, Occlusion-Gated Temporal Aggregation gates each node's attention contribution by its occlusion confidence, preventing occluded nodes from corrupting neighbour embeddings. HDST-GNN is trained end-to-end with a differentiable Sinkhorn head using joint cross-entropy and triplet loss. On VisDrone2019-MOT with oracle detections, HDST-GNN achieves 94.51% MOTA and 97.24% IDF1, outperforming SORT by +5.0 MOTA points and reducing identity switches by 81%. With real YOLOv8n detections, HDST-GNN reduces identity switches by 49% vs. SORT. Ablation studies confirm the independent contribution of each component.

</details>


### [28] [What's Under the Skin? Estimating Swine Body Condition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05611)
*Mk Bashar,Kuljit Bhatti,Gary Rohrer,Madonna Benjamin,Tami Brown-Brandl,Daniel Morris*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PigFormer is a two-stage deep-learning system that converts ceiling-mounted RGB-D depth frames into standardized height maps and uses a Slice Attention Encoder to predict subcutaneous backfat thickness, loin muscle depth, and total tissue thickness in pigs, achieving superior MAE results over ResNet-18 and ViT baselines on a multi-site sow dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate non-contact, continuous body condition monitoring in swine production due to poor correlation of traditional visual or caliper measures with tissue composition and the labor-intensive nature of ultrasound.

Method: Stage 1 (geometric front-end): raw depth -> height map via SAM3-to-MaskDINO segmentation distillation, ground-plane removal, orientation normalization. Stage 2 (Slice Attention Encoder): height map treated as sequence of cross-sectional slices to capture dorsal-spatial relationships. End-to-end system (PigFormer) trained on depth frames from a ceiling-mounted RGB-D camera.

Result: On 319 sow/gilt samples from two facilities, achieving backfat MAE of 2.43 mm and overall tissue MAE of 3.87 mm; outperforms single-stage ResNet-18 and ViT-small baselines.

Conclusion: PigFormer provides a practical, automated path for continuous, non-contact body condition monitoring in commercial swine production; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Sow body condition is an important indicator for growers as it has a large impact on lactation performance and piglet survival. However, body condition measures used during production, such as visual scoring and calipers, correlate poorly with underlying tissue composition. Ultrasound scans can provide direct measurements of subcutaneous backfat thickness and loin muscle depth, but their operation is labor intensive and not scalable for production. We present PigFormer, an end-to-end two-stage system that takes raw depth frames from a ceiling-mounted RGB-D camera and predicts subcutaneous backfat thickness, loin muscle depth, and total tissue thickness at the last rib. Stage 1 is a geometric front-end that converts raw depth into a standardized height map via SAM3-to-MaskDINO segmentation distillation, ground-plane removal, and orientation normalization. Stage 2 is a Slice Attention Encoder that treats each height map as a sequence of cross-sectional slices and captures spatial relationships along the full dorsal surface. On a multi-site dataset of 319 sow and gilt instances from two facilities, PigFormer achieves 2.43 mm backfat MAE and 3.87 mm overall MAE. It outperforms strong single-stage ResNet-18 and ViT-small baselines. PigFormer offers a practical path toward continuous, automated, non-contact body condition monitoring in commercial swine production. Code is available at https://github.com/iambashar/Pigformer.

</details>


### [29] [KV-Control: Parameter-Efficient K/V Injection for Trajectory-Controlled Text-to-Motion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05624)
*Tengjiao Sun,Pengcheng Fang,Xiaoyu Zhan,Yanwen Guo,Dongjie Fu,Xiaohao Cai,Hansung Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KV-Control is an attention-side control interface for frozen text-to-motion transformers that injects control-conditioned key/value memories into self-attention layers, enabling precise trajectory constraints with sub-centimeter accuracy while preserving text-conditioned motion quality, via a co-designed PartVQ substrate and T-Concat tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-conditioned 3D motion models must follow precise constraints (root paths, end-effector targets) without losing the pretrained motion prior. Existing approaches either duplicate large parts of the generator or rely on expensive test-time optimization, making practical animation workflows cumbersome.

Method: Co-design of a compact adapter: PartVQ for anatomy-aligned part codebooks; T-Concat to expose frame-part tokens as attention-addressable sites; KV-Control injects control-conditioned key/value memories at every self-attention layer, preserving query stream, cross-attention, FFN, and backbone weights. The adapter adds trainable injection parameters atop a shared trajectory encoder, enabling memory-based constraint enforcement without overwriting the pretrained prior.

Result: Achieves sub-centimeter accuracy in root and multi-joint constraint tracking under the inherited refinement protocol, while maintaining high-quality text-conditioned motion generation; provides a small, transparent control interface that reframes conditioning as memory retrieval.

Conclusion: Trajectory conditioning can be effectively implemented as memory retrieval within transformers, enabling precise, efficient, and interpretable control for text-to-motion without large-scale generator modification.

Abstract: Text-conditioned 3D human motion models now synthesize plausible motions from prompts, but practical animation and embodied-agent workflows rarely stop at text: a character may need to follow a sketched root path, hit an end-effector target, or satisfy a multi-joint trajectory while still preserving the gait, style, and intent described by language. This exposes a control trade-off. A trajectory controller should be precise without overwriting the pretrained text-conditioned motion prior, yet existing solutions either duplicate large portions of the generator to regain per-layer control access or move much of the cost to test-time optimization. We introduce KV-Control, a compact attention-side control interface for frozen masked text-to-motion transformers. The key idea is to make geometric constraints available as memory inside self-attention rather than injecting them through a global pose token or enforcing them only at the output side. To support this interface, we co-design a part-tokenized motion substrate and controller: \textbf{PartVQ} learns anatomy-aligned part codebooks, T-Concat exposes each frame--part token as an attention-addressable site, and KV-Control injects control-conditioned key/value memories at every self-attention layer while preserving the pretrained query stream, text cross-attention, FFN, and all backbone weights. The resulting adapter adds only trainable injection parameters atop a shared trajectory encoder, yet tracks root and multi-joint constraints with sub-centimeter accuracy under the inherited refinement protocol while retaining text-conditioned motion quality. KV-Control reframes trajectory conditioning as lightweight memory retrieval, providing a small, precise, and transparent control interface for text-to-motion generation.

</details>


### [30] [ShotCrop$^3$: Cropping Human-Centric Images into Cinematic Triple-Shot Compositions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05635)
*Dehong Kong,Lina Lei,Lingtao Zheng,Chenyang Wu,Ailing Zhang,Xinran Qin,Teng Ma,Jiaqi Xu,Zhixin Wang,Zhikai Chen,Xuecheng Qi,Renjing Pei,Fan Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Triple-Shot Compositions (TSC) to generate a three-shot set (establishing, medium, close-up) from a single image, with shot descriptions; introduces ShotCrop, a three-stage training pipeline (CoT supervised fine-tuning, semi-supervised high-confidence pseudo-labeling, and GRPO-S with a composite reward); presents TSC-Bench benchmark and reports ShotCrop outperforms GPT-5 by 2.82x in shot localization; aims to enable multi-shot visual storytelling for downstream workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in aesthetic composition research that focuses on a single crop, ignoring multi-shot narratives essential for downstream creative workflows (e.g., posters needing context, subject emphasis, and emotional/product details).

Method: Three-stage training for ShotCrop: (1) Chain-of-Thought supervised fine-tuning to develop basic reasoning and aesthetic cropping skills; (2) semi-supervised fine-tuning using high-confidence pseudo labels to amplify capabilities; (3) Group Relative Policy Optimization for ShotCrop (GRPO-S) with a composite reward tailored to triple-shot generation. Pseudo-labeling combines MLLM-based scoring, aesthetic assessment, and CLIP similarity to retain high-confidence signals. Output is a three-shot set (establishing, medium, close-up) plus brief shot descriptions for narration.

Result: Quantitative: ShotCrop achieves an average 2.82x improvement over GPT-5 in shot localization accuracy on TSC-Bench (1.2k expert-annotated test cases). Qualitative: demonstrates the viability of a dedicated multi-shot composition pipeline and a dedicated benchmark.

Conclusion: The work introduces a principled, multi-stage training framework (ShotCrop) and a corresponding evaluation benchmark (TSC-Bench) to enable reliable generation of narrative, three-shot compositions from a single image, with strong empirical gains over large-language-model baselines in localization tasks.

Abstract: Prior work on aesthetic composition typically produces a single aesthetically pleasing crop, overlooking the narrative value of composing multiple shots from one scene. In practice, multi-shot composition is critical for downstream creative workflows: commercial posters often require multiple crops with different emphases (e.g., context, subject, and emotion/product details) to present key story beats. Therefore, we propose \textbf{Triple-Shot Compositions (TSC)}, a composition task that generates a three-shot set -- establishing, medium, and close-up -- from a single human-centric image, each paired with a brief shot description to support visual narration. To learn TSC with limited expert annotations, we introduce \textbf{ShotCrop} which undergoes a three-stage training process: it first applies Chain-of-Thought supervised fine-tuning to establish basic reasoning and aesthetic shot-cropping skills, then performs semi-supervised fine-tuning with high-confidence pseudo labels to further enhance aesthetic capability, and is finally optimized with Group Relative Policy Optimization for \textbf{ShotCrop} (GRPO-S) using a composite reward tailored for it. Specifically, our pseudo-labeling strategy combines MLLM-based scoring, aesthetic assessment, and CLIP similarity to retain high-confidence training signals. In addition, we present TSC-Bench, a benchmark of 1.2k expert-annotated test cases. Notably, ShotCrop achieves an average improvement of \textbf{2.82} times over GPT-5 in shot localization accuracy.

</details>


### [31] [Multi-Task Crack Foundation Model for Engineering-Reliable Crack Representation and Topology Preservation in Civil Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05641)
*Blessing Agyei Kyem,Joshua Kofi Asamoah,Eugene Denteh,Armstrong Aboah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CrackGeoFM is a multi-task framework that freezes a visual backbone and adds crack-specific modules to jointly predict masks, skeletons, and calibrated uncertainty, achieving state-of-the-art crack segmentation with strong topology preservation and few-shot adaptation across 20 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current segmentation models often maximize overlap (IoU) while producing fragmented crack structures and uncalibrated uncertainty, leading to unreliable topology and domain-shift sensitivity in infrastructure crack assessment.

Method: A frozen visual backbone is customized with Crack-Domain Feature Adaptation (CFAM) to adapt features to crack patterns, a Frequency-Guided Crack Enhancement Module (FCEM) to emphasize high-frequency crack cues, and a Structure-Aware Multi-Task Decoder (SMTD) to jointly predict masks, skeletons, and uncertainty. The approach is evaluated across 20 crack datasets and includes few-shot adaptation with only five labeled images.

Result: CrackGeoFM achieves state-of-the-art segmentation and topology preservation, provides calibrated uncertainty, and demonstrates effective few-shot adaptation.

Conclusion: The framework enables reliable, generalizable, and engineering-oriented crack analysis for infrastructure assessment by delivering accurate masks, connected crack geometry, and stable uncertainty under domain shift.

Abstract: Reliable crack assessment requires not only accurate pixel-level masks but also connected crack geometry and confidence estimates that remain stable under domain shift. However, existing segmentation models can achieve high overlap scores while fragmenting cracks, missing fine branches, and providing no calibrated uncertainty. To address this gap, this paper proposes CrackGeoFM, a multi-task framework that combines a frozen visual foundation backbone with crack-specific adaptation for mask prediction, skeleton reconstruction, and uncertainty estimation. The framework integrates a Frequency-Guided Crack Enhancement Module (FCEM) to enhance high-frequency crack cues, a Crack-Domain Feature Adaptation Module (CFAM) to adapt frozen backbone features to crack-domain patterns, and a Structure-Aware Multi-Task Decoder (SMTD) to jointly decode masks, skeletons, and uncertainty. Across 20 crack datasets, CrackGeoFM achieves state-of-the-art segmentation, improved topology preservation, calibrated uncertainty, and effective few-shot adaptation with only five labeled images. These results support reliable, generalizable, and engineering-oriented crack analysis for infrastructure assessment.

</details>


### [32] [CoFi-UCGen: Coarse-to-Fine Unsupervised Conditional Generation without Label Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05652)
*Shengxi Li,Zhaokun Hu,Ce Zheng,Mai Xu,Jingyuan Xia,Si Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel unsupervised coarse-to-fine conditional image generation framework (CoFi-UCGen) that disentangles global semantics from fine-grained variations using adversarial semantic reciprocal learning and bit-codes, enabling hierarchical diffusion-based generation without labels and achieving superior quality and control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of UCGen with unstructured semantic representations by enabling explicit coarse-to-fine semantic decomposition that preserves semantic consistency between images and latent spaces, without relying on labels or pre-trained features.

Method: 1) Propose adversarial semantic reciprocal learning theory to enforce semantic consistency/completeness between images and latents. 2) Introduce bit-codes to learn a structured coarse-grained latent space and demonstrate distinct global semantics encoded by bit-codes while preserving independent noise sampling. 3) Build a fine-grained semantic basis atop bit-codes and implement a hierarchical diffusion-model modulation with layer-wise injection from coarse conditions to control fine-grained attributes.

Result: Empirical results show CoFi-UCGen outperforms existing UCGen methods in image quality, semantic consistency, and control accuracy without any label priors or pre-trained extractors, validating coarse-to-fine semantic decomposition for UCGen.

Conclusion: Explicit coarse-to-fine semantic decomposition is effective for UCGen, marking the first successful attempt at both coarse- and fine-grained conditional generation without labels and offering improved control and semantic fidelity.

Abstract: Unsupervised conditional image generation (UCGen) aims to control generation without relying on manually annotated labels, yet remains challenging due to unstructured semantic representations across granularities. To address this, we propose a novel coarse-to-fine UCGen framework (CoFi-UCGen) that explicitly disentangles global semantics from fine-grained variations, which to the best of our knowledge, sets out the first successful attempt for both coarse- and fine-grained conditional generation without any labels. More specifically, we first propose the adversarial semantic reciprocal learning theory to ensure the semantic consistency and completeness between images and latent spaces. Based on the consistency, we propose the bit-codes to learn a structured coarse-grained latent space, and further prove distinct global semantics inherent from our bit-codes while preserving independent noise sampling for generation. Building upon these bit-codes, we establish a fine-grained semantic basis and introduce a hierarchical modulation mechanism in diffusion models, by enabling layer-wise injection from coarse conditions to progressively control fine-grained attributes during generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that without any label priors or pre-trained feature extractors, our CoFi-UCGen consistently outperforms existing UCGen methods in terms of image quality, semantic consistency, and control accuracy, verifying the effectiveness of explicit coarse-to-fine semantic decomposition for the challenging UCGen task.

</details>


### [33] [V2V-Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Video-to-Video Generation Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05665)
*Tao Liu,Leela Krishna,Gouti Pavan Kumar,Sreeja K,Vishav Garg*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes V2V-Bench, a holistic 11-dimension benchmark for video-to-video generation evaluation, and shows model strengths and strong alignment with human judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluation of V2V is hard because outputs must satisfy editing instructions and maintain frame-level correspondence; existing T2V and I2V metrics fail to capture this, necessitating a dedicated benchmark.

Method: Construct V2V-Bench with 11 dimensions across five categories (temporal alignment, structural fidelity, transformation quality, video quality, semantic alignment); curate diverse source videos with challenging editing tasks; evaluate three models (Grok Imagine, Gemini Veo3, Open Sora 2).

Result: Grok excels in editing fidelity; Veo3 excels in visual quality; six V2V-specific dimensions yield Spearman r = 0.905 with human judgments.

Conclusion: V2V-Bench provides a comprehensive, high-correlation benchmark for V2V evaluation and reveals complementary strengths, informing model development and evaluation.

Abstract: Video-to-video (V2V) generation is difficult to evaluate because outputs must both follow editing instructions and preserve frame-level correspondence with the source video, which existing T2V and I2V metrics do not capture. We introduce V2V-Bench, a 11-dimension benchmark organized into five categories: temporal alignment, structural fidelity, transformation quality, video quality, and semantic alignment. V2V-Bench pairs diverse source videos with challenging editing tasks and evaluates two commercial models, Grok Imagine and Gemini Veo3, and one open-source model, Open Sora 2. Results show complementary model strengths: Grok performs better on editing fidelity, while Veo3 achieves stronger visual quality. On six V2V-specific dimensions, V2V-Bench reaches a Spearman correlation of 0.905 with human judgments.

</details>


### [34] [LongSpace: Exploring Long-Horizon Spatial Memory from Perception to Recall in Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05677)
*Shiqiang Lang,Jing Liu,Haoyang He,Peiwen Sun,Yuanteng Chen,Tao Liu,Lan Yang,Longteng Guo,Honggang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LongSpace-Bench provides a long-horizon spatial memory benchmark for room-tour videos, and LongSpace introduces a memory-enabled framework for long-video spatial reasoning that chunks videos, folds 3D cues into early decoder layers, and uses layer-aware memory for retrieval; together they improve long-video spatial reasoning in MLLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in long-horizon spatial reasoning for MLLMs that must remember prior views, layouts, routes, etc., by providing a benchmark and a memory-based model.

Method: LongSpace-Bench: room-tour video benchmark for long-horizon spatial memory (scene perception, spatial relations, spatial memory). LongSpace: process long videos in chunks, integrate 3D structural cues into early decoder layers, build layer-aware memory with question-guided retrieval.

Result: Empirical results on multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks show improved long-video spatial understanding; explicit spatial memory is beneficial for long-horizon video MLLMs.

Conclusion: Explicit spatial memory is a key capability for long-horizon video MLLMs; the proposed benchmark and memory framework provide both evaluation and method for improved long-term spatial reasoning.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have advanced image and video understanding and can increasingly handle longer visual inputs. Long-horizon tasks such as autonomous driving and robotic navigation require more than recognizing the current view, as models must remember and retrieve previously observed spatial layouts, routes, viewpoint changes, and object states. To evaluate this capability, we introduce LongSpace-Bench, a room-tour video benchmark for long-horizon spatial memory, covering scene perception, spatial relations, and spatial memory. In this work, we further propose LongSpace, a memory framework for long-video spatial reasoning. LongSpace models long videos as sequential chunks, incorporates 3D structural cues into early decoder layers, and constructs layer-aware memory for question-guided retrieval. Experiments on multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks show that LongSpace improves long-video spatial understanding, further demonstrating explicit spatial memory as a key capability for long-horizon video MLLMs.

</details>


### [35] [T-SAR-JEPA: Self-Supervised Temporal Anomaly Detection in SAR Amplitude Stacks via Latent Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05700)
*Kerod Woldesenbet,Abem Woldesenbet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised Temporal-SAR-JEPA framework for anomaly detection in SAR amplitude stacks, using latent prediction with a ViT encoder and a temporal transformer, leveraging InSAR coherence as pseudo-ground-truth; achieves 77.0% ROC-AUC on DFC 2026 Hawaii eruption window and high spatial coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle labeling scarcity in SAR anomaly detection by learning temporal dynamics from unlabeled amplitude stacks; exploit coherence as auxiliary pseudo-ground-truth for validation.

Method: Domain-adapt a ViT-Base/16 encoder (SAR-JEPA) on 39,300 Capella patches using local masked reconstruction with gradient feature prediction. A temporal transformer with sinusoidal time encoding forecasts future latent states from K=7 acquisitions. Progressive unfreezing reduces validation loss.

Result: On DFC 2026 (300 time-series, 3 AOIs), achieves ROC-AUC 77.0% during the Hawaii eruption window, outperforming RX, PaDiM, Linear AR, and LSTM baselines (~50%). Spatial coherence is 99.9% (p<0.001, permutation test).

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective self-supervised temporal anomaly detection in SAR amplitude stacks via latent prediction and forward-looking dynamics; pseudo-ground-truth from coherence provides a robust evaluation signal; code available for replication.

Abstract: We present T-SAR-JEPA, a self-supervised framework for temporal anomaly detection in SAR amplitude stacks via latent prediction. A ViT-Base/16 encoder from SAR-JEPA is domain-adapted on 39,300 Capella patches using local masked reconstruction with gradient feature prediction. A temporal transformer with sinusoidal time encoding forecasts future latent states from K=7 acquisitions, with progressive unfreezing substantially reducing validation loss. The model operates on amplitude alone; InSAR coherence serves exclusively as independent pseudo-ground-truth. On the DFC 2026 dataset (300 time-series, three AOIs), T-SAR-JEPA achieves ROC-AUC of 77.0% on the Hawaii eruption window, outperforming RX, PaDiM, Linear AR, and LSTM baselines (~50%). Spatial coherence of 99.9% (p < 0.001, permutation test) confirms structured detections. Code: https://github.com/TerraLatent/t-sar-jepa

</details>


### [36] [Parallel Jacobi Decoding for Fast Autoregressive Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05703)
*Boya Liao,Ying Li,Siyong Jian,Huan Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Parallel Jacobi Decoding (PJD) introduces a training-free, two-dimensional draft expansion approach for autoregressive image generation. It preserves competitive quality while achieving 4.8×–6.4× faster inference by exploiting local spatial correlations and adjusting the attention mask for stable convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive (AR) image synthesis is bottlenecked by sequential token prediction. Although Jacobi-style decoding offers speedups, gains saturate due to error propagation in 1D drafts. Images exhibit strong local spatial correlations, suggesting that 2D parallel refinement could yield better performance.

Method: A training-free decoding strategy that expands draft tokens in the two-dimensional spatial domain and performs spatially parallel refinement. It includes an adjusted attention mask to mitigate error accumulation and improve convergence stability without retraining the model.

Result: Empirical evaluation on diverse datasets shows PJD achieves 4.8×–6.4× acceleration across multiple autoregressive image generation models while maintaining competitive generation quality.

Conclusion: PJD delivers significant speedups without retraining by leveraging 2D spatial correlations and an adjusted attention mechanism, offering a simple, scalable, and effective decoding paradigm for autoregressive image generation.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) models have demonstrated remarkable performance in generating high-fidelity images. However, their inherently sequential next-token prediction leads to significantly slower inference. Recent studies have introduced Jacobi-style decoding to accelerate autoregressive image generation. Extending the draft sequence initially improves efficiency, yet the acceleration quickly saturates as error propagation in the one-dimensional sequence hinders convergence. Observing that images exhibit strong local spatial correlations, we propose Parallel Jacobi Decoding (PJD), a training-free decoding approach that expands draft tokens in the two-dimensional spatial domain to enable efficient spatially parallel refinement. PJD adjusts the attention mask to mitigate error accumulation and improve convergence stability. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets show that PJD achieves 4.8x-6.4x acceleration across multiple autoregressive image generation models while maintaining competitive generation quality.

</details>


### [37] [Real-Time Threat Detection from Surveillance Cameras using Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05708)
*Gajendra Mandal,J. P. Patra,Priyansh Mahant*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A real-time surveillance system using YOLOv8 to detect guns, knives, and blunt objects in Indian environments, combining a small custom dataset (336 blunt-object images) with a public gun/knife dataset (7,623 images) into a 7,959-image, three-class dataset. The model achieves real-time detection, with training duration positively affecting recall and average precision for blunt objects, and no observed overfitting, suggesting suitability for deployment in campuses, public spaces, and transport hubs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To replace/manual surveillance with automated, real-time object detection to enhance public safety, reduce human fatigue, and enable faster responses in densely populated urban settings, with a focus on India-specific surveillance scenarios.

Method: Create a two-source training dataset by merging a custom mobile-camera collection (336 labeled blunt-object images: iron rods, wooden sticks, plastic rods) with a public dataset of guns/knives (7,623 images). Consolidate into 7,959 images across three classes: gun, knife, blunt object. Train a YOLOv8-based object detector for real-time inference. Evaluate how training duration affects recall and average precision, particularly for the blunt object class, and assess overfitting.

Result: The detector achieves a balance between accuracy and efficiency suitable for real-world deployment. Increasing training duration significantly improves recall and mean average precision for blunt objects, with no signs of overfitting.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers effective real-time surveillance by detecting multiple weapon-related object classes with a dataset-augmented approach, making it applicable to real-world environments such as campuses, public spaces, and transportation areas.

Abstract: Ensuring public safety in densely populated urban environments remains a critical challenge, necessitating the deployment of intelligent and automated video surveillance systems. Traditional surveillance approaches rely heavily on manual monitoring, which is inefficient and susceptible to human fatigue, delayed response, and observational errors. To overcome these limitations, this work presents a real-time object detection-based surveillance framework. The proposed system focuses on detecting guns, knives, and region-specific blunt objects commonly involved in violent activities in Indian surveillance scenarios. A key contribution of this work is the use of a custom-created dataset collected using a mobile camera, consisting of 336 labeled images of blunt objects such as iron rods, wooden sticks, and plastic rods. This dataset is combined with a publicly available dataset of 7,623 images of guns and knives, forming a consolidated dataset of 7,959 images across three classes: gun, knife, and blunt object. The combined dataset is used to train a YOLOv8-based object detection model for real-time performance. Experimental evaluation shows that increasing the training duration significantly improves recall and average precision for the blunt object class without signs of overfitting. Overall, the proposed framework achieves an effective balance between accuracy and efficiency, making it suitable for deployment in real-world surveillance environments such as campuses, public spaces, and transportation areas.

</details>


### [38] [ViCuR: Visual Cues as Recoverable Privilege for Multimodal On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05718)
*Kanghui Tian,Siyuan Liu,Ziang Yan,Sheng Xia,Shuai Dong,Yi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViCuR replaces answer-side privileged supervision with visually grounded cues derived from the same input in on-policy distillation for multimodal reasoning, mitigating train-test mismatch and enabling cue recoverability by the student.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Answer-side privilege in multimodal on-policy distillation creates a train-test mismatch and encourages shortcut imitation. There is a need for privileged supervision that is recoverable from the student's observed data (visual input) to promote grounded reasoning.

Method: Introduce ViCuR: a visually grounded privileged-teacher distillation framework. Replace answer-side supervision with query-related visual cues obtained from the input. Add a lightweight cue recovery module that uses sink-token cross-attention during prefill to aggregate task-relevant visual evidence into an internal representation, without changing inference or adding cue-generation losses.

Result: Across seven benchmarks with Qwen3-VL-2B and 8B students, ViCuR consistently improves over answer-based on-policy self-distillation by +1.19 and +1.24 on average performance. Extends to stronger-teacher OPD, surpassing OPD baselines by +0.64 and +1.08, with consistent out-of-domain gains at the 8B scale.

Conclusion: In multimodal on-policy distillation, the design of teacher privilege is as important as teacher strength; visually grounded cues that are recoverable by the student yield robust improvements and better generalization.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves reasoning by training a student on trajectories sampled from its own policy under supervision from a teacher. In multimodal reasoning, a common extension is to use a privileged teacher that observes training-time-only signals such as reference answers or rationales. However, such answer-side privilege creates a train-test mismatch: the teacher's supervision may depend on signals unavailable to the student, encouraging shortcut imitation rather than visually grounded reasoning. We propose ViCuR, a visually grounded privileged-teacher distillation framework that replaces answer-side privilege with visual cues (query-related evidence in the input). Because these cues are derived from the same visual input available at inference, their evidence is recoverable by the student. To support this, ViCuR introduces a lightweight cue recovery module that uses dedicated sink-token cross-attention during prefill to aggregate task-relevant visual evidence into an internal representation, without changing the inference interface or requiring auxiliary cue-generation losses. Across seven benchmarks with Qwen3-VL-2B and 8B students, ViCuR consistently improves over answer-based on-policy self-distillation by +1.19 and +1.24 on overall average performance. It also extends naturally to stronger-teacher OPD, surpassing OPD baselines by +0.64 and +1.08, with consistent out-of-domain gains at the 8B scale. These results show that, in multimodal on-policy distillation, the design of teacher privilege is as important as teacher strength.

</details>


### [39] [TextWand: A Unified Framework for Scene Text Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05730)
*Shuyu Wang,Zhile Guan,Hongxiu Chen,Yule Duan,Weiqi Li,Xin Shan,Ronggang Wang,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TextWand is a unified model for scene text editing (remove, generate, replace) that uses Overlay-Reference Positional Encoding (ORPE) for layout fidelity and exemplar-driven style, plus Region-Adaptive Suppression (RAS) for clean erasure; it introduces TextWand-Bench as a comprehensive benchmark and reportedly outperforms existing models across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no general-purpose, end-to-end framework for editing text in scenes; existing approaches treat removal, generation, and replacement separately and lack a unified benchmark to evaluate across tasks. A single model with precise control over text appearance and background integrity could improve consistency and usability in editing pipelines.

Method: Proposes TextWand, a single model that decomposes editing into rendering and erasure primitives. Introduces ORPE to enforce pixel-level layout fidelity and enable exemplar-driven style transfer; introduces RAS to ensure complete and clean text erasure; builds TextWand-Bench to benchmark general-purpose scene text editing across removal, generation, and replacement tasks.

Result: Extensive experiments show TextWand achieves superior text content accuracy, layout and style consistency, and overall image quality compared with leading open-source and closed-source models across the targeted tasks.

Conclusion: TextWand provides a general-purpose solution for scene text editing, with novel encoding and suppression techniques improving fidelity and erasure. The TextWand-Bench benchmark enables robust evaluation, and results indicate practical viability, with potential future work in efficiency, generalization to diverse fonts/styles, and more complex scenes.

Abstract: We propose TextWand, a general-purpose framework that unifies scene text removal, generation, and replacement into a single model. By decomposing complex editing tasks into the atomic primitives of rendering and erasure, TextWand achieves precise control over both text appearance and background integrity. Specifically, we introduce a novel design, Overlay-Reference Positional Encoding (ORPE), to enforce pixel-level layout fidelity and exemplar-driven style control, alongside a new strategy, Region-Adaptive Suppression (RAS), to ensure clean text erasure. To address the absence of a comprehensive benchmark for general-purpose scene text editing among existing single-task datasets, we construct TextWand-Bench. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TextWand outperforms existing leading open-source and closed-source models by delivering superior text content accuracy, layout and style consistency, and overall image quality across scene text removal, generation and replacement tasks.

</details>


### [40] [VTI-CoT: Visual-Textual Interleaved Chain of Thought for Video Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05736)
*Shufan Zhang,Ziyue Lin,Bairun Wang,Lei Jin,Xuanding Ding,Xinzhu Ma,Kunlin Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes VTI-CoT: a visual-textual interleaved chain-of-thought framework for video reasoning, with an automated multimodal CoT data pipeline and OCR-based CoT token compression, achieving SOTA within similar parameter scales and improved efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CoT video reasoning relies on text-only deduction, neglecting visual cues; humans review visual segments during reasoning; lack of multimodal CoT data; long CoT sequences hinder training.

Method: Interleave textual reasoning steps with corresponding frames; develop automated annotation to create multimodal CoT data; apply OCR-based compression to condense long CoT sequences into a single canvas.

Result: State-of-the-art among models with same parameter scale; notable training efficiency gains; experiments confirm effectiveness of multimodal interleaving and compression.

Conclusion: Integrating visual and textual reasoning improves video understanding; OCR-based compression makes training more scalable; framework lays groundwork for multimodal CoT data generation and efficient inference.

Abstract: Video reasoning aims to understand complex temporal events and causal relationships within videos. Recently, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has been introduced to this field to enhance reasoning accuracy. However, existing CoT-based video reasoning methods primarily rely on text-only information for logical deduction, overlooking critical visual information during the inference process. Inspired by the human cognitive mechanism of reviewing visual segments during inference, we propose VTI-CoT, a Visual-Textual Interleaved CoT framework. VTI-CoT integrates textual reasoning steps with corresponding visual frames. Given the scarcity of visual-textual interleaved CoT in existing datasets, we develop an automated annotation pipeline to construct high-quality multimodal CoT data. Further, reasoning over long-form videos entails increasingly long CoT token sequences, which severely hinders training convergence and efficiency. To address this, we employ Optical Character Recognition (OCR)-based compression techniques to compress CoT supervision signals into a single canvas. Experimental results demonstrate that VTI-CoT achieves state-of-the-art performance among models of the same parameter scale while significantly improving training efficiency.

</details>


### [41] [Let It Be Simple: One-Step Action Generation for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05737)
*Yitong Chen,Shiduo Zhang,Jingjing Gong,Xipeng Qiu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: High-noise biased diffusion training enables effective one-step action generation for diffusion-based VLA models without extra machinery; achieves near or better performance than multi-step decoding on LIBERO benchmarks and real-robot tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA action generation has asymmetric conditioning vs action output; standard diffusion training can be repurposed to produce fast one-step actions.

Method: Maintain velocity prediction; no teacher, distillation, or auxiliary losses; bias training-time noise toward high-noise states; test on MNIST grid-to-sequence and robot-policy benchmarks; evaluate on LIBERO variants; large VLM with 30M action head; one-step decoding.

Result: One-step policies with high-noise bias are comparable to or surpass ten-step policies trained with uniform noise; LIBERO-Long results show 95.6% with a 1.4B model and 30M action head; real-robot YAM RSS evaluation corroborates the trend across architectures.

Conclusion: Strong one-step VLA action generation can emerge from standard diffusion training without dedicated few-step diffusion machinery.

Abstract: Diffusion-based vision-language-action (VLA) models often inherit the image-generation view: actions are generated by iterative denoising. We argue that VLA action generation has a different condition-target structure: the policy is conditioned on rich observations, language, and state, but predicts only a compact, low-dimensional action chunk. Under this asymmetry, strong one-step action generation should not necessarily require the advanced one-step methods developed for image synthesis. We keep standard velocity prediction and add no teacher model, distillation stage, or auxiliary objective; in our main recipe, we simply bias the training time distribution toward high-noise states. We first isolate the effect in a controlled MNIST grid-to-sequence task, then test it with extensive robot-policy experiments. Across standard LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and LIBERO-Pro, one-step policies trained with high-noise biased schedules generally match ten-step decoding under the same recipe, and on standard LIBERO can exceed ten-step policies trained with a uniform time distribution. A real-robot bimanual YAM RSS evaluation gives a small-sample cross-architecture check of the same sampler trend. On a 1.4B VLM model with a 30M action head, one-step decoding reaches 95.6\% on LIBERO-Long. These results show that strong one-step VLA action generation can emerge from standard diffusion training, without importing the full few-step diffusion machinery developed for image generation.

</details>


### [42] [Cosine Misleads: Auxiliary Losses Reshape Vision Language Models, Not Their Latents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05753)
*XiuYu Zhang,Junfeng Fang,Zhenkai Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cosine or MSE alignment between supervised latent tokens and visual targets does not predict VLM accuracy in latent visual reasoning; PRISM diagnostics show latent tokens are largely bypassed, with decoding possible downstream and performance tied to shared LM parameters via an information bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test the common assumption that aligning latent tokens with visual targets improves answer accuracy in latent visual reasoning and diagnose the true information flow.

Method: Construct five LVR variants with varying alignment losses; assess correlation between alignment metrics (cosine similarity, MSE) and accuracy; introduce PRISM: a linear probe to locate where the answer is decodable and a corruption test to measure whether the latent is load-bearing; perturb latent tokens and measure accuracy; evaluate decodability of the answer downstream vs at the latent; interpret results under an Information Bottleneck lens.

Result: Cosine alignment correlates negatively with accuracy (r = -0.94) across all variants; corrupting the latent shifts accuracy by at most ~4 points; the answer is decodable downstream of the latent but not at the latent; the size of the decodability gap predicts how much each variant relies on its latent under perturbation; the auxiliary objective reshapes the language model via shared parameters rather than via the latent variable it nominally optimizes.

Conclusion: The central assumption of LVR—that aligning supervised latent tokens with visual targets improves performance—is not supported here; PRISM reveals that latent tokens are largely bypassed and that information flow is governed by shared LM parameters, consistent with an Information Bottleneck interpretation; implications include reevaluating LVR training objectives and incorporating inference-time diagnostics to assess where information actually flows.

Abstract: Latent visual reasoning (LVR) inserts supervised latent tokens between perception and answer generation in vision-language models (VLMs). The field uses alignment between these latents and their visual targets, i.e., cosine similarity or mean squared error (MSE), as both the training loss and the quality metric, assuming that better alignment yields a better answer. We test this with a designed matrix of five LVR variants and find the assumption inverted: cosine alignment is negatively correlated with accuracy across all five (r=-0.94). To explain this, we introduce PRISM, a pair of inference-time diagnostics: a linear probe that asks where the answer is decodable, and a corruption test that asks whether the latent is load-bearing. The supervised latents are largely bypassed. Corrupting them shifts accuracy by at most four points. The answer is decodable downstream of the latent but not at it, and the size of this decodability gap predicts how much each variant relies on its latent under perturbation. Consistent with an Information Bottleneck reading of the loss, the auxiliary objective reshapes the language model via shared parameters rather than via the latent variable it nominally optimizes.

</details>


### [43] [DRIFT: A Residual Flow Adapter for Decoding Continuous Outputs in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05758)
*Zhuoming Liu,Jinhong Lin,Kwan Man Cheng,Lin Zhang,Shayok Bagchi,Yin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DRIFT is a general framework that adapts pretrained vision-language models to continuous-output tasks by combining a coarse base predictor with a flow-based refinement module that iteratively corrects residuals around a strong prior; this residual formulation reduces optimization difficulty and yields strong improvements across perception and planning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete, autoregressive text outputs from modern VLMs hinder precise continuous outputs (e.g., temporal event boundaries, robotic actions). A flexible approach is needed to support continuous decoding while leveraging pretrained models.

Method: A base predictor provides a coarse estimate of the target output. A generative refinement module based on flow matching iteratively refines the prediction by modeling a localized residual distribution around the base estimate. This residual formulation shifts learning from a global output distribution to a localized correction around a strong prior.

Result: DRIFT consistently outperforms regression- and generative-based baselines across diverse tasks and architectures (MLLMs, VLAs, WAMs) on perception (visual grounding) and planning (robotic control).

Conclusion: DRIFT offers a versatile, optimization-friendly framework for adapting pretrained VLMs to continuous decoding tasks, achieving robust gains across multiple tasks and model families.

Abstract: Many modern vision-language models (VLMs) build on autoregressive decoding of discrete tokens. While text-based output interfaces enable scalable pretraining and strong zero-shot generalization across diverse tasks, they are poorly suited for problems that require precise continuous outputs, such as localizing temporal boundaries of events or generating robotic control actions. To address this challenge, we propose DRIFT, a general framework for adapting pretrained VLMs to continuous decoding tasks. DRIFT combines a base predictor, which provides a coarse estimate of the target output, with a generative refinement module based on flow matching that iteratively improves the prediction. This residual formulation transforms the generative modeling problem from learning a global output distribution to modeling a localized residual distribution around a strong prior, substantially simplifying optimization. We evaluate DRIFT on both perception and planning tasks, including visual grounding and robotic control. Across multiple tasks and architectures spanning MLLMs, VLAs, and WAMs, DRIFT consistently outperforms a strong set of regression- and generative-based solutions.

</details>


### [44] [Physics-Guided Deep Unfolding for Blind Cross-Sensor Spectral Super-Resolution via Learning the Spectral Transformation Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05759)
*Zhaolin Li,Jinsong Chen,Shanxin Guo,Tuo Zhang,Xinglong Zhang,Pan Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A physics-guided deep unfolding network (PGU-Net) for blind cross-sensor spectral super-resolution jointly estimates hyperspectral images and a learnable spectral transformation function, achieving state-of-the-art results on benchmark datasets and real UAV data while providing interpretable degradation estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-sensor SSR is ill-posed when the spectral response function (SRF) is unknown and varies with sensor and scene content. A method that jointly estimates the HSI and the degradation operator with physical guidance can improve reconstruction and robustness in blind settings.

Method: Unrolls an alternating optimization procedure into an end-to-end trainable network with multiple stages. Each stage updates the HSI and a learnable spectral transformation function (STF) using a combination of learnable proximal networks and differentiable closed-form solvers, ensuring physical interpretability while preserving strong representation capacity.

Result: PGU-Net accurately recovers the STF and improves SSR reconstruction over state-of-the-art methods on benchmark datasets (CAVE, NTIRE 2022) across multiple SRFs. Real UAV cross-sensor data (Headwall Nano HSI and DJI P4 Multispectral) confirm robustness under blind conditions, with STF estimates showing content-dependent variations, e.g., land-cover related differences.

Conclusion: The proposed PGU-Net effectively addresses blind cross-sensor SSR by jointly estimating the HSI and a learnable STF within a physics-guided deep unfolding framework, offering both high reconstruction accuracy and interpretable degradation modeling; it also highlights the sensitivity of the degradation operator to scene content and sensor pairings.

Abstract: Hyperspectral imaging provides rich spectral information for quantitative remote sensing, yet hyperspectral sensors remain costly and thus unavailable in many UAV deployments. Spectral super-resolution (SSR) seeks to reconstruct hyperspectral images (HSIs) from multispectral images (MSIs). Most existing SSR methods assume a fixed and known spectral response function (SRF) and are therefore limited to single-sensor settings. In practical cross-sensor scenarios, the spectral degradation from HSI to MSI is unknown and varies with sensor characteristics and scene content, which renders HSI reconstruction ill-posed. This paper proposes a physics-guided deep unfolding network, termed PGU-Net, to address blind cross-sensor SSR by jointly estimating the HSI and a learnable spectral transformation function (STF). PGU-Net unrolls an alternating optimization procedure into an end-to-end trainable architecture with stages, where each stage sequentially updates the HSI and the STF. Both modules combine learnable proximal networks with differentiable closed-form solvers, enabling physical interpretability while retaining strong representation capacity. Experiments on benchmark datasets (CAVE and NTIRE 2022) with multiple SRFs demonstrate accurate recovery of the STF (degradation operator) and improved reconstruction performance over state-of-the-art SSR methods. Furthermore, evaluations on a real UAV cross-sensor dataset (Headwall Nano HSI and DJI P4 Multispectral MSI) verify the effectiveness and robustness of PGU-Net under truly blind conditions, and suggest that the estimated STF may exhibit land-cover-related differences.

</details>


### [45] [ExpSpeech-Net: Multimodal Fusion of Expression and Speech for Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05760)
*Ruchika Sharma,Rudresh Dwivedi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight multi-modal deepfake detector (SqN-R-DFD) using SqueezeNet + RNN, ISLBT image features, MPNCC audio features, and SASMA feature selection achieves near 95% accuracy with high precision, enabling real-time detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deepfakes erode credibility and existing detectors are often resource-intensive; there is a need for efficient, real-time, practical detection methods suitable for everyday online content.

Method: Backbone combines SqueezeNet with an RNN. Feature extraction includes ISLBT-based features for images and MPNCC for audio signals. A Sandpiper-Assisted Slime Mould Algorithm (SASMA) performs feature selection to balance inputs. The model integrates multimodal cues (facial expressions and speech) for detection.

Result: Reported metrics: accuracy 94.5%, precision 99.3%, F-measure 96.8%, outperforming conventional methods.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that integrating lightweight, multimodal analysis with intelligent preprocessing and feature selection yields practical, real-time deepfake detection suitable for everyday applications.

Abstract: Deepfake videos are increasingly challenging the credibility of online content. Many existing detection methodology relies on complex, resource-intensive models, which limit their practical use. The study introduces the ExpSpeech-Net deepfake detection (SqN-R-DFD) model, which utilizes SqueezeNet and RNN (Recurrent Neural Network) as its backbone, providing a lightweight and efficient deepfake detection framework that simultaneously analyzes facial expressions and speech patterns. The approach incorporates advanced feature extraction, such as ISLBT-based features for image and MPNCC for signals, along with a smart feature-selection strategy using SASMA (Sandpiper-Assisted Slime Mould Algorithm), ensuring optimal and balanced input to the detection models. By combining SqueezeNet and an RNN, subtle inconsistencies in deepfake videos are captured effectively. The framework achieves 94.5% accuracy, precision of 99.3%, and F-measure of 96.8%, outperforming conventional methods. This demonstrates that integrating multiple modalities with intelligent preprocessing and feature selection enables practical, real-time deepfake detection suitable for everyday applications.

</details>


### [46] [Imagine Before You Predict: Interleaved Latent Visual Reasoning for Video Event Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05769)
*Tianxiang Jiang,Linquan Wu,Sheng Xia,Songze Li,Ziang Yan,Haoyu Yang,Yu Qiao,Yi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Future-L1 introduces interleaved latent visual reasoning for video event prediction, using continuous latent visual spans alongside text during autoregressive decoding, trained on Future-L1-50K with LA-DAPO RL, achieving state-of-the-art on FutureBench and TwiFF-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-only verbalization of intermediate reasoning loses fine-grained visual cues and can produce visually ungrounded hallucinations; preserving latent visual semantics may improve future state prediction.

Method: Interleave language tokens and continuous latent visual spans; construct Future-L1-50K by selecting examples where future visual hints help; align latent states to future-frame embeddings; optimize latent trajectories with LA-DAPO, a latent-aware RL objective with outcome-contrastive and temporal-diversity rewards.

Result: State-of-the-art results: FutureBench Qwen3-VL-8B from 61.0 to 85.4, surpassing Video-CoE by 10.4; TwiFF-Bench average from 2.44 to 3.04.

Conclusion: Preserving intermediate visual semantics in latent space enhances future-oriented video reasoning more than translating every step to text.

Abstract: Video event prediction (VEP) requires models to infer unobserved future states from partial video evidence. Existing video MLLMs usually verbalize intermediate future reasoning in text space: once visual evidence is verbalized, fine-grained motion, geometry, and interaction cues can be lost, leading to plausible but visually ungrounded hallucinations. We introduce Future-L1, an interleaved latent visual reasoning framework that lets an MLLM alternate between language tokens and continuous latent visual spans during autoregressive decoding. To train this capability, we construct Future-L1-50K by selecting examples where future visual hints help prediction and align latent states to future-frame embeddings, then further optimize sampled latent trajectories with LA-DAPO, a latent-aware RL objective with outcome-contrastive and temporal-diversity rewards. Future-L1 achieves new state-of-the-art results on both benchmarks: on FutureBench, it improves Qwen3-VL-8B from 61.0 to 85.4 and exceeds the previous best Video-CoE by 10.4 points; on TwiFF-Bench, it improves the average score from 2.44 to 3.04. These results suggest that future-oriented video reasoning benefits from preserving intermediate visual semantics in latent space rather than translating every reasoning step into text.

</details>


### [47] [LiAuto-GeoX: Efficient Grounded Driving Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05774)
*Jiawei Lian,Haoyi Sun,Yang Wu,Lifu Mu,Siyuan Wang,Le Hui,Ning Mao,Tao Wei,Pan Zhou,Kun Zhan,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A compact 155M-parameter onboard model (LiAuto-GeoX) distills a high-capacity driving geometry model from surround-view data using sparse LiDAR priors, enabling real-time dense 3D reconstruction and strong downstream autonomy performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time, ego-centric dense 3D reconstruction for autonomous driving requires scalable, geometry-faithful representations with long-range fidelity and cross-view consistency, while existing large models are too resource-intensive for onboard deployment.

Method: A two-stage approach: (1) learn a high-capacity driving geometry model from large-scale surround-view data with sparse LiDAR priors to ground geometry in distant/ambiguous regions; (2) geometry-preserving distillation to a compact 155M-parameter onboard model using (a) mask-guided depth-aware distillation to emphasize geometrically informative regions and (b) relative-pose relational distillation to enforce cross-view spatial consistency via pose-induced geometric relations.

Result: The method achieves 220 FPS on KITTI with dense reconstruction quality. It transfers geometry to downstream tasks, yielding 90.6 PDMS in trajectory prediction, 24.63 mIoU in occupancy prediction, and 47.67 IoU in future-frame prediction, demonstrating strong real-time performance and cross-task utility.

Conclusion: Efficient, geometry-grounded dense 3D reconstruction can serve as a scalable, foundational representation for autonomous driving, enabling real-time deployment and benefiting downstream perception and planning tasks.

Abstract: Dense 3D reconstruction has demonstrated immense potential for spatial understanding, yet its viability as a real-time, onboard representation for autonomous driving remains an open challenge. Existing large-scale visual geometry models typically require substantial computational resources and lack the long-range geometric fidelity, surround-view consistency, and real-time efficiency demanded by dynamic driving environments. To bridge this gap, we present \textbf{LiAuto-GeoX}, an efficient grounded driving transformer designed for deployable, ego-centric 3D scene understanding. Our approach begins by learning a high-capacity driving geometry model from large-scale surround-view data, utilizing sparse LiDAR priors to provide robust geometric grounding in distant, ambiguous, or structure-sparse regions. We then instantiate this capability into a highly compact 155M-parameter onboard model through a novel geometry-preserving distillation framework. This framework employs mask-guided depth-aware distillation to retain fine-grained metric structures by emphasizing geometrically informative regions, and relative-pose relational distillation to enforce cross-view spatial consistency through pose-induced geometric relations. Extensive evaluations reveal that \textbf{LiAuto-GeoX} runs at 220 FPS on KITTI while maintaining high-fidelity dense reconstruction, enabling real-time deployment. The learned geometry transfers seamlessly to downstream autonomy tasks, achieving 90.6 PDMS in trajectory prediction, 24.63 mIoU in occupancy prediction, and 47.67 IoU in future-frame prediction. These all demonstrate that efficient dense 3D reconstruction can transcend its traditional role as a perception target to serve as a scalable, foundational geometric representation for next-generation autonomous driving.

</details>


### [48] [Beyond Absolute Scores: Relative Edit-induced Difference for Generalizable Image Aesthetic Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05778)
*Qifei Jia,Xintong Yao,Minghao Li,Yajie Chai,Qiming Lu,Baoyue Shen,Yasen Zhang,Runyu Shi,Ying Huang,Yue Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Red-Aes introduces relative edit-induced difference learning for image aesthetic assessment, supported by the RED-20k dataset and a three-stage training regime with a relative ranking consistency reward; achieves state-of-the-art performance and better generalization across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional IAA relies on regressing absolute MOS, neglecting the dynamic, comparative nature of human aesthetic judgment and lacking causal reasoning, which harms generalization.

Method: Propose RED-Aes framework using controllable image editing models to simulate human aesthetic reasoning. Instead of predicting absolute scores, the model learns the factors driving aesthetic changes. Build RED-20k with editing-based image pairs, measured aesthetic differences, and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. Employ a three-stage training strategy guided by a relative ranking consistency reward, optimizing solely with relative supervision.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple public benchmarks and demonstrates superior generalization capabilities.

Conclusion: Relative, causally-inspired aesthetic learning improves generalization in IAA; the RED-20k dataset with CoT reasoning provides interpretable supervision and supports a shift from absolute MOS prediction to relative, change-driven learning.

Abstract: Traditional Image Aesthetic Assessment (IAA) methods mainly rely on regressing absolute Mean Opinion Scores (MOS). However, such a paradigm overlooks the inherently dynamic nature of human aesthetic perception, which relies on subconscious comparison against implicit visual references. Consequently, the lack of causal reasoning regarding aesthetic differences prevents models from learning generalizable aesthetic principles, thus limiting their generalization across diverse scenarios. In this work, we rethink the IAA task and propose Relative Edit-induced Difference Aesthetic learning (RED-Aes), a novel framework that leverages controllable image editing models to simulate the human aesthetic reasoning process. Instead of fitting absolute score distributions, RED-Aes explicitly learns the visual factors that drive aesthetic changes. To support this paradigm, we construct the RED-20k dataset, which comprises editing-based image pairs, quantitative aesthetic differences, and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. Furthermore, we introduce a three-stage training strategy guided by a relative ranking consistency reward, optimizing the model solely via relative supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RED-Aes achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple public benchmarks, exhibiting superior generalization capabilities.

</details>


### [49] [Next-Generation Parallel Decoder for LPDR: Architectural Optimization and Class-Balanced GAN-Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05785)
*Shawaiz Obaid,Nida Chandio,Neha Jamil,Muhammad Khuram Shahzad*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A real-time LPDR system enhanced with Cross-Spatial Hybrid Attention and Class-Balanced Synthetic Augmentation achieves higher minority plate recognition without sacrificing speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle spatial character mismatches and data imbalance in YOLOV5-PDLPR to improve robustness and fairness across plate categories.

Method: Introduce CSHA and CBSA; use 75,000 synthetic samples; maintain a parallel decoder; evaluate on CCPD, CLPD, PKU, and an application-specific dataset; measure recognition rate and FPS.

Result: Minority provincial license plate recognition improves from 78.2% to 91.5% while maintaining real-time processing at 152 FPS; evaluated across four benchmarks.

Conclusion: Spatially-aware parallel decoding with class-balanced augmentation is effective for high-speed LPDR in the presence of data imbalance.

Abstract: Real-Time License Plate Detection and Recognition (LPDR) forms the backbone of modern smart cities. Although the YOLOV5-PDLPR model substantially improved system efficiency through a parallel decoder approach, its performance is still affected by spatial character mismatches and data imbalance within the training set. This paper addresses these limitations by introducing Cross-Spatial Hybrid Attention (CSHA) and Class-Balanced Synthetic Augmentation (CBSA). An extensive study involving 75,000 synthetic samples is conducted and evaluated on four benchmarks: CCPD, CLPD, PKU, and an application-specific dataset. Experimental results demonstrate a substantial improvement in the recognition rate of minority provincial license plates from 78.2% to 91.5% while maintaining real-time processing performance of 152 FPS. The results indicate that spatially-aware parallel decoding combined with class-balanced augmentation provides an effective solution for high-speed license plate recognition systems.

</details>


### [50] [Emotion-Aware Image Generation from Korean Diary Text via LLM-based Prompt Translation and LoRA Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05816)
*Jihun Cho,Soo-Yeon Jeong,Sun-Young Ihm*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An emotion-aware text-to-image pipeline translates short diary text into children's drawing-style images by recognizing implicit sentiment with Qwen3-8B and generating with Stable Diffusion 3.5 (LoRA-tuned on children's drawings) using emotion-trigger words; it also analyzes the effectiveness of emotion triggers and critiques CLIP as a metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap where standard T2I models emphasize visual patterns and overlook contextual sentiment in text (e.g., diaries) and in particular aims to produce child-like drawings that reflect emotional content.

Method: 1) Use Qwen3-8B to infer implicit sentiment from short Korean diary entries. 2) Fine-tune Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium with LoRA on a dataset of children's drawings. 3) Drive image generation with emotion-based trigger words. 4) Conduct experiments to examine how different emotion triggers affect outputs and discuss CLIP-based evaluation limitations.

Result: Experiments indicate that emotion trigger words influence the generated images. The study also highlights that CLIP Score has limitations for evaluating emotion-aware image generation.

Conclusion: The proposed pipeline demonstrates promise for aligning image output with textual sentiment, particularly in a children's drawing style, but reliability of evaluation metrics like CLIP remains a concern and future work should refine emotion triggers and assessment methods.

Abstract: T2I models cannot effectively capture sentiment from various types of text, including diaries, as they primarily focus on visual object-related patterns rather than contextual emotional understanding. This paper proposes an emotion-aware text-to-image pipeline that generates children's hand drawing style images from short Korean diary entries. The proposed pipeline employs Qwen3-8B for recognising implicit sentiment from short diaries, and Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium fine-tuned with LoRA on children's drawing images with emotion-based trigger words for image generation. Additionally, this paper presents experiments examining the effect of emotion trigger words on generated images and discusses the limitations of CLIP Score as an evaluation metric for emotion-aware image generation.

</details>


### [51] [Gender Artifacts from Art History to Text-to-Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05829)
*Piera Riccio,Miriam Doh,Benedikt Höltgen,Noa Garcia,Nanne van Noord*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces StyleGender, the first dataset to study how gender representation interacts with artistic style in historical and generated images, and two Pixel/Mask SGA metrics to quantify gender signals at pixel and compositional levels; findings show gender signals influence style-conditional features, style keywords affect T2I prompts, and generation amplifies gender artifacts beyond historical sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap where AI style research treats style as a surface visual property; investigate how gender representation is encoded and amplified by style in both historical artworks and text-to-image generation.

Method: Assemble 74k images across 19 artistic styles with style and gender annotations; include art-historical images and T2I-generated images under controlled style/gender prompts; provide a semantically aligned set for direct art-history-to-generation comparison; propose two Set Gender Artifact (SGA) metrics: PixelSGA (pixel-level gender signals) and MaskSGA (compositional/structural signals).

Result: Found that gender representation shapes visual features across styles; style keywords propagate gender patterns into T2I generation; generative models tend to amplify gender artifacts beyond what is observed in historical sources.

Conclusion: The study highlights the importance of incorporating social-historical context, particularly gender, into AI-generated art research. The StyleGender dataset and SGA metrics enable systematic analysis of gender signaling across styles and generation, with implications for bias, representation, and future research directions.

Abstract: Artistic styles are rooted in specific socio-historical contexts that encode social hierarchies, including distinct constructions of gender. Yet in AI research, style has long been treated as a surface-level visual property: a filter of color, brushstroke, and texture applied to otherwise content-neutral scenes. We introduce the first dataset to investigate the interplay between gender representation and style in both historical and generated images. StyleGender comprises 74k images spanning 19 artistic styles, comprising art historical images with style and gender annotations, T2I-generated images under controlled style and gender prompts, and a semantically aligned set enabling direct art history-to-generation comparison. By proposing two Set Gender Artifact (SGA) metrics (PixelSGA and MaskSGA), capturing gender signals at the pixel level and in compositional structure, we show that (1) gender representation shapes visual features across artistic styles, (2) style keywords carry these patterns into T2I generation, and (3) generative models tend to amplify gender artifacts beyond what is observed in historical sources.

</details>


### [52] [Learning Geometric Representations from Videos for Spatial Intelligent Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05833)
*Haibo Wang,Lifu Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoVR enables 3D spatial awareness in multimodal LLMs using only 2D video data by multi-task geometry learning and distillation from 3D models, achieving state-of-the-art spatial reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs excel at 2D semantic understanding but lack intrinsic 3D awareness; scarcity of large-scale 3D data; need to enforce geometric consistency across video frames.

Method: Multi-objective learning with four geometric targets: inter-frame camera pose estimation, dense depth regression, metric scale calibration, and distillation of multi-scale 3D features from pre-trained 3D foundation models to reshape semantic latent space rather than shallow feature mixing.

Result: Empirical evaluation on spatial reasoning benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance; GeoVR establishes a new paradigm for endowing foundation models with spatial intelligence.

Conclusion: A 2D-only training paradigm can endow MLLMs with 3D awareness by explicit geometric constraints and cross-model knowledge distillation, enabling robust spatial reasoning in video and 3D contexts.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at 2D semantic understanding but lack intrinsic 3D awareness, resulting in representations that fail to maintain geometric and spatial consistency across video frames. Given the scarcity of large-scale 3D data, we present GeoVR, a novel framework that learns geometric representations using purely 2D video sequences. This approach effectively restructures the semantic latent space within MLLMs to unlock spatial intelligence. Rather than employing superficial feature mixing, GeoVR reshapes the internal representations of the MLLM by distilling geometry knowledge from pre-trained 3D foundation models. This is accomplished through a multi-objective learning strategy driven by four complementary geometric targets: (1) estimating inter-frame camera poses to embed varying viewpoint dynamics, (2) regressing dense depth maps to anchor physical distances, (3) predicting a metric scale factor for real-world calibration, and (4) distilling multi-scale 3D features to align the intermediate feature space. Guided by these explicit physical and geometric constraints, the model's internal representations naturally develop strong 3D awareness. Extensive experiments on spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GeoVR achieves state-of-the-art performance, establishing a new paradigm for endowing foundation models with spatial intelligence.

</details>


### [53] [Geometry-Aware Dataset Condensation for Diffusion Model Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05883)
*Xiao Cui,Yulei Qin,Mo Zhu,Wengang Zhou,Hongsheng Li,Houqiang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Geometry-aware dataset condensation for diffusion models via one-sided partial optimal transport, preserving distribution geometry while allowing unmatched mass in sparse regions; combines feature-statistics and semantic regularization with a two-stage discrete optimization to improve fidelity and coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
