Motivation: Diffusion-model training requires data that preserves the underlying distributional geometry. Existing condensation methods either generate low-fidelity synthetic samples or select real subsets that fail to maintain the distribution's geometry, hindering likelihood-based diffusion training.

Method: Recast subset selection as a geometry-aware distribution alignment problem using one-sided partial optimal transport to align a compact subset with the full data distribution while permitting unmatched mass in low-density regions. Augment with lightweight feature-statistics and semantic consistency regularization. Employ a two-stage discrete optimization strategy to optimize the alignment objective.

Result: Extensive experiments across diffusion variants, subset sizes, resolutions, and training rounds show superior fidelity and distributional coverage for diffusion model training compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Proposes a novel condensation approach (likely GADC) that better preserves distributional geometry for diffusion models, with open-source code. Demonstrates that geometry-aware alignment plus auxiliary regularization yields improved training outcomes.

Abstract: Dataset condensation aims to construct compact datasets from real data via synthesis or selection. However, existing approaches are ill-suited for diffusion model training: synthetic data generation often yields low-fidelity samples unsuitable for authentic modeling, while real subset selection typically fails to preserve the distributional geometry required by diffusion likelihood objectives. To address this, we propose to reformulate real subset selection as a geometry-aware distribution alignment problem. By incorporating one-sided partial optimal transport, our method selectively aligns a compact subset with the full data distribution while allowing unmatched mass in low-density regions, ensuring the preserved geometric structure necessary for effective diffusion model training. To further ensure distributional fidelity, we complement geometric alignment with lightweight feature-statistics and semantic consistency regularization. An efficient two-stage discrete optimization strategy is proposed to achieve this alignment objective. Extensive experiments across diffusion variants, subset sizes, image resolutions, and training rounds show that our method achieves superior fidelity and distributional coverage in diffusion model training. Codes are available at https://github.com/2018cx/GADC.

</details>


### [54] [Resonant Minds: Closed-Loop Social Avatars with Theory of Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05896)
*Jianxu Shangguan,Jing Xu,Hang Ye,Xiaoxuan Ma,Yizhou Wang,Wentao Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A closed-loop dual-agent framework unifies perception, social reasoning, and expression to synthesize lifelike digital humans with social intelligence, evaluated on a specialized dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between dialogue-focused LLMs and visually expressive talking-head models by integrating social cognition (Theory of Mind) with multimodal generation in a closed loop.

Method: A three-module system: perception (multimodal behavior from video), social reasoning (Theory-of-Mind-based inference with ensemble selection), expression (emotion-controllable video synthesis for speaker and listener dynamics); evaluated on a Hierarchical Persona-Scenario dataset with private social goals.

Result: Competitive or superior across dialogue quality and video generation metrics; in some cases beats full-information Script mode, indicating uncertainty-aware mental-state inference can yield more thoughtful dialogue.

Conclusion: Explicit mental-state reasoning under uncertainty enhances dialogue quality and bidirectional social dynamics; the proposed dataset supports evaluation under information asymmetry and demonstrates the value of integrating cognition with multimodal generation.

Abstract: Creating lifelike digital humans with genuine social intelligence requires unifying cognitive reasoning and multimodal generation within a coherent framework. Current approaches treat these as separate tasks: Large Language Models excel at dialogue but lack embodied expression, while diffusion-based talking head models achieve visual fidelity but ignore social cognition. To bridge this gap, we propose a closed-loop dual-agent framework integrating perception, social reasoning, and expression into a continuous interaction cycle. The perception module analyzes partners' multimodal behaviors from video, while the social reasoning module infers hidden mental states through Theory of Mind and selects responses via an ensemble mechanism. The expression module then generates emotion-controllable dual-agent videos synthesizing both speaker speech and expression alongside listener reactive behaviors, capturing bidirectional dynamics absent in prior work. We construct a hierarchical Persona-Scenario dataset with psychologically grounded personas and private social goals to support evaluation under information asymmetry. Experiments on this dataset demonstrate competitive or superior performance on both dialogue quality and video generation metrics. Notably, our method surpasses even the full-information Script mode on key dialogue quality dimensions, suggesting that explicit mental state inference under uncertainty can elicit more thoughtful dialogue than unrestricted information access.

</details>


### [55] [Self-Learning Expression Deformations for Data-Efficient Gaussian Avatars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05912)
*Jiahao Yang,Xiaohang Yang,Qing Wang,Yilan Dong,Gregory Slabaugh,Shanxin Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Self-Adaptive Gaussian Expression (SAGE) reduces data needs for dynamic facial avatars by jointly optimizing 2D Gaussian surfels with an SDF and employing self-supervised expression learning. It enables high-fidelity animation from minimal input across multiview, monocular, and one-shot regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic facial expression modeling with 3D Gaussian representations is unstructured and data-hungry. Conventional pipelines require extensive multiview and sequential data, hindering scalability. The work seeks data-efficient, flexible avatar reconstruction and animation.

Method: Joint optimization of 2D Gaussian surfels and a Signed Distance Field (SDF) to enforce compact, surface-aligned Gaussian distributions. A self-supervised expression learning phase replaces long expression sequences with geometric and appearance consistency constraints. The framework supports multiple regimes: multiview (single frame suffices), monocular (head rotations suffice, no expression sequences), and one-shot (no pretraining or priors).

Result: Achieves reconstruction and animation quality comparable to state-of-the-art methods while reducing data requirements by several orders of magnitude. Demonstrates strong data efficiency and flexible deployment across reconstruction regimes.

Conclusion: Self-supervised Gaussian deformation learning enables accessible, data-efficient avatar creation and advances the practicality of expressive 3D Gaussian avatars.

Abstract: Modeling dynamic facial expressions using 3D Gaussian representations remains challenging due to their unstructured nature. Conventional Gaussian avatar pipelines require extensive multiview and sequential expression data, limiting scalability and accessibility. In this work, we introduce Self-Adaptive Gaussian Expression (SAGE), a framework for self-learning expression-induced Gaussian deformations that enables high-fidelity, animatable avatars from minimal input data. Our method jointly optimizes 2D Gaussian surfels and a Signed Distance Field (SDF) to enforce compact, surface-aligned Gaussian distributions, while a self-supervised expression learning phase replaces long training sequences with geometric and appearance consistency constraints. This design allows flexible deployment across multiple reconstruction regimes: in the multiview setting, only a single frame (timestep) is required instead of thousands; in the monocular setting, only head rotations are needed without expression sequences; and in the one-shot setting, no pretraining or priors are necessary. Experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves reconstruction and animation quality comparable to state-of-the-art methods, while reducing data requirements by several orders of magnitude. Our results highlight the potential of self-supervised Gaussian deformation learning as a step toward accessible, data-efficient avatar creation.

</details>


### [56] [T-FunS3D: Task-Driven Hierarchical Open-Vocabulary 3D Functionality Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05975)
*Jingkun Feng,Reza Sabzevari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: T-FunS3D introduces a task-driven hierarchical open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation approach that builds an open-vocabulary scene graph from 3D point clouds and RGB-D data, identifies task-relevant instances, and localizes their functional components via a vision-language model; it achieves comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art with faster runtime and lower memory usage on SceneFun3D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation is needed for robotic perception to understand actionable parts of objects, not just whole objects. Existing methods either focus on object-level recognition or perform exhaustive, resource-intensive scene-wide segmentation, underscoring the need to balance granularity, accuracy, and speed.

Method: Inputs are 3D point clouds and posed RGB-D images. The method builds an open-vocabulary scene graph by detecting instances and extracting visual embeddings. Given a task description, it selects the most relevant instances in the scene graph and localizes their functional components by leveraging a vision-language model.

Result: On SceneFun3D, T-FunS3D is comparable to state-of-the-art in open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation, while offering faster runtime and reduced memory usage.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that a task-driven, hierarchical open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation framework can provide actionable robotic perception with improved efficiency, making it more practical for real-world applications.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation enables robots to localize functional object components in 3D scenes. It is a challenging task that requires spatial understanding and task interpretation. Current open-vocabulary 3D segmentation methods primarily focus on object-level recognition, while scene-wide part segmentation methods attempt to segment the entire scene exhaustively, making them highly resource-intensive and time consuming. Balancing segmentation performance in terms of granularity, accuracy, and speed remains a challenge. As one step towards alleviating this, we introduce T-FunS3D, a task-driven hierarchical open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation method that provides actionable perception for robotic applications. Our method takes as input the 3D point cloud and posed RGB-D images of an indoor scene. We construct an open-vocabulary scene graph by extracting instances and their visual embeddings in the environment. Given a task description, T-FunS3D identifies the most relevant instances in the scene graph and locates their functional components leveraging a vision-language model. Experiments on the SceneFun3D dataset demonstrate that T-FunS3D is comparable to state-of-the-art in open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation, while achieving faster runtime and reduced memory usage.

</details>


### [57] [CamFlow+: Hybrid Motion Bases for 2D Camera Motion Estimation with Stabilization Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05915)
*Haipeng Li,Zhen Liu,Zhanglei Yang,Hai Jiang,Tianhao Zhou,Zhengzhe Liu,Ping Tan,Bing Zeng,Shuaicheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CamFlow+ introduces a dense-flow, hybrid-basis model for 2D camera motion estimation that blends homography-based bases, stochastic homography-flow bases, and depth-derived translation bases with a depth-aware smoothness term. It relaxes single-plane constraints and improves camera-motion estimation and stabilization on GHOF-Cam, with code and datasets forthcoming.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the limitations of purely planar/homography-based methods that struggle with translation, depth variation, and parallax, by learning a dense representation that preserves camera motion regularity while handling non-planar scenes.

Method: A dense-flow representation (CamFlow+) that combines: (1) homography-derived physical bases, (2) stochastic bases sampled from homography flows, and (3) depth-translational bases derived from depth maps and intrinsics; includes a depth-aware smoothness term to regularize parallax in continuous-depth regions and preserve motion changes at depth boundaries; evaluated on GHOF-Cam."

Result: CamFlow+ yields improvements in both sparse and dense camera-motion estimation and enhances global and local stabilization in video, achieving the best top-1 preference rate in a blind user study; code and datasets will be released on the project page.

Conclusion: Hybrid-basis dense-flow modeling effectively relaxes single-plane constraints while maintaining motion regularity, improving camera-motion estimation and stabilization in non-planar scenes; the approach shows promise for robust, depth-aware motion modeling and will be accompanied by public code and data.

Abstract: Estimating 2D camera motion is fundamental to computer vision and computational photography. Existing homography-based methods work well for planar scenes or pure rotation, but struggle with camera translation, depth variation, and local parallax; local homography and mesh-based models improve flexibility but still rely on piecewise planar assumptions. We introduce CamFlow+, a hybrid-basis framework that represents 2D camera motion directly in dense-flow space. CamFlow+ combines homography-derived physical bases, stochastic bases sampled from homography flows, and depth-translational bases derived from depth and camera intrinsics, relaxing the single-plane constraint while preserving camera-motion regularity. A depth-aware smoothness term further regularizes translation-induced parallax in continuous-depth regions while preserving motion changes near depth boundaries. We evaluate CamFlow+ on GHOF-Cam, a camera-motion benchmark that masks out dynamic objects and ill-posed occlusion regions in an optical-flow benchmark to isolate camera-induced motion. Experiments show that CamFlow+ improves sparse and dense camera-motion estimation. In digital video stabilization, CamFlow+ also improves global and local stability, achieving the best top-1 preference rate in a blind user study. Code and datasets will be available on the project page: https://lhaippp.github.io/CamFlow+.

</details>


### [58] [Unveiling the Unknown: Open Vocabulary Object Detection with Scene Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05916)
*Yi Chen,Yinghao Lu,Zhehao Li,Chenchen Yan,Jiafei Wu,Chong Wang,Jiangbo Qian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Scene-guided Relational Modeling for open-vocabulary object detection using scene graphs with a Relation Attention Module and a scene-based textual alignment branch to fuse visual relations with captions, achieving superior novel-category AP on COCO and LVIS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current OVOD approaches rely on distilling knowledge from pre-trained vision-language models but ignore structured, image-specific relational cues (interactions and spatial arrangements) among objects, limiting detection of unseen categories.

Method: Propose a Scene-guided Relational Modeling framework that (1) builds scene graphs to capture semantic and spatial relations among candidate regions and context, (2) models interactions among neighboring regions, (3) employs a Relation Attention Module to amplify key relational cues, and (4) introduces a scene-based textual alignment branch that distills category knowledge from captions to guide relational alignment, enabling seamless integration of visual relations with semantic information.

Result: Empirical results show the method achieves superior performance compared to other OVOD approaches, with improved AP for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets.

Conclusion: Integrating scene graphs and caption-based alignment to fuse structured visual relations with semantic knowledge enhances open-vocabulary detection, enabling more accurate recognition of novel categories.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary object detection seeks to identify novel object categories that were not part of the training data. Many knowledge distillation-based approaches have shown promising performance by transferring knowledge from pre-trained vision-language models to object detection. However, these methods often overlook structured, image-specific relationships between objects, such as interactions and spatial arrangements. This oversight can significantly restrict the effectiveness of detecting novel categories. To address this issue, we propose a Scene-guided Relational Modeling detection framework. This framework utilizes scene graphs to capture structured semantic and spatial relationships between candidate regions and their contextual objects. It explicitly models interactions among neighboring regions and incorporates a Relation Attention Module to implicitly amplify the key relational cues extracted from the scene graph. Furthermore, we present a scene-based textual alignment branch that distills category knowledge from captions to guide relational alignment. This approach facilitates a seamless integration of visual relations with semantic information for enhanced detection performance. Comprehensive experiments show that our model achieves superior performance compared to other OVOD methods, improving the AP for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets.

</details>


### [59] [Synthetic Data Generation and Vision-based Wrinkle and Keypoint Detection for Bimanual Cloth Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06292)
*Ariel Herrera,Xueyang Kang,Atal Anil Kumar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A synthetic-to-real perception pipeline for textile manipulation: auto-annotated Blender-based keypoints combined with real data, CNN-based permutation-invariant keypoint detection and YOLO-based wrinkle grasping; a bimanual manipulation workflow transitioning to ironing; MPE 1.76 px; transfers to real fabrics without fine-tuning and outperforms baselines under occlusion and severe folds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust perception for textile manipulation under continuous deformation and self-occlusions and the lack of annotated real-world data; bridging simulation-to-real by synthetic data to enable reliable wrinkle-based grasping and ironing.

Method: Blender-based synthetic pipeline generating auto-annotated keypoints, combined with manually labeled renders and real data to train a wrinkle detector. A perception framework with a CNN for permutation-invariant keypoint detection and a YOLOv8-OpenCV pipeline to extract grasping points from structural wrinkles. A bimanual algorithm stretches garments via wrinkles and transitions to keypoint-based ironing once corners emerge.

Result: Keypoint model achieves Mean Position Error (MPE) of 1.7615 pixels. The perception system transfers to physical fabrics without fine-tuning and outperforms baselines that fail in high-occlusion states or yield false positives on severe folds.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates a robust perception-and-manipulation pipeline for textiles using synthetic-to-real data fusion, enabling reliable wrinkle-based grasping and ironing with improved performance under occlusion and severe folds, generalizing to real fabrics.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation of textiles remains challenging because continuous deformation and self-occlusions hinder the robust visual perception required to estimate the cloth's state. To address the lack of annotated real-world data, we developed a Blender-based synthetic pipeline exporting auto-annotated keypoints, and combined manually labeled renders with real-world data to train a wrinkle detector. We present a perception framework integrating a CNN for permutation-invariant keypoint detection and a YOLOv8-OpenCV pipeline to extract grasping points from structural wrinkles. A proposed bimanual algorithm uses this system to stretch fully folded garments via wrinkles, transitioning to keypoint-based ironing once corners emerge. The keypoint model achieves a Mean Position Error (MPE) of 1.7615 pixels. The perception system transfers to physical fabrics without fine-tuning, outperforming baselines that fail in high-occlusion states or yield false positives on severe folds.

</details>


### [60] [MemoryCard: Topic-Aware Multi-Modal Clue Compression for Long-Video Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05917)
*Qing Yang,Pengcheng Huang,Xinze Li,Zhenghao Liu,Yukun Yan,Yu Gu,Ge Yu,Gang Li,Maosong Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MemoryCard introduces a memory-based augmentation for long-video QA that organizes videos into event-level Memory Cards via self-reading, improving accuracy under similar visual-token budgets (up to 21.8% relative gain).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-video QA suffers from sparse, transient, and dispersed evidence across long videos; frame-centric methods struggle to capture coherent event-level semantics.

Method: A self-reading process segments videos (and aligned utterances) into semantically coherent units (topics/events). For each unit, generate an event-level gist and select representative moments, render these into Memory Cards used for retrieval and QA.

Result: MemoryCard yields consistent improvements in long-video QA performance under comparable visual-token budgets, with up to 21.8% relative accuracy gain; code released at GitHub.

Conclusion: MemoryCard demonstrates that event-level memory cards can effectively capture coherent semantics and improve efficiency and accuracy in long-video question answering.

Abstract: Long-video question answering remains challenging for Vision-Language Models (VLMs), as answer-relevant evidence is often sparse, transient, and temporally dispersed across lengthy video contexts. Existing frame-centric approaches improve efficiency through uniform sampling, query-aware frame selection, visual-token compression, and adaptive resolution strategies. However, they still rely on isolated and fragmented frames as the fundamental evidence units, limiting VLMs' ability to effectively capture coherent event-level semantics. To address this limitation, we propose MemoryCard, a video-memory-based augmentation framework that organizes long videos into self-contained Memory Cards. Specifically, MemoryCard first performs a self-reading process over videos and aligned utterances to segment the video into semantically coherent units, each corresponding to a distinct topic or event. For each unit, it generates an event-level video gist and selects representative visual moments, which are then rendered into unified Memory Cards for retrieval and question answering. Experimental results demonstrate that MemoryCard consistently improves long-video QA performance under comparable visual-token budgets, achieving up to a 21.8% relative improvement in accuracy. All code is available at https://github.com/NEUIR/MemoryCard.

</details>


### [61] [Faithful, Enriched, and Precise: Benchmarking Natural-Science Illustration Generation by T2I models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05949)
*Yifan Chang,Jiaxin Ai,Jianwen Sun,Yuandong Pu,Siqi Luo,Liangliang Zhao,Yuchen Ren,Minghao Liu,Yunfei Yu,Yu Qiao,Kaipeng Zhang,Yihao Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces FEPBench, a fine-grained benchmark for evaluating Text-to-Image models on scientific illustration, using atom-set annotations and multi-dimensional evaluation (instruction faithfulness, reasoning enrichment, semantic precision) across visual/textual/relational/layout aspects; reports bottlenecks in text rendering and reasoning in SOTA models; plans to release data and code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks largely assess holistic image quality and miss fine-grained, domain-specific reasoning and conciseness crucial for scientific illustrations. A need exists for structured annotation and multi-faceted evaluation to drive improvements in scientific T2I generation.

Method: Construct FEPBench from high-quality scientific illustrations across disciplines and layout types. Use multimodal language models and human experts to provide fine-grained atom-set annotations. Evaluate T2I models along three axes—instruction faithfulness, reasoning enrichment, and semantic precision—and decompose performance across visual, textual, relation, and layout elements.

Result: Findings indicate text-rendering bottlenecks, limited reasoning enrichment, and difficulty balancing generation richness with precision in SOTA models (e.g., GPT Image 2, Nano Banana Pro). Benchmark will provide actionable guidance for improving scientific illustration generation. Data, atom annotations, and evaluation code will be released.

Conclusion: FEPBench enables nuanced, multi-dimensional evaluation of scientific illustration generation, highlighting specific bottlenecks and offering a path toward improved model design and deployment in scientific contexts.

Abstract: Scientific illustrations are essential tools for communicating research findings, especially in natural science, where they visualize complex concepts and processes. As Text-to-Image (T2I) models become increasingly capable, researchers have started to use them for scientific illustration generation. However, existing benchmarks often assess outputs at a holistic level, overlooking fine-grained elements, while scientific reasoning ability and output conciseness remain under-quantified. We introduce FEPBench, a benchmark built from carefully selected high-quality scientific illustrations across multiple disciplines and layout types. With the assistance of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and human experts, we provide fine-grained atom set annotations and systematically evaluate T2I models along three dimensions: instruction faithfulness, reasoning enrichment, and semantic precision. Our evaluation further decomposes model performance across visual, textual, relation, and layout elements. Results show that even state-of-the-art (SOTA) closed-source models, such as GPT Image 2 and Nano Banana Pro, still suffer from text-rendering bottlenecks, limited reasoning enrichment, and difficulty balancing generation richness with precision. These findings provide practical guidance for improving and deploying T2I models in scientific illustration generation. Benchmark data, atom set annotations, and evaluation code will be released by us.

</details>


### [62] [Video-Rate Streaming Stylization on a Vision-Aware MLLM-Conditioned Edit Diffusion: Asymmetric Batched Inference on a Distilled UNet + MLLM Text Encoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05981)
*Yoshiyuki Ootani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A streaming pipeline counters the text-encoder bottleneck in aggressively distilled diffusion U-Nets for vision-aware editing, using three engineering mechanisms to achieve video-rate throughput on consumer GPUs while evaluating generalization of an oil-painting style.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When the diffusion U-Net is aggressively distilled to 4- or 1-step, the text encoder (often a large multimodal LLM) becomes the bottleneck, especially in vision-aware edit diffusion. There is a need for practical streaming techniques to maintain throughput at video rates.

Method: Three engineering mechanisms: (1) asymmetric side-stream / main-stream CUDA pipelining with batched text-encoder amortisation (and optional static-prompt caching); (2) a compile-friendly ControlNet-LLLite reformulation folding the U-Net + adapter stack into a single fused graph; (3) a periodic conditioning-refresh schedule with a hook subset to amortise per-frame conditioning. The study uses a 0.39B distilled edit U-Net with a 2.13B MLLM (Qwen3-VL) and reports streaming performance on consumer GPUs (RTX 3090 Ti/4090/5090) at 512x512.

Result: On RTX 3090 Ti at 512x512: 27.4 fps (B=8) and 29.6 fps (B=16) with end-to-end p50 latency ~0.5s and ~1.0s; RTX 4090: 54.9 fps; RTX 5090: 74.1 fps. The results are contextualized against StreamDiffusion re-runs, not presented as a universal benchmark. For the trained oil-painting style, the temporal adapter generalises to 19 unused DAVIS-2017 sequences and 15 non-DAVIS clips from seven sources; prompt-level generalisation to unseen style families is bounded and reported separately.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates practical streaming throughput for vision-aware diffusion editing with distilled U-Nets and heavy MLLM encoders by combining three architecture/algorithmic mechanisms; results show substantial speedups at video-rate scales on modern GPUs, with quantified but limited generalisation to unseen style prompts.

Abstract: Aggressive distillation of the diffusion U-Net inverts the per-frame bottleneck of real-time text-to-image pipelines: once the denoiser is a 4-step or 1-step distilled student, the text encoder becomes the critical path. This inversion is most acute in vision-aware edit diffusion, where the encoder is a multimodal large language model (MLLM). We study the case of a 0.39B distilled edit U-Net paired with a 2.13B MLLM text encoder (Qwen3-VL) and present a streaming pipeline targeted at this regime built around three engineering mechanisms: asymmetric side-stream / main-stream CUDA pipelining with batched text-encoder amortisation (and optional static-prompt caching), a compile-friendly ControlNet-LLLite reformulation that folds the entire U-Net + adapter stack into a single fused graph, and a periodic conditioning-refresh schedule with a hook subset that amortises the per-frame conditioning cost. On a single consumer RTX 3090 Ti at 512x512 the pipeline sustains 27.4 fps over a 480-frame run at batch size B=8 and 29.6 fps at B=16, with end-to-end p50 latency of approximately 0.5 and 1.0 seconds respectively; the same operating point measures 54.9 fps on RTX 4090 and 74.1 fps on RTX 5090. We report video-rate streaming throughput rather than interactive low latency, and locate our numbers against same-stack StreamDiffusion re-runs as systems context, not as a benchmark superiority claim. For the trained oil-painting style, the released temporal adapter generalises within in-clip noise to 19 unused DAVIS-2017 sequences and 15 non-DAVIS clips from seven sources; prompt-level generalisation to unseen style families is bounded and reported separately.

</details>


### [63] [Multimodal Sexism Identification and Characterization using Large Language Models and Gradient Boosting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05997)
*Kyriakos Chaviaras,Maria Lymperaiou,Athanasios Voulodimos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Feature-engineered late-fusion multimodal system for meme and short-video sexism detection. Memes benefit from LLM-derived semantic cues; videos require robust temporal modeling and careful feature dimensionality, with mixed generalization to unseen data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address multimodal sexism identification in memes and short-form videos (CLEF EXIST 2026 Lab tasks 2 and 3) and investigate how feature engineering and representation choices affect performance and generalization.

Method: A feature-engineered late-fusion pipeline using gradient-boosted regression models and hierarchical post-processing. For memes: combine visual, textual, demographic, biometric, and LLM-derived semantic indicators to capture cues like stereotyping and misogyny. For videos: explore feature selection, frame-based visual representations, OCR-based textual features, acoustic descriptors, and sensor metadata.

Result: Development results show LLM-derived semantic cues improve meme sexism identification. Video performance is sensitive to feature dimensionality and cross-modal noise. In development, compact feature selection is favored for videos, but official test results indicate this does not fully transfer to unseen data; unfiltered representations generalize better.

Conclusion: Targeted semantic feature engineering is useful for static memes; robust temporal modeling is needed for noisy short-form videos to improve generalization to unseen data.

Abstract: We present the AILS-NTUA submission to the EXIST 2026 Lab at CLEF, addressing multimodal sexism identification and characterization in memes (Task 2) and short-form videos (Task 3). Our system follows a feature-engineered late-fusion pipeline built around gradient-boosted regression models and hierarchical post-processing. For memes, we combine visual, textual, demographic, biometric, and LLM-derived semantic indicators designed to capture high-level cues such as stereotyping, objectification, irony, and misogyny. For videos, we investigate the effect of feature selection, frame-based visual representations, OCR-based textual features, acoustic descriptors, and sensor-derived metadata. Development results show that focused LLM-derived semantic cues improve meme sexism identification, while video performance is highly sensitive to feature dimensionality and cross-modal noise. For videos, development results favor compact feature selection, but official test results show that this conclusion does not fully transfer to unseen data, where the unfiltered representation generalizes better. Overall, our findings highlight the usefulness of targeted semantic feature engineering for static memes and the need for more robust temporal modeling in noisy short-form video settings.

</details>


### [64] [Deep Learning-based 3D Oral Cavity Reconstruction Using 2D Intraoral Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05998)
*Jihun Cho,Soo-Yeon Jeong,Eun-Jeong Bae,Sun-Young Ihm*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hardware-free 3D oral reconstruction is proposed from ten 2D intraoral images using MobileNetV2 encoding and multi-head attention for multi-view fusion; evaluated on Dental3DS with 950 samples, achieving 77.49% accuracy (NN with 0.035 threshold).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce cost, patient discomfort, and dependence on expensive intraoral scanners by enabling automated 3D reconstruction from standard photos.

Method: Ten 2D intraoral images from different angles are encoded by MobileNetV2 and fused via a multi-head attention mechanism to reconstruct a 3D model; trained on Dental3DS (950 upper jaw samples). Evaluation via nearest-neighbor accuracy with distance threshold 0.035.

Result: Achieves 77.49% accuracy; produces viable 3D models without dedicated hardware; however, vertices concentrate in high-density ground-truth regions, leading to uneven point distribution.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of cost-effective 2D-to-3D oral reconstruction; effective hardware-free approach but requires improvements to ensure uniform vertex distribution and possibly more robust evaluation and generalization across datasets.

Abstract: Oral 3D modelling is one of the most essential stages in dentistry, and many different approaches, such as impression taking and intraoral scanning, are commonly used for this phase, each with notable limitations. Impression taking, which involves placing alginate or silicone material in a tray and inserting it into the patient's oral cavity to form a negative mold, suffers from significant patient discomfort, material deformation errors, and difficulties in storage and transportation. Intraoral scanners, which directly scan oral structures in real time using structured light or laser technology, produce state-of-the-art results but are associated with substantially high equipment costs. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a software-based approach that reconstructs a 3D oral model using only ten 2D intraoral images captured from different angles, requiring no dedicated hardware devices. The proposed method reduces cost, eliminates the need for physical scanning equipment, minimises patient discomfort, and enables automated 3D reconstruction. The model is trained on the publicly available Dental3DS dataset, comprising 950 upper jaw samples, and employs MobileNetV2 as the image encoder combined with Multi-head Attention for multi-view feature fusion. The proposed model achieves an accuracy of 77.49%, measured by nearest-neighbor matching with a distance threshold of 0.035. However, predicted vertices tend to concentrate in high-density regions of the ground truth, resulting in uneven point distribution across the reconstructed model.

</details>


### [65] [ATT-CR: Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05999)
*Yang Wu,Ye Deng,Pengna Li,Wenli Huang,Kangyi Wu,Xiaomeng Xin,Jinjun Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ATT-CR introduces a scalable transformer for cloud removal by using triangular attention (TAN) with O(N) complexity and a gating module (FSGM) to filter cloudy features, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer-based cloud removal methods suffer from high self-attention complexity and interference from cloudy pixels treated as valid within attention, which degrades performance and scalability.

Method: Proposes two components: Triangular Attention (TAN), which uses lower and upper triangular matrices to approximate Softmax attention with O(N) complexity; and Feature Selected Gating Module (FSGM), which adaptively distinguishes between cloudy and clean features to minimize invalid information propagation.

Result: Extensive experiments on cloud removal benchmarks show ATT-CR delivers superior performance compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: ATT-CR effectively reduces computational costs and mitigates interference from cloudy pixels, achieving state-of-the-art results on cloud removal benchmarks.

Abstract: Cloud removal aims to accurately reconstruct the ground objects obscured by clouds in remote sensing images. Existing Transformer-based methods utilizing self-attention have shown impressive results by effectively modeling long-range dependencies in cloudy images. However, they suffer from the following issues: 1) the high computational complexity of self-attention limits scalability; 2) treating both cloudy and clean pixels as valid within the attention computation brings disturbances in subsequent layers, leading to suboptimal performance. To address these challenges, we propose the Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal (ATT-CR), a model that effectively reduces computational costs and mitigates interference from cloudy pixels. Specifically, it consists of two core components: Triangular Attention (TAN) and Feature Selected Gating Module (FSGM). TAN employs lower and upper triangular matrices to approximate Softmax attention with O(N) computational complexity, significantly reducing the computational costs. The FSGM, on the other hand, integrates with TAN to adaptively distinguish between cloudy and clean features, which minimizes the introduction of invalid information into subsequent layers. Extensive experiments on cloud removal benchmarks demonstrate that ATT-CR delivers superior performance compared to existing methods.

</details>


### [66] [Global-Local Monte Carlo Tree Search in Vision-Language Models for Text-to-3D Indoor Scene Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06002)
*Mengshi Qi,Wei Deng,Xianlin Zhang,Huadong Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A planning-based LVLM framework for text-to-3D indoor scene generation using global/local tree search, hierarchical scene representation, and PRM-guided MCTS, plus a diffusion-based texture predictor and a new 3DTindo-bench dataset; yields more realistic scenes than prior SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-3D indoor scene generation with LVLMs suffers from error propagation in chain-of-thought sequential decisions and limited ability to enforce spatial/layout commonsense; a planning approach that constrains exploration can improve realism and consistency.

Method: Model the task as a two-level tree search (global and local). Use a hierarchical scene representation with room, region, floor object, and supported object levels. Employ PRM-guided MCTS to prune search branches and balance exploration/exploitation, enabling fewer attempts. Decompose object placement into sub-steps for precise parameters. Predict textures with pre-trained diffusion models. Create 3DTindo-bench, a large-scale diverse dataset (65 scene types, 3,250 instructions).

Result: The method generates more realistic 3D indoor scenes than state-of-the-art approaches on the proposed benchmarks, demonstrating improved planning-based synthesis.

Conclusion: Framing text-to-3D scene generation as a planning problem with hierarchical structure and PRM-guided MCTS, complemented by diffusion-based texture prediction and a large diverse dataset, yields superior realism and scalability over existing LVLM-led sequential approaches.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models have achieved significant reasoning performance in various tasks.However, there are few studies on text-to-3D indoor scene generation with LVLMs. The main challenge is that prevailing LVLM-based methods employ chain-of-thought sequential decision mechanisms that cannot revise earlier decisions, causing error propagation.In this paper, we consider the task as a planning problem constrained by spatial and layout commonsense.To solve this problem, we model it as a tree search problem with global and local trees, which differs from existing sequential decision-making approaches.In the global tree, we place each object iteratively and explore multiple attempts like humans furnishing a room, where the problem space is represented as a tree.To effectively search the tree, we propose a hierarchical scene representation and a PRM-guided MCTS method.The hierarchical representation abstracts a scene into room level, region level, floor object level, and supported object level.The PRM-guided MCTS method uses the PRM to prune unnecessary branches and the MCTS algorithm to balance exploration and exploitation to get an optimal solution with fewer attempts.In the local tree, it further decomposes the placement of each object into finer sub-steps, including the specific placement parameters.To make the whole appearance of the scene consistent, we leverage pre-trained diffusion image generative models to predict textures for all the objects in the scene.As existing benchmarks for text-to-3D indoor scene generation remain limited in scale and diversity, we collect a new large-scale diverse dataset that contains 65 scene types and 3,250 instructions with diverse sizes, layouts, and styles, named 3DTindo-bench, to better assess the capability of the state-of-the-art models. Our experiments show that our method generates more realistic 3D scenes than state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [67] [ReSAGE-PAR: Representational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06020)
*Pablo Ayuso-Albizu,Pablo Carballeira,Juan C. SanMiguel,Paula Moral*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based data-augmentation pipeline (ReSAGE-PAR) for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR) that adapts diffusion models to PAR resolutions, evaluates generated images with vision-language alignment prompts, and uses a Bayesian classifier to produce binary pseudo-labels, achieving up to 8.7% accuracy gains and enabling scalable, domain-gap-bridging PAR enhancement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity and limited diversity in PAR; overcome domain gap between high-quality diffusion-video pretraining data and low-resolution surveillance crops; ensure reliable attribute verification to combat generative hallucinations.

Method: 1) Adapt pre-trained diffusion models to PAR resolutions via a tailored LoRA-based Image-to-Image approach. 2) Compute vision-language alignment scores between generated images and conditional prompts using a prompting strategy with label-consistent and inconsistent complements. 3) Use a Bayesian classifier to convert continuous alignment scores into binary pseudo-labels. 4) Integrate into PAR training to expand dataset and improve performance.

Result: ReSAGE-PAR preserves spatial priors in generated data, provides verifiable attributes, and yields significant improvements when used to expand PAR datasets—up to 8.7% gains on standard backbones and state-of-the-art performance increases. Public code available.

Conclusion: ReSAGE-PAR is an architecture-agnostic solution for scalable PAR enhancement, bridging domain gaps with a robust generate-score-auto-label loop and enabling high-fidelity dataset expansion for PAR.

Abstract: To address the limited diversity and data scarcity in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR), we explore image synthesis using diffusion models guided by attribute-based prompts. While this enables the controlled generation of pedestrian images, it faces two critical challenges: (i) the domain gap between high-quality pre-training data and low-resolution, non-standard surveillance crops, and (ii) the need for reliable attribute verification to prevent generative hallucinations. In this paper, we introduce a robust generate-score-autolabel pipeline called ReSAGE-PAR (REpresentational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in PAR) that bridges this domain gap and enables scalable, high-fidelity dataset expansion. First, we adapt pre-trained diffusion models to native PAR resolutions using a tailored LoRA-based Image-to-Image approach. Second, we extract vision-language alignment scores between the generated images and their conditioning prompts, utilizing a comprehensive prompting strategy that includes label-consistent and inconsistent complements. Finally, we formulate a Bayesian classifier that converts these continuous scores into reliable binary pseudo-labels. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of ReSAGE-PAR in preserving spatial priors and verifying attributes. When integrated into PAR training, ReSAGE-PAR consistently yields significant improvements-achieving gains of up to 8.7% on standard backbones and pushing state-of-the-art frameworks to new performance levels. This proves its value as an architecture-agnostic solution for scalable PAR enhancement. The complete codebase for ReSAGE-PAR is publicly available at http://www-vpu.eps.uam.es/publications/ReSAGE-PAR.

</details>


### [68] [Texture-preserving implicit neural representation for Cone beam CT truncated reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06039)
*Genyuan Zhang,Junyao Wang,Haoran Lan,Chuandong Tan,Songtao Zhu,Fenglin Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised 3D neural-scene reconstruction for truncated CBCT that maps coordinates to radiodensity, avoiding traditional filtering/backprojection, eliminating truncation artifacts and enabling continuous 3D extrapolation; its spectral bias is addressed by a physics-based iterative refinement that reintroduces high-frequency details from projections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Truncation and limited FOV in CBCT create artifacts; supervised methods depend on ground-truth data and fail to handle continuous 3D truncation variations; a self-supervised, coordinate-based approach can bypass filtering/backprojection and model 3D structure directly.

Method: Use a coordinate network to map (x,y,z) to radiodensity with projection supervision; bypass standard FBP; add a physics-based iterative refinement module that uses the extrapolated volume as initialization to progressively re-extract/inject high-frequency details from the projections.

Result: Experiments on simulated and real data show effective artifact suppression, robust 3D extrapolation, and better preservation of fine details compared to baselines, leveraging both neural prior and iterative refinement.

Conclusion: The proposed framework unifies artifact suppression and extrapolation of truncated CBCT with high-fidelity detail preservation, overcoming spectral bias and truncation-induced rings through a synergistic neural-scene + iterative refinement approach.

Abstract: Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) frequently suffers from data truncation, which introduces severe artifacts and limits the effective field of view (FOV). Existing deep learning methods for truncated cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) reconstruction suffer from serious limitations, including a strict reliance on supervised ground truth and a failure to account for continuous 3D spatial truncation variations. To address these challenges, we introduce a self-supervised 3D reconstruction framework based on neural scene representations. By directly mapping spatial coordinates to radiodensity under projection supervision, our approach inherently bypasses traditional filtering and backprojection operations, thereby fundamentally eliminating truncation-induced ring artifacts while enabling robust continuous 3D data extrapolation. However, coordinate networks are susceptible to an inherent spectral bias, which leads to a severe loss of clinically vital high-frequency textures. To resolve this bottleneck, we further incorporate a physics-based iterative refinement module into the neural scene representation architecture. Leveraging the artifact-free, extrapolated volume from the coordinate network as an optimal initialization, this module progressively re-extracts and injects high-frequency structural information from the original projections back into the volume. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method successfully unifies the exceptional artifact suppression and extrapolation capabilities of neural networks with the high-fidelity detail preservation of iterative algorithms.

</details>


### [69] [LoomVideo: Unifying Multimodal Inputs into Video Generation and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06042)
*Jianzong Wu,Hao Lian,Jiongfan Yang,Dachao Hao,Ye Tian,Yunhai Tong,Jingyuan Zhu,Biaolong Chen,Qiaosong Qi,Aixi Zhang,Wanggui He,Mushui Liu,Jinlong Liu,Hao Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A 5B-parameter unified video generation/editing model, LoomVideo, achieves zero-overhead conditioning, faster inference, and competitive performance by integrating a Multimodal LLM, Deepstack injection, Scale-and-Add conditioning, and Negative Temporal RoPE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome inefficiencies of existing unified video models that rely on very large (13B+) parameterizations and token-concatenation for editing, which expands sequence length and inflates self-attention costs.

Method: LoomVideo uses a 5B parameter architecture, replaces the text encoder with a Multimodal LLM, employs Deepstack injection to align MLLM features with the Diffusion Transformer, implements zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning for editing by directly adding the source latent to the noised target latent (no token concatenation), and incorporates Negative Temporal RoPE to handle multiple reference images.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance on standard benchmarks, with particular strength in e-commerce and fashion generation; delivers at least 5.41x faster inference speed than similarly capable models, demonstrating practical efficiency.

Conclusion: A compact, efficient, unified video foundation model is feasible, delivering strong generation/editing capabilities with significantly reduced compute via zero-overhead conditioning and efficient feature integration.

Abstract: Developing unified video generation and editing models capable of interpreting interleaved multimodal inputs is a promising yet challenging frontier field. Existing unified frameworks predominantly rely on massive models (typically 13B parameters or more) and incorporate source video conditions for editing by concatenating sequence tokens. This concatenation inevitably doubles the sequence length, quadrupling the computational complexity of the self-attention mechanism and introducing prohibitive overhead. To address these bottlenecks, we present LoomVideo, a highly efficient 5B-parameter unified architecture for both video generation and editing. LoomVideo replaces the standard text encoder with a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) and employs Deepstack injection mechanism to align multi-layer MLLM features with the Diffusion Transformer (DiT). Crucially, we introduce a zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning approach for video editing. By scaling and directly adding the clean source video latent to the noised target latent, this elegant design eliminates the need for token concatenation, drastically reducing computational cost while maintaining robust capabilities for complex, non-rigid edits. Furthermore, a Negative Temporal RoPE strategy is seamlessly integrated to handle multiple reference images. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our compact 5B model achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across comprehensive benchmarks, exhibiting exceptional superiority in e-commerce and fashion generation scenarios. Benefiting from the zero-overhead conditioning mechanism, LoomVideo achieves at least a 5.41x acceleration in inference speed compared to models of similar capabilities, paving the way for highly practical and efficient video foundation models.

</details>


### [70] [LLM-Conditioned Synthesis of Pathological Gaits via Structured Gait-Language Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06048)
*Mritula Chandrasekaran,Sanket Kachole,Jarik Francik,Dimitrios Makris*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LLM-guided multimodal framework synthesizes fixed-length skeleton gait sequences from textual descriptions to aid pathology classification; introduces a pathological tokeniser; improved GRU performance with real+synthetic data achieving 92.77% LOSO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scarcity of pathological gait datasets due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and variability; need synthetic data to augment training.

Method: Integrates motion tokenisation, pathology-aware language conditioning, LLM-based semantic augmentation, and language-to-gait generation; includes a novel pathological tokenizer to preserve pathology-specific motion; generates fixed-length sequences; evaluates with leave-one-subject-out for recurrent classifiers, best is GRU with real+synthetic.

Result: Synthetic sequences improve downstream classification for recurrent classifiers when combined with real data; best accuracy 92.77% with GRU in LOSO.

Conclusion: Demonstrates viability of LLM-guided synthetic gait data for pathology classification and the importance of specialized tokenisation to preserve pathology features; potential to alleviate dataset scarcity.

Abstract: Pathological gait datasets remain scarce due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and movement variability. Our work presents a multimodal LLM-guided framework for pathology-aware 3D gait data synthesis from structured textual descriptions. The proposed method generates fixed-length synthetic skeleton-based gait sequences for pathological gait classification tasks. The framework combines motion tokenisation, pathology-aware language conditioning, LLM-based semantic augmentation, and language-to-gait generation. A key contribution is the proposed pathological tokeniser, which is designed to preserve pathology-specific motion characteristics during discrete representation learning. Experiments suggest that the proposed synthetic sequences improve downstream classification for recurrent classifiers when combined with real data. The best result is obtained using a GRU classifier trained with real and synthetic samples, achieving 92.77\% accuracy under a leave-one-subject-out protocol.

</details>


### [71] [ReCache: Learning Budget-Aware Caching Schedules for Diffusion Models via REINFORCE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06060)
*Mishan Aliev,Eva Neudachina,Ilya Bykov,Aleksandr Oganov,Kirill Struminsky,Aibek Alanov,Denis Rakitin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReCache learns a recomputation schedule for diffusion model inference under a target compute budget, converting compute into a controllable input and outperforming fixed/adaptive baselines across caching strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To convert the opaque compute cost of diffusion sampling into a user-controllable resource by optimizing caching schedules rather than rely on hand-tuned thresholds.

Method: Policy-gradient training that optimizes a recomputation schedule given a FLOPs budget k, without backprop through full diffusion; uses uncached inference as targets and a reward based on generation quality; compatible with feature reuse and feature forecasting caching; no labeled data; budget-conditioned policy.

Result: Outperforms baselines: at ~5x FLOPs reduction on FLUX, LPIPS improves from 0.456 to 0.316 (−31%); at ~2.6x speedup on Wan, LPIPS improves from 0.480 to 0.169 (−65%); VBench score rises from 70.4 to 76.0 (+7%). Code released.

Conclusion: A practical, budget-aware caching strategy for diffusion models; policy-gradient learning enables adaptable, high-quality generation across budgets and is compatible with various caching mechanisms.

Abstract: Modern diffusion models generate high-quality images and videos, but their iterative denoising process makes inference expensive. Feature caching accelerates sampling by reusing or predicting intermediate activations across neighboring denoising steps, exploiting the redundancy of computations along the reverse trajectory. In this work, we focus on the caching schedule: selecting which denoising steps should be fully recomputed. Existing schedules are either fixed (e.g. uniform) or chosen adaptively from per-step error heuristics; in both cases, the actual compute cost is a side-effect of hand-tuned thresholds rather than a quantity the user can specify. We propose ReCache, which inverts this: given a target budget k, it learns the recomputation schedule that maximizes generation quality, turning compute into a directly controllable input. ReCache trains via policy gradients, sidestepping backpropagation through full diffusion inference, and uses no labelled data. Generations from uncached inference serve as matching targets, paired with a reward for generation quality. ReCache is compatible with any caching mechanism, including feature reuse and feature forecasting; for each mechanism, a single trained policy adapts across computational budgets at inference time. ReCache consistently outperforms scheduling baselines: under a $\times5.04$ FLOPs reduction on FLUX, it reduces LPIPS by 31% (from 0.456 to 0.316) compared to DiCache; on Wan 2.1 at a $\sim \times2.6$ speedup, it drops LPIPS by 65% (from 0.480 to 0.169) and boosts the VBench score by 7% (5.6 points, from 70.4 to 76.0) over uniform HiCache. Code is available at https://github.com/thecrazymage/ReCache.

</details>


### [72] [FontFusion: Enhancing Generative Text in Diffusion Models with Typographic Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06066)
*Marian Lupascu,Nipun Jindal,Ionut Mironica,Zhaowen Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FontFusion improves typography control in diffusion-based font generation via hierarchical tokens, position-aware embeddings, and multi-level token dropping; uses a dual encoder (DeepFont + DINOv2); achieves strong gains with plug-and-play compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the trade-off between precise font control and text legibility in diffusion typography, enabling faithful typography without sacrificing readability.

Method: A plug-and-play conditioning framework for Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures with three innovations: (1) hierarchical token representation linking text and font at multiple granularities, (2) position-aware embeddings for spatial typography-image binding, (3) multi-level token dropping to improve efficiency and generalization to unseen fonts; also identifies a dual encoder (DeepFont + DINOv2) as superior for typography tasks and demonstrates integration without retraining.

Result: FontFusion yields 76% relative improvement on decorative fonts versus single-encoder baselines and font consistency gains of ~68-76% over unconditioned models; demonstrates compatibility with existing DiT architectures without retraining.

Conclusion: FontFusion provides an effective, scalable conditioning mechanism for diffusion-based typography, balancing control and readability while preserving compatibility with current DiT models and enhancing performance across diverse font styles.

Abstract: Typography generation in diffusion models faces a persistent trade-off: enabling precise font control typically degrades text legibility, while maintaining readability often sacrifices typographic fidelity. We present FontFusion, a plug-and-play conditioning framework for Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures that resolves this dilemma through three core innovations: (1) a hierarchical token representation establishing explicit text-font relationships at multiple granularities, (2) position-aware embeddings creating spatial bindings between typography and image content, and (3) a multi-level token dropping strategy improving both computational efficiency and generalization to unseen fonts. Our systematic evaluation of font embedding spaces reveals that a dual encoder combining DeepFont and DINOv2 outperforms any single encoder for typography tasks. FontFusion demonstrates 76% relative improvement on challenging decorative fonts over single-encoder baselines and font consistency gains exceeding approximately 68-76% over unconditioned models, while integrating into existing DiT architectures without retraining.

</details>


### [73] [VZCrash: A Large-Scale IMU Dataset of Ego-Vehicle Crashes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06074)
*Tommaso Bianconcini,Henrique Piñeiro Monteagudo,Aurel Pjetri,Tomaso Trinci,Leonardo Taccari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A large-scale, real-world V2X crash dataset (VZCrash) with IMU and GPS data for 31k crashes and 158k negative samples, enabling benchmarking of crash detection models and demonstrating the critical role of data scale for real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a realistic, large-scale dataset of real-world vehicle crashes to train, benchmark, and evaluate crash detection algorithms, addressing limitations of small or synthetic datasets and the need for robust deployment.

Method: Dataset assembled from fleet data (73,010 vehicles) across the US over multiple years. Each sample contains 100 Hz acceleration and angular velocity, 1 Hz GPS speed, with labeled crashes and negatives. The study benchmarks a range of approaches from threshold-based heuristics to deep learning, and conducts scaling experiments to assess performance gains and deployment reliability.

Result: VZCrash constitutes the largest publicly available real-world crash dataset with rich IMU/GPS features and diverse samples. Benchmarking shows performance improvements with scale; data scaling significantly enhances model effectiveness, particularly for real-world deployment.

Conclusion: A scalable, high-volume real-world dataset substantially improves crash-detection modeling and generalization. The work emphasizes the importance of data scale for deployment, and suggests directions for future enhancements such as broader sensor inclusion and privacy-conscious data handling.

Abstract: We introduce VZCrash, the largest publicly available dataset of real-world vehicle collision data featuring Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) telemetry. The dataset contains more than 31,000 validated crashes and 158,000 negative samples, including hard cases and distractors. Each sample includes acceleration and angular velocity at 100 Hz, and GPS speed at 1 Hz. Events in VZCrash were captured by devices installed on a fleet of 73,010 commercial vehicles of different sizes driving in the United States over the span of several years. We also present an extensive experimental study enabled by the volume of the dataset. We first benchmark several different approaches, from a simple threshold-based heuristic to state-of-the-art deep learning models. Then, we present an experiment demonstrating the importance of scaling data to train high-quality crash detection models, and we show that scale is especially important when these models need to be deployed into a real-world environment.

</details>


### [74] [Knowledge Distillation for Visual Autoregressive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06078)
*Elia Peruzzo,Aritra Bhowmik,Guillaume Sautiere,Yuki M Asano,Amirhossein Habibian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VarKD is a student-sample distillation framework for visual autoregressive (AR) image models that selectively uses teacher signals and reduces token ambiguity, achieving consistent improvements over prior KD baselines on ImageNet across multiple AR backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge distillation for visual autoregressive image generation is underexplored, and existing language-model distillation methods do not transfer well due to long decoding horizons and visual token ambiguity.

Method: VarKD distills on student samples, applies teacher supervision selectively, and reduces token-level ambiguity to handle long-horizon AR generation and ambiguous visual tokens.

Result: VarKD consistently outperforms prior distillation baselines on ImageNet across multiple AR backbones, narrowing the gap to large-scale models.

Conclusion: Tailored distillation for visual autoregressive image models is beneficial; language-based KD strategies do not directly transfer; VarKD demonstrates effective compression of AR image models.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) image generation models are highly expressive but computationally intensive, motivating effective model compression. Knowledge distillation (KD) is a natural approach for model compression and has been widely studied in language modeling, yet its behavior in visual AR generation remains underexplored. In this work, we present the first systematic study of distillation strategies for AR image models. Our analysis shows that while standard distillation can yield meaningful gains, recent methods developed for language do not directly transfer to images: long decoding horizons and visual token ambiguity make teacher supervision unreliable especially under student-conditioned contexts. To address this, we propose VarKD, a distillation framework for visual autoregressive models that distills on student samples while selectively applying teacher supervision and reducing token-level ambiguity. Experiments on ImageNet across multiple AR backbones show that VarKD consistently outperforms prior distillation baselines, narrowing the gap to large-scale models.

</details>


### [75] [HyperVis: Continuous Latent Visual Relational Graphs on the Lorentz Hyperboloid for Compositional Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06100)
*Moshiur Farazi,Sameera Ramasinghe,Mahbub Ahmed Turza,Shafin Rahman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyperVis introduces a hyperbolic relational reasoning module for VLMs that eschews discrete scene-graph triplets in favor of a dense, cross-attention–derived visual relation tensor projected to a Lorentz hyperboloid, serving as both a training-time regularizer and an inference-time relational encoder to improve compositional reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scene-graph triplets collide with continuous visual features, degrading performance; a continuous, hierarchy-aware relational representation is needed to better capture inter-object relations without SGG bottlenecks.

Method: From N region proposals, compute a dense O(N^2) relational tensor via spatially-biased cross-attention; project relations onto a Lorentz hyperboloid; enforce hierarchy with IoA-driven entailment cones and exterior-angle repulsion; train with hyperbolic relational losses to shape LoRA representations; use hyperbolic prefix tokens at inference to boost discriminative compositional scoring; compare with Euclidean ablations.

Result: GQA accuracy improves to 61.03% with relational losses (vs 57.21% for LoRA without relational losses); SugarCrepe accuracy 79.94% (+6.25pp over baseline); curvature stabilizes at κ=4.0. Euclidean ablations show comparable regularization in flat space (GQA 60.81%), but hyperbolic gains are essential for compositionality (SugarCrepe +4.58pp over Euclidean) and entailment loss is ~6× higher in Euclidean training.

Conclusion: HyperVis demonstrates that hyperbolic relational representations can address the compositionality bottleneck in vision-language models, providing both training-time regularization and inference-time relational encoding that surpass baselines, with curvature behavior (κ≈4) indicating that the continuous visual manifold benefits from exponential-volume geometry.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle with compositional reasoning that requires understanding inter-object relationships. A natural remedy is to inject explicit scene graph triplets $\langle s, p, o \rangle$ from an off-the-shelf scene graph generator (SGG), but we show this backfires: discrete text labels collide with the continuous visual modality, degrading GQA accuracy from 60.38\% to 58.86\%. We propose \textbf{HyperVis}, which bypasses the SGG semantic bottleneck entirely. From $N$ class-agnostic region proposals, we compute a dense $O(N^2)$ visual relation tensor via spatially-biased cross-attention, project it onto a Lorentz hyperboloid, and enforce hierarchy through spatial physics, namely IoA-driven entailment cones and exterior-angle repulsion. We discover that HyperVis contributes in two complementary ways: (1) as a \emph{training-time regularizer}, the hyperbolic relational losses shape LoRA representations that improve generative VQA (GQA 61.03\% vs.\ 57.21\% for LoRA fine-tuning without relational losses, recovering and surpassing the baseline); and (2) as an \emph{inference-time relational encoder}, hyperbolic prefix tokens boost discriminative compositional scoring (SugarCrepe 79.94\%, $+$6.25pp over baseline). The learned curvature stabilises at $κ{=}4.0$, an order of magnitude above prior hyperbolic VLMs where $κ$ typically collapses toward zero, indicating that continuous visual features genuinely require the exponential volume of strongly curved space. A controlled Euclidean ablation confirms this decomposition: the relational pipeline regularises LoRA comparably in flat space (GQA 60.81\%), but the compositionality gain is specifically hyperbolic (SugarCrepe $+$4.58pp over Euclidean), with entailment loss ${\sim}6{\times}$ higher in Euclidean training. Codes are available at TBA.

</details>


### [76] [MS-DKC: A Dataset Knowledge Card Framework for Designing and Adapting Medical Image Segmentation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06103)
*Tariq M. Khan,Syed Saud Naqvi,Thantrira Porntaveetus,Hamid Alinejad-Rokny,Shahzaib Iqbal,Imran Razzak,Mohammad AU Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MS-DKC framework shows that dataset requirements, not architecture alone, should drive medical segmentation design; demonstrated across DRIVE, ISIC2018, and ACDC with dataset-conditioned priors and evidence leading to competitive metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the architecture-centric view in medical image segmentation by making dataset requirements explicit and traceable through a structured knowledge card that links dataset factors to failure modes, priors, and risk-aware design criteria.

Method: Introduce Medical Segmentation Dataset Knowledge Card (MS-DKC) with descriptors for image/acquisition, morphology, supervision, context-dependence, and deployment-risk; map descriptors to failure modes, design priors, and risk-aligned criteria; evaluate on DRIVE, ISIC2018, and ACDC using dataset-tailored models and analysis.

Result: On DRIVE: DKC-TNet-v2 Dice 0.8044, IoU 0.6730 with 35,103 params; SA-UNetv2-DKC-AmbRef Dice 0.8141, IoU 0.6865, sensitivity 0.8265, specificity 0.9804, AUC 0.9853. ISIC2018: MS-DKC-AttNextTopo-VCSF-NoAug Dice 0.8872, IoU 0.8214, precision 0.9173, Boundary F1 0.4878, ASSD 4.13; ACDC: four-class softmax with class-balanced Dice/CE supervision and class-wise surface evaluation; overall, dataset-conditioned design improves appropriateness and performance transparency.

Conclusion: The results support a dataset-conditioned design paradigm: different datasets require different priors, operating points, and evidence before deeming a model appropriate; MS-DKC enhances transparency and traceability beyond architecture-first comparisons.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation is often framed as a search for stronger architectures, but this can obscure a more fundamental question: what does the dataset require from the model? In medical imaging, this requirement is shaped by foreground occupancy, morphology, boundary ambiguity, topology sensitivity, annotation quality, acquisition variation, and operating point.
  This paper introduces the Medical Segmentation Dataset Knowledge Card (MS-DKC), a framework for making these factors explicit. MS-DKC records dataset evidence through image/acquisition, morphology, supervision, context-dependence, and deployment-risk descriptors. These descriptors are mapped to failure modes, design priors, and risk-aligned criteria, making segmentation design more traceable than architecture-first comparison.
  We evaluate MS-DKC on DRIVE, ISIC2018, and ACDC, representing distinct regimes. DRIVE contains sparse, thin, branching vessels, favoring detail-preserving models, sensitivity-aware optimization, threshold analysis, and topology-aware metrics. DKC-TNet-v2 achieved Dice 0.8044 and IoU 0.6730 with 35103 parameters, while SA-UNetv2-DKC-AmbRef reached Dice 0.8141, IoU 0.6865, sensitivity 0.8265, specificity 0.9804, and AUC 0.9853. ISIC2018 involves compact but appearance-variable lesions; validation-constrained score-function selection on Att-Next-Topo/ATTNext produced MS-DKC-AttNextTopo-VCSF-NoAug with Dice 0.8872, IoU 0.8214, precision 0.9173, Boundary F1 0.4878, and ASSD 4.13, while plausible additions failed to improve the risk-aligned profile. ACDC provides a multi-class cardiac case, where MS-DKC recommends four-class softmax segmentation, class-balanced Dice/CE supervision, and class-wise surface evaluation. Overall, the results support dataset-conditioned design: different datasets require different priors, operating points, and evidence before a model can be judged appropriate.

</details>


### [77] [Where, What, Why, and Importance: Structured Defect Grounding for Text-to-Image Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06113)
*Huaisong Zhang,Hao Yu,Yuxuan Zhang,Jiahe Wang,Xinrui Chen,Haoxiang Cao,Feng Lu,Wendong Zhang,Changqian Yu,Chun Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Structured Defect Grounding (SDG) for text-to-image models, plus SDG-30K dataset and SDG-Eval; models defects as (location, type, reason, importance) tuples; proposes diagnosis-to-alignment with a Vision-Language Model detector and BoxFlow-GRPO rewards for diffusion alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current dense-feedback approaches rely on heatmap regression that struggles with variable-cardinality defects and linking semantic causes to specific failures; there is a need for instance-level, structured, trainable, and measurable defect grounding to diagnose and improve image generation.

Method: Define SDG as structured set prediction where each defect is a (location, type, reason, importance) tuple. Build SDG-30K with box-grounded annotations across four modern T2I generators; propose SDG-Eval evaluation protocol; develop diagnosis-to-alignment framework using a Vision-Language Model as SDG detector and BoxFlow-GRPO to convert predicted defect sets into box-derived, importance-weighted spatial rewards for diffusion model alignment.

Result: SDG detector outperforms leading proprietary VLMs on structured defect grounding; SDG-guided rewards consistently improve T2I alignment and support localized image refinement.

Conclusion: SDG provides a unified, instance-level interface for diagnosing, evaluating, and improving modern generative models by enabling structured defect grounding, rigorous evaluation, and controllable refinement via alignment rewards.

Abstract: Despite generating increasingly photorealistic images, text-to-image (T2I) models still exhibit localized, subtle, and structurally complex failures. Diagnosing these failures requires instance-level feedback that answers where a defect occurs, what type it is, why it is defective, and its importance to overall image quality. While recent dense-feedback methods move beyond scalar supervision, their heatmap-centric representations still formulate diagnosis as pixel-field regression, making it difficult to localize variable-cardinality defects and bind semantic reasons to individual failures. To address this representation bottleneck, we propose Structured Defect Grounding (SDG), which casts T2I diagnosis as structured set prediction by modeling each defect as a (location, type, reason, importance) tuple. To make this formulation trainable and measurable, we introduce SDG-30K, a 30K-image dataset with box-grounded annotations across four modern T2I generators, together with a dedicated evaluation protocol, SDG-Eval. Building on this structured representation, we further present a diagnosis-to-alignment framework in which a Vision-Language Model (VLM) serves as the SDG detector, and BoxFlow-GRPO converts predicted defect sets into box-derived, importance-weighted spatial rewards for diffusion model alignment. Extensive experiments show that our SDG detector outperforms leading proprietary VLMs on structured defect grounding, while SDG-guided rewards consistently improve T2I alignment and support localized image refinement. These results establish SDG as a unified, instance-level interface for diagnosing, evaluating, and enhancing modern generative models.

</details>


### [78] [Diff-CA: Separating Common and Salient Factors with Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06120)
*Michaël Soumm,Alexandre Fournier Montgieux,Yunlong He,Pietro Gori,Alasdair Newson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-model-based framework for contrastive decomposition that factorizes conditioning into common and salient components under weak supervision, with identifiability guarantees and editable control via swapping/interpolating the salient factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contrastive Analysis seeks to separate shared versus distribution-specific factors. Prior generative methods (VAEs/GANs) struggle with reconstruction quality, limiting high-fidelity image generation and editing.
