We present \olive (\underline{O}nline \underline{L}ow-rank \underline{I}ncremental Learning for Efficient Adapti\underline{ve} Exoskeletons), a parameter-efficient online adaptation framework that continuously personalizes exoskeleton control during deployment. \olive decomposes the adaptive component of the control policy into a low-rank residual form~$\dW = \At\Bt^\top$ with rank~$r!\ll!\min(d,k)$, reducing online update cost from $\mathcal{O}(dk)$ to $\mathcal{O}(r(d{+}k))$ while preserving the stability of a pretrained base controller~$\Wz$. Parameters are updated via a reward-shaped policy gradient driven purely by on-body sensor feedback (EMG, IMU, vibration), eliminating dependence on offline reference trajectories. A gating mechanism modulates the strength of personalization based on contextual state, and a dynamic rank scheduler adapts the update dimensionality to terrain complexity -- allocating minimal capacity on simple flat terrain and expanding to higher-rank updates on demanding uneven surfaces -- enabling robust performance across diverse activities: flat walking, stair navigation, slopes, and uneven terrain. Experiments on the wearable platform demonstrate that \olive achieves +13, +22, and +15 percentage-point improvements in gait smoothness, effort reduction, and motion stability over the strongest baseline, converging within $\sim$1{,}800 walking steps at 7.4,ms end-to-end latency. Our code implementation is available at https://github.com/FastLM/OLIVE.

</details>


### [104] [A New Quaternion-Joint Cable-Driven Redundant Manipulator Configuration and its Control Through FABRIK and Residual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05236)
*Tanapath Pornthisan,Thanapat Kemthong,Thanyapisit Kangsathien,Pasut Aranchaiya,Paulo Garcia,Viboon Sangveraphunsiri*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A 4-segment, 8-joint cable-driven manipulator with quaternion joints expands workspace and achieves near-ground-truth control via residual reinforcement learning, outperforming FABRIK by ~1000x in pose accuracy while reducing hardware costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the demand for space-filling manipulators that can navigate obstructed workspaces; quaternion joints reduce motor count per DoF, enabling compact designs, but introduce kinematic complexity and fabrication sensitivity.

Method: Propose a 4-segment, 8-joint manipulator with quaternion joints; apply Residual Reinforcement Learning on top of FABRIK control, and present the complete FABRIK control process integrated with learning.

Result: Demonstrates a larger workspace than prior designs at lower hardware cost; Residual RL improves positional and orientational accuracy by three orders of magnitude over FABRIK; control implementation is simpler.

Conclusion: The methodology offers designers improved tools for designing and controlling quaternion-joint, cable-driven manipulators, and is applicable to new configurations and control systems.

Abstract: Robotic arms capable of traversing arbitrary spatial paths, especially in highly obstructed workspaces, are highly desired across several industries. Quaternion-joints have recently empowered a specific class of robotic arms -- cable-driven redundant manipulators -- beyond its prior capabilities. Specifically, quaternion-joints reduce the number of required motors per degree of freedom, paving the way for more compact solutions.An ongoing challenge is that the complexity of the kinematic model of quaternion joints challenges a priori decisions on manipulator configurations and imposes higher computational demands on the control system and its non-linearities amplify all discrepancies between design and physical artifact arising from fabrication imprecision. Here we show a that a 4-segment, 8-joint manipulator can achieve a broader workspace than extant configurations, at lower hardware cost, and that Residual Reinforcement Learning outperforms extant state-of-the-art methods -- specifically, the FABRIK algorithm -- on the control of such manipulator. Our results show that this configuration is more workspace-effective than prior designs, and that Residual Reinforcement Learning outperforms FABRIK by three orders of magnitude on positional and orientational accuracy, effecting precise control of the novel 4-segment, 8-joint manipulator. Additionally, the control implementation is simpler: we describe the complete FABRIK process for control and corresponding learning implementation. Our methodology is applicable to the design of new systems, providing designers with further tools for the development of this class of manipulators and corresponding control systems for novel configurations.

</details>


### [105] [Inverse Manipulation through Symbolic Planning and Residual Operator Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05248)
*Yigit Yildirim,Giuseppe Rauso,Riccardo Caccavale,Alberto Finzi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid inverse manipulation framework that derives STRIPS-like inverse operators from demonstrations via soft geometric predicates, uses a planner to satisfy a restoration objective, and applies a residual RL policy (Soft Actor-Critic) to fix remaining inverse predicates, demonstrated on ManiSkill3 PushCube; results show the residual controller makes inverse skills physically grounded.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symbolic inverse plans often fail to restore forward-execution effects under continuous interaction dynamics; reversing symbolic states is insufficient for real-world robot manipulation, necessitating a learning-based residual to handle unmodeled physics.

Method: Automatically extract STRIPS-like operators from demonstrations through soft geometric predicates; for each operator build an inverse restoration objective (preserve preconditions, restore delete effects, negate add effects); a task planner uses available action primitives to satisfy the objective; unresolved predicates trigger residual operator learning solved via reinforcement learning (Soft Actor-Critic).

Result: On ManiSkill3 PushCube, the symbolic inverse yields a coarse恢复 restoration (pick-and-place), while a residual SAC policy refines the cube pose to satisfy remaining inverse predicates.

Conclusion: Predicate-derived residual control can transform an approximate symbolic inverse into a physically grounded inverse skill, demonstrating a effective hybrid of symbolic planning and learning for inverse manipulation.

Abstract: Inverting a robotic task requires more than reversing symbolic state transitions or rewinding motor trajectories. In robot manipulation tasks, symbolic inverse plans often fail to fully restore the effects of forward executions under continuous interaction dynamics. We present a hybrid framework for inverse manipulation that derives inverse-skill objectives from STRIPS-like operators automatically extracted from demonstrations through soft geometric predicates. For each extracted operator, we construct an inverse restoration objective that preserves preconditions, restores delete effects, and negates add effects. A task planner first attempts to satisfy this objective using available action primitives. Unresolved symbolic predicates then induce a residual operator learning problem solved through Reinforcement Learning (RL). We evaluate the framework on the ManiSkill3 PushCube task. For a forward pushing skill, the symbolic inverse performs a coarse pick-and-place restoration, while a residual Soft Actor-Critic policy refines the cube pose to satisfy the remaining inverse predicates. Our results show that predicate-derived residual control can turn an approximate symbolic inverse into a physically grounded inverse skill.

</details>


### [106] [VASO: Formally Verifiable Self-Evolving Skills for Physical AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05395)
*Yunhao Yang,Neel P. Bhatt,Kevin Wang,Samuel Tetteh,Zhangyang Wang,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VASO couples formal verification with self-evolving LLM-generated robot skills by representing each skill as a dual-interface semantic contract, using a model checker to filter/verify plans against temporal specs, and updating contracts from counterexamples with textual gradients while weights stay frozen; achieves high formal-spec compliance with limited optimization samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the trust gap in foundation-model-generated skills by moving beyond trace-level execution feedback to formal, temporal-safety-aware verification that adapts skill contracts.

Method: Each skill has a formal interface (states/observations/commands mapped to logical propositions for model checking) and a planner-facing interface (guides executable behavior). A model checker first filters inconsistent contracts, then verifies induced plans against global/local temporal specifications. If verification fails, the counterexample trace is translated into a textual gradient to update the skill contract; foundation-model weights remain frozen. Evaluated on Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopters.

Result: 97.2% formal-specification compliance using fewer than 100 optimization samples, outperforming execution-feedback, prompt-optimization, and fine-tuning baselines.

Conclusion: First framework to close the loop between formal verification and self-evolving LLM-generated robot skills, where formal counterexamples become optimization feedback for reusable robot skill contracts rather than only verifying single plans or tuning prompts/weights.

Abstract: Reusable robot skills are becoming the basic units through which embodied agents turn open-ended instructions into long-horizon physical behavior. We argue that, while foundation models have collapsed the cost of creating these skills, the cost of trusting them has not. Existing skill-evolution loops refine skills through execution feedback, unit tests, environment reward, or LLM self-critique, but these signals provide only trace-level evidence: they show that a skill worked on sampled executions, not that skill-induced plans satisfy temporal safety contracts under untested conditions. We introduce VASO, a framework for verification-guided self-evolution of LLM-generated robot skill contracts. In VASO, each skill is represented as a semantic contract with two coupled interfaces: a formal interface that aligns robot states, observations, and control commands with logical propositions for model checking, and a planner-facing interface that guides executable behavior generation. A model checker first filters logically inconsistent skill contracts, then verifies plans induced by the skill against global and local temporal specifications. When verification fails, VASO translates the counterexample trace into a textual gradient that updates the reusable skill contract while keeping foundation-model weights frozen. On Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter tasks, VASO reaches 97.2% formal-specification compliance using fewer than 100 optimization samples, outperforming execution-feedback, prompt-optimization, and fine-tuning baselines. To our knowledge, VASO is the first framework that closes the loop between formal verification and self-evolving LLM-generated skills for physical AI agents: formal counterexamples become optimization feedback for reusable robot skill contracts, rather than merely verifying one-off plans, tuning planner prompts, or fine-tuning model weights.

</details>


### [107] [MoDex: A Diffusion Policy for Sequential Multi-Object Dexterous Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05407)
*Haofei Lu,Hongjia Liu,Yifei Dong,Florian T. Pokorny,Jens Lundell,Danica Kragic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based policy enabling sequential multi-object grasp with a single dexterous hand by conditioning on an opposition space; trained via imitation learning and RL fine-tuning; demonstrates improved success in sim and real hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome underutilization of dexterous manipulation and preserve degrees of freedom for subsequent grasps, enabling sequential grasping without releasing held objects.

Method: MoDex diffusion policy predicting next gripper pose from observations conditioned on opposition space and point cloud; opposition space controls which fingers participate to reserve unused DoF; two-stage training: imitation learning on expert demonstrations, then RL fine-tuning; sim-to-real transfer.

Result: MoDex achieves higher success rates than baselines in both simulation and real-world experiments, with improvements of 2.92-17.92% (sim) and 6.67-17.78% (real).

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective sequential multi-object grasping with a single hand, leveraging remaining DoF through an opposition-space conditioned diffusion policy; supports better sim-to-real transfer and outperforms evaluated baselines.

Abstract: This work addresses sequentially grasping multiple objects with a single dexterous hand without releasing those already held. Most dexterous grasping methods commit all of the hand's degrees of freedom to a single object, underutilizing its dexterity and leaving no redundancy for subsequent grasps. The proposed solution, MoDex, is a diffusion policy that predicts the next gripper pose directly from observations, conditioned on an opposition space and point cloud. The opposition space condition specifies which fingers participate in the current grasp, enabling the gripper to use only a subset of its available degrees of freedom while reserving the remaining degrees of freedom for subsequent grasps. To facilitate sim-to-real transfer, MoDex is trained in two stages: first through imitation learning on expert demonstrations, and subsequently through reinforcement learning fine-tuning, which consistently improves success rates over the pre-trained policy. We evaluate MoDex in simulation on a MuJoCo-based Franka Emika Panda robot equipped with an Allegro Hand and on the corresponding real-world hardware platform. Across both simulation and real-world experiments, MoDex achieves higher success rates than the evaluated learning-based baselines, improving performance by 2.92-17.92% and 6.67-17.78%, respectively. Project page: https://modex2026.github.io/.

</details>


### [108] [Learning from Demonstrations over Riemannian Manifolds using Neural ODEs: An Extended Abstract](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05422)
*Diana Cuervo Espinosa,Mahathi Anand,Angela P. Schoellig*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LfD on Riemannian manifolds: learn geodesics with neural ODEs to encode position and orientation, decode to task space for robot deployment; preliminary simulations with comparisons to other geodesic computation methods; outlines challenges and future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Euclidean LfD inadequately captures orientation and curved-space geometry; manifolds provide natural state spaces for combined position and orientation data; geodesics offer natural interpolants between states; neural ODEs reduce computational overhead in geodesic estimation.

Method: Represent demonstrations on Riemannian manifolds; numerically estimate geodesics using neural ordinary differential equations; decode the resulting geodesics back into the original task space for robot deployment; architecture discussion; qualitative simulation-based evaluation and comparisons to alternative geodesic computation mechanisms.

Result: Preliminary simulation results provide initial insights into the approach and a comparative assessment against other geodesic computation methods; results indicate feasibility and potential advantages, but are qualitative and not yet definitive.

Conclusion: The framework offers a pathway to natural motion generation for LfD on manifolds and feasible decoding to task space; highlights challenges (e.g., computational efficiency, accuracy of geodesic estimation, generalization) and identifies directions for future work toward real-world deployment.

Abstract: Learning from demonstratins (LfD) is usually performed over Euclidean spaces, while the robot state, e.g. orientation, naturally evolves over curved spaces. Therefore, to ensure natural, complex motion generation, we investigate learning from demonstrations over Riemannian manifolds that are capable of encoding both position and orientation data. Here, geodesic paths provide for natural motion between two arbitrary points within the manifold. We propose to numerically estimate geodesics via neural ordinary differential equations, mitigating large computational overhead of existing approaches. Finally, these geodesics can be decoded back into the original task space before deploying on the robot. In this extended abstract, we discuss the architecture of our framework, provide some initial insights from our simulation experiments, including comparison to other geodesic computation mechanisms, and discuss the challenges and prospects for future work.

</details>


### [109] [Uncertainty-Aware Adaptive Sensor Fusion for Autonomous Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05437)
*Simegnew Yihunie Alaba,Yuichi Motai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid ViT-MCNN VIO with UKF fusion and an uncertainty-aware loss, achieving improved KITTI ATE/RPE and high fps on A100 for robust navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance pose estimation accuracy and robustness in Visual-Inertial Odometry under noisy or incomplete sensor data by combining powerful temporal IMU modeling, visual motion cues, and uncertainty-aware learning.

Method: A Vision Transformer (ViT) captures temporal dependencies in IMU data; a Multiscale CNN (MCNN) learns optical-flow-based motion cues from images; an adaptive sensor fusion module weights IMU and visual features via estimated uncertainty; a UKF integrates the fused estimates; an uncertainty-aware loss explicitly models prediction uncertainty during training.

Result: On KITTI, the approach significantly outperforms baselines in Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE); achieves 155 FPS on NVIDIA A100, indicating suitability for real-time deployment.

Conclusion: The proposed framework delivers robust, accurate VIO suitable for resource-constrained autonomous systems, by leveraging temporal IMU modeling, visual motion cues, adaptive uncertainty-based fusion, and uncertainty-aware learning.

Abstract: This work introduces a hybrid deep learning approach integrated with an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) to enhance pose estimation accuracy in Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) for autonomous navigation. The proposed model employs a Vision Transformer (ViT) network to effectively capture temporal dependencies from inertial measurement unit (IMU) data and utilizes a Multiscale Convolutional Neural Network (MCNN) to learn optical flow-based motion cues from visual data. An adaptive sensor fusion module dynamically weights IMU and visual features by leveraging estimated uncertainty, thus improving robustness in diverse and challenging environmental conditions. Additionally, a novel uncertainty-aware loss function is proposed to explicitly incorporate prediction uncertainty into the learning process, enabling robust and accurate navigation under noisy, incomplete, or unreliable sensor inputs. Comprehensive evaluations of the KITTI dataset demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline approaches, achieving superior performance in terms of Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE). The lightweight and computationally efficient model processes data at 155 FPS on an NVIDIA A100 GPU, making it highly suitable for deployment in resource-constrained autonomous systems.

</details>


### [110] [FlowPRO: Reward-Free Reinforced Fine-Tuning of Flow-Matching VLAs via Proximalized Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05468)
*Yihao Wu,He Zhang,Junbo Tan,Xueqian Wang,Zhengyou Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlowPRO is a reward-free offline reinforcement fine-tuning framework for flow-matching Vision-Language-Action models, introducing RPRO with proximalized preference optimization; leverages teleoperation-based intervention and rollback to densify sparse corrections into per-state supervision; achieves state-of-the-art success on four long-horizon bimanual tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying post-training VLA models on real robots is bottlenecked by reliance on indirect failure signals (SFT/DAgger) and by reward-design and critic training challenges in reward-based RL. A reward-free offline approach with robust training signals is needed for practical deployment.

Method: Introduce RPRO (Robotic Flow-matching Proximalized Preference Optimization) as a contrastive optimizer with a proximal regularizer that caps the magnitude of the implicit reward within the flow-matching action head. Use a teleoperated intervention-and-rollback data collection to obtain paired positive/negative trajectories (τ^w, τ^l) from a single operator action; apply Smooth Interpolation and batch mixing to convert sparse corrections into dense per-state supervision while preserving the base policy. Evaluate on four long-horizon bimanual tasks with Flow-DPO as baseline.

Result: FlowPRO achieves the highest success rate across the four long-horizon tasks, outperforming four representative baselines. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each loss component (contrastive objective, proximal regularizer, interpolation-based densification).

Conclusion: A reward-free offline RL framework for flow-matching VLA models can reliably deploy on real robots. The RPRO objective mitigates reward-hacking tendencies of plain Flow-DPO, and the teleoperation-based data strategy with interpolation yields dense supervision that preserves base policy capabilities, leading to superior task success.

Abstract: Post-training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models into policies that can be reliably deployed on real robots remains a major bottleneck. SFT and DAgger exploit failure signals only indirectly, and reward-based RL is bottlenecked by the difficulty of real-world reward design and of training reliable critics. We present FlowPRO, a reward-free offline reinforced fine-tuning framework for flow-matching VLAs. Algorithmically, we propose RPRO (Robotic Flow-matching Proximalized Preference Optimization), a preference-optimization objective tailored to the flow-matching action head of VLA models. RPRO pairs a contrastive optimizer with an explicit proximal regularizer that anchors the absolute magnitude of the implicit reward, thereby eliminating the reward-hacking failure mode of plain Flow-DPO. On the data side, a teleoperated intervention-and-rollback paradigm produces naturally paired positive and negative trajectories $(τ^w, τ^l)$ on a real robot from a single operator action; a Smooth Interpolation procedure, combined with batch mixing, then converts these sparse corrections into dense per-state supervision while preserving the base policy's capabilities. On four long-horizon bimanual tasks, FlowPRO attains the highest success rate, outperforming four representative baselines, and ablations confirm the contribution of each loss component.

</details>


### [111] [Learning Contact Representation for Leg Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05501)
*Emre Girgin,Cagri Kilic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Self-supervised contact detection using only joint encoder data improves stance/swing phase detection and legged-robot odometry without force sensors, outperforming supervised baselines; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Odometry in legged robots relies on accurate leg-phase detection. Force sensors, while helpful, are not universally available and can miss disturbances like slippage. A method that uses existing joint encoder data for contact inference can improve robustness and reduce hardware requirements.

Method: A self-supervised representation learning framework is proposed to detect contact using standard joint encoder sensors. Learned representations model stance and swing phases probabilistically, enabling contact probability estimates without force-sensor augmentation.

Result: Experimental evaluations show superior performance of the self-supervised contact detector over supervised methods that require additional sensors or labeling, and over baseline probabilistic approaches. The authors also release their code publicly.

Conclusion: Joint-encoder-based, self-supervised contact detection provides robust, sensor-minimal contact inference for legged-robot odometry, with demonstrated improvements and open-source availability.

Abstract: The estimation of odometry in legged robots depends on the assumption that the velocity of the foot with respect to the world remains zero during the stance phase. Feedback for the main body velocity is derived from the kinematic serial chain of the feet making accurate leg phase detection is a critical subproblem. A considerable number of studies employ ground reaction force sensors mounted at the tip of the foot to classify, yet these sensors may not be universally available for all legged robots. Additionally, these sensors are often unresponsive to unaccounted disturbances, such as slippage, while the foot remains in contact with the ground. In this study, we propose a self-supervised representation learning framework for contact detection that utilizes the standard sensor set of joint encoders without reliance on force sensor augmentations. We employ learned representations to model the stance and swing phases probabilistically. The experimental results obtained confirm the efficacy of the proposed self-supervised contact detector. Our framework exhibited superior performance in comparison to supervised methods which necessitate sensor set augmentation and labeling, as well as baseline probabilistic approaches. Additionally, we make our code available to the public.

</details>


### [112] [Auditing Demonstration Curation Metrics: Action-Only Scorers Fail on the Structural Defects That Degrade Imitation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05588)
*Aarav Bedi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A controlled evaluation of seven demonstration-curation metrics for imitation learning reveals that (a) state-trajectory metrics detect structural defects that action-only metrics miss, (b) most actions-only metrics fail to improve downstream performance even when they detect defects, and (c) high detection accuracy does not ensure task gains; releasing a testbed and implementations for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine which demonstration-curation metrics genuinely identify demonstrations that harm policy performance, given that many metrics are validated on different data with varying protocols.

Method: Construct a controlled testbed where demonstration defects are injected with known types (subtle perturbations: correlated action noise, tremor, truncation; structural errors: wrong action at key moments). Audit seven curation metrics on two axes: (i) separation of defective vs clean demonstrations, and (ii) whether training a behavior-cloning policy on the metric-curated subset improves task success. Evaluate two defect regimes and compare against uncurated baselines.

Result: Subtle perturbations are detectable by multivariate outlier scoring; removing them recovers the downstream performance gap. Structural errors are invisible to action-only metrics; some metrics even rank defects higher quality. Only state-trajectory–based metrics detect structural errors, but even the best recover at most about one-third of the downstream gap. High metric accuracy does not guarantee downstream improvement. The authors release the testbed and the implementations for replication.

Conclusion: State-aware (state-trajectory) metrics are necessary to detect structural defects, but overall curation performance remains limited; a prudent, benchmarked approach is required to ensure curated demonstrations actually improve policy performance. Releasing the testbed enables standardized evaluation across methods.

Abstract: Imitation-learning policies inherit the quality of the demonstrations they are trained on, and a growing set of curation metrics promise to score and filter low-quality demonstrations automatically. These metrics are each validated on different data with different protocols, so it is unclear which of them actually identify the demonstrations that harm a policy. We build a controlled testbed in which demonstration defects are injected with known type, and audit seven curation metrics along two axes: how well each separates defective from clean demonstrations, and whether training a behavior-cloning policy on each metric's curated subset improves task success. We study two defect regimes. Subtle perturbations (correlated action noise, tremor, truncation) are detectable by multivariate outlier scoring and, once removed, recover the full downstream gap. Structural errors, where the demonstration executes a wrong action at a key moment, are invisible to every action-only metric we test, and two of them are inverted: they score defective demonstrations as higher quality and, used for curation, tend to leave the policy at or below the uncurated baseline rather than above it. Only metrics that examine the state trajectory detect structural errors, and even the best of them recovers just a third of the downstream gap. High detection accuracy does not guarantee downstream improvement. We release the testbed and all curation implementations.

</details>


### [113] [Discrete-WAM: Unified Discrete Vision-Action Token Editing for World-Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05645)
*Ziyang Yao,Haochen Liu,Yuncheng Jiang,Zeyu Zhu,Zibin Guo,Jingru Wang,Tianle Liu,Jianwei Cui,Kuiyuan Yang,Hongwei Xie,Jingwei Zhao,Guang Chen,Hangjun Ye*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Discrete-WAM introduces a unified discrete latent world-action model with diffusion-based generation enabling compositional causal reasoning and controllable, counterfactual futures in autonomous driving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap between end-to-end state-to-action mappings (which capture correlations but not action-conditioned dynamics) and continuous-latent world models (which often lack compositional structure for causal reasoning across counterfactual futures). Aims to enable action-conditioned dynamics, compositional generalization, and reliable decision-making in driving.

Method: Represents future visual states and ego actions as aligned discrete tokens; builds a unified discrete alignment between vision and action; employs a shared discrete diffusion framework with unified generative tasks; jointly formulates world modeling, world-action policy, and hierarchical decision-enabled policy; supports compositional generalization across diverse driving scenarios.

Result: Experiments on large-scale autonomous-driving benchmarks show competitive performance and enable controllable generation and counterfactual reasoning.

Conclusion: Offers a principled path toward more reliable decision-making by enabling compositional causal reasoning and counterfactual planning in autonomous driving through a unified discrete diffusion framework.

Abstract: Autonomous driving requires reasoning about how ego actions shape the evolution of the surrounding world. However, most end-to-end methods rely on direct state-to-action mappings, capturing correlations without explicitly modeling action-conditioned dynamics. Conversely, continuous-latent world models often lack compositional structure for causal reasoning across counterfactual futures. We introduce Discrete-WAM, a unified latent vision-action world policy that represents future visual states and ego actions as aligned discrete tokens, enabling compositional causal reasoning across alternative futures. Built upon this unified discrete alignment, Discrete-WAM establishes a shared discrete diffusion framework with unified generative tasks, jointly formulating world modeling, world-action policy, and hierarchical decision-enabled policy, supporting compositional generalization across diverse driving scenarios. Experiments on large-scale autonomous-driving benchmarks show that Discrete-WAM achieves competitive performance while supporting controllable generation and counterfactual reasoning, offering a principled path toward more reliable decision-making.

</details>


### [114] [Safe Embodied AI for Long-horizon Tasks: A Cross-layer Analysis of Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05660)
*Dabin Kim,Daemin Park,Sangyub Lee,Jinsik Kim,Yeongtak Oh,Jongho Shin,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A structured review of safety in long-horizon robotic manipulation, organized by planning-time, policy-time, and execution-time interventions, highlighting gaps and directions for cross-layer assurance and safer deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety is critical in embodied AI as reasoning and acting in the real world over extended horizons can cause harm; current literature is fragmented across planning, policy, and runtime, necessitating an integrated safety framework.

Method: A survey that categorizes safety literature by intervention locus (planning-time, policy-time, execution-time), assesses the strength of evidence (formal guarantees, statistical support, empirical safety heuristics), and differentiates backbone capability work, safety mechanisms, and benchmarks/evaluation studies.

Result: The framework clarifies roles of different literature streams and reveals persistent gaps: limited evidence for policy-time safety, weak formal support for contact-rich long-horizon manipulation, immature uncertainty-triggered intervention, and a shortage of manipulation-specific safety benchmarks.

Conclusion: Recommend cross-layer assurance, improved evaluation design, and safer deployment strategies for long-horizon robotic agents in real-world settings.

Abstract: Embodied AI systems are increasingly expected to reason and act over extended horizons in physical environments. This growing capability brings safety to the foreground, because failures in the physical world can harm people, damage objects, and disrupt workplaces. Although safe embodied AI has attracted substantial attention, the literature remains fragmented across planning, policy design, and runtime execution. Long-horizon robotic manipulation is a particularly revealing anchor domain for this problem because semantic misgrounding, subtask-level error propagation, execution drift, and contact-rich physical risk can accumulate within the same closed-loop system. This survey therefore provides a structured review of safety in long-horizon robotic manipulation from an embodied AI perspective. We organize the literature by intervention locus, covering planning-time, policy-time, and execution-time safety, and we analyze the strength of the evidence that each line of work provides, distinguishing formal guarantees, statistical support, and empirical safety heuristics. This framework clarifies the distinct roles of backbone capability papers, direct safety mechanisms, and benchmark or evaluation studies, while exposing where current safety claims are well supported and where they remain indirect. We identify persistent gaps, including limited evidence for policy-time safety, weak formal support for contact-rich long-horizon manipulation, immature uncertainty-triggered intervention, and a shortage of manipulation-specific safety benchmarks. We conclude by outlining research directions for cross-layer assurance, evaluation design, and safer deployment of long-horizon robotic agents in real-world settings.

</details>


### [115] [Preserving Full 6-DOF Actuation Under Abrupt Total Rotor Failures: Passive Fault-Tolerant Flight Control Using a Biaxial-Tilt Hexacopter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05663)
*Yipeng Yang,Yiqiao Tang,Hao Zhang,Jinqi Jiang,Jianfeng He,Rumo Chen,Xinghu Yu,Zhan Li,Huijun Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a biaxial-tilt overactuated hexacopter (BTO) enabling passive fault-tolerant flight after abrupt rotor failures, extends the AWS inscribed-sphere metric with a transient-wrench-jump term, and offers two non-fault-detection schemes (controller-layer HOFA+ LESO and allocator-layer MRAC with momentum-based wrench estimation), validated by simulations and flight tests showing improved recovery margins over uniaxial-tilt and coplanar designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional multirotors lose attainable wrench space under rotor failures, often requiring fault detection or optimization. The work aims to achieve safe, 6-DOF operation in the presence of abrupt, unknown rotor failures using passive strategies and a redesigned actuation architecture.

Method: 1) Extend inscribed-sphere AWS metric by incorporating a transient-wrench-jump term to quantify feasibility under up to three simultaneous rotor failures. 2) Develop two passive schemes that do not rely on fault detection or online optimization: (a) controller-layer scheme combining a high-order fully actuated (HOFA) controller with a linear extended state observer (LESO) for lumped disturbances; (b) allocator-layer scheme using model-reference adaptive control allocation with momentum-based wrench estimation to mitigate control-allocation biases.

Result: Simulations and flight experiments demonstrate stable hovering and 6-DOF trajectory tracking under single and multiple rotor failures. The BTO exhibits larger recovery margins than uniaxial-tilt and coplanar hexacopters. Additional onboard-sensor experiments (indoor/outdoor, wind, narrow-frame traversal, aerial writing) validate robustness in complex environments.

Conclusion: The BTO with passive fault-tolerant designs expands feasible wrench space under rotor failures and offers robust 6-DOF performance without fault-detection or online optimization, outperforming alternative tilted/arrangement designs in recovery margins and practical robustness.

Abstract: Conventional multirotors suffer from a rapid collapse of attainable wrench space (AWS) under abrupt total rotor failures, rendering full 6-DOF recovery physically impossible. This paper addresses passive fault-tolerant flight of a biaxial-tilt overactuated hexacopter (BTO) under abrupt total rotor failures that are a priori unknown to the controller. The control design and analysis focus on representative abrupt rotor-failure cases for which the post-failure system remains fully actuated, while no explicit fault detection, isolation, or fault-mode switching is assumed. First, we extend the inscribed-sphere metric of the AWS by incorporating the transient-wrench-jump term, enabling quantitative feasibility assessment under up to three simultaneous rotor failures and benchmarking against uniaxial-tilt and coplanar hexacopters. Second, we develop two computationally efficient passive schemes without relying on fault detection or online optimization. One scheme operates at the controller layer by combining a high-order fully actuated (HOFA) controller with a linear extended state observer (LESO) for lumped-disturbance rejection. The other scheme operates at the allocator layer by using model-reference adaptive control allocation with momentum-based wrench estimation to compensate for control-allocation biases. Simulations and flight experiments validate stable hovering and 6-DOF trajectory tracking under single and multiple rotor failures. Further systematic comparisons confirm that the BTO provides larger recovery margins than uniaxial-tilt and coplanar designs. Additional onboard-sensor-only experiments, including indoor tracking under wind disturbance, outdoor tracking under extreme conditions, narrow-frame traversal, and contact-based aerial writing, further validate the robustness of the proposed framework in complex operational environments.

</details>


### [116] [Dynamic Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery in Robotic Cellular Warehousing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05669)
*Cheng Ren,Ming Li,Xinping Guan,George Q. Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Dynamic Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery with internal order evolution in robotic cellular warehousing; introduces two event-triggered online replanning algorithms (Dynamic Token Passing and Cooperative Token Passing) to handle order updates; both reduce order flowtime in simulations compared with static and non-cooperative baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world warehouses experience dynamic task evolution where orders can gain new SKUs during execution. This necessitates online replanning to maintain efficiency and responsiveness in Robotic Cellular Warehousing Systems (RCWS).

Method: Extends token passing to dynamic MAPD with internal order evolution. Proposes two online replanning algorithms: (1) Dynamic Token Passing for localized replanning on order updates using add-order decomposition and priority-based token scheduling with collision-free guarantees; (2) Cooperative Token Passing where idle robots opportunistically assist newly added pickups to boost system-wide efficiency.

Result: Simulation in RCWS settings shows significant reductions in order flowtime compared with static and non-cooperative baselines, validating improved responsiveness and efficiency under dynamic orders.

Conclusion: Dynamic and cooperative token passing effectively handle internal order evolution in MAPD for RCWS, preserving collision-free execution while improving system-level performance; promising for practical deployment with further extensions to scalability and robustness.

Abstract: Robotic Cellular Warehousing Systems (RCWS) give rise to multi-agent pickup and delivery (MAPD) processes in which robots sequentially collect multiple stock-keeping units (SKUs) for each order. Unlike classical MAPD formulations that assume static tasks, real warehouse operations often involve dynamic order evolution, where new SKUs may be appended to an order while it is being executed. Motivated by this practical requirement, this letter formulates the Dynamic Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery problem considering internal order evolution for the first time. Building on the token passing paradigm, we propose two event-triggered online replanning algorithms. The first, Dynamic Token Passing, performs localized replanning upon order updates through add-order decomposition and priority-based token scheduling while preserving collision-free execution. The second, Cooperative Token Passing, further enables idle robots to opportunistically assist newly added pickups, improving system-level efficiency. Simulation results in RCWS environments demonstrate that the proposed methods significantly reduce order flowtime compared with static and non-cooperative baselines.

</details>


### [117] [Accelerating and Scaling MPC-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Humanoid Locomotion and Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05687)
*Junheng Li,Liang Wu,Sergio A. Esteban,Lizhi Yang,Ján Drgoňa,Aaron D. Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MPC-guided RL for humanoid control using a centroidal-dynamics MPC reward and a parallel GPU MPC solver (π^nMPC); enables scalable, training-time MPC guidance with superior locomotion/manipulation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce training overhead and avoid heavy MPC problem construction in reinforcement learning for humanoid control, making MPC guidance practical and scalable.

Method: Introduce centroidal-dynamics MPC reward for RL guidance; develop π^nMPC, a parallel-in-horizon, construction-free batched GPU MPC solver that operates on time-varying dynamics to minimize memory use and pre-compilation requirements.

Result: MPC-RL achieves superior performance in locomotion and manipulation across comparative studies and hardware validations; code available at the authors' repository.

Conclusion: Efficient, scalable MPC-guided RL for humanoid skills is feasible via a cross-cutting approach (centroidal MPC reward + π^nMPC), enabling practical deployment and strong performance.

Abstract: In humanoid motion control, model predictive control (MPC) offers physically grounded prediction and constraint handling, while reinforcement learning (RL) enables robust whole-body skills through large-scale simulation. However, using MPC inside RL often requires time-consuming problem construction or excessive training overhead, making such frameworks difficult to justify in practice. This work studies efficient training-time MPC guidance for humanoid locomotion and manipulation, termed MPC-RL. We introduce a centroidal-dynamics MPC reward formulation that leverages guidance from MPC trajectories in training time. To make this practical in massively parallel RL, we develop $π^n$MPC, a parallel-in-horizon and construction-free batched GPU MPC solver that operates directly on time-varying dynamics to avoid high memory usage and pre-compilation. Through a variety of comparative studies and hardware validations, we have found that MPC-RL achieves superior performance in locomotion and manipulation skills. The code base is available at https://github.com/junhengl/mpc-rl.

</details>


### [118] [DexFuture: Hierarchical Future-State Visuomotor Targeting for Bimanual Dexterous Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05699)
*Runfa Blark Li,Kuang-Ting Tu,Nikola Raicevic,Dwait Bhatt,Xinshuang Liu,Keito Suzuki,Ki Myung Brian Lee,Nikolay Atanasov,Truong Nguyen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexFuture is a hierarchical framework integrating a high-level future-state predictor with a low-level policy to enable fast, dynamic, bimanual dexterous tool use, achieving near-oracle performance at real-time speeds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of generating dynamically consistent future references for high-dimensional hand-tool-object systems without relying on privileged demonstration states or slow counterfactual planning. Improve speed and robustness over action-conditioned world-model (DexWM) planning.

Method: A two-tier design: a high-level horizon-conditioned transformer constructs structured visuomotor embeddings from egocentric RGB, proprioception, and geometric history to predict a multi-step future target trajectory; a low-level target-conditioned per-link transformer tracks these targets. This decouples coarse future reference generation from fine-grained control and semantic prediction from high-frequency execution.

Result: On OakInk2 bimanual tool-use tasks, DexFuture achieves 90% of privileged-oracle performance (vs 7% for a no-reference policy). It operates at 60 Hz, about 250x faster than DexWM-style CEM planning with a future action-conditioned world model.

Conclusion: DexFuture provides an effective, real-time hierarchical approach to dexterous tool use, balancing predictive horizon planning with fast, per-link control, and substantially narrows the gap to privileged planning while outperforming non-referenced baselines.

Abstract: Bimanual dexterous tool use remains challenging for robots due to high-dimensional hand configurations and complex hand-tool-object dynamics and contact. Most existing control policies depend on future configuration references provided from demonstrations, while future action-conditioned world models require slow online planning over high-dimensional action sequences. A significant challenge is generating a dynamically consistent future reference trajectory without relying on privileged states from demonstrations or slow counterfactual planning. We propose DexFuture, a hierarchical system that couples a high-level Future-State Visuomotor Target Predictor with a low-level Target-Conditioned Structured Dexterous Policy. Conditioned on egocentric RGB, proprioceptive and geometric history, the high-level predictor constructs structured hand-tool-object visuomotor embeddings and uses a horizon-conditioned transformer to generate a multi-step future target trajectory. Then, the low-level policy tracks them with a target-conditioned per-link transformer. This hierarchy decouples coarse future reference generation from fine-grained action control, and slow long-horizon semantic prediction from high-frequency execution. On OakInk2 bimanual tool-use tasks, DexFuture achieves 90% of the privileged-oracle performance, compared to 7% for a no-reference policy. DexFuture operates at 60 Hz, approximately 250 times faster than DexWM-style Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) planning with a future action-conditioned world model.

</details>


### [119] [PiL-World: A Chunk-Wise World Model for VLA Policy-in-the-Loop Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05773)
*Chong Ma,Taiyi Su,Jian Zhu,Jianjun Zhang,Zitai Huang,Yi Xu,Hanli Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PiL-World provides a chunk-wise world model for policy-in-the-loop VLA evaluation, enabling closed-loop rollouts conditioned on past observations without real robot execution; it improves rollout fidelity by leveraging head-view visual control and latent task histories and learns from both successes and failures, achieving substantial reduction in real-vs-estimated VLA success-rate error on three real dual-arm tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world models for robot evaluation are open-loop and rely on pre-collected action trajectories, which cannot evaluate VLA policies in a closed loop where each action depends on the most recent observation. A closed-loop evaluative framework is needed for realistic policy development, safety, and cost efficiency.

Method: PiL-World takes the current observation and the VLA rollout’s action trajectory and generates multi-view future observations that are consistent with the rollout and compatible with the policy inputs. It alternates between VLA inference and world-model prediction to enable closed-loop evaluation without real execution. Rollout fidelity is enhanced by conditioning video generation on action-derived visual control from head-view motion and latent task histories, while jointly predicting complementary multi-view observations. The model also learns from failed trajectories to better match the distribution of real policy executions.

Result: Imagined rollouts are highly consistent with real robot executions. Closed-loop world-model evaluation substantially reduces the error in real-world VLA success rates from 63.2% (baseline) to 12.0%.

Conclusion: PiL-World enables effective closed-loop VLA evaluation without real-time robot trials, improving fidelity and leveraging failure data to better capture execution distributions, demonstrated on three real dual-arm manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies operate in a closed loop in real-world robot tasks: a robot observes the scene, executes an action chunk, and conditions its next decision on the resulting observation. However, most existing world models for robot action evaluation are limited to open-loop prediction along pre-collected action trajectories. This prevents them from supporting closed-loop VLA evaluation, where each action chunk must be conditioned on the observation generated by the previous execution. To address this gap, we propose PiL-World, a chunk-wise world model designed for policy-in-the-loop VLA evaluation. Given the current observation and the action trajectory rolled out by a VLA policy, PiL-World generates multi-view future observations that are consistent with the VLA rollout and match the image inputs required by the policy. By alternating between VLA inference and world-model prediction, PiL-World enables closed-loop evaluation without real robot execution at every step. To improve rollout fidelity, PiL-World conditions video generation on action-derived visual control from head-view robot motion and latent histories that encode task execution context, while jointly predicting complementary multi-view observations. Beyond successful teleoperated demonstrations, it also learns from failed execution trajectories, helping the imagined rollouts better match the distribution of real policy executions. We evaluate PiL-World on three real dual-arm manipulation tasks. PiL-World generates imagined rollouts that are highly consistent with real robot executions. More importantly, compared with the baseline, it reduces the error between VLA success rates measured in real-world rollouts and those estimated through closed-loop world-model evaluation from 63.2% to 12.0%.

</details>


### [120] [Visuotactile and Explicitly Force-Controlled Robotic Ultrasound for Abdominal Volumetric Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05848)
*Adrian Piedra,R Brooke Jeffrey,Oussama Khatib*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Autonomous robotic ultrasound using stereo vision, haptics, and expert data to achieve adaptive 3D abdominal imaging with high-quality results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address variability and operator-dependence in abdominal ultrasound by capturing expert scanning strategies and enabling autonomous, patient-specific adaptive imaging; extend capabilities to 3D volumetric data for enhanced diagnostics.

Method: A stereo-vision-based robotic ultrasound system with touch-based feedback and expert-informed strategies records freehand motion and applied forces from radiologists, then replays this data to replicate characteristic scans. 3D abdomen topography is built from stereo data and refined with stiffness measurements to delineate the rib cage. The robot follows two scan paths (upward-angled sweep under the rib cage and a perpendicular sweep through soft tissue) using a 7-DOF torque-controlled manipulator with closed-loop force control to maintain probe contact. 

Result: Physical experiments show imaging quality comparable to expert scans and the ability to adapt to patient-specific topographies. The robotic system can perform 3D volume acquisition, exceeding expert capability in scope and potential diagnostic utility.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates effective integration of expert knowledge into autonomous robotic ultrasound, highlighting the value of perception-based autonomy combined with physical reasoning to advance diagnostic performance.

Abstract: In this paper, we present a robotic ultrasound acquisition system that integrates stereo vision, touch-based feedback, and expert-informed strategies to perform autonomous and adaptive abdominal scans. The system records freehand motion and force data from expert radiologists, creating a framework to capture transducer motion, applied forces, and anatomical scanning strategies. This expert data is replayed to replicate characteristic scans with the robot, forming a foundation for further autonomous capabilities. Using stereo vision, the system generates three-dimensional topography maps of the patient's abdomen, which are refined through stiffness measurements at key points to delineate the rib cage boundary. These combined techniques enable the robot to execute two distinct scanning paths: an upward-angled sweep beneath the rib cage to visualize structures near the upper abdomen and a perpendicular sweep across soft tissue regions. A compliant, torque-controlled seven degree-of-freedom robotic manipulator is controlled to maintain consistent probe contact through closed-loop force control over the varied anatomical surfaces. Physical experiments demonstrate that the system achieves high-quality imaging comparable to expert scans while dynamically adapting to patient-specific topographies. Furthermore, the robotic system surpasses expert capabilities by enabling three-dimensional volume acquisition, which enhances diagnostic potential and provides volumetric data for advanced analyses. This work highlights the integration of expert knowledge into autonomous robotic systems and underscores the potential of combining perception-based autonomy with physical reasoning for enhanced diagnostic performance.

</details>


### [121] [LadderMan: Learning Humanoid Perceptive Ladder Climbing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05873)
*Siheng Zhao,Yuanhang Zhang,Ziqi Lu,Pieter Abbeel,Rocky Duan,Koushil Sreenath,Yue Wang,C. Karen Liu,Guanya Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LadderMan enables robust humanoid ladder climbing and on-ladder manipulation using a two-stage learning pipeline that distills multiple climbing experts into a depth-based visuomotor policy, bridged to reality with vision foundation models, and paired with a dual-agent manipulation policy for teleoperation. It achieves zero-shot real-world transfer across ladder geometries and supports diverse tasks under ladder constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ladder climbing is highly challenging due to sparse footholds and handholds, complex whole-body coordination, and sensitivity to perception and control errors. A robust, deployable policy is needed for real-world humanoid operation in human-centered environments.

Method: (1) Learn multiple climbing experts from a single reference motion via hybrid motion tracking. (2) Distill these experts into a unified depth-based visuomotor climbing policy using hybrid imitation and reinforcement learning. (3) Use vision foundation models to bridge sim-to-real in depth perception. (4) Train a separate manipulation policy with a dual-agent formulation for stable on-ladder manipulation via teleoperation.

Result: The system demonstrates robust ladder climbing across diverse ladder geometries, zero-shot transfer to real hardware, and support for various on-ladder manipulation tasks under ladder constraints. Video results are provided online.

Conclusion: LadderMan presents a cohesive, scalable framework that combines multi-expert learning, depth-based visuomotor control, and teleoperation-enabled manipulation to achieve real-world ladder climbing and constrained manipulation with promising generalization.

Abstract: Humanoid robots hold great promise for operating in human-centered environments, yet ladder climbing remains one of the most challenging tasks due to sparse footholds and handholds, complex whole-body coordination, and sensitivity to perception and control errors. We present \textbf{LadderMan}, a unified system that enables humanoid robots to robustly climb diverse ladders and perform manipulation under such constrained conditions. Our climbing policy is built on a scalable two-stage learning pipeline, where we use hybrid motion tracking to learn multiple climbing experts from a single reference motion, and distill these experts into a unified depth-based visuomotor climbing policy via hybrid imitation and reinforcement learning. To enable real-world deployment, we leverage vision foundation models to bridge the sim-to-real gap in depth perception. Building on the learned climbing policy, we further train a separate manipulation policy using a dual-agent formulation, allowing stable on-ladder manipulation via teleoperation. Experiments demonstrate that LadderMan achieves robust ladder climbing across a wide range of geometries, successfully transfers to real-world hardware in a zero-shot manner, and supports various manipulation tasks under challenging ladder constraints. Video results are available at https://ladderman-robot.github.io .

</details>


### [122] [TAGA: Terrain-aware Active Gaze Learning for Generalizable Agile Humanoid Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05880)
*Peizhuo Li,Hongyi Li,Mingfeng Fan,Fangzhou Xu,Shuhao Liao,Yuxuan Ma,Zicheng Zeng,Ze Wang,Yongbin Jin,Yuhong Cao,Hongtao Wang,Guillaume Sartoretti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TAGA introduces a terrain-aware active gaze mechanism for humanoid locomotion, using perception-action coupling and reinforcement learning to focus on informative height-map regions, improving data efficiency and enabling robust real-world terrain traversal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid locomotion requires broad perceptual coverage while maintaining fine local geometry understanding under tight onboard compute; humans selectively sample terrain information to balance coverage and detail.

Method: Fusion of vision, proprioception, and motion commands to guide an attention-based policy. The model learns anticipatory cues and actively attends to specific height-scan regions, using only informative observations for downstream processing. Gaze emerges via reinforcement learning without explicit supervision, increasing observation information density under compute constraints.

Result: Policy shows robust, generalizable locomotion in simulation and on hardware, including terrain-aware foothold selection, elevated-platform traversal, competitive sparse-foothold traversal, and the largest reported real-world gap traversal distance of 1.2 m among perceptive humanoid systems, with stability under perceptual disturbances.

Conclusion: Gaze behavior can emerge from reinforcement learning alone and enhances perceptual locomotion under computational constraints, enabling finer-grained navigation over larger terrains and improved training efficiency.

Abstract: Agile humanoid locomotion across diverse challenging terrain demands both wide perceptual coverage and precise local geometry understanding. Motivated by the way humans selectively look at relevant terrain during locomotion, we introduce TAGA, a Terrain-aware Active Gaze learning framework for Attention-based humanoid control. By fusing vision, proprioception, and motion commands, our framework guides the model to learn anticipatory cues and actively attend to specific areas of the height scan, selectively using these informative regions for the downstream network. This adaptively increases the information density of observations under tight onboard computational constraints, thus enabling fine-grained perceptive locomotion over larger-scale terrains. We find that such gaze behaviors can naturally emerge through reinforcement learning alone, without requiring additional supervision or explicit guidance, significantly improve training efficiency. As a result, the trained policy demonstrates robust and generalizable locomotion in simulation and on hardware, including reliable terrain-aware foothold selection, elevated-platform traversal, competitive sparse-foothold traversal, and the largest reported real-world gap traversal distance of 1.2m among perceptive humanoid locomotion systems, while maintaining stability under severe perceptual disturbances and environmental interference.

</details>


### [123] [A Novel Method with Encoder-Decoder for Cross-Sensor Adaptation in Surface Shape Sensing with Sparse Strain Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05903)
*Shuo Wang,Heng Luo,Dian Jin,Xiaoming Tao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Encoder-decoder shape sensing with sparse strain sensors enhanced by meta-learning and few-shot adaptation for cross-sensor generalization; enables rapid, data-efficient adaptation across sensor arrays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sensor-array variations cause performance drops; retraining per array is costly in data, time, and computation.

Method: Encoder–decoder architecture for surface shape sensing using sparse strain sensors; integrates meta-learning and few-shot adaptation to generalize across sensor-array groups.

Result: After cross-sensor adaptation, a newly deployed array achieves ~4.0 mm error using <5% newly labeled data with adaptation time <1 s; baseline errors were ~23.0 mm and ~20 minutes of data collection; >65% more points with errors below 5.0 mm.

Conclusion: The method substantially reduces data collection cost and training burden for surface shape sensing and has broad potential in soft robotics and wearable devices.

Abstract: Performance variations in sensor arrays, caused by intrinsic differences or installation conditions, can lead to inconsistent results during shape sensing. To obtain accurate results, a large amount of data is usually required, and a separate model must be retrained for each sensor array, thereby increasing the cost and time of data acquisition, transmission, and computation. To address this issue, this work proposes an encoder-decoder architecture for surface shape sensing based on sparse strain sensors and further incorporates meta-learning and few-shot adaptation strategies to enable adaptation across different groups of sensor arrays. Experimental results demonstrate that, after the cross-sensor adaptation, a newly deployed sensor array achieves a sensing error of approximately 4.0 mm relying on less than 5.0% newly labeled data and requiring an adaptation time of under 1 second, which represents a substantial improvement from 23.0 mm error without adaptation and 20-minute data collection time required to train a new model. Moreover, the number of points with errors below 5.0 mm increased by more than 65.0%. These results indicate that the proposed method can substantially reduce the cost and training burden of surface shape sensing, and it has broad potential applications in soft robotics and wearable devices.

</details>


### [124] [Learning of Robot Safety Policies via Adversarial Synthetic Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05952)
*Nikolai Dorofeev,Alexey Odinokov,Rostislav Yavorskiy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An adversarial, agentic gamification framework to discover hazard scenarios and learn robot safety policies via Red Team/Blue Team dynamics, enabling efficient edge-case discovery and scalable safety for Physical AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the difficulty of capturing high-risk edge cases in complex real-world robotics with random simulations or manual enumeration; leverage adversarial scenario generation and learning to embed safety.

Method: Model scenario generation as a Red Team–Blue Team game where a Red Team constructs hazardous situations and a Blue Team incrementally refines safety policies; iterative hazard discovery through synthetic scenarios; combine classical risk modeling with adversarial scenario generation and modern learning paradigms; ongoing work with a proposed architecture.

Result: Conceptual framework and problem formulation with an initial architecture; results are not yet empirical, but the approach aims to enable efficient discovery of high-risk edge cases and scalable safety embedding in Physical AI.

Conclusion: Proposes a scalable pathway to embed safety into Physical AI systems operating in complex environments; outlines the problem and architecture and identifies next steps for implementation and empirical validation.

Abstract: In this work, we propose an agentic gamification framework for hazard-informed learning of robot safety policies through synthetic scenarios. We model scenario generation as an adversarial game between two agents: a Red Team that explores the space of potential failures by constructing hazardous situations, and a Blue Team that incrementally refines safety policies to prevent them. This iterative process enables efficient discovery of high-risk edge cases that are unlikely to be captured through random simulation or manual enumeration. By combining classical risk modeling with adversarial scenario generation and modern learning paradigms, this work provides a scalable pathway for embedding safety into Physical AI systems operating in complex real-world environments. The paper describes ongoing work. The contribution is a problem formulation and a proposed solution architecture.

</details>


### [125] [Towards a Data Flywheel for Embodied Intelligence in Logistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05960)
*Anlan Yu,Zaishu Chen,Zhiqing Hong,Daqing Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A data-centric framework for industrial embodied intelligence—the logistics data flywheel—that uses world models to generate supervision and data aggregation (WM-DAgger) to enable continual, robust imitation learning from daily operations and multimodal, in-the-wild data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data efficiency, robustness, and scalability for learning-based policies in logistics by converting operations into reusable data assets and closing the loop with deployment feedback.

Method: Proposes a data flywheel: convert daily operations into data assets; use World Models to provide supervision for long-tail tasks; develop WM-DAgger to synthesize out-of-distribution recovery data for imitation learning; outline alignment of multimodal data (human demonstrations, videos, logs) for policy learning and continual improvement.

Result: Initial result WM-DAgger: a World-Model-based data aggregation framework that synthesizes OOD recovery data for robust imitation learning.

Conclusion: Ongoing work to scale with large-scale multimodal data and deployment feedback to continually improve policies.

Abstract: Embodied intelligence is moving from laboratory demonstrations toward industrial deployment, with the logistics industry serving as a key application scenario. Learning-based policies offer a promising path beyond traditional perception-planning-control pipelines, but their scalability depends on how embodied data can be collected, organized, and reused. This research studies a data-centric framework for industrial embodied intelligence by constructing a logistics data flywheel. Our framework converts daily operations into reusable data assets, uses World Models to generate reliable supervision for long-tail parcel manipulation, and feeds deployment feedback back into policy improvement. As an initial result, \textit{WM-DAgger} introduces a World-Model-based data aggregation framework that synthesizes out-of-distribution recovery data for robust imitation learning. Building on this result, ongoing work explores how large-scale in-the-wild multimodal data, including labeled human demonstrations, unlabeled operational videos, and system-level robot logs, can be aligned for policy learning and transformed into feedback for continual system improvement.

</details>


### [126] [World-Language-Action Model for Unified World Modeling, Language Reasoning, and Action Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05979)
*Yi Yang,Zhihong Liu,Siqi Kou,Yiyang Chen,Yanzhe Hu,Jianbo Zhou,Boyuan Zhao,Zhijie Wei,Xiao Xia,Xueqi Li,Pengfei Liu,Zhijie Deng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: WLA (world-language-action) is a new embodied foundation model class that unifies world modeling and language reasoning via an autoregressive Transformer to jointly predict textual subtasks, subgoal images, and robot actions, enabling fast inference and strong long-horizon multi-task performance, including cross-embodiment learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge world modeling (egocentric video-based prediction) with language-enabled reasoning to handle complex, long-horizon robotic tasks, combining a world-modeling interface with language capabilities and a scalable, test-time adjustable architecture.

Method: WLA uses an autoregressive Transformer backbone to predict the next state, including semantic textual intent and fine-grained dynamics, supervised by a World Expert through a world-modeling objective; an Action Expert leverages the learned state-action correlations; meta-queries allow world predictions to implicitly influence action generation and can be disabled during inference; a test-time activated world-prediction mode enables scaling. WLA-0 is a 2B-parameter prototype achieving 40 ms inference on RTX 5090.

Result: Empirical evaluations show state-of-the-art performance on multi-task and long-horizon tasks: 92.94% success on RoboTwin2.0 Clean and 56.5% on RMBench; demonstrates effective learning from cross-embodiment robot videos without action annotations.

Conclusion: WLA-0 demonstrates promising integration of world modeling and language reasoning for embodied AI, with fast inference, test-time scaling, and potential to acquire new tasks from cross-embodiment data; points to future work in scaling models and expanding cross-domain capabilities.

Abstract: We propose world-language-action (WLA) models as a new class of embodied foundation models. WLA takes textual instructions, images, and robot states as inputs to jointly predict textual subtasks, subgoal images, and robot actions, conjoining the \emph{world modeling interface} to learn from extensive egocentric videos as in the world-action model (WAM) and the \emph{language reasoning} capacities to solve complex long-horizon tasks as in vision-language-action (VLA) models. At the core of WLA lies an \emph{autoregressive (AR)} Transformer backbone, instead of a bidirectional diffusion Transformer as in WAMs, to predict the \emph{next state}, comprising the \emph{semantic-level} textual intention and complementary \emph{fine-grained} physical dynamics. The physical dynamics are supervised by the world modeling objective based on a dedicated World Expert, and are leveraged to ease the characterization of the state-action correlation for the Action Expert. WLA leverages meta-queries to make the world prediction \emph{implicitly} impact the action generation so that the former can be disabled during inference. The world prediction can also be activated to enable test-time scaling for improved robot control. Our WLA-0 prototype, with 2B active parameters, achieves 40 ms per inference on an NVIDIA RTX 5090. Evaluations across simulated and real-world environments demonstrate that WLA-0 achieves state-of-the-art multi-task and long-horizon learning abilities, e.g., 92.94\% success rate on RoboTwin2.0 Clean and 56.5\% success rate on RMBench. WLA-0 also holds the promise to learn novel tasks directly from \emph{cross-embodiment robot videos} without action annotations.

</details>


### [127] [Merging model-based control with multi-agent reinforcement learning for multi-agent cooperative teaming strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06011)
*Christian Llanes,Spencer W. Jensen,Samuel Coogan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel MA-AC-MPC framework that integrates multi-agent actor-critic reinforcement learning with model predictive control to produce safe and dynamically feasible cooperative policies, validated in pursuit-evasion and a heterogeneous drone-rover landing task with hardware results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To combine MARL's capacity for learning cooperative policies under long-horizon, non-differentiable rewards with MPC's robustness, safety guarantees, and fast short-horizon re-planning, enabling safe, dynamically feasible actions in cooperative multi-agent tasks.

Method: Develop MA-AC-MPC by extending actor-critic MPC to the multi-agent setting. Compare against MA-AC-MLP on a pursuit-evasion scenario. Use augmented proportional navigation for pursuers. Demonstrate in a heterogeneous hardware scenario (drone + omni-wheeled rover) with repeatable, 100% landing success for MA-AC-MPC vs 60% for MA-AC-MLP; assess robustness in hardware across environments.

Result: MA-AC-MPC demonstrates robust, safe, dynamically feasible coordination in multi-agent tasks. In simulation (pursuit-evasion), it outperforms MA-AC-MLP. In hardware, it achieves perfect success (100%) in a heterogeneous landing task, compared to 60% for the MA-AC-MLP baseline, indicating improved reliability and robustness.

Conclusion: The MA-AC-MPC framework effectively combines MARL with MPC to deliver safe, cooperative, dynamically feasible actions in multi-agent systems, with empirical advantages over a purely learning-based baseline and demonstrated hardware robustness in heterogeneous environments.

Abstract: In this work, we propose a framework that combines multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) with model-based control to achieve safe, dynamically feasible actions in cooperative multi-agent tasks. Multi-agent reinforcement learning provides the advantage of learning cooperative policies for multi-agent teams from discrete non-differentiable rewards in a long planning horizon. Model-predictive control is robust and offers safe, dynamically feasible actions in a fast replanning framework for short horizons. We propose an algorithm that extends actor-critic model predictive control for MARL which we refer to as multi-agent actor-critic model predictive control (MA-AC-MPC). We demonstrate the capabilities of this algorithm by applying it to a multi-agent pursuit-evasion scenario. Specifically, we compare the evader team's strategy using the MA-AC-MPC model and a multi-layer perceptron model (MA-AC-MLP). The pursuer team uses augmented proportional navigation as it is accepted as an advanced adversarial control law. We also provide an example with a heterogeneous environment where a drone and omni-wheeled rover cooperate to achieve repeatable and successful landing with 100% success rate in hardware for MA-AC-MPC compared to 60% for MA-AC-MLP. We demonstrate the robustness of the proposed MA-AC-MPC algorithm in hardware for both environments.

</details>


### [128] [RealDexUMI: A Wearable Universal Manipulation Interface for Dexterous Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06033)
*Chaoyi Xu,Yixuan Jiang,Jiahui Huan,Yuhui Fu,Haoyu Zhou,Weitian Yuan,Jiayi Yu,Wanpeng Zhang,Haoqi Yuan,Zongqing Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A wearable universal manipulation interface (RealDexUMI) with a shared end-effector and isomorphic glove enables retargeting-free, intuitive dexterous manipulation; achieves 88.75% success across eight tasks and cross-embodiment transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of dexterous manipulation data collection: retargeting losses, embodiment conversion, and costly teleoperation, by providing intuitive, contact-aware, scalable data collection to enable deployable dexterity.

Method: Hardware/software fusion: a palm-side isomorphic teleoperation glove maps human finger joints to robot-hand joints; a shared end-effector module combines a lightweight dexterous hand, in-hand vision, and fingertip tactile sensing; yields zero-gap data with matched observations between collection and deployment; evaluated across eight real-robot tasks with fine-grained, contact-rich, long-horizon, and bimanual manipulation.

Result: Eight real-robot tasks; average success rate 88.75%; generalizes to unseen initial poses; transferable across three embodiments.

Conclusion: RealDexUMI demonstrates deployable dexterous data collection with intuitive control and rich sensing, enabling cross-embodiment transfer and broad generalization; a step toward scalable dexterous manipulation pipelines.

Abstract: Learning dexterous manipulation requires demonstrations that preserve fine hand-object interactions while remaining executable at deployment. Existing pipelines either lose deployable dexterity through retargeting or embodiment conversion, or rely on robot-specific teleoperation that is costly to scale and often lacks intuitive, contact-aware control for dexterous data collection. We present RealDexUMI, a wearable universal manipulation interface built around a shared dexterous end-effector module that integrates a lightweight dexterous hand, in-hand vision, and fingertip tactile sensing. A palm-side isomorphic teleoperation glove maps human finger inputs to robot-hand joint commands, enabling real-time, retargeting-free, intuitive, and precise hand control. The shared hand and sensing modules yield zero-gap end-effector data, with matched in-hand observations, tactile signals, contacts, and hand actions between collection and deployment.
