Across eight real-robot tasks spanning fine-grained, contact-rich, long-horizon, and bimanual manipulation, policies trained on RealDexUMI data achieve an average success rate of 88.75%, generalize to unseen initial poses, and transfer across three embodiments. Website: https://research.beingbeyond.com/realdexumi

</details>


### [129] [Gotta Grow Fast: Design and Benchmarking of a Tip Mount for High-Speed Vine Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06040)
*Antonio Alvarez Valdivia,Robert Reeve,Ankush Dhawan,Ciera McFarland,Chad Council,Margaret McGuinness,Nathaniel Hanson*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Triangular roller tip mount reduces internal resistance during growth by rolling contact, enabling reliable tip-mounted sensor integration on TPU-coated vine robots; validated with a custom tail-tension testbed showing lower tail tension and improved repeatability versus prior designs; CAD and testbed provided for replication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrating cameras and sensors at the tip of soft growing vines is hindered by continual material turnover, interlayer friction, added tip weight, and constriction, necessitating a mounting solution that preserves eversion dynamics while enabling sensor/tool mounting.

Method: Iterative failure analysis to design a triangular roller tip mount; developed a testbed that isolates tip mounting effects by measuring tail tension during eversion; comparative experiments across multiple mount variants, including prior designs, using a TPU-coated ripstop nylon vine robot; CAD and testbed resources provided.

Result: The triangular roller mount achieves the lowest tail tension and most repeatable growth among tested variants; enables consistent eversion on TPU-coated ripstop nylon; provides a validated mount design and a repeatable benchmarking framework.

Conclusion: Offers a validated tip mount design and benchmarking framework to advance sensor and tool integration in soft growing robots; CAD for the mount and testbed are publicly available for replication and further work.

Abstract: Soft, growing vine robots extend through tip eversion, a mechanism that enables navigation through cluttered environments. However, integrating cameras and other sensors at the tip is uniquely challenging because the material forming the tip is constantly renewed as the robot grows. This continual material turnover, combined with friction between internal layers, added tip weight, and fabric constriction, complicates sensor and tool mounting. These limitations hinder the deployment of vine robots for inspection and search tasks, where rapid growth while carrying tip-mounted sensors is essential. In this work, we present a triangular roller tip mount that reduces internal resistance during growth by rolling rather than sliding against the robot body. The design was refined through iterative failure analysis, enabling, for the first time, consistent eversion on a TPU-coated ripstop nylon vine robot. To quantitatively evaluate mount performance, we introduce a custom testbed that isolates tip mounting effects by measuring tail tension during eversion. Comparative experiments across multiple mount variants, including prior designs, show that our triangular roller mount achieves the lowest tail tension and most repeatable growth performance. These results establish both a validated tip mount design and a repeatable benchmarking framework for advancing sensor and tool integration in soft growing robots. CAD for the mount and testbed is available at: https://sprout-mitll.github.io/tip_mounts/.

</details>


### [130] [Sample-efficient Low-level Motion Planning for Robotic Manipulation Tasks via Zero-shot Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06041)
*Yuanzhi He,Victor Romero-Cano,José J. Patiño,Juan David Hernández,William Sawtell,Gualtiero Colombo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: iCEM+TL combines transfer learning and reward redesign to boost iCEM-based planning for complex tasks like stacking and shelf placement, achieving up to 23% higher success in simulation and validating on a real robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of iCEM in complex manipulation tasks and long training times as robotic systems scale, by reusing knowledge from simpler tasks and task-specific reward shaping.

Method: Introduce iCEM+TL: transfer key iCEM parameters from easier upstream tasks to guide complex downstream tasks; apply Reward Redesign (RR) via task decomposition for stacking and shelf placement to optimize task-specific performance.

Result: Simulation shows up to 23% improvement in success rate; validated on a real Franka Emika robot in a stacking task, indicating practical feasibility.

Conclusion: iCEM+TL with RR is a promising approach to improve sample efficiency and transferability for complex robotic manipulation tasks, with demonstrated potential in real-world deployment.

Abstract: As robotic systems become more sophisticated, the growing complexity of their motion planning models and the longer training times pose substantial challenges. Evolutionary algorithms such as the Sample-efficient Cross-Entropy Method (iCEM) have recently demonstrated promising potential for low-level real-time planning by leveraging efficient knowledge reuse strategies to improve performance. Although effective in many control tasks, iCEM's performance can be constrained in more complex scenarios, particularly those requiring stacking, sliding, and shelf placement. In this work, we propose a novel iCEM+TL framework that explicitly leverages Transfer Learning (TL), where key iCEM parameters are transferred from simpler upstream tasks to guide more complex downstream tasks. Additionally, we applied Reward Redesign (RR) through task decomposition for stacking objects and shelf placement to optimize task-specific performance. Results from the simulation show that our framework achieves success rate improvements of up to 23%. The framework is further validated on a real Franka Emika robot in a stacking task, demonstrating its practical feasibility for real-world deployment.

</details>


### [131] [L-SDPPO: Policy Optimization of Spiking Diffusion Policy for Intra-vehicular Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06049)
*Liwen Zhang,Dong Zhou,Guanghui Sun,Yifei Zheng,Yuhui Hu,Kaihong Ouyang,Zuoquan Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduce L-SDPPO with Spiking Diffusion Policy and SDLI to reduce energy while handling multimodal actions in microgravity; outperforms baselines on five intra-vehicular tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intra-vehicular robotics require robust control in microgravity with unpredictable object drift and limited power; diffusion policies model complex multimodal actions but are energy-intensive.

Method: Propose L-SDPPO: Spiking Diffusion Policy optimized with reinforcement learning; incorporate SDLI (statedependent latency injection) to emulate biological neural delays for dynamic spatiotemporal perception.

Result: Achieves higher task success rates and lower energy consumption than state-of-the-art robotic manipulation methods across five representative intra-vehicular tasks.

Conclusion: Demonstrates viability of an energy-efficient, robust intra-vehicular robotic manipulation framework suitable for spacecraft operations.

Abstract: Intra-vehicular robots in spacecraft help reduce astronaut workload and improve mission efficiency. Recent research focuses on using deep learning methods to achieve the acute control required for operations in these complex environments. However, objects exhibit unpredictable, unconstrained drift without gravitational damping. These factors demand robustness against complex multimodal action distributions. Diffusion policies (DP) can model these complex actions, but their iterative sampling process consumes too much energy for the limited power budgets of spacecraft. We therefore propose a low-energy intra-vehicular robotic manipulation framework, L-SDPPO, in which the Spiking Diffusion Policy (SDP) is optimized with a reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm. Furthermore, to address the insufficient perception of dynamic spatiotemporal features in microgravity, we propose the statedependent latency injection (SDLI) mechanism, which mimics biological neural delays to dynamically regulate the timing of input information. Evaluation on five representative intra-vehicular daily tasks (e.g., hatch opening and precision container capping) shows that our method consistently achieves higher success rates and lower energy consumption, compared to the state-of-the-art robotic manipulation methods. These results demonstrate our method is a viable intra-vehicular robotic manipulation method.

</details>


### [132] [A Conversational Framework for Human-Robot Collaborative Manipulation with Distributed Generative AI models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06061)
*Arash Ghasemzadeh Kakroudi,Roel Pieters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A distributed ROS 2-based framework integrates language and vision-language models for human-robot collaborative manipulation, with modular nodes for language, grounding, orchestration, and motion; image-space targets are mapped to robot-frame goals; a web dashboard requires operator confirmation; evaluated on Franka FR3 for reliability/latency under scene ambiguity and LLM/VLM config variants; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable flexible, scalable, and responsive human-robot collaboration in manipulation tasks by tightly integrating language understanding, visual grounding, and motion execution across distributed hardware, while maintaining safety through operator oversight.

Method: A distributed ROS 2 architecture with separate nodes for language understanding, visual grounding, task orchestration, and motion execution. From free-form commands, the system emits structured action requests (pick/place/handover). A vision-language model provides image-space targets that are converted to robot-frame goals using depth data and calibration. A web dashboard displays intermediate intents/grounding overlays and requires explicit operator confirmation prior to motion. Experimental evaluation on a Franka FR3 platform assesses end-to-end reliability and latency under increasing scene ambiguity and compares different LLM/VLM configurations within the same pipeline.

Result: The abstract reports end-to-end reliability and latency evaluations and a comparative analysis of LLM/VLM configurations on the Franka FR3 platform; no numerical results are provided in the abstract, but the study demonstrates the framework’s effectiveness and the impact of ambiguity and configuration choices.

Conclusion: A modular, distributed ROS 2 framework can effectively fuse language, grounding, and motion in human-robot collaboration, with safety and controllability via a required operator confirmation step. The work facilitates comparing LLM/VLM configurations within a single pipeline and provides open-source code and documentation for replication.

Abstract: This paper presents a distributed conversational framework for human-robot collaborative manipulation that integrates local language and vision-language models (VLMs) with a Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2)-based execution stack. Language understanding, visual grounding, orchestration, and motion execution run as separate ROS 2 nodes, enabling flexible deployment across distributed hardware while maintaining a responsive control loop. From free-form user commands, the system generates structured action requests for pick, place, and handover. It uses a VLM to return image-space targets, which are converted into metric robot-frame goals using depth and calibration. A web dashboard exposes intermediate intent and grounding overlays (pixel, depth, and robot-frame) and requires explicit operator confirmation before any motion is executed. Experiments on a Franka FR3 platform evaluate end-to-end task reliability and latency under increasing working table scene ambiguity and compare alternative LLM/VLM configurations in the same pipeline. Code and full documentation are available at [github.com/cogrob-tuni/franka-llm](https://github.com/cogrob-tuni/franka-llm).

</details>


### [133] [3D Underwater Path Planning via Generative Flow Field Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06077)
*Zachary Cooper-Baldock,Paulo E. Santos,Russell S. A. Brinkworth,Karl Sammut*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: cGAN-based surrogates (PatchGAN and 2D3DGAN with self-attention) efficiently synthesize 128^3 CFD wake fields to accelerate 3D AUV path planning, achieving near-CFD performance at microsecond-scale inference and recovering ~45-60% of CFD gains against uniform current.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the prohibitive computational cost of CFD for onboard, real-time path planning in complex 3D propeller wake environments, enabling faster, energy-efficient decisions.

Method: Develop a hierarchical pipeline that converts scalar operating conditions into full 128^3 voxel flow fields, using two cGAN architectures (regularised PatchGAN and 2D3DGAN with self-attention) as drop-in CFD surrogates within an energy-weighted A* planner; evaluate across 19,800 trajectories and 550 flow conditions under four knowledge levels (uniform, CFD ground-truth, PatchGAN, 2D3DGAN-SA).

Result: Inference times ~28–146 μs per wake field; full CFD wake knowledge reduces energy by 5.7–12.5% and lowers high-velocity wake-core encounters by up to 77.8% relative to uniform-current planning; cGAN surrogates recover ~45–60% of CFD gains while maintaining edge-device feasibility.

Conclusion: This work is the first systematic quantification of downstream planning value from cGAN-predicted hydrodynamic fields in 3D maritime robotics, demonstrating viability of ML surrogates to enable real-time, energy-aware path planning in complex wake environments.

Abstract: Autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) launch and recovery (LAR) into the hull of an advancing host platform requires traversal of a complex, three-dimensional propeller wake whose hydrodynamic structure cannot be characterised by a uniform current model. High-fidelity Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations resolve this structure with sufficient accuracy for path planning, but their computational cost renders them impractical for onboard use. We address this gap by integrating two conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) architectures -- a regularised PatchGAN and a 2D3DGAN with self-attention -- as drop-in replacements for RANS CFD data within a three-dimensional, energy-weighted A* path planning framework. Both generators are driven by a hierarchical pipeline that synthesises full $128^3$ voxel flow field volumes from scalar operating condition inputs alone, with end-to-end inference times of approximately 28-146 $μ$s, compared to hours for a single RANS computation. We benchmark all four environmental knowledge levels: uniform current, ground-truth CFD, PatchGAN, and 2D3DGAN~SA across 19,800 independently generated trajectories spanning 550 distinct flow conditions. Full CFD wake knowledge reduces energy expenditure by 5.7-12.5% and high-velocity wake-core encounters by up to 77.8% relative to uniform-current planning, with both benefits scaling with operating severity. The cGAN surrogates recover approximately 45-60% of the CFD energy benefit and high-velocity cell avoidance benefit while operating at inference speeds compatible with edge device use. These results provide the first systematic quantification of the downstream path planning value of cGAN-predicted hydrodynamic fields in a three-dimensional maritime robotics application.

</details>


### [134] [Towards Realistic 3D Sonar Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06130)
*Youssef Attia,Davide Costa,Francesco Wanderlingh,Filippo Campagnaro,Enrico Simetti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A modular, acoustically-grounded 3D sonar simulation integrated in NVIDIA Isaac Sim, validated with hardware-in-the-loop; shows progress toward acoustics-driven volumetric sensing but qualitative validation and remaining sim-to-real gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current sonar simulation relies on geometry-driven rendering that neglects refraction, multi-path, and phase formation; need realistic physics-based acoustic modelling to accelerate algorithm development for underwater robotics.

Method: Develop modular architecture integrating GPU-accelerated graphics with physically grounded acoustic propagation; volumetric 3D sonar model in Isaac Sim; modeled after Water Linked 3D-15 sensor; integration into underwater simulation; hardware-in-the-loop with modified FastLIO2 SLAM on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano; fuse synthetic sonar, DVL, IMU, pressure; qualitative comparison with harbor sheet-pile real data to assess sim-to-real gap.

Result: Hardware-in-the-loop validation performed; qualitative alignment with real data; identified remaining sim-to-real gap and proposed roadmap toward fully acoustics-driven volumetric sensing.

Conclusion: This work advances realistic sonar simulation and provides a foundation for acoustics-driven underwater perception; need quantitative validation and broader testing; expect improvements with further physics fidelity and sensor models.

Abstract: As underwater robotics research increasingly addresses complex 3D perception and autonomous navigation, the fidelity of sonar simulation has become a key factor in algorithm development. Current simulation frameworks typically rely on geometry-driven rendering, approximating 3D sonar as an underwater equivalent to LiDAR, which fails to account for fundamental acoustic phenomena such as refraction, multi-path interference, and phase-dependent signal formation. This paper proposes a modular architecture for realistic 3D sonar simulation that integrates GPU-accelerated graphics engines with physically grounded acoustic propagation principles. We implement a volumetric 3D sonar model within the NVIDIA Isaac Sim environment, modeled after the Water Linked 3D-15 sensor, and integrate it into a comprehensive underwater simulation framework. The system is validated through a hardware-in-the-loop configuration, where a modified FastLIO2 SLAM pipeline, executed on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, performs sensor fusion using synthetic 3D sonar, DVL, IMU, and pressure data. Finally, a qualitative comparison between simulated outputs and real-world data from harbor sheet-pile inspections is provided, characterizing the remaining sim-to-real gap and establishing a roadmap toward fully acoustics-driven volumetric sensing.

</details>


### [135] [MotionDisco: Motion Discovery for Extreme Humanoid Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06139)
*Ilyass Taouil,Michal Ciebelski,Shafeef Omar,Haizhou Zhao,Angela Dai,Aaron M. Johnson,Majid Khadiv*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MotionDisco discovers long-horizon, contact-rich humanoid loco-manipulation motions from scratch using an LLM-guided evolutionary search, without teleoperation or motion retargeting, and transfers them to real robots via RL trackers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon loco-manipulation with many contact interactions has a combinatorial search space; traditional methods rely on demonstrations or retargeting and struggle for scalable discovery.

Method: Use large language model-guided evolutionary search over interaction sequences combined with a sequential kinodynamic trajectory optimizer and pruning; ablation studies; train RL tracking policies to deploy on real humanoid.

Result: Demonstrates successful whole-body trajectories across challenging tasks; ablation confirms value of LLM-guided search; real-robot deployment via learned tracking policies; claims first fully automated evolutionary search discovery and deployment.

Conclusion: Presents a novel framework to autonomously discover and deploy long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation skills; suggests effectiveness of LLM-guided search and trajectory optimization; supplementary videos available.

Abstract: We present MotionDisco, a framework that discovers contact-rich, long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation motions from scratch, without relying on teleoperation or motion retargeting from human demonstrations. This is challenging because the space of possible contact interactions grows combinatorially with the task horizon and the number of objects in the scene. MotionDisco enables rapid discovery of novel motions by coupling a large language model (LLM) guided evolutionary search over sequences of interactions with an efficient sequential kinodynamic trajectory optimizer and pruning strategy, enabling the rapid discovery of novel skills. Through extensive ablation studies, we show that our LLM-guided search discovers successful whole-body trajectories across several challenging long-horizon tasks. Finally, by training reinforcement learning tracking policies on the discovered trajectories, we transfer the motions to a real humanoid robot. This is the first work to discover and deploy long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation skills entirely through automated evolutionary search. Supplementary videos of the experiments are available at: https://youtu.be/DHiVz34QYlw.

</details>


### [136] [AffordanceVLA: A Vision-Language-Action Model Empowering Action Generation through Affordance-Aware Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06155)
*Qize Yu,Jiadi You,Yuran Wang,Jiaqi Liang,Bowen Ping,Yang Tian,Yue Chen,Minghong Cai,Zeying Gong,Ruihai Wu,Yinchuan Li,Junwei Liang,Yingcong Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AffordanceVLA proposes a structured, intermediate representation of affordances to bridge perception, language, and action in Vision-Language-Action models for robotic manipulation, using Which2Act, Where2Act, How2Act in a Mixture-of-Transformer with curriculum training and data augmentation, achieving robust results in both sim and real-world.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The gap between VLM semantic spaces and embodied control leads to poor perception-action mappings; a task-oriented intermediate representation is needed to align perception with manipulation actions.

Method: Three components (Which2Act, Where2Act, How2Act) mapping to object-level grounding, 2D affordance maps, and 3D geometry; integrated into Mixture-of-Transformer with specialized experts; three-stage training with progressive data curriculum; automated data augmentation to compensate sparse dense affordance labels.

Result: Extensive experiments show strong performance across diverse manipulation scenarios in simulation and real world.

Conclusion: AffordanceVLA provides spatially grounded, semantically conditioned, and action-coupled intermediate representations that bridge vision-language-action; demonstrated robustness and generalization.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models leverage the rich world knowledge of pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to enable instruction-following robotic manipulation. However, the structural mismatch between VLM semantic spaces and embodied control policies often hinders the learning of precise perception--action mappings. To address this challenge, we propose \textbf{AffordanceVLA}, a unified framework that introduces structured affordance forecasting as a task-oriented intermediate representation to establish a more precise and robust perception--action mapping. Specifically, we progressively model manipulation priors through three complementary components: 1) \textbf{Which2Act} for object-centric grounding via visual latent prediction to suppress distractions; 2) \textbf{Where2Act} for 2D interaction localization via affordance map estimation; and 3) \textbf{How2Act} for 3D geometric reasoning to guide manipulation policies. These affordance cues provide spatially grounded, semantically conditioned, and action-coupled intermediate representations, thereby naturally bridging vision, language and action. We integrate these modules into a Mixture-of-Transformer (MoT) architecture with specialized experts and train the model using a three-stage training strategy with a progressive data curriculum. To overcome the scarcity of dense affordance labels in robotic datasets, we also develop a robust automated data augmentation pipeline. Extensive experiments on simulation and real-world demonstrate that AffordanceVLA achieves strong performance across diverse manipulation scenarios.

</details>


### [137] [ActiveMimic: Egocentric Video Pretraining with Active Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06194)
*Xingyao Lin,Guojin Zhong,Tianyi Lu,Ziyi Ye,Yichen Zhu,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ActiveMimic is a pretraining framework that uses egocentric human video to learn active perception by recovering synchronized camera and wrist trajectories from a single body-worn camera, modeling camera motion as a viewpoint action, and jointly learning perception and manipulation before adapting to a robot. It closes the gap between egocentric video and robot data pretraining, achieving robot-like performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot-pretraining with egocentric human video lags behind robot-data pretraining due to missing active perception signals—camera motion during manipulation is treated as noise. Recovering viewpoint dynamics from body-worn video could provide the crucial signal to enable effective pretraining.

Method: ActiveMimic recovers synchronized camera and wrist trajectories from a single body-worn RGB camera, treats camera motion as a viewpoint action, and jointly learns active perception and manipulation from in-the-wild egocentric video, followed by adaptation to a target robot.

Result: In real-world experiments across tasks with varying active perception demands, ActiveMimic outperforms baselines pretrained on human video and matches state-of-the-art models pretrained on robot data.

Conclusion: Active perception capability in this context arises from pretraining on egocentric human video rather than robot-specific fine-tuning, highlighting active perception as the key to unlocking egocentric video for robot pretraining.

Abstract: Egocentric human video offers a scalable alternative to robot data for pretraining, yet models pretrained on such video consistently underperform those pretrained on robot data. We attribute this gap to a missing signal, the active perception behavior in egocentric videos, where humans continuously reposition their viewpoint during manipulation, inducing camera motion that standard pipelines treat as noise. To address this, we present ActiveMimic, a pretraining framework that recovers synchronized camera and wrist trajectories from a single body-worn RGB camera, models camera motion as a viewpoint action, and jointly learns active perception and manipulation from in-the-wild egocentric human video before adapting to a target robot. Empirically, real-world experiments across tasks with diverse active perception demands show that ActiveMimic consistently surpasses baselines pretrained on human video and matches state-of-the-art models pretrained on robot data. Further analysis provides evidence that active perception capability originates from egocentric human video pretraining rather than robot-specific fine-tuning, confirming active perception as the key to unlocking egocentric human video for robot pretraining.

</details>


### [138] [TAM: Torque Adaptation Module for Robust Motion Transfer in Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06218)
*Dongwon Son,Florian Shkurti,Jason Lee,Naman Shah,Beomjoon Kim,Dieter Fox*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TAM is a proprioception-driven torque adaptation module that sits between policy commands and the robot, enabling cross-robot zero-shot transfer for dynamic manipulation by learning residual torques from history; trained in randomized sim and fine-tuned per robot with no real-robot data, it improves zero-shot real-robot performance across multiple policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Policy transfer across robots suffers from sim-to-real gaps, unknown payloads, and dynamics differences. Traditional domain randomization or system identification either over-constrain the task or require data collection for each robot. TAM aims to decouple robot variability from policy training.

Method: TAM consists of a history encoder that embeds proprioceptive history into a latent state and a torque adaptor that computes residual torque corrections. It sits between the low-level controller and the robot's torque interface, and is agnostic to the policy’s observations and action space, enabling reuse across different action spaces. Training uses randomized simulation with multi-robot pretraining followed by robot-specific fine-tuning, requiring no real-robot data.

Result: Zero-shot real-robot evaluation on a Franka Panda across dynamic manipulation tasks: a vision-based box-pushing policy (RL), a flip policy (BC), and an MPC ball-on-plate balancing policy. TAM improves zero-shot real-robot execution compared to online system identification and RMA baselines, demonstrating robust dynamic manipulation performance.

Conclusion: By offloading domain randomization to TAM and relying on proprioceptive history, TAM enables cross-robot transfer of manipulation policies without policy-domain randomization and without real-robot data during fine-tuning, improving robustness in dynamic manipulation.

Abstract: A policy tuned for one robot often behaves differently on another, whether due to the sim-to-real gap, unknown payloads, or the differing dynamics of two instances of the same robot. In contact-rich, dynamic manipulation, even small motion discrepancies can result in failure to track reference motion, since they disrupt the timing and modes of contact. Common remedies, such as domain randomization or system identification, either produce overly conservative task policies or require data that must be recollected for each robot or payload. We introduce the Torque Adaptation Module (TAM), a learned module that adapts the torque commands sent to the robot to match the behavior of an ideal robot. TAM operates between the low-level controller that tracks the policy's actions and the robot's torque interface. It includes a history encoder that embeds proprioceptive history into a latent state and a torque adaptor that computes residual torque corrections. Because TAM depends only on proprioceptive history and not on policy observations, or the action space, the same TAM weights can be reused to adapt policies with different action spaces (joint targets, end-effector targets, or direct torques). The policies themselves do not need to be trained with domain randomization of robot parameters. Instead, we offload the need for domain randomization to TAM by training it entirely in randomized simulation, using multi-robot pretraining followed by a robot-specific fine-tuning step that still requires no real-robot data. We evaluate TAM zero-shot on a real Franka Panda robot across dynamic manipulation tasks that include a vision-based box pushing policy (from RL), a flip policy (from BC), and an MPC ball-on-plate balancing. Our experiments show that TAM improves zero-shot real-robot execution compared to online system identification and RMA baselines and enables robust dynamic manipulation performance.

</details>


### [139] [CLEAR: Cognition and Latent Evaluation for Adaptive Routing in End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06219)
*Yining Xing,Zehong Ke,Zhiyuan Liu,Yanbo Jiang,Wenhao Yu,Jianqiang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CLEAR combines ultra-fast generative planning with deep semantic reasoning for autonomous driving by replacing iterative diffusion with a single-step latent drift in a VAE, guided by an adaptive scheduler and QA-driven scene states to select optimal trajectories, achieving state-of-the-art planning on NAVSIM v1 without dense annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end driving models must balance multi-modal maneuver generation with real-time inference; diffusion-based planning yields diverse behaviors but incurs prohibitive latency for safety-critical deployment. A fast, semantically aware planner is needed to meet latency and safety constraints.

Method: - Use Drive-JEPA as the visual encoder. - Replace multi-step denoising with a single-step conditional drift in a VAE latent space, adding a conditioning coefficient α to trade off diversity vs. expert precision. - Fine-tune Qwen-3.5-0.8B on driving QA pairs to produce scene-aware hidden states. - Adaptive Scheduler selects α and sample count N from predefined schemes. - Cross-attention scorer ranks candidate trajectories to select the best one. - Evaluate on NAVSIM v1. - Claim no dense geometric annotations or iterative sampling required.

Result: On NAVSIM v1, CLEAR achieves a state-of-the-art PDMS of 93.7, demonstrating that high-fidelity, multi-modal planning can be performed efficiently with minimal annotations and without iterative sampling.

Conclusion: The approach shows that fast, semantically informed planning is feasible for autonomous driving, balancing diversity and precision through adaptive conditioning and QA-guided state representations, enabling real-time, multi-modal trajectory selection without heavy geometric supervision.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving models often struggle to balance multi-modal maneuver generation with real-time inference constraints. While diffusion models successfully capture diverse driving behaviors, their iterative denoising process incurs unacceptable latency for safety-critical deployment. To address this, we propose CLEAR (Cognition and Latent Evaluation for Adaptive Routing), a framework that combines ultra-fast generative planning with deep semantic reasoning. CLEAR employs Drive-JEPA as the visual encoder and replaces the multi-step denoising chain with a single-step conditional drift in a VAE latent space, introducing a conditioning coefficient to balance diversity and expert precision. Meanwhile, we fully fine-tune Qwen~3.5~0.8B on driving QA pairs to extract scene-aware hidden states. These states guide both an Adaptive Scheduler, which selects the conditioning coefficient $α$ and sample count $N$ from a discrete set of predefined schemes, and a cross-attention scorer that selects the optimal trajectory from candidates. On the NAVSIM v1 benchmark, CLEAR achieves a state-of-the-art PDMS of 93.7. Our results demonstrate that high-fidelity, multi-modal planning can be executed efficiently without dense geometric annotations or iterative sampling.

</details>


### [140] [MPCoT: Reward-Guided Multi-Path Latent Reasoning for Test-Time Scalable Vision-Language-Action](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06245)
*Boyang Zhang,Lianlei Shan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MPCoT introduces a reward-guided multi-path latent reasoning framework that preserves the original 8-step action interface while maintaining M parallel hypotheses refined for K steps and softly aggregated before action decoding, achieving improved long-horizon VLA performance on LIBERO and CALVIN with ablations on depth, width, and reward-guided supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language-Action policies are brittle in long-horizon, high-uncertainty control. One-pass action decoding limits deliberation; explicit chain-of-thought improves reasoning but adds token latency and a misaligned text-to-action interface. The work seeks deliberation that does not incur reasoning tokens while retaining a clean action interface.

Method: Initialize M hypotheses; refine them for K weight-tied steps; softly aggregate them prior to decoding. Use a training-only path-preference objective to evaluate candidate action branches based on expert-action consistency, world-model/VLM-based progress, and success feedback to align the latent path scorer with downstream execution quality. Preserve the original 8-step action interface, generate zero reasoning tokens, and expose configurable inference controls (K, M).

Result: Under matched protocols on LIBERO and CALVIN, MPCoT improves long-horizon performance. Ablations confirm depth-width effects, confidence-weighted aggregation, and reward-guided path supervision.

Conclusion: A multi-path latent reasoning framework with reward-guided path supervision can enhance long-horizon VLA control while preserving a token-free action interface and configurable inference controls.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies remain brittle in long-horizon and high-uncertainty control, where one-pass action decoding provides limited inference-time deliberation. Explicit chain-of-thought can increase reasoning depth, but introduces token latency and an indirect text-to-action interface. We propose MPCoT, a reward-guided multi-path latent reasoning framework that initializes $M$ hypotheses, refines them for K weight-tied steps, and softly aggregates them before action decoding. A training-only path-preference objective evaluates candidate action branches with expert-action consistency, world-model/VLM-based progress, and success feedback to align the latent path scorer with downstream execution quality. MPCoT preserves the original 8-step action interface, generates zero reasoning tokens, and exposes configurable inference controls (K,M). Under matched protocols on LIBERO and CALVIN, MPCoT improves long-horizon performance, with ablations confirming depth-width effects, confidence-weighted aggregation, and reward-guided path supervision.

</details>


### [141] [Breaking Time: A Fully Gaussian Framework for Distributed and Continuous-Time SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06250)
*Davide Ceriola,Simone Ferrari,Luca Di Giammarino,Leonardo Brizi,Giorgio Grisetti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: G-solver combines Gaussian Belief Propagation with Gaussian Process motion priors to enable fully Gaussian, distributed continuous-time SLAM for heterogeneous, asynchronous sensors, including multi-camera setups, with interpolation-enabled trajectories and data-driven hyperparameters; runtimes are competitive and an open-source implementation is provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to fuse diverse, asynchronous sensor streams (e.g., rolling shutter cameras, LiDAR, radar, event sensors) in a principled continuous-time framework. Existing methods struggle with synchronization, scalability, and maintaining a probabilistic trajectory representation suitable for interpolation.

Method: Introduce G-solver, a fully Gaussian, distributed framework that uses Gaussian Belief Propagation for scalable, decentralized inference and Gaussian Process priors for continuous-time trajectory modeling. The GP prior provides a probabilistic trajectory with data-driven hyperparameters and enables accurate interpolation. The approach naturally extends to multi-camera systems without extra synchronization, and is implemented in an open-source package.

Result: Evaluations on synthetic and real data, including rolling shutter scenarios and distributed multi-camera optimization, show accurate and stable trajectory estimates with runtimes comparable to existing continuous-time methods.

Conclusion: The G-solver framework achieves scalable, decentralized continuous-time SLAM with probabilistic interpolation, leveraging GBP and GP priors to fuse heterogeneous sensors without specialized synchronization. The open-source release facilitates deployment and further research.

Abstract: Continuous-time SLAM provides a principled framework for fusing heterogeneous sensors while estimating smooth trajectories, and is particularly well-suited for handling heterogeneous, asynchronous sensor streams with non-uniform readout patterns, such as rolling shutter cameras, LiDAR scanners, radar sweeps, or event-based sensors. In this work, we introduce G-solver, a fully Gaussian and distributed framework that combines Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP) with Gaussian Process (GP) motion priors for continuous-time trajectory estimation. Our GP model provides a probabilistic representation of the trajectory, enabling consistent interpolation and the use of data-driven hyperparameters, while GBP offers a scalable message-passing formulation well-suited for decentralized settings. The resulting solver naturally extends to multi-camera scenarios without specialized synchronization or engineering effort. We evaluate the approach on synthetic and real data, including rolling shutter and distributed multi-camera optimization, demonstrating accurate and stable estimation with runtimes comparable to existing continuous-time methods. An open-source implementation is released.

</details>


### [142] [RadiusFPS: Efficient Farthest Point Sampling on CPUs and GPUs via Spherical Voxel Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06255)
*Ziyang Yu,Xiang Li,Qiong Chang,Jun Miyazaki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RadiusFPS accelerates Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) via spherical voxel pruning and a coordinate-wise point-skip test, preserving the FPS update rule. RadiusFPS-G provides a warp-level GPU implementation with memory-efficient, fused kernels. It achieves up to 2.5x speedups over GPU FPS on indoor/outdoor datasets and maintains comparable accuracy, enabling faster end-to-end inference when combined with FastPoint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical FPS has a high time complexity that becomes a latency bottleneck for high-rate 3D sensors (e.g., LiDAR). There is a need for faster, memory-efficient FPS that preserves sampling behavior for reliable downstream perception in real-time robotic systems.

Method: Index the point cloud with spherical voxels to derive a conservative geometric bound that prunes redundant distance computations per iteration; include a coordinate-wise point-skip test to remove residual updates; preserve the standard FPS update rule under the same initialization and tie-breaking policy. Implement RadiusFPS-G as a warp-level GPU solution that fuses voxel selection, pruning, and distance updates into memory-coalesced kernels to avoid global-memory round-trips.

Result: RadiusFPS-G achieves up to 2.5x speedup over GPU-based FPS on indoor (S3DIS, ScanNet) and outdoor (SemanticKITTI) benchmarks, using roughly half the GPU memory while maintaining comparable segmentation accuracy. It matches or exceeds QuickFPS among evaluated methods. When paired with the FastPoint sampler, end-to-end inference is the fastest among all configurations.

Conclusion: RadiusFPS (and RadiusFPS-G) provide a practical, high-quality FPS acceleration framework suitable for latency- and memory-constrained robotic perception, enabling real-time operation without sacrificing down-stream accuracy.

Abstract: Point clouds are a primary sensory representation for robotic perception, underpinning LiDAR-based autonomous driving, simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM), and navigation. Within these pipelines, Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) is the most well-known downsampling operator, as its uniform coverage preserves the geometric structure on which downstream perception relies. However, the large time complexity of classical FPS scales poorly with the million-point-per-second rates of modern 3D sensors, making it a dominant latency bottleneck that conflicts with the real-time and limited onboard compute budgets of robotic systems. Therefore, we propose RadiusFPS, an FPS acceleration framework based on spherical voxel pruning that preserves the standard FPS update rule under the same initialization and tie-breaking policy. By indexing the point cloud with spherical voxels, RadiusFPS derives a conservative geometric bound that prunes redundant distance computations in each iteration, complemented by a coordinate-wise point-skip test that removes residual updates. We further introduce RadiusFPS-G, a warp-level GPU implementation that fuses voxel selection, pruning, and distance update into memory-coalesced kernels, eliminating costly global-memory round-trips. On indoor (S3DIS, ScanNet) and outdoor LiDAR (SemanticKITTI) benchmarks, RadiusFPS-G attains up to 2.5x speedup over GPU-based FPS and matches or exceeds QuickFPS among the evaluated methods while using roughly half its GPU memory, with comparable segmentation accuracy. When coupled with the learning-based FastPoint sampler, the resulting pipeline achieves the fastest End-to-End inference among all evaluated configurations. These properties make high-quality FPS-style sampling practical for latency- and memory-constrained robotic vision.

</details>


### [143] [Multi-Resolution Tactile Imitation Learning for Contact-Rich Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06281)
*Rickmer Krohn,Erik Helmut,Niklas Funk,Jan Peters,Vignesh Prasad,Georgia Chalvatzaki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MiTaS proposes a multi-resolution tactile sensing framework that fuses RGB, GelSight Mini, and Evetac event data through modality-specific stems and transformer-based fusion to enhance manipulation tasks; achieves ~80% average success across five contact-rich tasks, outperforming vision-only and simpler visuo-tactile baselines; co-training with multi-tactile data yields additional gains; analysis confirms sensor contributions across task phases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of single-sensor or single-resolution sensing in manipulation learning by leveraging heterogeneous tactile sensors with complementary temporal characteristics to better handle contact-rich interactions.

Method: A multi-sensor architecture with modality-specific convolutional stems and a transformer-based fusion module that integrates RGB, GelSight Mini (vision-based tactile), and high-frequency Evetac event data into a unified representation. This representation conditions a flow-matching imitation-learning policy for downstream contact-rich manipulation tasks. The approach includes experiments on five tasks, ablations against vision-only and visual-tactile baselines, and a co-training study showing gains without relying on Evetac at evaluation. Sensor-reading and attention analyses are provided to elucidate sensor contributions.

Result: MiTaS achieved an average success rate of about 80% across tasks. Vision-only baselines reached ~31% and visual-tactile baselines ~54%, highlighting substantial performance gains. Co-training a visuo-tactile model with multi-tactile data yielded over 10 percentage points improvements in certain tasks, even when Evetac was not available during policy evaluation. Detailed analyses substantiate the different sensors' roles during task execution.

Conclusion: Multi-resolution tactile sensing with modality-specific stems and transformer-based fusion effectively leverages heterogeneous tactile data to improve manipulation learning. Different sensors contribute at distinct task phases, and the approach offers robust performance and insights into sensor importance, suggesting a generalizable pathway for integrating diverse tactile modalities in manipulation.

Abstract: Touch sensing is beneficial for solving a wide variety of manipulation tasks. While there exists a wide range of tactile sensors with different properties, exploiting the fusion of multiple heterogeneous tactile sensors to improve manipulation learning remains underexplored. We present Multi-Resolution Tactile Sensing (MiTaS), a representation framework that leverages multiple tactile sensors operating at different temporal resolutions in order to solve complex contact-rich manipulation tasks. We propose a novel architecture using modality-specific convolutional stems and transformer-based fusion that effectively fuses information from an RGB camera stream, a vision-based GelSight Mini sensor and a high-frequency event-based Evetac sensor. This multi-sensor representation then conditions a flow-matching policy for solving downstream tasks. Experimental results across five contact-rich manipulation tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of multi-resolution tactile features in imitation learning. MiTaS achieves an average success rate of 80 %, while vision-only (31 %) and visual-tactile (54 %) baselines cannot solve the task reliably. Co-training a visuo-tactile model with multi-tactile data boosts performance by over 10 \% in certain tasks, without having access to the Evetac sensor during policy evaluation. A detailed sensor-reading and attention analysis reveals the importance of different sensors throughout task execution, validating our multi-resolution tactile sensing approach. Project Page: http://mitas-touch.github.io.

</details>


### [144] [Attitude-Aided Linear Calibration of Triaxial Accelerometers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06308)
*Yongqiang Yu,Tian Huang,Yipeng Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ALAC offers a linear, attitude-aided calibration for MEMS accelerometers using a combined error matrix (CEM) to jointly estimate bias and gravity, enabling a closed-form CHLS solution with just five oriented measurements and online extension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome reliance on costly reference setups or nonlinear optimization in MEMS accelerometer calibration, enabling efficient, low-cost, self-calibration using orientation data.

Method: Introduce a combined error matrix (CEM) to represent sensor errors; jointly estimate bias and gravity vector (gravity field) while accounting for platform misalignment; decompose CEM to recover scale, non-orthogonality, and alignment rotation; under static gravity formulate a constrained homogeneous least-squares (CHLS) problem solved in closed form; require five arbitrarily oriented measurements; extend to recursive online calibration.

Result: Empirical validation on a stationary robot-mounted accelerometer and a quasi-static public IMU trajectory shows ALAC outperforms reference-based and online baselines in accuracy and noise robustness; matches iterative self-calibration under filtered data; surpasses all baselines on raw measurements; demonstrates robustness and practicality for low-cost MEMS IMUs and online calibration.

Conclusion: ALAC provides a robust, practical calibration framework for MEMS inertial platforms, enabling accurate offline and online calibration with minimal measurements while accounting for bias, gravity, and platform misalignment through a unified linear model.

Abstract: Triaxial MEMS accelerometers are widely used for inertial sensing, navigation, and sensor fusion, but existing calibration methods often rely on costly reference setups or nonlinear iterative optimization, limiting their efficiency and applicability to low-cost or self-calibrating systems. We present attitude-aided linear accelerometer calibration (ALAC), a method that operates on any platform providing orientation information, such as turntables, robotic arms, or inertial measurement units. ALAC constructs a combined error matrix (CEM) to represent sensor errors in a unified calibration model and enables linear least-squares estimation. The bias and gravity vector are jointly estimated, implicitly accounting for platform misalignment, and matrix decomposition of the CEM recovers scale, non-orthogonality, and alignment rotation parameters. Under static gravity, calibration is formulated as a constrained homogeneous least-squares (CHLS) problem and solved in closed form using standard linear algebra. Only five arbitrarily oriented measurements are required, and a recursive extension supports online or in-field calibration. Experiments on a stationary robot-mounted accelerometer and a quasi-static public IMU trajectory show that ALAC, in both offline and online modes, outperforms reference-based and online baselines in accuracy and robustness to sensor noise. On the same dataset, it matches iterative self-calibration under filtered conditions and surpasses all evaluated baselines on raw measurements. These results demonstrate a robust and practical calibration scheme for MEMS-based inertial platforms, especially low-cost IMUs and online calibration scenarios.

</details>


### [145] [Meridian: Metric-Semantic Primitive Matching for Cross-View Geo-Localization Beyond Urban Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06312)
*Mason Peterson,Qingyuan Li,Yixuan Jia,Fernando Cladera,Carlos Nieto-Granda,Camillo Jose Taylor,Jonathan P. How*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Meridian: a GNSS-denied localization method that matches high-level metric-semantic primitives between aerial images and ground RGB-D to achieve robust, training-free, cross-environment global localization with an average trajectory error of 2.4 m over 19 km.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of accurate global localization in GNSS-denied, natural outdoor environments where current methods depend on environment-specific training and struggle with repetitive geometries and featureless terrains.

Method: Proposes Meridian, matching metric-semantic primitives across aerial imagery and ground robot RGB-D data. Introduces consistency metrics to estimate a distribution over submap poses and to robustly reject outliers in pose graph optimization, enabling accurate trajectory estimation without any area-specific training or fine-tuning.

Result: Localizes a ground robot across diverse environments (autonomous driving dataset, park/campus, wilderness camp) with an average optimized trajectory error of 2.4 m over 19 km.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates strong generalization without training data tailored to a target area, offering robust GNSS-denied global localization in heterogeneous environments by leveraging cross-domain primitives and probabilistic pose-graph optimization.

Abstract: Successful robot automation requires accurate global localization to support repeatability, task planning, goal specification, and safe operation. However, reliable localization in GNSS-denied environments remains an open problem. Overhead aerial imagery offers a promising solution, but existing approaches primarily target structured urban environments and have been rarely demonstrated in unstructured natural terrain. Limitations of the state-of-the-art include a reliance on models trained for specific environments, as well as difficulty handling repetitive geometries and featureless landscapes commonly found in natural outdoor areas. To overcome these challenges, we present Meridian, a method for matching high-level metric-semantic primitives across aerial images and ground robot RGB-D camera data that achieves accurate global localization and generalizes well across diverse environments, all without any training or algorithmic fine-tuning on area-specific data. We formulate novel consistency metrics to estimate a distribution over robot submap poses and to reject outlier hypotheses in a robust pose graph optimization step for accurate robot trajectory estimation. We demonstrate that our algorithm can localize a ground robot across a wide variety of environments, including an autonomous driving dataset, a park and campus area, and a wilderness camp, with an average optimized trajectory error of 2.4 m over 19 km of ground traversal.

</details>


### [146] [VOLT: Vision and Language Trajectory Segmentation for Faster-than-Demonstration Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06323)
*Robert Ramirez Sanchez,Daniel J. Evans,Dylan P. Losey,Siddarth Jain*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VOLT enables faster-than-demonstration robotic policies by segmenting video demos into slow (precision-required) and fast (constrained) parts using vision-language cues, downsampling only the appropriate segments and training with standard imitation-learning methods such as diffusion policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans often demonstrate tasks more slowly than robots would execute them; uniformly speeding up demonstrations can degrade performance. There is a need to selectively accelerate parts of tasks while preserving precision where required.

Method: Introduce VOLT, a vision-and-language trajectory segmentation method that analyzes video demonstrations to identify when acceleration is safe. It segments demonstrations into slow and fast parts, selectively downsampling the fast segments, and outputs reformatted trajectories that can be used with standard imitation learning pipelines (e.g., diffusion policies).

Result: VOLT improves task execution speed while maintaining strong performance; baseline methods misidentify acceleration points, leading to overly cautious or unreliable policies; segmentation quality is critical for achieving speedups.

Conclusion: Accurate, context-aware segmentation of demonstrations enables reliable faster-than-demonstration policies and integrates effectively with existing imitation-learning approaches.

Abstract: Humans often take longer to demonstrate a task than a robot would need to execute it. Rather than learning to replicate the demonstration at the same pace, many industrial and practical applications require robots to perform tasks as quickly as possible. In this paper, we investigate several hypotheses for learning policies that operate faster-than-demonstrations. Our experiments show that the most effective strategy is to downsample recorded demonstrations and train the robot's policy on this accelerated data. However, uniformly downsampling an entire trajectory can be problematic. Some parts of a task can be safely sped up (e.g., unconstrained motion), while others demand slower, more precise motion (e.g., object interactions or fine manipulation). To address this challenge, we introduce VOLT, a vision-and-language trajectory segmentation method that reasons over video demonstrations, and leverages contextual cues to determine when acceleration is appropriate and when careful precision is required. VOLT identifies segments where slow, deliberate motion is necessary, then selectively downsamples the remaining segments. The resulting reformatted trajectories can be used with standard imitation learning approaches, such as diffusion policies. Our results highlight that segmentation quality is critical -- baseline methods often misidentify when acceleration is possible, leading to overly cautious or unreliable policies. Compared to state-of-the-art alternatives, VOLT allows robots to execute tasks faster while maintaining strong performance.

</details>


### [147] [Waypoints Matter: A Systematic Study for Sampling-Based Trajectory Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06366)
*Josep M. Barbera,Antonio Artuñedo,Jorge Villagra*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Waypoint placement is a key design variable for sampling-based trajectory planners; inter-waypoint spacing ds dominates performance. Uniform spacing with proper ds often matches or surpasses alternative strategies; curvature-aware methods offer limited gains on complex roads, while RDP* underperforms vs uniform. Treat ds as the primary tuning parameter; geometry-aware strategies should be reserved for curvature-rich corridors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify how waypoint placement affects the reliability and feasibility of sampling-based trajectory planners and to identify effective deployment strategies for waypoints.

Method: Systematic sweep of three placement strategies (uniform spacing, augmented Ramer-Douglas-Peucker variant [RDP*], and a curvature-conditioned allocation) across 449 configurations and five CommonRoad maps, with a fixed trajectory primitive and candidate budget; evaluate planner performance with respect to feasibility and reliability.

Result: Inter-waypoint spacing ds is the primary performance driver; large reliability differences arise from placement alone. Uniform sampling at a well-tuned spacing matches or exceeds both RDP* and the curvature-centered variant. The curvature strategy yields a small, consistent advantage on geometrically complex roads under reliability-first and balanced weightings; RDP* never outperforms uniform spacing.

Conclusion: ds should be treated as the dominant tuning parameter in waypoint design for sampling-based planners. Geometry-aware strategies are beneficial only in curvature-rich corridors where feasibility limits performance; otherwise, uniform spacing suffices and is often optimal.

Abstract: Real-time autonomous driving commonly relies on sampling-based trajectory planners that link candidate trajectories to target waypoints along the road centerline. The placement of these waypoints directly impacts both the existence and quality of feasible trajectories. Yet, its effect on planner performance remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we treat waypoint placement as a first-class design variable. We hold the trajectory primitive and candidate budget fixed, and systematically sweep three placement strategies (uniform spacing, an augmented Ramer-Douglas-Peucker variant (RDP*), and a novel curvature-conditioned allocation) across 449 configurations and five CommonRoad maps of increasing geometric complexity. Our results show that the nominal inter-waypoint spacing $d_s$ is the primary performance driver, with large differences in planner reliability attributed to placement alone. Uniform sampling at a well-tuned spacing matches or surpasses both RDP* and the centered curvature variant. The curvature variant offers a small but consistent advantage on geometrically complex roads under reliability-first and balanced weightings, while RDP* never outperforms uniform sampling. These findings suggest that $d_s$ should be treated as the dominant tuning parameter, with geometry-aware strategies reserved for curvature-rich corridors where feasibility is the limiting factor.

</details>


### [148] [Ensuring Interaction Safety in Multitask Exoskeleton Control: A Simulation-Trained Variable Impedance Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06370)
*Muyuan Ma,Houcheng Li,Haotian Zhai,Lijun Han,Xinpan Meng,Xiuze Xia,Long Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A simulation-trained, Lyapunov-stabilized variable impedance control framework for multitask exoskeletons that reduces metabolic cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable safe, adaptive control across diverse tasks in wearable exoskeletons while guaranteeing interaction safety.

Method: 1) Generate synthetic human-exoskeleton data via PPO-based simulation of muscle activations with exoskeleton torque compensation. 2) Train a dual-modality policy that fuses semantic instructions with proprioceptive history to predict reference trajectories and variable impedance gains for nine tasks. 3) Apply Lyapunov stability constraints to bound stiffness variations, ensuring asymptotic stability of the coupled system.

Result: Experimental results show a reduction in metabolic cost compared with baseline methods, indicating feasibility of safe multitask exoskeleton control.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates feasible, safe multitask exoskeleton control with formal stability guarantees and practical metabolic benefits.

Abstract: Wearable exoskeletons can augment human phys ical capabilities during complex activities. However, ensuring adaptation across diverse tasks while guaranteeing interaction safety remains a critical challenge. To address this, a simulation trained variable impedance control approach with stability guarantees is proposed. First, a simulation-based human exoskeleton motion data generation pipeline is established, utilizing Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to synthesize human muscle activations while the exoskeleton provides direct compensation for human biological joint torques. Subsequently, the generated dataset is used to train a dual modality policy that fuses semantic instructions with proprioceptive history, enabling the prediction of reference trajectories and variable impedance gains for nine different motion tasks. To guarantee safety, the network outputs are constrained by a stability criterion derived from Lyapunov stability theory, which bounds stiffness variations to ensure the asymptotic stability of the coupled system. Experimental results indicate that the proposed framework reduces metabolic cost in real-world scenarios com pared with standard baseline methods. These findings suggest the feasibility of the proposed framework for safe, multitask exoskeleton control.

</details>


### [149] [RiskFlow: Fast and Faithful Safety-Critical Traffic Scenario Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06423)
*Qi Lan,Yining Tang,Yu Shen,Yi Zhou,Yuhao Wei,Jie Li,Guofa Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes RiskFlow, a closed-loop, multi-agent traffic generator that reframes future trajectory generation as action-space transport, enabling a single-pass generation of accelerations/yaw-rates with output-space guidance to emphasize risky interactions while maintaining realism and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome diffusion-based methods' computational cost and accumulation of errors in long rollouts, which lead to motion artifacts and unrealistic trajectories in safety-critical autonomous driving simulations.

Method: Learn an average velocity field over a finite interval to map Gaussian action sequences to future acceleration and yaw-rate commands in a single forward pass. Train with a JVP-based objective for stability and efficiency. At test time, apply output-space guidance to steer agents toward risky interactions and regularize off-road behavior, followed by trajectory reconstruction via vehicle dynamics.

Result: RiskFlow achieves strong adversariality-realism trade-off across multi-agent and long-horizon scenarios on nuScenes with tbsim closed-loop evaluation, outperforming representative baselines in realism while maintaining safety-critical generation capabilities and substantially reducing inference time.

Conclusion: RiskFlow provides an efficient, controllable alternative to iterative diffusion-based generation for safety-critical multi-agent traffic scenes, combining a fast single-pass generative model with test-time guidance and physics-based trajectory reconstruction to produce realistic yet adversarially challenging scenarios.

Abstract: Safety-critical traffic scenario generation is essential for evaluating autonomous driving systems under rare but high-risk interactions. Existing diffusion-based methods offer strong controllability in closed-loop generation, but their iterative denoising process is computationally expensive and may accumulate sampling and guidance errors over long rollouts, causing unrealistic motion artifacts such as jitter, abnormal acceleration, and off-road behavior. To address these issues, we propose RiskFlow, a closed-loop safety-critical multi-agent traffic generation framework that formulates future trajectory generation as transport in the action space. Instead of relying on iterative denoising, RiskFlow learns an average velocity field over a finite interval to transform Gaussian action sequences into future acceleration and yaw-rate commands with a single forward pass, using a JVP-based objective for efficient and stable training. At test time, RiskFlow applies output-space guidance to the generated actions, steering selected critical agents toward risky interactions while regularizing off-road behavior, and reconstructs physically feasible trajectories through vehicle dynamics. Experiments on nuScenes with tbsim closed-loop evaluation show that RiskFlow achieves a strong adversariality-realism trade-off across multi-agent and long-horizon settings. Compared with representative baselines, RiskFlow consistently improves realism while maintaining competitive safety-critical generation capability, and substantially reduces inference time for evaluation.

</details>


### [150] [Flow-based Policy Adaptation without Policy Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06461)
*Luzhe Sun,Jingtian Ji,Haoran Chen,Jiawei Zhou,Matthew R. Walter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GLOVES offers a flow-based, selective action-level adapter that nudges non-expert actions toward an expert distribution, using a reverse-flow score as an intervention gate and requiring limited demonstrations; aims for robust, reusable shared-control across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on full autonomy by leveraging pretrained policies, foundation models, or human operators while ensuring actions align with task-specific expert behavior and preserving agent intent.

Method: Learn a local normalizing-flow that transports arbitrary actions toward the expert-action distribution. Use reverse-flow evaluation as an in-distribution score to gate interventions: pass through actions that look consistent with the expert, correct anomalous/OOD actions. Requires limited expert supervision (few demonstrations or reusable skill segments) and stitches local expert patterns during execution to enable cross-task robustness.

Result: Claims improved task success and preservation of agent intent, with a natural in-distribution scoring mechanism to trigger interventions. Demonstrates applicability across tasks/environments; code and demos available.

Conclusion: GLOVES provides a lightweight, reusable shared-control module for robust action adaptation, combining selective correction with interpretability via the reverse-flow score and minimal supervision.

Abstract: Leveraging prior knowledge from pretrained policies, foundation models, or human operators offers an efficient alternative to learning robot skills from scratch. However, these agents often provide actions that are suboptimal, noisy, or misaligned with task-specific expert behavior. We propose GLOVES, a family of flow-based adaptation methods that correct non-expert actions by transporting them toward an expert action distribution. Rather than replacing agentic control with full autonomy, GLOVES performs selective action-level adaptation, improving task success while preserving agent intent. The learned flow also provides a natural in-distribution scoring mechanism through reverse flow evaluation. We use this signal as an intervention gate: actions that appear consistent with the expert distribution are passed through unchanged, while anomalous or out-of-distribution (OOD) actions are corrected. In this way, assistance is only provided when necessary. GLOVES requires only limited expert supervision, using a small number of demonstrations or reusable successful skill segments. By learning local expert action patterns and stitching them during execution, GLOVES provides a lightweight shared-control module for robust action adaptation across tasks and environments. Code and demos are available at ripl.github.io/GLOVES_web.

</details>


### [151] [TempoVLA: Learning Speed-Controllable Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06491)
*Dong Jing,Jingchen Nie,Tianqi Zhang,Jiaqi Liu,Huaxiu Yao,Zhiwu Lu,Mingyu Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TempoVLA enables controllable execution speed in Vision-Language-Action models by combining data-side Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation (VSTA) with model-side speed conditioning, yielding fast or slow, semantically-preserving motions and improved data efficiency, validated in simulation, real-world tasks, and with large multimodal models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLAs typically inherit a fixed speed from demonstrations; deceleration and speed adaptation, especially to match low-risk vs. high-risk phases, are underexplored but desirable for safe and efficient manipulation.

Method: Two-part approach: (1) VSTA—a data-side augmentation that retimes demonstrations to any target speed by merging or splitting actions while preserving motion semantics; (2) model-side conditioning that feeds the target speed into the policy, enabling the VLA to execute at the desired pace. Optional collaboration with a large multimodal model to achieve dynamic speed control.

Result: VSTA achieves requested speeds with negligible motion error; TempoVLA supports flexible speed control in both directions (faster and slower) and, when combined with data augmentation, improves default 1x performance; integration with a large multimodal model enables dynamic acceleration during low-risk phases and deceleration during high-risk phases.

Conclusion: TempoVLA provides a principled and effective mechanism for controllable execution speed in VLAs, combining data augmentation and policy conditioning to match task risk profiles and enhance data efficiency, with potential for end-to-end dynamic speed control via LMM collaboration.

Abstract: Robot manipulation alternates between low-risk transit phases that call for fast execution and high-risk contact stages that demand slow, precise motion. Yet existing Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) only inherit a single fixed speed from training demonstrations. Prior efforts to accelerate VLAs through model compression, KV-cache reuse, or reinforcement learning only shift the policy from one fixed speed to another, and leave deceleration almost unexplored. We observe that the magnitude of each predicted action already governs how fast the robot moves, opening a direct route to controllable execution speed. We turn this observation into TempoVLA, a single VLA whose execution speed is controlled by an explicit condition. TempoVLA combines two coupled components. (1) A data-side Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation (VSTA) that re-times demonstration to any target speed by merging or splitting actions while preserving its motion semantics. (2) A model-side conditioning mechanism that feeds the speed to the policy. Statistics show that VSTA reaches the requested speed with negligible motion error. Experiments in simulation and on real-world tasks demonstrate that TempoVLA achieves flexible speed control in both directions, while VSTA additionally boosts the default $1\times$ performance via better data utilization. Furthermore, by cooperating with a large multimodal model, TempoVLA realizes dynamic speed control, accelerating through low-risk phases and decelerating for high-risk ones.

</details>


### [152] [HANDOFF: Humanoid Agentic Task-Space Whole-Body Control via Distilled Complementary Teachers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06493)
*Lizhi Yang,Junheng Li,Nehar Poddar,Yiling Hou,Gio Huh,Robert Griffin,Georgia Gkioxari,Aaron Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HANDOFF is a unified humanoid whole-body controller that uses a compact explicit interface to connect planning and control; it is distilled into a mixture-of-experts from three specialists via multi-teacher KL distillation, enabling robust manipulation and NL-driven task rollouts on a Unitree G1 with a VLM-driven planner.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the need for dense kinematic or spatial references in existing whole-body controllers and provide an intuitive, general, modular interface between task planning and control for real-world humanoid manipulation.

Method: Introduce HANDOFF, a single humanoid whole-body controller distilled via multi-teacher KL distillation under a context-conditioned gating scheme into a mixture-of-experts from three specialists: (1) whole-body motion tracking with safety-filtered data, (2) locomotion, and (3) fall-recovery; trained with safety-filtered data and deployed on the Unitree G1; task execution guided by a VLM-driven agentic planner without task-specific data or controller fine-tuning.

Result: HANDOFF matches state-of-the-art velocity tracking on the Unitree G1 and offers one of the largest robust manipulation workspaces; hardware feasibility demonstrated through multiple natural-language-driven task roll-outs driven by a VLM planner without task-specific data or controller fine-tuning.

Conclusion: A compact, explicit planning-to-control interface with a single, scalable HANDOFF controller enables robust, diverse manipulation and NL-driven task execution for humanoid robots without task-specific controller adaptation, supported by a multi-teacher distillation framework into a mixture-of-experts.

Abstract: For a humanoid robot to be deployed in the real world, the choice of command space (i.e., the interface between task planning and whole-body control) is crucial. Existing whole-body controllers typically demand dense kinematic or spatial references that planners struggle to synthesize from task semantics. We instead propose a compact, explicit interface that is intuitive, general, modular, and expressive enough for diverse manipulation skills. To this end, we introduce HANDOFF, a single humanoid whole-body controller that follows this interface and is distilled via multi-teacher KL distillation under a context-conditioned gating scheme into a mixture-of-experts student from three complementary specialists: whole-body motion tracking with safety-filtered data, locomotion, and fall-recovery. On the Unitree G1, HANDOFF matches state-of-the-art velocity tracking and offers one of the largest robust manipulation workspaces. We further demonstrate hardware feasibility through multiple natural-language-driven task roll-outs, powered by a VLM-driven agentic planner with no task-specific data or controller fine-tuning.

</details>


### [153] [Ask-to-Clarify: Resolving Instruction Ambiguity through Multi-turn Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.15061)
*Xingyao Lin,Xinghao Zhu,Tianyi Lu,Sicheng Xie,Hui Zhang,Xipeng Qiu,Zuxuan Wu,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Ask-to-Clarify introduces a two-stage, interactive embodied agent framework that first resolves ambiguous human instructions via multi-turn dialogue and then executes actions using a diffusion-based policy, with a connection module tying perception/instructions to action conditions; trained with staged knowledge insulation and a runtime router to switch between asking and acting, and evaluated on 8 real-world tasks with favorable results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome one-way instruction execution in vision-language embodied agents by enabling interactive clarification with humans, achieving robust collaboration and adaptive behavior in real-world settings.

Method: A two-component system: a visual-language model (VLM) for collaborative dialogue and a diffusion-based action module. A connection module generates diffusion conditions from the VLM output, adjusting observations by instructions to create reliable conditioning. Training uses two-stage knowledge insulation: first fine-tune the collaboration (dialogue) component on ambiguity-resolving data, then integrate the action component while freezing the collaboration module. Inference uses a signal detector to route between asking questions and acting.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 8 real-world tasks shows the framework outperforms state-of-the-art VLAs, suggesting improved capability for interactive, collaborative embodied agents.

Conclusion: The Ask-to-Clarify framework, with its staged training and routing mechanism, advances embodied AI toward truly collaborative agents that can ask for clarification before acting, improving robustness in ambiguous real-world instructions.

Abstract: The ultimate goal of embodied agents is to create collaborators that can interact with humans, not mere executors that passively follow instructions.
