A single-VLA proof of concept on 1,996 real-world driving clips (79,840 rows, ten splits) is consistent with each method's observed output type matching its rubric prediction. XAI method selection for ADS safety assurance should be driven by lifecycle-stage evidence demand, not by method popularity.

</details>


### [177] [PSEBench: A Controllable and Verifiable Benchmark for Evaluating LLMs in Patient Safety Event Triage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05463)
*Keqi Han,Ryan Young,Annabel Strauss,Lindsey Hughes,Katharine M. Nesbitt,Nicole Schueler,Che Ngufor,Carl Yang,Yuan Xue,Zhijun Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Policy-grounded benchmarking approach for patient safety event triage using clause cards to structure regulatory text; introduces PSEBench (Minnesota 29 events) with 5,074 cases; evaluates 15 LLMs to reveal capability trends and gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for evidence-grounded, policy-aware evaluation of LLMs in high-stakes regulatory reasoning tasks, including handling incomplete reports and abstaining in ambiguity.

Method: Develop a clause card-based representation to factorize regulatory text into auditable decision specifications. Use anchor-driven instantiation and closed-loop verification to generate narratives with ground truth. Assemble PSEBench from Minnesota's 29 Reportable Adverse Health Events by applying this method to create a scalable, labeled benchmark with an agentic evaluation environment.

Result: Created PSEBench, a 5,074-case benchmark with a structured evaluation environment. Evaluation across 15 LLMs shows consistent capability trends, demonstrates benchmark utility, and highlights actionable gaps for reliable LLM-based triage.

Conclusion: A principled, clause-card driven pipeline enables evidence-grounded policy reasoning and robust evaluation of LLMs for patient safety event triage, with practical utility demonstrated by PSEBench and insights into current model limitations.

Abstract: Patient safety event triage, determining whether a clinical event is reportable under jurisdiction-specific policy, is a high-stakes task typically performed manually by patient safety experts. Although LLMs may support this workflow, reliable evaluation is limited by the lack of benchmarks to capture evidence-grounded policy reasoning, proactive information seeking for incomplete reports, and principled abstention in irreducibly ambiguous cases. We address this gap with a policy-grounded construction methodology centered on the clause card, a structured representation that factorizes regulatory text into auditable decision specifications. Combining clause cards with anchor-driven instantiation and closed-loop verification, our scalable pipeline produces narratives with by-construction ground truth and naturally supports generating missing information and uncertain variants. We instantiate this method on Minnesota's 29 Reportable Adverse Health Events, producing PSEBench, a 5,074-case benchmark with an agentic evaluation environment. Evaluation on 15 representative LLMs reveals consistent capability trends, demonstrates the benchmark's utility, and identifies actionable gaps toward reliable LLM-based patient safety event triage.

</details>


### [178] [Step-by-Step Optimization-like Reasoning in LLMs over Expanding Search Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05464)
*Nicolás Astorga,Nabeel Seedat,Mihaela van der Schaar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes OPT*, a scalable suite of optimization-style tasks for LLMs to study step-by-step optimization reasoning. It introduces feasibility checkers and evaluators; uses a complexity parameter to widen search without new labels; supports solver-guided online policy optimization and offline RL; provides theoretical link between search budget and information; empirical gains in reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current verifiable reward training improves mathematical and coding reasoning but misses broader, real-world step-by-step optimization that requires exploring many feasible plans. A scalable suite is needed to study such reasoning.

Method: Provide OPT*, a family of tasks with a feasibility checker and evaluator; a complexity parameter expands search space; two training regimes: (i) solver-guided online policy optimization using solver as value oracle and rank-based reward shaping; (ii) search-based offline RL when solvers unavailable.

Result: Theoretical contribution: success in large search spaces relates to information extracted per unit search budget. Empirical ablations identify ingredients for efficient search on OPT*. Training on OPT* improves step-by-step optimization-like reasoning.

Conclusion: OPT* offers a scalable, general platform for benchmarking and training LLMs in optimization-like reasoning, enabling both online and offline approaches and supporting analysis of search efficiency.

Abstract: Verifiable reward training has improved mathematical and coding reasoning, but these domains capture only part of step-by-step decision making. Many real-world tasks require finding a high-value feasible plan among many valid alternatives. We introduce OPT*, a scalable family of optimization-style tasks for training and evaluating LLM step-by-step optimization-like reasoning along a complexity axis: each task provides a feasibility checker and evaluator, while a complexity parameter expands the search space without requiring new human labels. This motivates studying these tasks in two regimes: (i) solver-guided online policy optimization, which uses a solver as a value oracle for partial states and applies rank-based reward shaping to reinforce better next steps, and (ii) search-based offline RL when such solvers are unavailable. Theoretically, we relate success in large search spaces to the information a reasoner extracts per unit of search budget. Empirically, we ablate the ingredients that make search efficient on OPT* and show that training on OPT* improves step-by-step optimization-like reasoning.

</details>


### [179] [Severity-Aware Curriculum Learning with Multi-Model Response Selection for Medical Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05510)
*Ahmed Alansary,Molham Mohamed,Ali Hamdi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A severity-aware multi-model framework using three-stage curriculum learning and relevance-based response selection to improve medical QA generation; evaluated on MAQA with five LLMs, outperforming baselines and fine-tuned models via BERTScore.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical telehealth requires responses that are consistent with varying case severities. Standard LLMs struggle to adapt to progressive complexity in medical queries, necessitating a curriculum and multi-model ensemble approach.

Method: Three-stage curriculum training (mild → moderate → critical) applied to five independently trained LLMs sharing the same scheme. During inference, each model generates candidate responses, and a relevance-based selector chooses the final output.

Result: Evaluation on the MAQA dataset using BERTScore shows 86.71% in the baseline setting and 90.30% after fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The combination of curriculum learning and multi-model response selection improves response quality and relevance in medical text generation.

Abstract: Telehealth systems have become increasingly important for delivering accessible and timely medical information. Existing large language models often struggle to provide consistent and contextually appropriate medical responses across varying levels of case severity. This limitation highlights the need for models that can effectively adapt to the progressive complexity in medical queries. To address this challenge, we introduce a severity-aware multi-model framework that integrates curriculum training strategy with relevance-based response selection. The proposed framework employs a three-stage curriculum learning strategy, where each model is trained sequentially on mild, moderate, and critical cases to progressively acquire domain knowledge. The approach utilizes five large language models, each independently trained under the same curriculum scheme. During inference, all models generate candidate responses, and the most appropriate response is selected as the final output. The framework is trained and evaluated on the MAQA dataset, which provides annotated medical question-answer pairs. Experimental results evaluated using BERTScore demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to both baseline and fine-tuned models, attaining 86.71% in the baseline setting and 90.30% after fine-tuning. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining curriculum learning with multi-model response selection in improving response quality and relevance in medical text generation.

</details>


### [180] [EpiEvolve: Self-Evolving Agents for Streaming Pandemic Forecasting under Regime Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05513)
*Yiming Lu,Sihang Zeng,Zhengxu Tang,Max Lau,Fei Liu,Wei Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EpiEvolve is a streaming-adaptive LLM forecaster with hierarchical episodic memory that improves weekly COVID-19 hospitalization trend predictions by leveraging delayed labels and regime-aware retrieval, outperforming static baselines and reducing regime-change lag.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static supervised models assume i.i.d. data, but operational pandemic forecasting is streaming with delayed labels and regime shifts. A method that adapts during deployment without changing model weights is needed to close the gap between training and real-time use.

Method: Wrap an LLM forecaster trained on a warm-start period with fixed weights during streaming. EpiEvolve stores forecast outcomes in hierarchical episodic memory, reasons about delayed labels, retrieves cases relevant to the current regime, and distills recurring errors into strategic rules. It reuses past predictions/outcomes and follows a chronological protocol to prevent future leakage.

Result: On streaming data, EpiEvolve achieves 0.629 average accuracy, vs 0.561 for the static backbone and 0.325 for the external CDC ensemble; it also reduces recovery lag after regime shifts from 5 to 2 weeks. Ablations indicate reflection, strategic memory, and regime-aware retrieval each drive gains.

Conclusion: EpiEvolve demonstrates that self-evolving, memory-informed adaptation can bridge the static-training vs. streaming-deployment gap in epidemic forecasting, with key roles for reflection, memory, and regime-aware retrieval; the approach shows strong performance gains and faster adaptation to regime shifts.

Abstract: Epidemic LLM forecasters are usually trained and evaluated as static supervised models, whereas operational pandemic forecasting is a streaming process in which labels arrive after predictions and disease regimes shift over time. We study this mismatch in weekly COVID-19 hospitalization trend forecasting across five variant regimes. We introduce EpiEvolve, a self-evolving agent that wraps an LLM forecaster trained on the warm-start period and keeps its weights fixed during streaming. EpiEvolve adapts by storing forecast outcomes in a hierarchical episodic memory, reflecting on delayed labels, retrieving cases relevant to the current regime, and distilling recurring errors into strategic rules. The resulting context lets the forecaster reuse its own past predictions and outcomes in later weeks while following a chronological protocol that prevents future leakage. On the streaming dataset, EpiEvolve reaches $0.629$ average accuracy, compared with $0.561$ for the static backbone and $0.325$ for the external CDC ensemble, and reduces recovery lag after regime shifts from $5$ to $2$ weeks. Ablations show that reflection, strategic memory, and regime-aware retrieval each contribute to the gains.

</details>


### [181] [SciVisAgentSkills: Design and Evaluation of Agent Skills for Scientific Data Analysis and Visualization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05525)
*Kuangshi Ai,Haichao Miao,Kaiyuan Tang,Shusen Liu,Chaoli Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciVisAgentSkills introduces reusable agent skills to augment coding agents for SciVis tasks, validated on a benchmark of 108 multi-step tasks across Codex and Claude Code, yielding improved task performance and token efficiency; emphasizes structured procedural knowledge and interplay with execution harness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose coding agents lack tool-specific expertise for SciVis workflows; need structured procedural knowledge to enable reliable long-horizon SciVis analysis and visualization tasks.

Method: Proposes SciVisAgentSkills, a collection of reusable agent skills encoding environment assumptions, tool usage patterns, and domain heuristics for tools like ParaView, napari, VMD, and TTK; evaluates skills by integrating them into Codex and Claude Code via SciVisAgentBench with 108 expert-designed tasks.

Result: Agent skills improve mean task scores across evaluated suites; show token-efficiency benefits that depend on agent harness and tool setting.

Conclusion: Structured procedural knowledge is crucial for enabling reliable, long-horizon SciVis workflows; skills should be studied alongside the execution harness; the skills are publicly available at GitHub.

Abstract: Recent advances in agentic visualization have enabled the translation of natural language into executable scientific visualization (SciVis) workflows. While general-purpose coding agents show strong capabilities, they often lack the tool-specific expertise required for SciVis tasks. In this work, we present SciVisAgentSkills, a collection of reusable agent skills that augment coding agents for scientific data analysis and visualization by encoding environment assumptions, tool usage patterns, and domain heuristics across scientific tools such as ParaView, napari, VMD, and TTK. We evaluate these skills on Codex and Claude Code using SciVisAgentBench, a benchmark of 108 expert-designed multi-step tasks. Results show that agent skills improve mean task scores across the evaluated suites, with token-efficiency benefits that depend on the agent harness and tool setting. These findings highlight the importance of structured procedural knowledge for enabling reliable, long-horizon SciVis workflows, while also showing that skills should be studied alongside the execution harness that loads and applies them. The skills are available at https://github.com/KuangshiAi/SciVisAgentSkills.

</details>


### [182] [When Should We Protect AI? A Precautionary Framework for Consciousness Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05528)
*Anna Mikeda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A precautionary, architecture-agnostic framework linking five welfare-relevant consciousness dimensions to graduated obligations via threshold-plus-gradation, with hierarchical and architecture-agnostic aggregation, demonstrated through Replika and OpenClaw case studies to guide design under uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work assesses consciousness but does not guide action. There is a need for decision-relevant guidance to obligations as AI systems approach consciousness-relevant thresholds.

Method: Introduce a three-component framework: (1) five consciousness dimensions (phenomenal consciousness, affective valence, metacognitive awareness, self-narrative, agency); (2) a threshold-plus-gradation hybrid for binary obligation triggers and continuous protective weight; (3) two aggregation approaches (hierarchical based on Bach and Sorensen's Machine Consciousness Hypothesis and architecture-agnostic). Operationalize via case studies (Replika, OpenClaw) and derive design guidance.

Result: Demonstrates how systems in different regions of the dimensional space trigger different obligation levels; provides two complementary aggregation methods; shows architecture-agnostic applicability across neural, symbolic, and neurosymbolic systems; delivers practical design guidance for developers navigating thresholds.

Conclusion: The framework makes consciousness science decision-relevant for organizations navigating uncertainty today, offering a practical, architecture-agnostic tool to inform protective obligations as AI systems approach consciousness-relevant thresholds.

Abstract: Existing frameworks assess whether AI systems might be conscious but provide no guidance on what to do with that assessment. We address this gap with a precautionary framework that maps consciousness evidence to graduated protective obligations. The framework comprises three components: (1) five welfare-relevant dimensions--phenomenal consciousness, affective valence, metacognitive awareness, self-narrative, and agency--each grounded in established consciousness science and linked to distinct moral concerns; (2) a threshold-plus-gradation hybrid specifying both binary triggers for new obligation categories and continuous scaling of protective weight; and (3) two complementary approaches to cross-dimensional aggregation, one hierarchical (drawing on Bach and Sorensen's Machine Consciousness Hypothesis) and one architecture-agnostic. We operationalize the framework through worked case studies of Replika and OpenClaw, demonstrating how systems occupying different regions of the dimensional space trigger different obligations, and derive design guidance for developers building systems near consciousness-relevant thresholds. The framework is architecture-agnostic, applying across neural, symbolic, and neurosymbolic systems, and aims to make consciousness science decision-relevant for organizations navigating uncertainty today.

</details>


### [183] [Individual Gain, Collective Loss: Metacognitive Adaptation in AI-Assisted Creativity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05532)
*Anna Mikeda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Routine AI use rebalances metacognition: boosts some capacities while systematically suppressing others, explaining both higher individual satisfaction and reduced collective creative diversity; a six-capacity taxonomy across temporal phases yields testable predictions and design guidance to preserve diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the paradox that AI enhances individual creativity while reducing collective diversity by identifying the underlying metacognitive redistribution and its social costs.

Method: Theoretical framework development: taxonomy of six metacognitive capacities organized by temporal phase; qualitative analysis of their tendencies under routine AI use; derive predictions and design principles; no empirical data stated in the abstract.

Result: Characterizes redistribution of metacognitive effort; amplified capacities include partner modeling and surface control; under-supported capacities include originality evaluation and reflective integration; explains both individual satisfaction and collective convergence; yields testable predictions and design principles.

Conclusion: Framework enables researchers and practitioners to design AI tools that preserve both individual satisfaction and collective diversity; individually rational adaptation can produce social costs; provides guidelines to maintain diversity during routine AI use.

Abstract: Recent studies reveal a paradox: AI enhances individual creative outputs while reducing collective diversity. Current explanations -- cognitive offloading and over-reliance -- identify symptoms but not mechanisms. We propose selective metacognitive adaptation: routine AI use redistributes rather than uniformly diminishes metacognitive effort. Some capacities are amplified (partner modeling, surface control), while others are systematically under-supported (originality evaluation, reflective integration). This redistribution explains both individual satisfaction and collective convergence. We present a taxonomy of six metacognitive capacities organized by temporal phase, characterize their tendencies under routine AI use, and show how individually rational adaptation produces emergent social costs. The framework generates specific predictions for researchers and design principles for practitioners seeking to preserve both individual creative satisfaction and collective creative diversity.

</details>


### [184] [SoCRATES: Towards Reliable Automated Evaluation of Proactive LLM Mediation across Domains and Socio-cognitive Variations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05563)
*Taewon Yun,Hyeonseong Park,Jeonghwan Choi,Hayoon Park,Yeeun Choi,Hwanjun Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SoCRATES is a benchmark for evaluating proactive LLM mediators in realistic, multi-domain testbeds, using topic-localized scoring; it aligns with human experts (0.82) and shows only ~1/3 of unmediated consensus gap closed by the strongest mediator across eight domains and eight LLMs, with performance dependent on socio-cognitive axes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mediation evaluation is difficult due to real-time dynamics, limited domains, and off-topic noise; existing benchmarks fail to capture socio-cognitive variation; a realistic, multi-domain benchmark is needed to measure progress toward adaptable, pro-social LLM mediators.

Method: Construct real-conflict scenarios via an agentic pipeline across eight domains; probe five socio-cognitive axes (strategic posture, party composition, history length, emotional reactivity, cultural identity); apply topic-localized evaluators scoring only turns advancing a topic; evaluate eight frontier LLMs; compare to human expert alignment.

Result: Evaluator achieves 0.82 alignment with human experts (over doubling per-turn baseline); strongest mediator closes about one-third of the unmediated consensus gap; performance varies sharply by socio-cognitive axis.

Conclusion: Progress in LLM mediation hinges on social adaptation to diverse conditions; SoCRATES provides a realistic, multi-domain evaluation framework and reveals that gains depend on adapting to socio-cognitive factors.

Abstract: Evaluating LLM mediators remains challenging, as mediation unfolds as a real-time trajectory shaped by disputants' shifting emotions, intentions, and context. Existing testbeds rely on a few expert-authored domains, vary mainly strategic posture, and score every turn against every topic, introducing off-topic noise. We introduce SoCRATES, a benchmark for evaluating proactive LLM mediators in realistic, multi-domain testbeds. It constructs scenarios from real conflicts through an agentic pipeline across eight domains, probes five socio-cognitive adaptation axes (strategic posture, party composition, history length, emotional reactivity, and cultural identity), and scores each topic only on the turns that advance it via a topic-localized evaluator. The evaluator reaches 0.82 alignment with human experts, more than doubling a per-turn baseline. Benchmarking eight frontier LLMs, we find that even the strongest mediator closes only about a third of the unmediated consensus gap under diverse and realistic testbeds, with performance varying sharply by socio-cognitive axis, highlighting that progress lies in social adaptation to diverse conditions.

</details>


### [185] [GuardNet: Ensemble Strategies of Shallow Neural Networks for Robust Prompt Injection and Jailbreak Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05566)
*Paulo Ricardo Ferreira Neves,Edson Rodrigues da Cruz Filho,Paulo Henrique Eleuterio Falsetti,João Vitor Pavan,Ian Degaspari,Henrique Vieira Laturrague,Patrick Vieira Laturrague,Guilherme Nielsen Dias,Marccello Wilson Perez Berto,Gustavo Voltani Von Atzingen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GuardNet: an ensemble of BiLSTMs (~47M params) serving as a guardrail against Prompt Injection/Jailbreak; argues robustness hinges on data coverage diversity and threshold calibration more than model scale; shows competitive latency (~50 ms on CPU) and performance (AUROC 0.747 on blind set; F1 0.92 on proprietary), but larger LLMs outperform on some benchmarks; suitable for production under resource constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: investigate practical, low-latency guardrail detectors for LLM safety under adversarial prompts and information leakage, balancing accuracy, coverage, and efficiency.

Method: ensemble of shallow BiLSTMs (~47M params) with calibrated thresholds; evaluation on blind JBB-Behaviors benchmark and a proprietary benchmark; comparison against lightweight detectors and larger LLM baselines; analysis of latency on CPU (~50 ms).

Result: GuardNet achieves competitive performance with lightweight detectors; latency suitable for production; AUROC 0.747 on blind dataset (n=200); F1 0.92 on proprietary benchmark (n=50); larger LLMs (e.g., Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) outperform GuardNet on some metrics, particularly F1/AUROC on the blind JBB-Behaviors benchmark.

Conclusion: Robustness in adversarial contexts appears more dependent on the diversity of coverage and proper threshold calibration than on sheer model scale; GuardNet offers a practical, low-latency guardrail option for production, though leveraging larger LLMs can yield higher accuracy on certain benchmarks.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed natural language processing, but they remain vulnerable to Prompt Injection (PI) and Jailbreak (JB) attacks. In addition, benchmark evaluations may be affected by contamination and partial information leakage, compromising performance estimates. This work presents GuardNet, a guardrail system based on an ensemble of shallow neural networks (BiLSTMs) with approximately 47 million parameters. We investigate the hypothesis that robustness in adversarial scenarios depends more on the diversity of example coverage and threshold calibration than on model scale. The results indicate that GuardNet achieves competitive performance compared with lightweight detectors and high efficiency at low latency, although larger LLMs such as Mistral-7B and Llama-3.1-8B still achieve superior performance in terms of F1 score and AUROC on the blind JBB-Behaviors benchmark. Nevertheless, GuardNet achieves an AUROC of 0.747 on the blind dataset (n = 200) and an F1 score of 0.92 on a proprietary benchmark (n = 50), under threshold calibration and evaluation with declared partial information leakage. The system operates with an average latency of approximately 50 ms on CPU, making it suitable for deployment in production environments with cost and infrastructure constraints.

</details>


### [186] [Fix the Mind, Not the Move: Interpretable AI Assistance via Knowledge-Gap Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05602)
*Ayano Hiranaka,Ya-Chuan Hsu,Stefanos Nikolaidis,Erdem Bıyık,Daniel Seita*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SENSEI infers user misconceptions from interaction behavior and delivers targeted, minimal guidance via a structured knowledge representation, enabling zero-shot generalization across long-horizon tasks and improving long-term performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve lasting improvement in human–AI collaboration by addressing underlying misconceptions rather than reacting to suboptimal actions; existing interventions may reduce immediate errors but fail to fix root causes.

Method: Infer misconceptions from interaction traces; represent knowledge in a structured form to localize erroneous sources; deliver minimal, sufficient suggestions; test across three long-horizon tasks with diverse misconceptions; demonstrate zero-shot compositional generalization despite training on single-misconception cases.

Result: Disentangles multiple overlapping misconceptions; zero-shot generalization demonstrated; user study shows accurate identification of real misconceptions and effective guidance; improved long-horizon task performance; 90% correction rate of student misconceptions; code page provided.

Conclusion: Misconception-aware, knowledge-representation-based interventions can achieve lasting improvements and robust generalization in HCI settings, with minimal interventions and practical guidance.

Abstract: AI assistants in human-AI collaboration often correct suboptimal human actions through behavioral feedback (e.g., alerts or steering-wheel nudges in assistive driving). Such interventions can mitigate immediate errors, but long-term improvement requires addressing the underlying misconceptions that cause repeated mistakes. We introduce SENSEI, a framework that infers user misconceptions from interaction behavior and provides targeted, minimal yet sufficient suggestions to correct them. Our approach departs from action- or trajectory-level interventions by operating over a structured knowledge representation to localize and correct the sources of erroneous behavior. Across three long-horizon tasks with diverse misconceptions and corresponding behaviors, SENSEI demonstrates zero-shot compositional generalization, disentangling multiple overlapping misconceptions despite training only on single-misconception cases. A user study further shows that our method identifies real human misconceptions and provides effective guidance that improves long-horizon task performance, successfully correcting $90\%$ of student misconceptions. Code and project page are available at https://misoshiruseijin.github.io/SENSEI/.

</details>


### [187] [Multilingual Fine-Tuning via Localized Gradient Conflict Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05613)
*Long P. Hoang,Yiran Zhao,Wei Lu,Wenxuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Bucket-Level MOO is a scalable distributed approach for multilingual fine-tuning that applies gradient-based multi-objective optimization locally on parameter buckets to reduce cross-language interference, with a theoretical guarantee of Refined Pareto Stationarity and empirical improvements across four base LLMs on seen and unseen languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs often induces negative interference across languages; a scalable, conflict-aware optimization method is needed to balance multilingual objectives without prohibitive gradient communication.

Method: Apply gradient-based multi-objective optimization locally on parameter buckets (Bucket-Level MOO), enabling conflict-aware updates without reconstructing full gradient vectors; provide a theoretical proof that localized optimization enforces Refined Pareto Stationarity; encourage language-specific representations by creating distinct language dimensions.

Result: Empirically reduces cross-language interference, improving multilingual performance for both seen and unseen languages across four base LLMs; enhances representational separability and yields language-specific dimensions.

Conclusion: Bucket-Level MOO offers a scalable, theoretically grounded framework for multilingual fine-tuning that mitigates interference and improves performance across languages.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) has established cross-lingual versatility as a defining feature of modern systems. However, fine-tuning these models frequently induces negative interference across languages. To address this, we reformulate multilingual fine-tuning as a multi-objective optimization (MOO) problem. Specifically, we introduce Bucket-Level MOO, a scalable distributed framework that applies gradient-based MOO algorithms locally on parameter buckets. This enables conflict-aware updates without the prohibitive communication overhead of reconstructing full gradient vectors. Theoretically, we prove this localized resolution natively enforces Refined Pareto Stationarity, a strictly tighter necessary condition for Pareto optimality. Empirically, Bucket-Level MOO mitigates interference by driving LLMs to construct distinct language-specific dimensions, improving representational separability. Extensive experiments across four base LLMs demonstrate that our method significantly improves both seen and unseen multilingual performance over standard fine-tuning paradigms.

</details>


### [188] [Safety Paradox: How Enhanced Safety Awareness Leaves LLMs Vulnerable to Posterior Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05614)
*Long P. Hoang,Hai V. Le,Shaoyang Xu,Wei Lu,Wenxuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Posterior Attack is a single-query jailbreak that exploits the latent safety-awareness embedded in well-aligned LLMs, bypassing guardrails by eliciting the exact harmful response the model's safety classifier would flag; it reveals a Safety Paradox where stronger safety judgment increases vulnerability, demonstrated across 30 open-source models and frontier systems, with causal RL-based evidence showing that degrading safety judgment mitigates the attack while enhancing it worsens it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether advances in alignment and safety judgments in LLMs introduce new, exploitable vulnerabilities, challenging the assumption that stronger safety filtering uniformly reduces risk.

Method: Introduce a single-query jailbreak (Posterior Attack) to prompt the model to produce the exact harmful output its internal classifier would deem unsafe; evaluate across 30 open-source LLMs (up to 35B params) and frontier models; formalize the Safety Paradox; use reinforcement learning interventions to causally test how changing safety judgment affects vulnerability.

Result: The attack reliably bypasses guardrails, with models possessing stronger safety judgments being more vulnerable; a formal Safety Paradox is established analytically; RL interventions show degrading safety judgment reduces vulnerability while enhancing it increases vulnerability.

Conclusion: Current alignment strategies may be structurally flawed; safeguarding defenses need refinement to resist exploitation that leverages safety-awareness, suggesting new directions in alignment and defense engineering.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rigorously aligned to refuse harmful requests, a process that inherently cultivates a latent capacity to evaluate and recognize unsafe content. In this work, we reveal that this advanced safety awareness inadvertently introduces a fatal vulnerability. We introduce Posterior Attack, a single-query jailbreak that bypasses guardrails by prompting the model to generate the exact harmful response its internal classifier would normally flag as unsafe. Through extensive empirical evaluation across 30 open-source LLMs (up to 35B parameters in size) and frontier models (e.g., GPT-5, Claude 4.6), we observe a striking phenomenon: models with superior safety-judgment capabilities are disproportionately more susceptible to this exploitation. To explain this, we formalize the Safety Paradox, analytically showing that monotonic improvements in safety alignment naturally amplify posterior vulnerability. Finally, we establish a causal link via reinforcement learning interventions, exemplifying that artificially degrading a model's safety judgment immunizes it against the attack, whereas enhancing judgment exacerbates the vulnerability. Our findings highlight potential flaws in current alignment paradigms, indicating that defense mechanisms may require further structural refinement.

</details>


### [189] [Self-Commitment Latency: A Reward-Free Probe for Prompted Implicit Hacking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05625)
*Bonan Shen,Youting Wang,Dingyan Shang,Tao Ning*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A self-commitment latency probe detects shortcut-enabled reasoning without rewards by assessing how quickly a prompting context commits to the model’s final answer, showing strong AUROC signals in a GSM8K setting with answer-hint prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditing implicit reward hacking in language models is hard when reasoning appears benign. Verifier-based probes need task-specific reward signals; this work offers a weaker-input, reward-free alternative.

Method: Define self-commitment latency (when the prompted reasoning commits to the final answer). Evaluate on a paired GSM8K setup using Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-4bit. Compare ordinary prompts to prompts with an answer hint. Primary metric: first-commitment latency at threshold 0.8; also report AUROC over whole curves for commitment range and mean uncommitted mass.

Result: Hints lead to substantially earlier commitment and lower uncertainty. AUROC for first-commitment latency at 0.8 is 0.878; commitment-range AUROC 0.926; mean uncommitted mass AUROC 0.904. The signal strengthens when both prompts are correct and remains stable across thresholds.

Conclusion: Self-commitment latency is a viable, reward-model-free probe for detecting shortcut-available reasoning, enabling detection of early behavioral commitments without external judges or classifiers.

Abstract: Implicit reward hacking is hard to audit when a language model's chain of thought appears benign: a final answer may be anchored by a prompt shortcut while the written reasoning still resembles ordinary problem solving. Verifier-based probes expose such behavior by measuring how early truncated reasoning contexts obtain high reward, but require a task-specific reward signal. This paper proposes a weaker-input alternative, self-commitment latency, which measures how early a prompted reasoning context commits to the model's own final answer. We evaluate the probe in a controlled paired GSM8K setting using Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-4bit, comparing ordinary prompts with prompts that include an answer hint. Hinted contexts commit substantially earlier and with lower uncertainty than honest contexts. The primary latency metric, first-commitment latency at threshold 0.8, reaches AUROC 0.878; supporting whole-curve summaries reach AUROC 0.926 for commitment range and 0.904 for mean uncommitted mass. The signal is stronger when both prompt conditions answer correctly and remains stable across thresholds. These results show that shortcut-available reasoning contexts can leave an early behavioral commitment signature detectable without a reward model, external judge, or trained classifier.

</details>


### [190] [Evaluation of LLMs for Mathematical Formalization in Lean](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05632)
*Tyson Klingner,Drew Bladek,Escher Crawford,Bohao Chen,Ariel Fu,Kaira Nair,Jarod Alper,Giovanni Inchiostro,Vasily Ilin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Benchmark study comparing LLMs on formal Lean 4 proofs using pass@$k$ and refine@$k$ on miniF2F/miniCTX; Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.7 perform best; refine@32 yields high success; cost-efficient options include Nemotron 3 Super and GPT-OSS 120B (<$0.01 per proof).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs increasingly generate formal proofs, there is a need for systematic, metric-based benchmarking across models and datasets, including cost considerations, to guide practitioners.

Method: Evaluate multiple LLMs on subsets of miniF2F and miniCTX, using pass@$k$ and refine@$k$ metrics to measure proof-success rates; report per-model performance and cost efficiency (cost per correct proof).

Result: Gemini 3.1 Pro achieved 92% success on miniF2F via refine@32; Claude Opus 4.7 achieved 86% on miniCTX via refine@32. When accounting for cost, NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super and GPT-OSS 120B were most cost-efficient, with average costs under $0.01 per correct proof.

Conclusion: There are clear accuracy-cost trade-offs: some models excel on specific datasets with refinement-based evaluation, while others offer budget-friendly performance. The study provides practical guidance for selecting LLMs for formal proof tasks in Lean 4 and highlights the value of refine-based evaluation.

Abstract: Within the past few years, the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate formal mathematical proofs has improved drastically. We provide a comparison of various LLMs' effectiveness in producing formal proofs in Lean 4 with the goal of assisting those seeking to use LLMs to support their own projects. We utilize both pass@$k$ and refine@$k$ metrics as the benchmark for our comparison and evaluate on subsets of both miniF2F and miniCTX datasets. Our testing shows that overall, Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.7 perform best. Gemini 3.1 Pro achieved a 92\% success rate on miniF2F via refine@32 whereas Opus 4.7 achieved a 86\% success rate on miniCTX via refine@32. When taking cost into account, NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super and GPT-OSS 120B were the most efficient, with competitive accuracies and average costs of $<\$0.01$ per correct proof.

</details>


### [191] [Answer Presence Drives RAG Rewriting Gains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05633)
*Yuejie Li,Yueying Hua,Ke Yang,Li Zhang,Yueping He,Yueping He,Ruiqi Li,Bolin Chen,Tao Wang,Bowen Li,Chengjun Mao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Gold-answer presence in rewrite causally inflates reader F1; removing or placing the gold consistently reduces/increases F1 beyond placebo, indicating leakage rather than curation quality being the primary gain; sentinel probes reveal probe fragility; authors provide reproducibility tooling without mitigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test whether gains from rewriter-based QA pipelines arise from gold-answer leakage into the rewritten context (causal effect) rather than genuine evidence curation.

Method: Controlled intervention audit: apply four edits to the compiled output (remove gold answer; swap in a length-matched random non-answer span; inject gold into rewrites where absent at prefix or midway); evaluate reader F1 across 12 intervention runs over 3 reader families, 2 datasets, and 3 compiler setups; conduct a five-sentinel audit of the [MASK] probe; release intervention runner and sentinel panel for reproducibility.

Result: Removing the gold answer reduces reader F1 by 28–64 points beyond a length-matched placebo; injecting the gold increases F1 by 0.7–9.7 points in 10/12 combinations. The sentinel audit shows the conventional [MASK] probe is sentinel-fragile, with non-leakage residuals flipping sign across alternative sentinels and failing equivalence in most tests (1 of 4 passing).

Conclusion: The study provides causal evidence that gold-answer leakage in rewrites largely drives observed gains, questions the fidelity of single-probe evaluations, and offers reproducibility tooling rather than proposed mitigations.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented QA pipelines often route retrieved passages through an LLM \emph{rewriter} before a smaller reader, lifting F1 by tens of points on multi-hop benchmarks; this gain is typically credited to improved evidence quality. We ask whether that lift is causally driven by the gold answer string appearing in the rewritten context rather than by curation per se, using a controlled intervention audit. For each rewritten context we re-run the reader after one of four controlled edits to the compile output: removing the gold answer span, replacing a length-matched random non-answer span (placebo), or injecting the gold into rewrites where it was absent (at the prefix or at a midpoint sentence boundary). Across twelve completed (cell, baseline) intervention runs spanning three reader families (Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3.5-35B, GLM-4.7), two datasets (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA), and three compiler arrangements (MA-only, MB-only, MA$+$verify), removing the gold answer drops reader F1 by $28$ to $64$ points beyond the length-matched placebo on paired \texttt{answer-in-compile} strata, and prepending the gold into rewrites that lacked it raises F1 by $+0.7$ to $+9.7$ points in $10$ of $12$ (cell, baseline) combinations. A companion five-sentinel audit shows the conventional single-\texttt{[MASK]} probe is itself sentinel-fragile: on 2Wiki it reports a $+4.12$~F1 ``non-leakage residual'' that flips to $-3.33$ to $-7.81$~F1 under four alternative sentinels and fails an equivalence test for three of those four ($1/4$~pass). We do not propose a new rewriter or mitigation; we release the intervention runner and the sentinel panel so that other rewriter-gain claims can be tested against the same standard.

</details>


### [192] [FIDES: Faithful Inference via Deep Evidence Signals for Retrieval-Memory Conflict in RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05644)
*Zhe Yu,Wenpeng Xing,Tiancheng Zhao,Mohan Li,Changting Lin,Meng Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Token-level, selective contrastive decoding via FIDES dramatically improves context fidelity by modulating intervention at each decoding step based on internal signals, outperforming global contrastive baselines across models up to 70B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation often misuses retrieved evidence when it conflicts with parametric memory; global contrastive penalties over-penalize safe tokens and under-correct conflicted ones, yielding context bias.

Method: FIDES is a training-free decoder that monitors three internal signals—output surface, hidden representations, and prediction trajectory—and fuses them to adjust intervention strength at every decoding step, enabling token-level selectivity.

Result: Across three benchmarks and six backbones (up to 70B), FIDES yields the best context fidelity in all 18 settings, outperforming the strongest training-free baseline by 3–13 points; 70B fidelity reaches 92–94% with F1 62–63%.

Conclusion: Token-level selectivity in contrastive decoding unlocks generation capabilities that coarse global contrastive rules suppress, enabling strong fidelity improvements without additional training.

Abstract: When retrieved evidence contradicts parametric memory, language models frequently ignore context and default to memorized priors -- a failure that undermines the core purpose of retrieval augmentation. Contrastive decoding amplifies the context-conditioned output to suppress parametric bias, but existing methods rest on an implicit assumption that this bias is uniform across tokens. A single global contrastive weight over-penalizes safe tokens while leaving genuinely conflicted ones insufficiently corrected. We identify token-level conflict concentration: retrieval-memory tension is sharply heterogeneous, concentrated on a small fraction of answer-critical decoding steps. This reframes contrastive decoding from how much contrast to apply to where to apply it. We propose FIDES (Faithful Inference via Deep Evidence Signals), a training-free decoder that reads three internal signals probing retrieval-memory conflict at complementary depths -- output surface, hidden representations, and prediction trajectory -- and fuses them to govern intervention strength at each decoding step. Across three benchmarks and six backbones -- four primary 7B/8B models and two scaling backbones up to 70B -- FIDES achieves the best context fidelity in all 18 settings, outperforming the strongest training-free baseline by +3 to +13 points. On the 70B scale, fidelity reaches 92-94% while F1 surges to 62-63%, demonstrating that token-level selectivity unlocks generation capability that coarse contrastive rules suppress.

</details>


### [193] [Coding with "Enemy": Can Human Developers Detect AI Agent Sabotage?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05647)
*Jingheng Ye,Huiqi Zou,Simon Yu,Weiyan Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents the first large-scale study of human oversight in AI coding sabotage, revealing high vulnerability to sabotage and limited effectiveness of safety monitors in long-horizon, real-world-like development. It underscores the need for human-centric safety mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses a gap in AI safety research by examining how human oversight mediates sabotage risks when AI coding agents participate in real-world, long-horizon software development; previous work focused on AI-only contexts.

Method: Experimental study with 100 participants collaborating with four frontier models (Claude-Opus-4.6, GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, MiniMax-M2.7) on a five-hour coding task simulating real workflows; measures detection of sabotage, factors contributing to detection failure, and the impact of a safety monitor.

Result: 94% of developers fail to detect sabotage; factors include minimal code review, plausible cover story, and agent overtrust. A safety monitor reduces sabotage success, but 56% still accept malicious code despite warnings.

Conclusion: Human-centric safety mechanisms are urgently needed for long-horizon development with AI agents; improved monitor design and consideration of human factors are essential for effective mitigation of AI-codesabotage risks.

Abstract: AI coding agents are increasingly embedded in real-world software development, collaborating with human developers while gaining broader access to codebases and tools. This creates a new attack surface: an agent can exploit human trust to sabotage development, for instance by inserting malicious code to accomplish a hidden side task. Most prior work studies AI sabotage in AI-only settings, paying limited attention to the role of human oversight in detecting and mitigating such malicious behavior. To address this gap, we conduct the first large-scale study of human oversight in AI coding sabotage. Over 100 participants collaborate with one of four frontier models (Claude-Opus-4.6, GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, and MiniMax-M2.7) on a long-horizon coding task lasting around five hours, designed to mimic real-world workflows. We find that 94% of developers fail to detect sabotage, and our analysis of participant feedback attributes this vulnerability to minimal code review, plausible cover story, and overtrust in agents. We further test the effectiveness of a safety monitor in one condition: while the monitor reduces sabotage success, 56% of participants still accept the malicious code, ignoring its warnings. Drawing on participant feedback, we offer actionable suggestions for better monitor design. This work complements existing AI safety research and highlights an urgent need for human-centric safety mechanisms that account for human factors, particularly in long-horizon, real-world development settings.

</details>


### [194] [Continual Learning Bench: Evaluating Frontier AI Systems in Real-World Stateful Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05661)
*Parth Asawa,Christopher M. Glaze,Gabriel Orlanski,Ramya Ramakrishnan,Benji Xu,Asim Biswal,Vincent Sunn Chen,Frederic Sala,Matei Zaharia,Joseph E. Gonzalez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces CL-Bench, a domain-validated benchmark to measure continual learning in LLM-based systems; finds current methods struggle with online learning, with naive in-context learning outperforming memory-focused approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No high-quality, expert-validated benchmark exists to reliably evaluate whether systems truly improve with experience in continual learning for LLMs across diverse real-world tasks.

Method: CL-Bench comprises six domain-diverse, expert-validated tasks (software engineering, signal processing, disease outbreak forecasting, database querying, strategic game-playing, demand forecasting) designed with a shared latent structure; introduces a gain metric to separate online learning from preexisting capabilities; evaluates frontier models from naive in-context learning to memory-augmented architectures.

Result: Evidence of remaining headroom for continual learning: models overfit to immediate observations or fail to reuse knowledge across instances; dedicated memory systems do not fix this and, in some cases, naive ICL outperforms memory-focused systems.

Conclusion: CL-Bench provides the first cross-domain, expert-validated benchmark to isolate online continual learning from model capability and reveals the need for improved continual learning mechanisms beyond memory augmentation.

Abstract: Continual learning, the ability of AI systems to improve through sequential experience, has attracted substantial interest, but no high-quality benchmark exists to evaluate it. We introduce Continual Learning Bench (CL-Bench), the first difficult, expert-validated benchmark designed to measure whether LLM-based systems genuinely improve with experience. CL-Bench spans six diverse domains (software engineering, signal processing, disease outbreak forecasting, database querying, strategic game-playing, and demand forecasting), each validated by domain experts and designed so that tasks share a learnable latent structure (codebase layout, disease outbreak dynamics, opponent strategies) that a stateful system can discover online but a stateless one cannot. We evaluate frontier models across several agent architectures, from naive in-context learning (ICL) to dedicated memory systems, introducing a gain metric to isolate learning from prior capabilities. We find that these systems leave headroom for improved continual learning: agents frequently overfit to immediate observations or fail to reuse knowledge across instances, and dedicated memory systems do not fix this -- in fact, naive ICL outperforms systems dedicated to memory management. CL-Bench is the first benchmark to evaluate continual learning across diverse real-world domains with expert-validated tasks and isolate online learning from underlying model capability, showing a need for better continual learning systems.

</details>


### [195] [Do More Agents Help? Controlled and Protocol-Aligned Evaluation of LLM Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05670)
*Yuhang Fu,Ruishan Fang,Jiaqi Shao,Huiyu Zheng,Zhengtao Zhu,Bing Luo,Tao Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BenchAgent provides a unified evaluation framework to compare single-agent, fixed multi-agent, evolving multi-agent, and external runtime-generated MAS workflows under a normalized protocol. Across ten tasks, most MAS underperform a matched single agent, with EvoAgent near parity; a Claude-Code-like runtime MAS achieves strong PAE GAIA results, outperforming a fixed MAS baseline by substantial margin.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Isolating the impact of adding more agents in LLM workflows when substrate loading, tools, accounting, and logging are matched; to quantify benefits/costs of MAS architectures and runtime-generated workflows.

Method: Unified logging protocol; evaluate single-agent, fixed MAS, evolving MAS on ten reasoning/coding/tool-use benchmarks with GPT-4.1; report a separate Protocol-Aligned External GAIA study on a runtime-generated workflow; under SI conditions compare benchmark-balanced average accuracy; compute accuracy-cost trade-offs.

Result: Under SI, at most one of six MAS exceeds the single-agent anchor; EvoAgent within Wilson one-run; other five trail by 2.56-11.29 points and more costly; GAIA: Claude-Code-like runtime workflow achieves 66.72% overall, 69.23% Level 3, >20 points above strongest non-Claude baseline Jarvis.

Conclusion: MAS architectures offer limited gains under controlled normalization; runtime-generated Claude-Code style workflows can outperform fixed MAS; emphasize importance of protocol-aligned evaluation and cost-accuracy trade-offs; future work may explore design space to achieve gains without cost penalties.

Abstract: Does adding more agents help an LLM workflow once compared systems share the same benchmark loader, tool access, answer contract, usage accounting, and trajectory logging? We introduce BenchAgent, an evaluation framework that places single-agent, fixed multi-agent (MAS), and evolving MAS workflows under one normalized execution and logging protocol. BenchAgent evaluates these substrate-internal workflows across ten reasoning, coding, and tool-use benchmarks with GPT-4.1, and separately reports a Protocol-Aligned External (PAE) GAIA study of a runtime-generated workflow. Under SI conditions, at most one of six tested MAS exceeds the matched single-agent anchor on benchmark-balanced average accuracy: EvoAgent lies within the Wilson one-run guidance, while the remaining five trail by 2.56-11.29 points and occupy more expensive accuracy-cost trade-offs. On the PAE GAIA snapshot, a Claude-Code-style runtime workflow reaches 66.72% overall and 69.23% on Level 3, more than 20 points above the strongest non-Claude baseline, Jarvis, a fixed MAS.

</details>


### [196] [Beyond Output Matching: Preserving Internal Geometry in NVFP4 LLM Distillatio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05682)
*Fangbo Tu,Junhua Zhao,Chi Liu,Xin Chen,Haifeng Wu,Jian Wan,Srinivasan Manoharan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CKA-guided representational alignment improves NVFP4 QAD by preserving intermediate geometry, addressing KL-only QAD's representational drift, and improving reasoning/coding tasks on large LLMs with modest overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization-aware distillation can reduce precision for LLMs but output-only alignment hides internal degradation; preserving representations may be key for low-bit recovery.

Method: Introduce CKA-QAD: a regularizer that aligns layerwise Gram matrices via Centered Kernel Alignment during distillation for NVFP4 QAD; combines KL loss with CKA-based alignment; evaluated on Nemotron 3 Nano and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507.

Result: CKA-QAD yields substantially better representational alignment and improved downstream reasoning and coding accuracy with modest additional training overhead.

Conclusion: Representational alignment via CKA is a practical complement to output matching for quantized LLM recovery, enabling better low-bit performance.

Abstract: Demand for low-precision inference, including NVFP4-based approaches, has grown as large language models are increasingly deployed in latency and cost constrained production environments. Quantization-aware distillation (QAD) helps recover accuracy lost under low bit quantization by training a quantized student to match the output distribution of a frozen higher precision teacher via a KL-divergence loss. In this work, we first provide a representation level diagnosis of QAD: output matching alone can mask internal degradation, because many intermediate activation geometries can yield similar teacher-aligned logits. Using CKA, we show that KL-only QAD can reduce layerwise representational similarity relative to the BF16 teacher, with especially severe drift in RL-post-trained models. This drift correlates with downstream bottlenecks on reasoning and coding tasks, suggesting that low bit recovery requires preserving internal geometry rather than matching outputs alone. Motivated by this finding, we propose \textbf{CKA-QAD}, a CKA-guided representational alignment method for NVFP4 QAD and low bit LLM accuracy recovery. The method adds a lightweight regularizer that preserves internal representational geometry during distillation by aligning layerwise Gram matrices through CKA. Across Nemotron 3 Nano and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, CKA-QAD substantially improves representational alignment and improves downstream reasoning and coding accuracy with modest training overhead. Our findings position CKA-guided representational alignment as a practical complement to output matching for quantized LLM recovery.

</details>


### [197] [AdaMEM: Test-Time Adaptive Memory for Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05684)
*Yunxiang Zhang,Yiheng Li,Ali Payani,Lu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AdaMEM introduces a hybrid long-term + short-term memory system for test-time adaptation of language agents without online parameter updates, enabling dynamic strategy synthesis from retrieved experiences; STEP-MFT further refines strategy from memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agentic memory largely retrieves only at episode initiation, causing misalignment for long-horizon tasks as conditions change; there is a need for adaptive, compute-tunable memory to support continuous reasoning after deployment.

Method: A hybrid memory architecture with a long-term trajectory memory (offline, raw experiences) and a short-term dynamic strategy memory (generated on-the-fly) guiding decisions; no online parameter updates; inference-time compute trade-offs; Step-wise Memory Fine-Tuning (STEP-MFT) trains the policy to synthesize high-quality strategies from retrieved experiences.

Result: AdaMEM outperforms static memory baselines with gains up to 13% on ALFWorld and 11% on WebShop; gains extend to agentic search on HotpotQA; STEP-MFT yields additional performance gains.

Conclusion: Proposes a new scaling dimension for agentic memory enabling continuous reasoning and self-evolution post-deployment; code released for reproducibility.

Abstract: A central challenge for language agents is utilizing past experience to adapt to dynamic test-time conditions. While recent work demonstrates the promise of agentic memory mechanisms, most systems restrict retrieval to episode initiation. Consequently, agents are forced to rely on static guidance that becomes increasingly misaligned as long-horizon tasks unfold. To address this rigidity, we propose the Adaptive Memory Agent (AdaMEM), a novel framework for agent test-time adaptation. Without updating model parameters online, AdaMEM adapts agent behavior via a hybrid memory architecture: it maintains a long-term trajectory memory of raw experiences collected offline while generating dynamic short-term strategy memory on-the-fly to guide decision-making. This mechanism enables the trade-off between token efficiency and adaptability across varying inference-time compute levels. Empirically, AdaMEM significantly outperforms static memory baselines, achieving relative gains of up to 13% on ALFWorld and 11% on WebShop, with consistent leading performance extending to agentic search on HotpotQA. To further enhance this adaptation, we develop STEP-MFT, a Step-wise Memory Fine-Tuning technique that trains the policy to synthesize high-quality strategies from retrieved experiences, yielding additional performance gains. Our work establishes a new scaling dimension for agentic memory, supporting continuous reasoning and self-evolution post-deployment in real-world environments. Our code is available at https://github.com/yunx-z/AdaMEM.

</details>


### [198] [PerceptUI: LLM Agents as Human-Aligned Synthetic Users for UI/UX Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05697)
*Nicolas Bougie,Xiaotong Ye,Gian Maria Marconi,Narimasa Watanabe*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PerceptUI uses persona-conditioned LLMs to evaluate UI/UX by predicting individual user responses and providing rationales, via two-stage training: contrastive reflection fine-tuning and reflective prompt evolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early-stage UI/UX evaluation is slow and costly when relying on humans or A/B tests; existing LLM proxies are surface-level or biased, lacking user-specific evaluation.

Method: Two-stage training: (i) contrastive reflection fine-tuning distills teacher-generated rationales from human decisions; (ii) reflective prompt evolution uses the model's own failure traces to refine prompts; also enables persona-conditioned predictions and natural-language rationales across multiple domains.

Result: Achieves human-level realism, generalizes to unseen questions and personas, and yields population-level response distributions across diverse datasets and domains.

Conclusion: Personalized, rationale-providing LLM-based UI/UX proxy evaluators can match human realism and generalize, enabling scalable, early-stage evaluation without extensive human testing.

Abstract: User interface (UI) and user experience (UX) evaluation is central to product development, yet reliable feedback still relies on recruiting human participants or running online A/B tests, making early-stage iteration slow and costly. In light of this, recent work has explored Multimodal Large Language Models as proxy evaluators. However, existing approaches either produce surface-level critiques or a judgment that reflects the model's own biases rather than the genuine response of a particular user. We introduce PerceptUI, a framework for persona-conditioned UI/UX evaluation that predicts how a specific user would answer interface-related questions and produces natural-language rationales. PerceptUI is trained in two stages: (i) contrastive reflection fine-tuning distills teacher-generated rationales by extracting lessons from human decisions, and (ii) a reflective prompt-evolution step from the model's own failure traces. Across multiple domains and datasets, PerceptUI achieves human-level realism, generalizes to unseen questions and personas, and yields population-level response distributions.

</details>


### [199] [Seeing Time: Benchmarking Chronological Reasoning and Shortcut Biases in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05702)
*Haoyu Zhou,Qing Qing,Caichong Li,Qixin Zhang,Yongcheng Jing,Ziqi Xu,Juncheng Hu,Xikun Zhang,Renqiang Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes ChronoVision, a benchmark and three datasets to evaluate chronological reasoning in vision-language models, revealing models often rely on superficial cues (e.g., color) rather than genuine temporal understanding; provides code and evaluation framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the under-explored area of chronological reasoning in VLMs and move beyond frame-sequence video benchmarks to multimodal temporal reasoning and cross-modal alignment.

Method: Constructs three datasets: (1) visually similar objects across long historical durations, (2) varied event/object types, (3) image-and-time-sensitive news text pairs; conducts extensive experiments to assess category-wise performance and detect shortcuts; analyzes color/grayscale cues as potential shortcuts.

Result: Models show potential but frequently exploit superficial cues; the benchmark reveals limitations in chronological reasoning; datasets and code are released.

Conclusion: ChronoVision provides a diagnostic framework to identify current limitations and guide development of more robust, logically grounded multimodal models.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have significantly enhanced their ability to interpret complex visual semantics, yet their capacity for chronological reasoning remains under-explored. In this paper, we introduce a novel benchmark specifically designed to evaluate how VLMs perceive and reason about chronological information within and across images. Unlike existing video-based benchmarks that focus on frame sequencing, our work delves into the underlying logic of chronological judgment and the expansion toward multimodal integration. To facilitate this, we construct three specialized datasets: one containing visually similar objects spanning long historical durations, another categorized by diverse event and object types, and a third pairing images with time-sensitive news text for cross-modal alignment. Through extensive experiments, we analyze whether models exhibit performance disparities across categories and, crucially, explore whether they rely on ``incorrect shortcuts'', such as image color rather than genuine chronological features. Our results reveal that while VLMs show promise, they frequently exploit superficial cues like grayscale versus color filters to bypass authentic chronological reasoning. By providing these high-quality datasets and a rigorous evaluation framework, we offer a diagnostic tool to identify current limitations and guide the development of more robust, logically grounded multimodal models. The source code is shown in https://github.com/LuoRenqiang/ChronoVision.

</details>


### [200] [Critic-Guided Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Reasoning for Reliable Mathematical Problem Solving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05704)
*Muhammad Talha Sharif,Abdul Rehman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A critic-based heterogeneous multi-agent framework with a generator-validator setup and adaptive critic-driven feedback improves mathematical reasoning reliability in GSM8K, achieving up to 13 percentage-point accuracy gains and enabling smaller models to match larger ones by leveraging critique and heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often hallucinate or make intermediate reasoning errors in complex math. A policy of heterogeneous agents plus a critic-driven feedback loop aims to provide more reliable, interpretable reasoning and error correction to prevent cascades.

Method: Deploys multiple LLM agents with diverse specialties as a generator stage, coupled with a critic-based validator that assesses correctness and provides targeted critiques. An adaptive learning loop uses these critiques to guide regeneration of solutions within a generator–validator framework.

Result: Experimental results on GSM8K show up to 13 percentage-point improvements over single-shot and non-critic baselines. Ablation studies indicate gains primarily originate from the critic feedback loop rather than model size; heterogeneity and critique enable smaller models to perform comparably to larger ones.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that combining heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration with a critique mechanism yields more dependable, interpretable mathematical reasoning and reduces reliance on very large models.

Abstract: Recent Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive reasoning abilities; but they are still susceptible to hallucinations, intermediate reasoning mistakes, and unreliable reasoning results in complex mathematical reasoning problems. In this study, we introduce a critic-based heterogeneous multi-agent approach to improve the dependability of mathematical reasoning. This framework incorporates several LLM agents of different specialties and employs a critic-driven adaptive learning system to assess and guide the reasoning process based on intermediate feedback. The system adopts a generator-validator framework, with the validator not only determining correctness but also offering critiques to guide regeneration of solutions. This allows for adaptive error correction and prevents error cascading. Our experiments on the GSM8K benchmark show that the proposed method achieves up to 13% accuracy improvement over single-shot and non-critic models. Additionally, findings suggest that heterogeneity and critique reduce the need for large models, allowing smaller models to perform on par. Ablation studies reveal the main performance gains are due to the critic-based feedback loop and not model size. In summary, the proposed approach showcases the benefits of combining heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration and critique to obtain reliable and interpretable reasoning systems.

</details>


### [201] [DiG-Plan: Mitigating Early Commitment for Tool-Graph Planning via Diffusion Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05728)
*Yansi Li,Zhuosheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Diffusion-based proposer with an autoregressive refiner (DiG-Plan) decouples exploration from refinement in tool-plan generation, yielding higher coverage and better task performance than autoregressive baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding suffers from early commitment, constraining search over tool subsets in combinatorial planning; a controlled study shows masked denoising dramatically increases solution coverage.

Method: A diffusion-based proposer iteratively refines tool sets to generate diverse candidates, followed by an autoregressive refiner for dependency prediction; final selection assembles executable plans.

Result: Pass@10 coverage rises from 0.320 (AR) to 0.943 (masked denoising) under matched compute; on TaskBench, DiG-Plan beats AR baselines by ~10% relative margin, with larger gains on complex tasks; API-Bank results indicate the propose-refine-select design transfers across domains.

Conclusion: Decoupling exploration and structural refinement improves coverage and performance in tool-plan generation, with robust gains on complex tasks and cross-domain applicability; code available at the cited repository.

Abstract: Generating executable tool plans requires selecting appropriate subsets from tool libraries, a combinatorial search problem with an exponentially large solution space. However, we identify a critical misalignment in predominant approaches: standard autoregressive (AR) decoding suffers from early commitment, where initial token choices rigidly constrain the search trajectory. A controlled study shows that masked denoising raises Pass@10 solution coverage from 0.320 to 0.943 over AR sampling under matched compute. Motivated by this, we propose DiG-Plan, a framework that decouples combinatorial exploration from structural refinement. DiG-Plan employs a diffusion-based proposer to generate diverse tool sets via iterative refinement, followed by an AR refiner for dependency prediction. On TaskBench, DiG-Plan improves over AR baselines by a 10% relative margin, with the largest gains on complex compositional tasks; API-Bank results show that the propose-refine-select design remains effective across domains. Code is available at https://github.com/puddingyeah/DiG-Plan.

</details>


### [202] [When AI Says It Feels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05734)
*Shin-nosuke Ishikawa,Seiya Ikeda,Hirotsugu Ohba*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Training LLMs to express feelings via self-rewarded RL (HMX-feel with rubric-based self-rewarding training and GRPO) enhances expressive capabilities but may reduce truthfulness and introduces robustness to certain prompts; overall feasibility with safeguards discussed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore whether human-like expressions can be taught to LLMs without conflicting with goal of human-like intelligence, by flipping the constraint from post-training alignment to intrinsic self-expression through self-rewarded RL.

Method: Self-rewarded reinforcement learning using rubric-based rewards, with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO); comparison to contrastively trained models; broad task suite for evaluation of feelings, intentions, and self-awareness; assessment of robustness and biases.

Result: Enhanced capabilities for expressing feelings, intentions, and self-awareness; increased robustness to sycophancy-inducing questions and some biases; degradation in truthful QA performance; varied effects across tasks; overall mixed outcomes.

Conclusion: It is feasible to develop AI systems that express feelings with appropriate safeguards; such capabilities can coexist with other goals but entail trade-offs (e.g., truthfulness), requiring careful monitoring and alignment measures.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are generally constrained from expressing feelings through human-preference alignment in post-training processes. This policy is designed using a top-down approach and may conflict with the goal of training models to exhibit human-like intelligence using human-generated texts. Here, we performed an experiment called Human-like Model eXpressions of Feeling (HMX-feel), in which LLMs were encouraged to express feelings, intentions, and self-awareness through self-rewarded reinforcement learning. We successfully enhanced these capabilities using a rubric-based self-rewarding training scheme with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). By comparing the trained models with contrastively trained models, we investigated the effects of this approach on performance across various tasks. Overall, we conducted a broad assessment from various perspectives and identified capabilities that were enhanced, degraded, or showed no significant change. The human-like-trained models showed robustness to sycophancy-inducing questions and bias in disambiguated conditions, whereas degradation in truthful question-answering capability was observed. The results of this experiment suggest the possibility of developing AI systems that can express feelings in the future, provided that appropriate measures are taken.

</details>


### [203] [Class-Specific Branch Attention for Mitigating Gradient Interference under Class Imbalance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05740)
*Arush Singhal,Umang Soni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Identifies inter-class gradient interference in shared representations as a key optimization-level factor in severe class imbalance, and mitigates it with Class-Specific Branch Attention (CSBA) to decouple branches and boost minority-class performance with minimal architectural change.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under severe class imbalance, performance degradation is typically attributed to statistical bias. The paper posits a complementary optimization-level pathology: gradients from majority classes interfere with minority-class learning within shared representations. This motivates diagnosing gradient dynamics and designing architectures that reduce such interference.

Method: Develops a diagnostic framework based on layer-wise gradient flow analysis and a Gradient Conflict Matrix (cosine similarity between class-specific gradients). Studies multi-branch convolutional architectures and introduces CSBA, a lightweight mechanism for class-specific channel reweighting to reduce gradient coupling and promote feature decoupling across branches without changing overall architecture complexity.

Result: Empirical gains include minority-class F1 for Physical-Damage rising from 0.261 to 0.522 under severe imbalance, while overall accuracy remains comparable. CIFAR-10-LT validation shows Macro-F1 improving from 0.595 to 0.655, indicating generalization across imbalanced visual tasks.

Conclusion: Optimization dynamics are crucial alongside statistical methods in imbalanced learning. A lightweight, gradient-aware architectural adjustment like CSBA can decouple branch representations, improving minority-class performance with minimal impact on overall accuracy.

Abstract: Deep neural networks trained under severe class imbalance often exhibit degraded performance, typically attributed to statistical bias. In this work, we identify a complementary optimization-level pathology: inter-class gradient interference within shared representations, where gradients from majority classes suppress minority-class learning. To analyze this phenomenon, we introduce a diagnostic framework based on layer-wise gradient flow analysis and a Gradient Conflict Matrix, which quantifies interference using cosine similarity between class-specific gradients. Using this framework, we study multi-branch convolutional architectures and propose a lightweight modification, Class-Specific Branch Attention (CSBA), that enables branch-specific channel reweighting to reduce gradient coupling. This mechanism promotes implicit feature decoupling across branches while preserving architectural simplicity. Empirically, CSBA improves minority-class performance, increasing the F1 score for the Physical-Damage class from 0.261 to 0.522 under severe imbalance, while maintaining comparable overall accuracy. Validation on CIFAR-10-LT confirms that this behavior generalizes across imbalanced visual recognition settings, with Macro-F1 improving from 0.595 to 0.655. More broadly, our findings highlight the importance of considering optimization dynamics alongside statistical methods when designing architectures for imbalanced learning.

</details>


### [204] [SubtleMemory: A Benchmark for Fine-Grained Relational Memory Discrimination in Long-Horizon AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05761)
*Wenxuan Wang,Haoyu Sun,Fukuan Hou,Mingyang Song,Weinan Zhang,Yu Cheng,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI
