Method: Analysis of NHTSA public crash data, California DMV disengagement reports, MIT Moral Machines dataset, and five-jjurisdiction regulatory comparisons.

Result: Perception and classification errors are major failure modes; ethical frameworks differ; regulatory frameworks vary across jurisdictions increasing deployment uncertainty.

Conclusion: Advocate adaptive, cooperative governance integrating engineering standards, ethics discourse, and institutional oversight.

Abstract: Autonomous driving technology has the potential to reduce the large number of road traffic accidents caused by human error each year, but it also brings new types of risks that need to be evaluated from the aspects of technology, ethics and regulations. Based on public crash data from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), disengagement reports from the California Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV), the MIT Moral Machines dataset, and a comparative regulatory analysis of five jurisdictions, we have found that the main types of technical failure modes are perception and classification errors. These account for a relatively large proportion of the reported accidents, and it can be concluded that there are different ethical frameworks for autonomous vehicle decision-making, and inconsistent regulations in different areas increase the uncertainty of widespread application. Generally speaking, the problems of technology, ethics and regulation are closely related and need to be solved together. Therefore, this paper recommends a more adaptive and cooperative governance approach that combines engineering standards, ethical discussion, and institutional supervision.

</details>


### [257] [Unsupervised Skill Discovery for Agentic Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06416)
*Zhisong Qiu,Kangqi Song,Shengwei Tang,Shuofei Qiao,Lei Liang,Huajun Chen,Shumin Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery (DataCOPE) improves data-analytic agents by deriving reusable skills from unlabeled exploration, using a verifier to rank trajectories and a skill manager to distill skills; evaluated on report-style and reasoning-style tasks with strong held-out gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervision for useful data-analysis skills is expensive and success criteria vary across formats; there is a need to discover reusable skills from unlabeled exploration.

Method: DataCOPE coordinates three components: a Data-Analytic Agent for trajectory generation, an Unsupervised Verifier for signal extraction (adaptive checklist verifier for reports; answer agreement verifier for reasoning), and a Skill Manager for contrastive skill distillation. Verifier signals assess trajectory quality/consistency; skills are distilled to improve future trajectories.

Result: Empirical evaluation on two datasets (Deep Data Research for reports, DABStep for reasoning) shows consistent performance gains over baselines. Across four model settings, mean improvements of 9.71% (report-style) and 32.30% (reasoning-style).

Conclusion: Unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery with DataCOPE can reliably improve held-out performance across diverse analytical formats, indicating generalizability of the approach.

Abstract: Inference-time skill augmentation provides a lightweight way to improve data-analytic agents by injecting reusable procedural knowledge without updating model parameters. However, discovering effective skills for data analysis remains challenging, as reliable supervision is expensive and success criteria vary across analytical formats. This raises the key question of how to discover reusable data-analysis skills from unlabeled exploration alone. We propose DataCOPE, an unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery framework for data-analytic agents. DataCOPE derives verifier signals from the exploration trajectories and uses them to characterize relative quality or aggreement among trajectories. It iteratively coordinates a Data-Analytic Agent for trajectory generation, an Unsupervised Verifier for signal extraction, and a Skill Manager for contrastive skill distillation. For report-style analysis, we instantiate the verifier as an Adaptive Checklist Verifier that derives task-specific criteria, scores reports by verifiable coverage, and iteratively refines the checklist. For reasoning-style analysis, we instantiate it as an Answer Agreement Verifier that groups trajectories by answer agreement and uses self-consistency as an auxiliary signal. We evaluate DataCOPE on report-style analysis from Deep Data Research and reasoning-style analysis from DABStep. Across both settings, DataCOPE consistently improves held-out performance over baselines. Averaged across four model settings, DataCOPE improves the mean score by 9.71% and 32.30% on report-style and reasoning-style tasks respectively.

</details>


### [258] [Agent Memory: Characterization and System Implications of Stateful Long-Horizon Workloads](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06448)
*Yasmine Omri,Ziyu Gan,Zachary Broveak,Robin Geens,Zexue He,Alex Pentland,Marian Verhelst,Tsachy Weissman,Thierry Tambe*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: First systems-level study of agent memory; taxonomy, profiling, empirical evaluation of 10 systems on two benchmarks, and 10 actionable recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how memory designs affect system-level cost and performance for long-horizon LLM agents across sessions; current literature lacks unified benchmarks and profiling.

Method: Develop a four-axis taxonomy of agent memory; build a phase-aware cost profiler for construction, retrieval, generation; empirically evaluate ten memory systems across two benchmarks; synthesize design guidance.

Result: Characterization showing how design choices shift costs across write/read paths; quantified costs across components; cross-system comparisons; identification of tradeoffs and cost drivers.

Conclusion: Provides 10 design recommendations for memory construction, scheduling, caching, freshness-latency tradeoffs, and fleet-scale management; framework to guide scalable agent memory design.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed on long-horizon tasks requiring sustained reasoning over extended interaction histories. Realizing this at scale requires agents to persistently store, retrieve, and update their own memory across sessions. A rich ecosystem of agent memory systems has emerged spanning flat retrieval, LLM-mediated extraction, consolidating fact stores, and agentic control flows. Yet, their system-level behavior remains uncharacterized. We present the first systems characterization of agent memory. First, we introduce a system-oriented taxonomy classifying agent memory systems along four axes. Second, we build a phase-aware profiling harness attributing cost to construction, retrieval, and generation. Third, we characterize ten representative systems across two benchmark suites, uncovering how design choices shift cost across the write and read paths. Finally, we derive 10 system recommendations covering construction scheduling, capability floors, amortization via query volume, freshness-latency tradeoffs, and fleet-scale management.

</details>


### [259] [Vortex: Efficient and Programmable Sparse Attention Serving for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06453)
*Zhuoming Chen,Xinrui Zhong,Qilong Feng,Ranajoy Sadhukhan,Yang Zhou,Michael Qizhe Shieh,Zhihao Jia,Beidi Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Vortex is a Python-embedded frontend system with a page-centric tensor backend that enables rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention for LLMs, achieving substantial throughput gains (up to 3.46x over full attention; up to 4.7x on GLM-4.7-Flash; 1.37x on 229B) while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The deployment and evaluation of sparse attention algorithms at scale is highly engineering-intensive, hindering exploration of sparse attention designs. There is a need to translate theoretical efficiency gains into real-world throughput and to enable experimentation with large models.

Method: A Python-embedded frontend language atop a page-centric tensor abstraction to express diverse sparse attention algorithms, paired with an efficient backend tightly integrated into modern LLM serving stacks, enabling rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation.

Result: Vortex enables automated generation and refinement of diverse sparse attention algorithms by AI agents, with the best achieving up to 3.46x throughput gains over full attention while maintaining accuracy. It also extends sparse attention to emerging architectures and very large models, achieving up to 4.7x throughput on GLM-4.7-Flash and 1.37x on 229B-M2.7 on NVIDIA B200 GPUs.

Conclusion: Vortex accelerates the design and iteration of sparse attention algorithms by providing a practical, scalable platform that translates theoretical improvements into real-world throughput gains, and broadens experimentation to larger models and architectures.

Abstract: Sparse attention is becoming increasingly important for serving large language models (LLMs) as generation lengths continue to grow. However, deploying and evaluating new sparse attention algorithms at scale remains highly engineering-intensive, slowing both human researchers and AI agents in exploring the sparse attention design. To address this challenge, we present Vortex, a system that combines a Python-embedded frontend language atop a page-centric tensor abstraction for expressing a broad range of sparse attention algorithms, with an efficient backend tightly integrated into modern LLM serving stacks. Vortex enables rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention algorithms, effectively translating their theoretical efficiency gains into real-world throughput improvements. As a result, Vortex substantially accelerates the design and iteration of sparse attention algorithms. First, AI agents use Vortex to automatically generate and refine diverse algorithms, the best reaching up to $3.46\times$ higher throughput than full attention while preserving accuracy. Second, Vortex extends sparse attention to emerging architectures and very large models that are otherwise hard to experiment with, reaching up to $4.7\times$ higher throughput on the MLA-based GLM-4.7-Flash and $1.37\times$ on the 229B-parameter MiniMax-M2.7 on NVIDIA B200 GPUs.

</details>


### [260] [Benchmark Everything Everywhere All at Once](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06462)
*Shiyun Xiong,Dongming Wu,Peiwen Sun,Yuang Ai,Bokang Yang,Wencheng Han,Xiao-Hui Li,Xiangyu Yue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Benchmark Agent is an autonomous system that fully automates benchmark construction for LLMs/MLLMs, enabling scalable, reusable benchmarks with minimal human input.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks are labor-intensive to build, hard to reuse, and tend to saturate quickly, failing to discriminate advancing models. There is a need for sustainable, scalable, continually evolving benchmarks.

Method: An autonomous agentic framework that handles user query analysis, subtask design, data annotation, and quality control to generate benchmarks. Implemented to produce 15 benchmarks across text, multimodal, and domain-specific reasoning. Evaluation includes human judgments, LLM-as-judge, and consistency checks.

Result: Generated high-quality benchmark samples with minimal human involvement across 15 benchmarks. Reveals domain-specific reasoning gaps in current models and demonstrates sustainability and scalability. Preview and code will be publicly available.

Conclusion: Continual evaluation and rapidly evolving benchmarks can significantly advance the research community by enabling scalable, discriminative evaluation of LLMs/MLLMs; Benchmark Agent offers a practical pathway to such benchmarks.

Abstract: Benchmarks are fundamental for evaluating and advancing LLMs and MLLMs by providing standardized and explicit measures of performance. However, their construction is labor-intensive and hard to reuse, raising concerns about sustainability and scalability. Moreover, existing benchmarks often quickly reach performance saturation after their release, resulting in insufficient discrimination among state-of-the-art models. To address these challenges, we introduce Benchmark Agent, a fully autonomous agentic system designed for benchmark building. Our framework orchestrates the complete benchmark construction pipeline, from user query analysis and subtask design to data annotation and quality control. To assess Benchmark Agent, we implement it to produce 15 representative benchmarks, spanning diverse evaluation scenarios, including text understanding, multimodal understanding, and domain-specific reasoning. Extensive experiments, including human evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge assessment, and consistency checks, demonstrate Benchmark Agent can generate high-quality benchmark samples with minimal human involvement. More importantly, through continual evaluation, we observe several insightful findings, including that current models struggle with certain domain-specific reasoning tasks. We believe that rapidly evolving benchmarks can contribute significantly to the research community. The preview and code will be publicly available at the demo page and code repository.

</details>


### [261] [Goedel-Architect: Streamlining Formal Theorem Proving with Blueprint Generation and Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06468)
*Jui-Hui Chung,Ziyang Cai,Zihao Li,Qishuo Yin,Rohit Agarwal,Simon Park,Rodrigo Porto,Narutatsu Ri,Ziran Yang,Shange Tang,Xingyu Dang,Hongzhou Lin,Mengdi Wang,Danqi Chen,Chi Jin,Liam H Fowl,Sanjeev Arora*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Blueprint-based, parallelized theorem proving for Lean 4 that builds a dependency graph (blueprint) of lemmas/definitions, refines it via failed lemmas, and achieves state-of-the-art results with substantially lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome inefficiencies of recursive, dead-end lemma decomposition by using a global blueprint that guides proof construction and leverages parallel proving to scale across lemmas.

Method: Generate a blueprint of definitions/lemmas with declared dependencies; optionally seed with a natural language proof; use a parallel, tool-equipped Lean prover to discharge open lemmas; refine the global blueprint based on failures; backbone: open-weight DeepSeek-V4-Flash (284B-A13B); iterative completion improves sparse search trees; compare to mainstream recursive-decomposition approaches.

Result: Achieves 99.2% pass@1 on MiniF2F-test and 75.6% pass@1 on PutnamBench. With NL proof seeding: 100% remaining MiniF2F, 88.8% on PutnamBench (597/672), 4/6 on IMO 2025, 11/12 on Putnam 2025, 3/6 on USAMO 2026. Claimed as state-of-the-art for open-source pipelines at up to 500x lower cost than comparable pipelines.

Conclusion: Blueprint-guided, parallelized theorem proving with iterative refinement delivers high accuracy and efficiency, outperforming recursive-decomposition baselines while offering substantial cost advantages.

Abstract: We introduce Goedel-Architect, an agentic framework for formal theorem proving in Lean 4 centered on blueprint generation and refinement. A blueprint is a dependency graph of definitions and lemmas that builds up to the main theorem. First, Goedel-Architect generates a blueprint of formally stated definitions and lemmas, along with declared dependencies. This blueprint is optionally guided by a natural language proof. Then, a tool-equipped Lean prover component closes each open lemma node in parallel using relevant dependencies. Failed lemmas in turn drive refinement of the global blueprint. This strategy contrasts with other mainstream approaches which use recursive lemma decomposition, and can inefficiently loop on dead-end strategies. Using the open-weight DeepSeek-V4-Flash (284B-A13B) as the backbone, Goedel-Architect attains 99.2% pass@1 on MiniF2F-test and 75.6% pass@1 on PutnamBench. With an optional natural-language proof seeding the initial blueprint on the harder problems, we additionally close the remaining two MiniF2F-test problems (reaching 100%), lift PutnamBench to 88.8% (597/672), and solve 4/6 on IMO 2025, 11/12 on Putnam 2025, and 3/6 on USAMO 2026. This represents state-of-the-art performance for an open-source pipeline at a price point up to 500x less than comparable open-source pipelines.

</details>


### [262] [MLEvolve: A Self-Evolving Framework for Automated Machine Learning Algorithm Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06473)
*Shangheng Du,Xiangchao Yan,Jinxin Shi,Zongsheng Cao,Shiyang Feng,Zichen Liang,Boyuan Sun,Tianshuo Peng,Yifan Zhou,Xin Li,Jie Zhou,Liang He,Bo Zhang,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MLEvolve is an LLM-driven self-evolving multi-agent framework for end-to-end ML algorithm discovery that overcomes long-horizon optimization challenges by enabling cross-branch information flow, retrospective memory, and decoupled planning and coding; it achieves state-of-the-art results on MLE-Bench and outperforms AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization, with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon tasks in ML engineering require sustained self-evolution; existing MLE agents suffer from information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control, limiting optimization progress.

Method: Extend tree search to Progressive MCGS with graph-based reference edges for cross-branch information flow; introduce Retrospective Memory combining a cold-start knowledge base and a dynamic global memory; implement adaptive coding modes to separate strategic planning from code generation.

Result: MLEvolve achieves state-of-the-art metrics on MLE-Bench under a 12-hour budget, including average medal rate and valid submission rate, and outperforms AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks; demonstrates cross-domain generalization; code released at GitHub.

Conclusion: MLEvolve provides a robust framework for long-horizon ML algorithm discovery with improved information flow, memory, and planning-coding decoupling, enabling faster and more generalizable ML evolution.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly applied to long-horizon tasks such as scientific discovery and machine learning engineering (MLE), where sustained self-evolution becomes a key capability. However, existing MLE agents suffer from inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control, which together hinder long-horizon optimization. We present MLEvolve, an LLM-based self-evolving multi-agent framework for end-to-end machine learning algorithm discovery. By extending tree search to Progressive MCGS, MLEvolve enables cross-branch information flow through graph-based reference edges and gradually shifts the search from broad exploration to focused exploitation with an entropy-inspired progressive schedule. To allow the agent to evolve with accumulated experience, we introduce Retrospective Memory, which combines a cold-start domain knowledge base with a dynamic global memory for task-specific experience retrieval and reuse. For stable long-horizon iteration, we further decouple strategic planning from code generation with adaptive coding modes. Evaluation on MLE-Bench shows that MLEvolve achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple dimensions including average medal rate and valid submission rate under a 12-hour budget (half the standard runtime). Moreover, MLEvolve also outperforms specialized algorithm discovery methods including AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization. Our code is available at https://github.com/InternScience/MLEvolve.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [263] [Efficient Mean Curvature Computation on High-Dimensional Data Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06329)
*Alexandre L. M. Levada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-fold approach to estimate local mean curvature efficiently: (1) an exact algebraic identity eliminates the full matrix H, lowering per-point cost to O(m^2); (2) replace full eigendecomposition with a truncated SVD of a k×m data matrix and approximate null-space contributions analytically under Haar measure, achieving total cost O(k^2 m + k m p^2) with p=k-1. Empirically 50–300× speedups with negligible accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Curvature is a valuable geometric feature for high-dimensional data; naive local curvature estimation via k-NN patches leads to O(m^4) complexity per point, untenable for high-dimensional settings; scalable, accurate, and data-driven curvature estimation is needed for modern ML pipelines.

Method: Derive an exact algebraic identity from eigenvector orthogonality and trace cyclicity to remove the matrix H, reducing cost to O(m^2) post-eigendecomposition. Then replace the full covariance with a truncated SVD on the k×m centered data matrix (rank ≤ k-1), reducing to O(k^2 m). Derive an analytical approximation for the null-space contribution using the expected outer product under the Haar measure. Combine into an estimator with total cost O(k^2 m + k m p^2), where p = k-1.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real-world datasets demonstrates 50–300× speedups compared to the original implementation, with negligible accuracy loss when using the fast estimator.

Conclusion: The proposed two-part approach yields a scalable, data-driven local curvature estimator, making curvature a practical geometric feature for a broad set of ML tasks, from classical to deep learning pipelines.

Abstract: Estimating local mean curvature at each point of a high-dimensional dataset is a key ingredient of geometry-aware machine learning algorithms, such as the Mean Curvature Boundary Points (MCBP) method. The naive implementation of this computation, based on a local shape operator approximated from k-nearest neighbor patches, involves an explicit construction of a matrix $H$ whose trace form yields an $O(m^4)$ cost per point, rendering the approach intractable for datasets with more than a few dozen features. This paper introduces two complementary contributions that together reduce this cost by several orders of magnitude. The first contribution is an exact algebraic identity. This identity, derived from the orthogonality of the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix and the cyclicity of the trace operator, eliminates $H$ entirely and reduces the per-point cost to $O(m^2)$ after the eigendecomposition. The second contribution addresses the remaining $O(m^3)$ bottleneck of the full eigendecomposition. Since the local covariance matrix has rank at most $k-1 \ll m$, we replace it with a truncated SVD of the $k \times m$ centered data matrix, an $O(k^2 m)$ operation, and derive an analytical approximation for the contribution of the null-space eigenvectors based on the expected value of their outer product under the Haar measure. The resulting estimator has total cost $O(k^2 m + k m p^2)$, where $p = k-1$. Experiments on real-world datasets confirm speedups of 50 to 300 times relative to the original implementation, with negligible loss when the fast estimator is used to replace the original version. By providing a scalable and data-driven estimate of local curvature, the proposed method establishes curvature as a practical geometric feature for a broad range of machine learning tasks, from classical to modern deep learning pipelines.

</details>


### [264] [The Evaluation Blind Spot: A Stereological Theory of Benchmark Coverage for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05169)
*Jason Z Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical framework links benchmark coverage to effective dimensionality, giving bounds on visible coverage, plus a practical core-set algorithm and a minimax rate result for C^2 functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify and optimize LLM benchmark coverage, identify robust core benchmarks, and understand how hidden structure governs evaluation informativeness and stability across evolving leaderboards.

Method: Develop a stereological theory using visible Hausdorff distance bounds for convex capability profiles consistent with the same scores; estimate effective dimensionality d_eff from leaderboards; use chi-squared projection and isotropic priors for simulational studies of ranking stability; apply a submodular greedy algorithm with Nemhauser (1-1/e) guarantee to select a stable core of benchmarks; perform counterfactual validations across internal benchmarks and external categories; analyze eigenstructure to predict irreplaceability of evaluations; resolve Gardner's Problem 1.5 for C^2 support functions via optimal recovery on S^(D-1).

Result: Bounds show visible Hausdorff distance epsilon + C R m^{-1/(d_eff-1)} with matching lower bound; d_eff on three leaderboards lies in [2.86, 4.80]; blind spot dominates score gaps by ~100x and exceeds noise by 52-127x; ranking stability under priors yields top-1 swap rates between 0.38 and 0.49; 92% of 500 trials swap top-1; a core set of 4 benchmarks guarantees stability with a submodular greedy method (1-1/e), with 7 of 12 achieving 90% coverage and high temporal transfer (93-97% retention); eigenstructure predicts irreplacable evaluations (rho = -0.69, p = 0.013) and informative external evaluations (rho = +0.38); minimax rate for C^2 functions established as Theta(R/(kappa m^{2/(D-1)}))).

Conclusion: The work provides a principled, geometry-informed framework for benchmark coverage and core-set selection, linking theoretical bounds and optimal recovery to practical stability of LLM evaluations, and resolves a classical minimax-rate problem for smooth support functions.

Abstract: We give a stereological theory of LLM benchmark coverage. For any suite with effective dimensionality d_eff, the visible Hausdorff distance between two convex capability profiles consistent with the same scores is bounded by epsilon + C R m^(-1/(d_eff-1)), with matching Lipschitz lower bound. Empirically, three independent leaderboards (Open LLM v2, an extended 12-benchmark suite, LiveBench) all have d_eff in [2.86, 4.80] on their competitive frontier; the structural blind spot exceeds the observed runner-up score gap by two orders of magnitude and dominates statistical noise by 52-127x. Under a chi-squared projection model, the isotropic prior is the optimistic case; across six hidden-capability priors and four ambient dimensions the simulated half-split swap rate of the top two models stays in [0.38, 0.49], and a 500-trial random visible/held-out split shows that 92% of trials swap the top-1 ranking with on average 2.83 of 5 top-5 models changing. A submodular greedy algorithm with the Nemhauser (1 - 1/e) guarantee finds a stable core of 4 benchmarks; 7 of 12 suffice for 90% coverage, and the trained subset transfers across temporal quarters with 93-97% retention. A counterfactual validation across 12 internal benchmarks and 27 Chatbot Arena categories confirms that the eigenstructure predicts which evaluations are irreplaceable (rho = -0.69, p = 0.013 for removal disruption) and which external evaluations bring new information (rho = +0.38). As a second, independent theoretical contribution, we resolve Gardner's Problem 1.5 (1995) for C^2 support functions, establishing the minimax rate Theta(R/(kappa m^(2/(D-1)))) in general dimension via optimal recovery theory on S^(D-1).

</details>


### [265] [ERRORQUAKE: Heavy-Tailed Error Severity Distributions in Open-Weight Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05170)
*Jason Z Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Severity distributions (0-4) across 8 domains and 5 difficulties reveal non-redundant information beyond error rate; many model pairs have non-overlapping severity tails, validated by human ratings; severity shifts by error type and model size; severity should accompany accuracy in reporting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Error-rate alone masks distributional differences in error severity across LLMs. Since different error types (e.g., retrieval failures vs fabrication) have different impact, a continuous severity profile provides a more informative evaluation and supports cross-model comparisons of open-weight LLMs.

Method: Introduce Errorquake-10k: a 10k-query benchmark with responses scored on a 0-4 severity scale, across 8 domains and 5 difficulty tiers. Fit per-model severity distributions for 21 open-weight models. Estimate a severity distribution index b (Gutenberg-Richter upper-tail slope) with 95% bootstrap CIs. Validate with 519-item three-rater human study (ICC(2,k=3)=0.85; rank correlation rho=0.89; dense-model scaling rho_s=-0.86). Prove a Non-Reducibility Theorem: I(b; model | epsilon)=1.56 bits; 64.5% of cross-model b variance unexplained by epsilon. Develop a severity mechanism taxonomy (kappa=0.83): low-severity errors are mainly retrievals (71%), high-severity errors are fabrications (39%), with composition varying by model size (p<0.0001).

Result: Across 210 model pairs, 85 pairs have disjoint 95% confidence intervals for b at matched accuracy (epsilon about 0.586, Delta b=0.47). The human validation confirms measurement reliability and ranking validity, and shows a strong correlation between dense-model scaling and human performance. Severity distribution captures discriminative information not captured by error rate, with different error-type compositions across severity levels and models.

Conclusion: Severity distribution should be reported alongside accuracy in LLM benchmarking. It provides non-redundant, discriminative information about model behavior, informing error analysis and model tuning for open-weight LLMs.

Abstract: At matched accuracy, open-weight LLMs differ substantially in the shape of their error severity distribution -- a difference invisible to the scalar error rate. Hallucination benchmarks report a single error count and treat all errors as equivalent, yet a wrong date and a fabricated court ruling differ by orders of magnitude. We introduce Errorquake-10k, a 10,000-query benchmark scoring each response on a continuous 0-4 severity scale across 8 domains and 5 difficulty tiers, and we fit per-model severity distributions for 21 open-weight models. For each model we estimate a severity distribution index (b, the Gutenberg-Richter upper-tail slope) with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Headline: across the 210 model pairs, 85 have disjoint 95% b confidence intervals at matched accuracy (|Delta epsilon| < 0.05) on human-consensus scoring, e.g. deepseek-v3.2 vs. ministral-14b at epsilon = 0.586 and Delta b = 0.47. A 519-item three-rater human validation study confirms measurement reliability (ICC(2,k=3) = 0.85), validates the LLM-judge ranking (rho = 0.89), and confirms the dense-model scaling correlation on human data (rho_s = -0.86). We prove a Non-Reducibility Theorem showing that severity profile and error rate are informationally non-redundant (I(b; model | epsilon) = 1.56 bits; 64.5% of cross-model b variance is unexplained by epsilon). A severity mechanism taxonomy (kappa = 0.83) reveals that error type shifts categorically with severity: low-severity errors are retrievals (71%); high-severity errors are fabrications (39%) -- and this composition differs by model size (p < 0.0001). Severity distribution should be reported alongside accuracy; it carries discriminative information that the error rate cannot.

</details>


### [266] [Staged Factorial Screening for Budget-Constrained Micro-Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05186)
*Felipe Chavarro Polania*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Staged fractional-factorial screening with budget-aware evaluation suggests a bridge-centered approach for budget-constrained micro-pretraining; short screens identify high-penalty directions, then refine within reduced space across two hosts to 24h, yielding robust anchors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether a staged fractional-factorial workflow can recover early effect structure under tight budgets and identify robust training anchors across hardware constraints.

Method: Perform 613 experiments across pilot/follow-up screens at 2, 5, 10 minutes; analyze full 16-condition seeded reruns at 5 and 10 minutes; targeted seeded anchor checks; compare same-host greedy, matched-cost random baselines; include 60-minute bridge package and bounded Windows A100/Linux L40S anchor continuations up to 24h on two hosts.

Result: Total penalties from batch, depth, width are largest at short budgets and relax with more budget. D, A, B, C show non-zero effects at 5/10 min after BH correction; E does not. Random search finds strong incumbents only in a small region and without clear factor attribution. The 60-minute bridge anchor has the lowest mean but confounds workflow refinement with capacity. Across 12h/24h continuations, bridge maintains the lowest sample mean and non-bridge ordering is host-sensitive. A bounded method emerges: use short screens to identify penalties, confirm anchors with repeats, and refine locally within reduced space.

Conclusion: Advocate a bridge-centered, bounded workflow: screen quickly to identify high-penalty directions, validate promising anchors with repeated runs, and refine within the reduced design space across two hosts for up to 24 hours. Hardware-invariant ranking or generic hyperparameter-optimization superiority is not supported.

Abstract: Budget-constrained micro-pretraining often requires triaging many candidate recipes on a shared accelerator before larger search budgets are spent. We study whether a staged fractional-factorial workflow can recover stable early effect structure in this setting. On a fixed autoresearch-derived single-GPU training loop, we run 613 experiments across pilot and follow-up screens at 2, 5, and 10 minutes; full 16-condition seeded reruns at 5 and 10 minutes; targeted seeded anchor checks; same-host greedy and matched-cost random baselines; a 60-minute bridge package; and bounded Windows A100 and Linux L40S anchor continuations through 24 hours. Main penalties from total batch, depth, and width are largest at short budgets and relax as budget increases. Within the predeclared seeded full-screen families, D, A, B, and C retain non-zero estimates at 5 and 10 minutes after within-budget Benjamini-Hochberg correction, while E does not. Random search can reach strong incumbents in this 32-condition space, but repeatedly in the same low-penalty region and without factor attribution. The 60-minute bridge anchor has the lowest mean, although that package does not separate workflow refinement from the larger bridge model's capacity advantage. In bounded 12-hour and 24-hour three-anchor continuations on both hosts, the bridge has the lowest sample mean while the non-bridge ordering stays host-sensitive. We therefore present a bounded methods result: use short designed screens to identify high-penalty directions, confirm promising anchors under repeated runs, and refine locally inside the reduced space. The evidence supports a bridge-centered recommendation through 24 hours on two hosts, not hardware-invariant ranking or general hyperparameter-optimization superiority.

</details>


### [267] [PyCC.id: A package for hypothesis-driven equation discovery with structural identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05191)
*Federico J. Gonzalez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Skeleton-based, hypothesis-driven ODE discovery with structural identifiability, implemented in PyCC to support multi-paradigm discovery from time-series data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inverse data-driven equation discovery is ill-posed and often yields multiple plausible models. Incorporating hypotheses and constraints reduces the search space but still leaves candidate models; skeletons anchored in characteristic curves provide a framework with identifiability checks and modularity across discovery paradigms.

Method: Define a skeleton tied to a family of ODEs; incorporate domain hypotheses and priors to refine the model iteratively; leverage structural identifiability of skeletons; support modular integration of neural networks, symbolic regression, and sparse regression within the framework.

Result: Skeletons offer structural identifiability properties useful for validating/discarding models; the framework enables using multiple equation discovery paradigms; PyCC is presented as a Python library to define skeletons and hypotheses for ODE discovery from time-dependent data.

Conclusion: The work presents PyCC, a flexible tool that unifies skeleton-, hypothesis-, and multi-paradigm equation discovery from time-series data, enabling researchers to systematically incorporate prior knowledge and identifiability checks.

Abstract: Data-driven equation discovery is fundamentally an inverse problem that seeks to infer the governing differential equations of a system directly from time-series measurements. A known issue is the ill-conditioned nature of the inverse problem, which frequently produces multiple mathematical models that fit the data similarly well. One path to address this issue is by incorporating known hypotheses and constraints into the training phase beforehand. While this approach effectively reduces the search space, it still results in multiple candidate models, forcing practitioners to rely on post-hoc manual filtering based on their own domain expertise. A recent approach incorporates structural `skeletons' inspired by characteristic curves (CCs), defining a hypothesis-driven methodology. In this methodology, practitioners define a skeleton, which is associated with a family of ordinary differential equations (ODEs), and then add their hypotheses and priors based on their domain knowledge to refine the obtained model iteratively. An important advantage of this approach is that some skeletons have demonstrable structural identifiability properties, which are useful for checking whether the skeleton is correct or should be discarded. Furthermore, this formalism enables the use of multiple equation discovery paradigms due to its modularity (such as neural networks, symbolic regression, and sparse regression). In this work, we present the Python library PyCC, which condenses these efforts into a flexible tool that allows researchers and engineers to seamlessly define their skeletons and hypotheses to discover ODEs from time-dependent data.

</details>


### [268] [Temporal Preference Concepts and their Functions in a Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05194)
*Ian Rios-Sialer,Shantanu Darveshi,Shuai Jiang,Avigya Paudel,Anastasiia Pronina,Ipshita Bandyopadhyay,Justin Shenk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies the subgraph and residual-stream encoding of temporal preference in a distilled LLM, with mid-to-upper-layer localization; shows LLMs discount the future less steeply than humans in a context-sensitive manner, and that steering vectors can shift temporal preference; demonstrates mechanistic interpretability enables targeted control of planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how LLMs represent and resolve near-term vs. long-term tradeoffs, aiming to enable reliable, controllable planning and decision making.

Method: Causal localization of a temporal-preference subgraph using gradient-based attribution and activation patching; analysis of residual-stream geometry at localized layers; behavioral comparison with human discounting; exploration of steering-vector manipulation.

Result: Identified mid-to-upper-layer nodes forming a subgraph responsible for temporal preference; time-horizon geometry encoded in the residual stream at these layers; unintervened LLMs discount the future less steeply than humans with context-dependent instability; steering vectors can shift temporal preference.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that mechanistic interpretability can bring us closer to reliable control over how LLMs plan and reason.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being deployed to make decisions that require trading off near-term gains against long-term consequences, yet little is known about how they internally represent or resolve these tradeoffs. In this work, we causally localize an underlying subgraph for temporal preference in a distilled LLM (Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507), identifying mid-to-upper-layer nodes through converging evidence from gradient-based attribution and activation patching. We find that the geometry of time horizon is encoded in the residual stream at the expected localized layers. A behavioral analysis reveals that unintervened LLMs discount the future several times less steeply than humans, yet this preference is unstable across contexts, motivating explicit control rather than implicit reliance on training. Finally, we find suggestive evidence that steering vectors can shift temporal preference. Our work demonstrates how mechanistic interpretability can bring us closer to reliable control over how LLMs plan and reason

</details>


### [269] [State commitment learning: training language models to distinguish computation from memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05201)
*Fei Ding,Yongkang Zhang,Runhao Liu,Yuhao Liao,Zijian Zeng,Huiming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: State commitment learning separates persistent state from temporary computation in reasoning LMs, enabling an objective that ensures outputs remain usable after erasing hidden thoughts. It introduces persistent-state sufficiency, Counterfactual Erasure RL (CERL), and the Erasure Dependence Protocol, showing across domains that reducing reliance on hidden scratch work improves robustness without hurting accuracy, outperforming correctness-only RL and long-answer SFT baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate the problem that hidden computation (dead ends, scratch work) remains in context and biases future predictions, undermining reliability of downstream reasoning.

Method: Define persistent state vs temporary computation; introduce a trainable persistent-state sufficiency criterion; develop CERL which compares two paths under the same prefix—one with hidden thoughts, one with erased thoughts—and rewards the erased path if correct; formalize the Erasure Dependence Protocol; evaluate across math, long-chain logic, scientific QA, and multi-turn tool use.

Result: CERL substantially reduces dependence on hidden thoughts in model outputs without sacrificing accuracy, consistently beating correctness-only RL and long-answer SFT baselines.

Conclusion: Training to explicitly distinguish and erase hidden scratch work yields more robust, verifiable reasoning; the proposed protocol and CERL framework offer a practical route to safer, more reliable LMs across diverse reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reasoning language models do not distinguish tokens used for computation from tokens that constitute persistent state: once generated, all hidden thoughts remain in context and influence future predictions. As a result, downstream reasoning may depend on failed attempts, dead ends, and private scratch work that should not be safely relied on later. We recast this phenomenon as a new training objective, state commitment learning: training models to explicitly distinguish information that should be committed as persistent state from temporary computation that can be discarded. We define a counterfactual criterion, persistent-state sufficiency, which makes it trainable and measurable whether an answer remains usable after hidden thoughts are erased. We then propose Counterfactual Erasure RL (CERL), which evaluates, under the same prefix, both a path that keeps hidden thoughts and a path that erases them, and gives reward only when the erasure path remains correct. We also introduce the Erasure Dependence Protocol and show across mathematics, long-chain logic, scientific QA, and multi-turn tool-use evaluation that CERL substantially reduces answer dependence on hidden thoughts without sacrificing accuracy, consistently outperforming correctness-only RL and long-answer SFT baselines.

</details>


### [270] [Gradient Descent with Large Step Size Restores Symmetry in Deep Linear Networks with Multi-Pathway](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05219)
*Hee-Sung Kim,Sungyoon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Large-step discrete gradient descent on multi-pathway deep linear networks yields shared representations rather than persistent single-path dominance. While gradient flow predicts depth-driven symmetry breaking, edge-of-stability dynamics induce re-balancing, and single-paths are sharp minima while distributing signals across pathways reduces sharpness, with reductions scaling with the number of pathways and depth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how discrete GD with large steps influences the competition among multiple pathways in deep linear networks and whether it agrees with gradient-flow predictions of symmetry breaking or yields different representations.

Method: Theoretical analysis of sharpness for single-path versus distributed-path configurations in multi-pathway deep linear networks under discrete GD with large step sizes; examination of early training GF-like symmetry breaking and subsequent edge-of-stability oscillations that drive re-balancing across pathways; derivation of how depth and pathway count affect sharpness and dynamics.

Result: Single-path solutions are sharp minima; distributing signals across pathways lowers sharpness by a factor that decreases as the number of pathways and depth increase. Early training aligns with gradient-flow depth-driven symmetry breaking, but EOS-induced oscillations override this tendency, driving a re-balancing phase where signals redistribute across pathways. Overall, large-step GD favors shared representations rather than persistent single-pathway dominance.

Conclusion: Depth shapes pathway competition in multi-pathway deep linear networks, and large-step gradient descent promotes shared representations over persistent single-pathway dominance, reconciling gradient-flow predictions with edge-of-stability dynamics.

Abstract: Recent analyses of multi-pathway Deep Linear Networks use Gradient Flow to predict a "winner-takes-all" specialization in which path symmetry breaks and each feature concentrates in a single pathway. In this work, we show that discrete Gradient Descent (GD) with a large step size tells a different story. We prove that single-path solutions are sharp minima, whereas distributing signals across pathways reduces sharpness by a factor that decreases with both the number of pathways and depth. Consequently, while early training reproduces the depth-driven symmetry breaking predicted by GF, oscillations at the Edge of Stability subsequently override this tendency and drive the network into a re-balancing phase, where signals redistribute across pathways. Together, these results clarify how depth shapes pathway competition and explain why large-step GD favors shared representations rather than persistent single-pathway dominance.

</details>


### [271] [Differentiable Efficient Operator Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05232)
*Xiaohuan Pei,Jiyuan Zhang,Yuanfan Guo,Weiguo Feng,Tao Huang,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Chang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Differentiable operator search unifies token-reduction operators in multimodal models, enabling joint optimization of where/how many tokens to keep and how to process reduced tokens, achieving competitive efficiency-accuracy trade-offs and discovering hybrid operators beyond manual designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual token-reduction operators are diverse and heuristic; viewing them as regimes of a shared operator space enables automated, differentiable search to improve efficiency under token-budget constraints.

Method: Efficient Operator Search (EOS): a differentiable framework that jointly searches layer activation, retention budget, and operator behavior. The search space is parameterized; the policy optimizes task performance under one-sided budget and cost constraints; recovers hand-designed baselines as special cases; discovers hybrid operators.

Result: On multimodal benchmarks, EOS achieves competitive accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, especially under aggressive visual-token reduction; it can recover existing baselines and identify novel operator hybrids.

Conclusion: Efficient multimodal inference can be reframed from manual operator design to differentiable operator search, enabling flexible, data-driven operator configurations.

Abstract: Efficient multimodal foundation models often rely on manually designed token-reduction operators, such as pruning, merging, pooling, and adaptive reweighting. Although these operators appear different, we show that they can be interpreted as distinct regimes of a shared operator space. Based on this view, we introduce Efficient Operator Search, a differentiable framework that jointly searches where to reduce tokens, how many tokens to retain, and how reduced token information should be processed. The proposed search space parameterizes layer activation, retention budget, and operator behavior, while the search policy optimizes task performance under one-sided budget and cost constraints. This formulation recovers representative hand-designed baselines as special cases and further discovers hybrid operators beyond isolated manual designs. Experiments on multimodal benchmarks show that the searched operators achieve competitive accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, especially under aggressive visual-token reduction. These results suggest that efficient multimodal inference can be reframed from manual operator design to differentiable operator search.

</details>


### [272] [DiffSlack: Learning under Nonlinear Inequality Constraints via Learnable Slack Variables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05247)
*Ziqian Wang,Chenxi Fang,Zhen Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiffSlack is a differentiable projection layer for nonlinear inequality-constrained neural prediction. It reformulates inequalities as equalities with learnable slack variables predicted by the network, providing a data-driven warm start for a damped Gauss-Newton projection. A two-stage curriculum stabilizes training. Evaluated on vehicle path planning with 200 nonlinear constraints, it achieves higher planning success and stronger constraint satisfaction than baselines, with ablations showing reduced sensitivity to supervision quality. Validated in CARLA and real-world vehicles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enforcing hard nonlinear inequality constraints in neural networks is challenging, especially with many coupled constraints and large-scale problems. Existing hard-constraint methods either restrict the constraint set structure or incur high computational overhead. There is a need for a scalable, differentiable projection method to embed hard inequalities.

Method:  Reformulate inequalities a_i(x) <= 0 as equalities a_i(x) + s_i = 0 with nonnegative slack variables s_i. Predict s_i as part of the network output, providing a data-driven warm start for damped Gauss-Newton projection onto the augmented feasible manifold. Use a two-stage curriculum to stabilize training and enhance constraint satisfaction. Implement a differentiable projection layer that preserves end-to-end differentiability while mapping predictions onto the augmented feasible set.

Result: Compared with learning-based baselines on vehicle path planning with 200 nonlinear inequality constraints (collision avoidance, curvature limits, waypoint spacing), DiffSlack achieves higher planning success rates and stronger geometric constraint satisfaction under a similar inference budget. Ablation studies show hard projection reduces sensitivity to supervision quality. Closed-loop tracking in CARLA and real-world vehicle experiments confirm executability of generated trajectories.

Conclusion: DiffSlack offers a practical, scalable approach to embedding hard nonlinear inequality constraints into neural networks for engineering applications, combining a differentiable projection with learnable slack variables and curriculum-based training to improve constraint satisfaction and feasibility.

Abstract: Enforcing nonlinear inequality constraints in neural networks remains challenging, especially when the output is subject to many coupled constraints. Existing hard constraint methods often impose structural restrictions on the constraint set or introduce substantial computational overhead for large-scale nonlinear problems. Here, we propose DiffSlack, a differentiable projection layer for nonlinear inequality-constrained neural prediction. DiffSlack reformulates inequalities as equalities with learnable slack variables, which are predicted as part of the augmented network output and provide a data-driven warm start for damped Gauss-Newton projection. The projection layer maps raw predictions onto the augmented feasible manifold while preserving end-to-end differentiability. A two-stage curriculum further stabilizes training and improves constraint satisfaction. We evaluate DiffSlack on vehicle path planning with 200 nonlinear inequality constraints from collision avoidance, curvature limits, and waypoint spacing. Compared with existing learning-based baselines, DiffSlack achieves a higher planning success rate and stronger geometric constraint satisfaction under a comparable inference budget. Ablation studies further show that the hard projection layer reduces sensitivity to supervision quality. Closed-loop tracking in CARLA and real-world vehicle experiments confirms the executability of the generated trajectories. These results demonstrate that DiffSlack provides a practical and scalable approach to embedding hard inequality constraints into neural networks for engineering applications.

</details>


### [273] [Alpha-RTL: Test-Time Training for RTL Hardware Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05253)
*Peilong Zhou,Zhirong Chen,Cangyuan Li,Haoyu Gao,Kaiyan Chang,Ziming Qu,Ying Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces TTT-RTL, a per-design test-time training framework for RTL design using LLMs and executable EDA feedback; achieves large improvements in PPA/ADP metrics via online policy adaptation, state reuse, and KL budget control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of pre-trained or frozen-policy RTL generation by enabling per-design adaptation to physical EDA feedback, bridging function correctness and hardware efficiency.

Method: Per-design test-time RL: sample RTL variants, verify via syntax and simulation, score using synthesis-derived PPA; maintain a PUCT-indexed design-state pool; update policy with an entropic policy-gradient objective; use adaptive KL-budget controller based on reference KL, effective sample size, and reward saturation to stabilize learning.

Result: On RTLLM v2.0 (Nangate 45nm): 65.1% reduction in geometric-mean PPA product vs. reference; stronger than frozen-policy baseline by 26.1%. On XuanTie C910 FPU (Sky130): 59.4% ADP reduction. Ablations show policy adaptation, state reuse, KL-budget control contribute.

Conclusion: Test-time training with executable EDA feedback enables LLM-based RTL generation to optimize hardware metrics beyond functional correctness, indicating practical viability for physically constrained RTL synthesis with LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown increasing promise in generating
  functionally correct register-transfer-level (RTL) hardware designs.
  Recent systems improve further through EDA-integrated reinforcement
  learning with syntax, simulation, and PPA rewards, but train a general
  RTL generator before deployment while test-time approaches search with
  a frozen policy. We instead perform reinforcement learning at test time,
  allowing the LLM policy to adapt to executable EDA feedback for the
  specific RTL problem at hand. We propose TTT-RTL, to our knowledge the
  first per-design test-time training framework that closes the loop
  between an LLM policy and an EDA pipeline for RTL optimization. TTT-RTL
  samples candidate implementations, verifies them through syntax checking
  and simulation, scores valid designs using synthesis-derived PPA product,
  reuses high-reward variants through a PUCT-indexed design-state pool,
  and updates the policy with an entropic policy-gradient objective. To
  stabilize policy updates under sparse or plateaued rewards, we introduce
  an adaptive KL-budget controller that adjusts the entropy constraint
  using reference KL, effective sample size, and reward saturation signals.
  On RTLLM v2.0 under Nangate 45nm, TTT-RTL reduces the geometric-mean
  PPA product by 65.1% over the reference, outperforming the strongest
  published frozen-policy agent baseline at 26.1%. On an industrial
  XuanTie C910 FPU leading-zero-anticipation unit under Sky130, TTT-RTL
  achieves a 59.4% ADP reduction, and ablations confirm that policy
  adaptation, state reuse, and KL-budget control each contribute. These
  results suggest that test-time training with executable EDA feedback can
  move LLM-based RTL generation beyond functional correctness toward
  physically optimized hardware.

</details>


### [274] [Flash-WAM: Modality-Aware Distillation for World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05254)
*Arman Akbari,Ci Zhang,Arash Akbari,Lin Zhao,Yixiao Chen,Weiwei Chen,Xuan Zhang,Geng Yuan,Yanzhi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flash-WAM enables real-time joint video-action diffusion inference by modality-aware step distillation. It selects per-modality consistency functions to suit respective noise regimes, achieving large latency reductions with preserved task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the latency bottleneck of world-action models (WAMs) that require many denoising steps for joint video-action generation, preventing real-time control; existing single-modality distillation fails due to asymmetry between video and action streams.

Method: Introduce Flash-WAM, a consistency-distillation inspired framework that assigns a per-modality consistency function: linear-gradient-scaling for actions (low-noise regime) and variance-preserving for video (high-noise regime). Grounded by a structural analysis of the consistency-function family under the boundary condition. Applied to LingBot-VA and RoboTwin 2.0, achieving single-step inference per modality.

Result: Significant latency reduction: RoboTwin 2.0 per-chunk latency from 8.1s to 0.348s (23×). Real-world performance averaged 60% on Unitree G1; simulation benchmarks: 85.5% RoboTwin 2.0, 95.7% LIBERO. Naive consistency distillation at same budget drops to 24%.

Conclusion: Modality-aware step distillation enables real-time joint video-action generation with preserved task success; naive, one-size-fits-all distillation underperforms in this joint setting.

Abstract: World-action models (WAMs) jointly generate future video and robot actions through iterative diffusion, achieving strong performance on manipulation benchmarks but requiring tens of denoising steps, a cost that precludes real-time control. Step distillation has emerged as the natural remedy, but off-the-shelf methods break down in the joint video-action setting because video and action streams use different SNR-shifted noise schedules and reach training with substantially different marginal noise distributions, an asymmetry that single-modality distillation methods cannot accommodate. We introduce \textbf{Flash-WAM}, a modality-aware step-distillation framework inspired by consistency distillation that selects the consistency function for each modality to match its noise regime: a linear-gradient-scaling parametrization for the action stream's low-noise regime, paired with a variance-preserving parametrization for the video stream's high-noise regime, grounded in a structural analysis of the consistency-function family that characterizes the achievable gradient scaling under the consistency boundary condition. Instantiated on LingBot-VA, Flash-WAM compresses inference to a single step in each modality. On RoboTwin 2.0, this reduces per-chunk latency from $8.1$ seconds to $348$ ms on NVIDIA L40S, a $23{\times}$ speedup that enables real-time inference. Flash-WAM preserves task success on simulation benchmarks ($85.5\%$ RoboTwin 2.0, $95.7\%$ LIBERO) and substantially recovers real-world performance ($60\%$ average on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot), while naive consistency distillation drops to $24\%$ at the same step budget.

</details>


### [275] [Scaling Laws for Behavioral Foundation Models over User Event Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05257)
*Rickard Brüel Gabrielsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Study of compute scaling laws for behavioral foundation models trained on sequences of user actions, revealing that the optimal compute/architecture choices depend on data regime and evaluation metric; embedder is typically 2% of parameters and compute-optimal; scaling of data efficiency and negative sampling interacts with compute; the evaluation metric becomes part of the scaling law.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between scaling laws known for language models and outcome metrics for behavioral foundation models, to identify compute-optimal training recipes under different compute budgets and evaluation metrics.

Method: Large-scale empirical study (~600 runs) on real interaction data, varying four axes: two-part parameter split (embedder vs contextualizer/decoder), critical batch size, model/data allocation, and negatives used after freezing the embedder. Training across 1e15–1e19 FLOPs. Assessed both training objectives and deployed ranking metrics across compute budgets.

Result: Embedder remains compute-optimal at ~2% of parameters across budgets; compute-optimal training is data-heavy at low compute but shifts toward Chinchilla data-efficiency at higher compute; objective function (loss) and ranking metrics disagree and scale with compute; negative sampling favors more negatives with larger budgets; at 1e19 FLOPs, memory constraints, not FLOPs, limit negatives; evaluation metric becomes part of the scaling law, influencing the compute-optimal recipe.

Conclusion: Behavioral foundation models exhibit scaling behaviors where the evaluation metric itself influences optimal training settings; careful alignment between training objectives, sampling strategies, and evaluation metrics is essential, and efficient design favors a small, compute-efficient embedder with compute-aware negative sampling and metric-consistent evaluation.

Abstract: Foundation models are increasingly trained on sequences of user actions in recommendation, payments, fraud, and commerce, but these models still lack the kind of compute calibration that scaling laws provide for language models. We study a common two-part behavioral-model architecture: a feature-based event embedder maps each multi-modal item to a vector, and a decoder-only transformer predicts the next event from the resulting sequence. Across roughly 600 runs on real interaction data, spanning $10^{15}$-$10^{19}$ training FLOPs, we jointly vary four deployment-relevant axes: the two-part parameter split, critical batch size, model/data allocation, and the number of sampled negatives used after freezing the embedder. A small embedder ($s^{\star}\!\approx\!2\%$ of parameters) is compute-optimal at every budget we test because embedder parameters are both more expensive per step and exposed to far more repeated items than contextualizer parameters. Compute-optimal training is data-heavy relative to text at low compute, but its $D/N$ ratio moves toward the Chinchilla heuristic as compute increases. The sampled training objective and deployed ranking metrics disagree in ways that themselves scale: critical batch size, optimal negative count after freezing, and the agreement between loss and ranking quality all shift with compute and with the chosen evaluation metric. For negative sampling, larger budgets increasingly prefer more negatives; by $10^{19}$ FLOPs the active constraint is candidate-axis memory rather than FLOPs. In behavioral foundation models, the evaluation metric is therefore part of the scaling law: changing it can change the compute-optimal recipe.

</details>


### [276] [Policy-Conditioned Counterfactual Credit for Verifiable Reinforcement Learning of Long-Horizon Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05263)
*Renwei Meng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CVT-RL introduces a verifiable, constrained policy-gradient framework to curb evidence drift and tool abuse in long-horizon language RL, achieving higher success, better evidence quality, and reduced hacking under verifiable interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current process rewards are largely correlational and do not estimate a step's contribution to final verified success under interventions, leaving long-horizon language agents prone to unsupported evidence chains, belief drift, and shortcut actions that pass terminal checks.

Method: Dense verifiable rewards, intervention-validity gating, and a policy-conditioned counterfactual contribution (PCCC) estimator. Controlled interventions include deletion, semantic substitution, evidence substitution, and tool-output perturbation; continuations are sampled from a frozen reference policy; a selection-adjusted doubly robust estimator augments the advantage. Belief control uses only prefix-observable labels; an augmented Lagrangian constrains unsupported claims, skipped verification, tool tampering, and unsafe calls.

Result: On long-context QA benchmarks (ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, web/tool tasks), CVT-RL raises average task success to 78.9% from 71.8% (compute-matched baseline) and 75.4% (information-matched baseline); boosts evidence F1 to 82.8%; reduces measured hacking from 7.2% to 3.9% (independent human audit shows 4.6% vs 8.1%); adaptive detector-evasion attacks raise hacking to 7.1%. Results are statistically significant (p<0.01 after Holm correction).

Conclusion: Carefully scoped counterfactual credit with validity gating, diagnostics, and verifiable constraints provides a reproducible route toward more reliable long-horizon RL for language agents.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards improves reasoning and tool use, yet long-horizon language agents still learn unsupported evidence chains, belief drift, and shortcut actions that satisfy terminal checks. Existing process rewards are mostly correlational: they reward retrieval-, reflection-, or verification-like steps without estimating whether the step contributes to final verified success under a specified intervention. We propose CVT-RL, a constrained policy-gradient algorithm with dense verifiable rewards, intervention-validity gating, and a policy-conditioned counterfactual contribution (PCCC) estimator. Deletion, semantic substitution, evidence substitution, and tool-output perturbation define separate controlled interventions; continuations are sampled from a frozen reference policy, and a selection-adjusted doubly robust estimator augments the advantage. Belief control uses only prefix-observable labels, while an augmented Lagrangian constrains unsupported claims, skipped verification, tool tampering, and unsafe calls. On long-context QA, ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, and web/tool tasks, CVT-RL improves average task success from 71.8% for compute-matched non-causal RL and 75.4% for an information-matched counterfactual-process baseline to 78.9%, improves evidence F1 from 78.9 to 82.8 over the information-matched baseline, and reduces measured hacking from 7.2% to 3.9%. Independent human audit estimates 4.6% hacking for CVT-RL versus 8.1% for the information-matched baseline, and adaptive detector-evasion attacks raise hacking only to 7.1%. Stratified bootstrap and mixed-effects tests give p<0.01 after Holm correction for all primary metrics. Carefully scoped counterfactual credit, paired with validity gating, diagnostics, and verifiable constraints, provides a reproducible route toward more reliable long-horizon RL for language agents.

</details>


### [277] [REGEN: Reference-Guided Synthetic Multivariate Time Series Generation for Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05264)
*Moulik Gupta,Dhruv Kumar,Murari Mandal,Saurabh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReGeN: a reference-guided generative pipeline for multivariate time series that decomposes references into a phase-aligned periodic backbone, per-variable stochastic residuals via deep-kernel GP, and lag-aware cross-variable dependencies via an SCM, enabling controllable sampling that preserves domain structure and improves forecasting and pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity in robust multivariate forecasting. Overcome limitations of prior-based and black-box data-driven generators by leveraging domain-grounded reference sequences to guide synthesis while maintaining control over periodicity, variability, and cross-variable dynamics.

Method: Decompose each reference into three components: (1) phase-aligned periodic backbone capturing dominant morphology; (2) per-variable stochastic residuals modeled with a deep-kernel Gaussian process; (3) lag-aware cross-variable dependencies injected through a structural causal model with fitted coupling coefficients. Sampling these components at controllable temperature broadens distributional coverage while preserving reference-grounded structure.

Result: ReGeN-generated data substantially substitutes for real sibling data with minimal forecasting degradation; in strongly periodic domains like traffic, the synthetic data can outperform the real source. A foundation model pretrained on ReGeN corpora outperforms those pretrained on prior-based or data-driven synthetic alternatives.

Conclusion: Structural exploitation of reference data is crucial in low-data regimes; how reference data is leveraged can matter as much as the amount of data, with ReGeN demonstrating that controlled, structure-preserving synthesis can achieve or exceed real-data benefits for downstream forecasting and pretraining.

Abstract: Training robust multivariate time series forecasting models requires large, diverse corpora, yet many real-world domains provide only a handful of observed sequences. Existing generators fail to resolve this mismatch: prior-based approaches (e.g., CauKer, TimePFN) produce domain-agnostic samples, while data-driven methods (e.g., TimeGAN) treat references as black-box supervision, forfeiting explicit control over periodic structure, local variability, and cross-variable dynamics.
  We propose ReGeN, a reference-guided generative pipeline that treats observed sequences not as examples to imitate, but as structural scaffolds for controllable synthesis. ReGeN decomposes each reference into three interpretable components: a phase-aligned periodic backbone capturing dominant domain morphology; per-variable stochastic residuals modeled with a deep-kernel Gaussian process; and lag-aware cross-variable dependencies injected through a structural causal model with fitted coupling coefficients. Sampling these components at controllable temperature broadens distributional coverage while preserving domain-grounded structure.
  We show that ReGeN-generated data consistently substitutes for real sibling data with minimal forecasting degradation, and in strongly periodic domains such as traffic, can outperform the real source itself. We further show that a foundation model pretrained on ReGeN corpora outperforms those pretrained on prior-based and data-driven synthetic alternatives. This suggests that in low-data regimes, how reference data is structurally exploited can matter as much as how much data is available.

</details>


### [278] [Data-efficient flood depth prediction through domain-aware coreset selection and tabular foundation models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05265)
*Lipai Huang,Adithi Srinath,Manas Singh,Junwei Ma,Ali Mostafavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A domain-aware coreset strategy enables data-efficient, transferable flood depth surrogates using tabular foundation models; achieves near-supervised accuracy with minimal per-watershed data and transfers across watersheds without retraining, though performance varies for out-of-distribution cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for accurate, fast, and transferable flood depth surrogates that generalize across watersheds without extensive per-watershed labeled data or retraining.

Method:  Stratify storms by return period and affected watershed; sample hexagons using a target-aware spatial selector; condition a tabular foundation model at inference time (domain-aware coreset construction).

Result: With only 0.7% of the per-watershed training data, the model achieves mean R^2 = 0.663 across nine Houston-area watersheds, 98.5% of the supervised reference (R^2 = 0.673). It transfers to held-out watersheds without task-specific retraining, outperforming a coreset-trained supervised baseline. On real storms, it exceeds the supervised reference for a far out-of-distribution case and trails for a mostly in-distribution case.

Conclusion: Domain-aware coreset construction enables data-efficient, transferable flood predictions using tabular foundation models, eliminating the need for per-watershed training while maintaining competitive accuracy.

Abstract: Near-real-time flood depth prediction demands surrogate models that are accurate, fast, and transferable across watersheds. Supervised surrogates can match physics-based simulators in accuracy but need millions of training rows per watershed and cannot extrapolate beyond their original mesh. We propose a domain-aware coreset construction pipeline that conditions a tabular foundation model at inference time. The pipeline stratifies storms by return period and most-affected watershed, then samples hexagons with a target-aware spatial selector. With 0.7% of the per-watershed training pool, the model attains a mean $R^2$ of 0.663 across nine Houston-area watersheds, within 98.5% of the supervised reference ($R^2$ = 0.673). It transfers to held-out watersheds without task-specific retraining, staying ahead of a coreset-trained supervised baseline. On real storms it exceeds the supervised reference on a far out-of-distribution case and trails it on a mostly in-distribution one. Domain-aware coreset construction lets tabular foundation models deliver data-efficient, watershed-transferable flood predictions without per-watershed training.

</details>


### [279] [Sharp Low-Degree Thresholds for Planted-vs-Planted Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05266)
*Anda Skeja,Daniel Gutiérrez Espinoza,Fiona Skerman,Alexander S. Wein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sharp thresholds for low-degree tests in planted-vs-planted models (planted submatrix and planted dense subgraph) are established, matching the known low-degree recovery thresholds; weak testing has a smooth transition rather than a sharp threshold; a latent-variable expansion with signal-pruning underpins a general framework for these problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine when low-degree polynomial tests can distinguish between two structured planted mechanisms with vanishing error, and to relate testing thresholds to recovery thresholds in canonical planted-model settings.

Method: Develop a latent-variable expansion inspired by low-degree recovery; analyze low-degree polynomial tests for planted submatrix and planted dense subgraph; identify and prune non-signal contributions to isolate the signal; prove sharp upper and lower bounds and characterize weak-testing behavior; generalize into a framework for planted-vs-planted testing.

Result: Established matching sharp thresholds for counting communities problems in the planted submatrix and planted dense subgraph models; the testing threshold coincides with the low-degree recovery threshold down to the exact constant; weak testing exhibits a smooth transition rather than a sharp threshold.

Conclusion: Introduces a unified planted-vs-planted testing framework grounded in latent-variable expansions and signal-pruning; connects sharp testing thresholds to recovery thresholds and provides tools applicable to other planted-model scenarios.

Abstract: We establish the first sharp thresholds for low-degree polynomial tests in planted-vs-planted settings, where the goal is to determine with vanishing error which of two structured planted mechanisms generated the observed data. We prove matching low-degree upper and lower bounds for counting communities in the planted submatrix and planted dense subgraph models. The resulting testing threshold coincides, down to the sharp constant, with the known low-degree recovery threshold. In contrast, the task of weak testing, where the goal is to outperform random guessing, does not have a sharp threshold but rather a smooth transition, which we identify. To prove our results, we develop a framework for planted-vs-planted testing that builds on a latent-variable expansion originating in low-degree recovery and employs new methods to identify and prune non-signal contributions.

</details>


### [280] [Learning Manifold and Itô Dynamics with Branched Neural Rough Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05272)
*Luke Thompson,Dai Shi,Lequan Lin,Junbin Gao,Andi Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Branched NRDEs (B-NRDEs) extend NRDEs to Itô-style dynamics on manifolds via Hopf-algebraic branching, enabling quadratic variation visibility and intrinsic manifold preservation while maintaining efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NRDEs are efficient on irregular samples but cannot express Itô quadratic variation or manifold covariant derivatives; a framework is needed to align driving algebra with calculus and preserve constraints.

Method: Introduce Branched NRDEs using Grossman–Larson rooted trees for Euclidean Itô dynamics, Munthe-Kaas–Wright planar rooted trees for manifold covariant derivatives, and the shuffle algebra to recover Stratonovich cases; recast log-ODE step as geometric numerical integration on the state-space manifold; train with branched signature-kernel objective to expose quadratic variation.

Result: Demonstrate on rough Bergomi volatility, sim-to-real SO(3) dynamics forecasting, and SPD covariance dynamics; show Itô-consistent law matching and intrinsic constraint preservation; unifies stochastic/manifold-valued dynamics beyond Euclidean–Stratonovich.

Conclusion: B-NRDEs provide a principled, algebraic-geometry-aware framework that extends NRDEs to Itô dynamics on manifolds, enabling accurate, constraint-preserving, and trainable stochastic systems.

Abstract: Neural rough differential equations (NRDEs) stay accurate under irregular sampling while taking far fewer integration steps than standard neural differential equations, summarising a finely sampled driver by its log-signature and advancing the hidden state over coarse intervals using the log-ODE method.
