This efficiency rests on the shuffle algebra, the algebraic counterpart of Stratonovich calculus. This reliance means NRDEs cannot expose the quadratic-variation terms Itô dynamics require, nor the ordered covariant derivatives that govern Itô flows on connection-equipped manifolds. Ameliorating this, we introduce Branched Neural Rough Differential Equations (B-NRDEs), a Hopf-algebraic framework that recasts the NRDE log-ODE step as geometric numerical integration on the state-space manifold, matching the driving algebra to the governing calculus: Grossman--Larson rooted trees for Euclidean Itô dynamics, Munthe-Kaas--Wright planar rooted trees for ordered covariant derivatives on manifolds, and the shuffle algebra in the classical Stratonovich case. This yields intrinsic coarse-step dynamics that exactly preserve manifold constraints. Finally, we introduce a branched signature-kernel objective to enable Itô-consistent law matching by making quadratic-variation terms visible during training. On rough Bergomi volatility, sim-to-real $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ dynamics forecasting, and SPD covariance dynamics, B-NRDEs offer a unified, effective approach to stochastic and manifold-valued dynamics beyond the Euclidean--Stratonovich setting.

</details>


### [281] [Anomaly Detection for Electro-Hydrostatic Actuators using LSTM Autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05274)
*Nehal Afifi,Abdelmonem Elhendawi,Felix Leitenberger,Nadine Piat,Sven Matthiesen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reconstruction-based LSTM autoencoder for offline anomaly detection on univariate EHA sensor signals (temperature and pressure) achieving high accuracy and low false alarms; leverages temporal pattern learning to outperform conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EHAs generate high-volume, high-frequency sensor data; traditional statistical and ML methods struggle to capture temporal dependencies, leading to limited detection accuracy and higher false-alarm rates. There is a scarcity of data-driven evaluations for EHA anomaly detection across varying conditions.

Method: An offline anomaly-detection framework using a reconstruction-based LSTM autoencoder for univariate EHA signals (temperature and pressure). Normal data used to train; anomaly scores derived from reconstruction error calibrated using validation-set error distributions. Evaluated under multiple fault-injection scenarios with metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, plus sensitivity analyses over varying operating conditions.

Result: The approach yields average accuracy 99.0%, precision up to 100%, recall 90.2%–99.6%, and F1-scores 93.1%–99.8% across evaluated sensors, indicating high detection sensitivity and very low false-alarm rates.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of data-driven offline anomaly detection for EHAs. Indicates potential for reliable detection in high-volume sensor data; future work will adapt the framework for online real-time deployment.

Abstract: Electro-Hydrostatic Actuators (EHAs) are widely used in aerospace and industrial systems, where timely detection of sensor anomalies is essential to ensure safe and reliable operation. However, the large volume and high sampling frequency of EHA sensor data pose challenges for accurate and efficient anomaly detection. Conventional statistical and classical machine-learning methods such as Z-score, Interquartile Range (IQR), Median Absolute Deviation (MAD), Isolation Forest, Gaussian Mixture, and k-means often fail to capture the temporal dependencies inherent in EHA signals, resulting in limited detection accuracy and elevated false-alarm rates. Furthermore, systematic evaluations of data-driven anomaly detection approaches for EHA systems remain scarce, particularly under varying operational conditions. This study presents an offline anomaly-detection framework for univariate EHA sensor signals, focusing on temperature and pressure data collected from a controlled test bench. The method employs a reconstruction-based Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) autoencoder, calibrated and evaluated using validation-set reconstruction-error distributions. Performance is assessed across multiple fault-injection scenarios using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, complemented by sensitivity analyses under varying operating conditions. The LSTM autoencoder achieved an average accuracy of 99.0\%, precision up to 100\%, recall between 90.2\% and 99.6\%, and F1-scores from 93.1\% to 99.8\%, demonstrating high detection sensitivity and a very low false-alarm rate across all evaluated sensors. These results highlight the feasibility of data-driven offline anomaly detection for EHAs. Future work will focus on adapting the developed framework for an online (real-time) environment.

</details>


### [282] [Agentic Monte Carlo: Simulating Reinforcement Learning for Black-Box Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05296)
*Dae Yon Hwang,Raunaq Suri,Valentin Villecroze,Anthony L. Caterini,Jesse C. Cresswell,Noël Vouitsis,Brendan Leigh Ross*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Agentic Monte Carlo (AMC) optimizes black-box LLM agents at test time by sampling from a posterior over trajectories, using the fixed LLM as prior and Sequential Monte Carlo to steer policy via a learned value function, achieving RL-like improvements without changing the LLM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome API-only access limitations that block parameter-level RL; exploit RL-Bayesian equivalence to optimize behavior without retraining the LLM.

Method: Define a trajectory posterior conditioned on rewards; prior is the fixed black-box LLM; use Sequential Monte Carlo (particles) to sample trajectories; learn a value function to guide sampling; do not modify the LLM parameters; compare with prompting baselines and GRPO.

Result: Three AgentGym environments; AMC outperforms prompting baselines and even GRPO when test-time compute scales; demonstrates feasibility of principled RL-style optimization for black-box LLM agents.

Conclusion: AMC enables principled, test-time RL-like optimization for black-box LLM agents without retraining the model; competitive performance with scalable compute; code available.

Abstract: LLM agents operate in two distinct regimes: open-weight agents amenable to reinforcement learning (RL) and black-box agents whose behaviour must be controlled purely at test time. Although black-box agents are often backed by state-of-the-art proprietary LLMs, API-only access precludes parameter-level optimization, rendering most RL methods inapplicable. To address this limitation, we turn to a known equivalence between RL and Bayesian inference. We propose Agentic Monte Carlo (AMC) to directly sample from the optimal policy of a black-box agent rather than training it through RL. The optimal policy is a posterior over trajectories whose prior we define as the fixed black-box LLM agent. We employ Sequential Monte Carlo to sample from this posterior by learning a value function to steer the agent while leaving the underlying black-box model unchanged. We validate AMC on three diverse environments from the AgentGym benchmark, demonstrating significant improvements over prompting baselines and even outperforming Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) as we scale the test-time compute of our method. AMC demonstrates the feasibility of performing principled RL-style optimization of black-box LLM agents. Code is available at https://github.com/layer6ai-labs/Agentic-Monte-Carlo

</details>


### [283] [Statistically Reliable LLM-Based Ranking Evaluation via Prediction-Powered Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05308)
*Abhishek Divekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends Prediction-Powered Inference (PPI) to bias-corrected, unbiased estimates of ranking metrics by fusing a small human-labeled set with a large LLM-judged set. Introduces a reduction from O(2^|C|) to O(2^K) to handle hierarchical metrics like Precision@K. Demonstrates improved estimation on ESCI (21% SE reduction) and practical impact in production (best of three variants identified, +407 bps daily sales).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable unbiased, bias-robust estimation of ranking metrics when using LLM judgments, while reducing annotation burden and computational cost. Address the mismatch between per-document annotations and per-query metrics; improve practical decision-making via accurate, efficient estimation.

Method: Extend Prediction-Powered Inference (PPI) to ranking metrics; prove unbiasedness regardless of LLM error profiles; adapt to hierarchical metrics by reducing output-space complexity from 2^|C| to 2^K; validate on ESCI benchmark with a small human-labeled set plus LLM judgments; demonstrate production-level effectiveness via A/B testing and domain annotation.

Result: PPI remains provably unbiased under any LLM error profile. On ESCI, augmenting 30 human annotations with Claude 3 Sonnet judgments reduces the standard error of Precision@4 from 4.45 to 3.50 (21% relative). In production, the framework correctly identified the best of three system variants using 100 human labels and 2 hours of domain-expert annotation; A/B testing confirmed the ranking with +407 bps in daily sales.

Conclusion: The framework delivers bias-corrected, efficient ranking-metric estimates by integrating limited human labels with large LLM judgments, with theoretical unbiasedness, computational efficiency for hierarchical metrics, and demonstrated practical impact in benchmark and production settings.

Abstract: With PRECISE, we extended Prediction-Powered Inference to produce bias-corrected estimates of ranking evaluation metrics by combining a small human-labeled set with a large LLM-judged set. PPI is provably unbiased regardless of the LLM judge's error profile. We make it applicable to hierarchical metrics like Precision@K, where annotations are per-document but the metric is per-query, by reducing the output-space computation from O(2^|C|) to O(2^K). On the ESCI benchmark, augmenting 30 human annotations with Claude 3 Sonnet judgments reduces the standard error of Precision@4 estimates from 4.45 to 3.50 (a 21% relative reduction). In a production system, our framework correctly identified the best of three system variants from 100 human labels and 2 hours of domain-expert annotation; A/B testing confirmed this ranking with +407 bps in daily sales.

</details>


### [284] [Multimarginal flow matching with optimal transport potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05327)
*Raghav Kansal,David Crair,Nghia Nguyen,Scott Pope,Bradley Parry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multimarginal flow matching via OT potentials (OTP-FM): a simulation-free, efficient extension of flow matching that leverages intermediate marginals and dynamic OT to constrain dynamic transport maps across sequential distributions; achieves state-of-the-art results with flexible spatiotemporal dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incorporating intermediate observed marginals in dynamic transport helps constrain flows between endpoints, enabling more accurate and interpretable models of temporal evolution in systems that sample sequential distributions.

Method: Extend conditional Flow Matching (FM) by introducing potential terms from dynamic Optimal Transport (OT) into the FM objective, creating an OTP-FM framework. This yields a simulation-free, efficient algorithm for multimarginal FM that softly steers flows toward intermediate marginals.

Result: OTP-FM achieves state-of-the-art performance and training efficiency on diverse datasets (single-cell RNA sequencing, oceanographic, and meteorological data).

Conclusion: By marrying FM with OT potentials, OTP-FM enables flexible multimarginal flow learning with improved accuracy and efficiency, extending FM to leverage intermediate marginals in dynamic transport.

Abstract: Flow matching (FM) has emerged as a powerful framework for learning dynamic transport maps between two empirical distributions. However, less explored is the setting with intermediate observed marginals that can help constrain the flows between the endpoints. This "multimarginal" regime is central to modeling temporal evolution in dynamical systems in many scientific domains that can sample sequential distributions. We tackle this problem with a novel approach that leverages the connection between FM and dynamic optimal transport (OT), softly steering the flow towards the intermediate marginals through potential terms in the dynamic OT action. By extending the conditional FM learning target to incorporate these potentials, we derive an efficient, simulation-free algorithm for multimarginal FM that offers considerable flexibility in the spatiotemporal dynamics of the learned flows. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and training efficiency of OT-potential FM (OTP-FM) on diverse single-cell RNA sequencing, oceanographic, and meteorological datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/Bexorg-Inc/OTP-FM.

</details>


### [285] [A prism hierarchy of learning regimes in large linear autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05335)
*Eugene Golikov,Yaroslav Gusev,Dmitry Yarotsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Five extreme learning regimes for large weight-tied linear autoencoders are structurally organized as faces of a triangular prism; explicit gradient-flow loss evolutions are derived for four regimes and align well with experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a systematic, geometric taxonomy of all qualitatively different extreme learning regimes for a nonconvex, weight-tied autoencoder, beyond scattered limiting-case analyses.

Method: Develop a loss-expansion hierarchy and map regimes to the two-dimensional faces (2-faces) of a triangular prism; analyze gradient-flow dynamics; derive closed-form or explicit expressions for train and population loss evolutions in regimes 1–4; validate with experiments.

Result: Identification of five basic regimes (large-data, small-data, mean-field, narrow-latent, free); explicit expressions for training and population loss evolution in regimes 1–4; strong empirical agreement.

Conclusion: The work offers a comprehensive, geometry-inspired framework capturing the full spectrum of gradient-flow dynamics for large weight-tied linear autoencoders, enabling precise predictions and guiding understanding of how data, initialization, and latent dimensionality interact.

Abstract: Theoretical studies of machine learning models commonly consider different limiting regimes in which the learning dynamics of gradient descent becomes theoretically tractable. It is, however, desirable to have a systematically obtained picture of all qualitatively different extreme learning regimes for a particular type of models. In this paper we propose such a picture for large weight-tied linear autoencoders characterized by input and latent dimensions, initialization magnitude, and training set size. This model is nonlinear in the weights and its gradient flow does not have a general theoretical solution. We show that at the level of the formal loss-expansion hierarchy, its extreme regimes are naturally associated with faces of a triangular prism. In particular, there are five basic extreme regimes associated with the 2-faces of the prism: (1) large-data, (2) small-data, (3) mean-field, (4) narrow-latent, and (5) free. For regimes (1,2,3,4), we derive explicit expressions for both train and population limiting loss evolutions under gradient flow, obtaining very good agreement with experimental results.

</details>


### [286] [PJ-RoPE: A Fourier-Jet-Affine Position Space for Relative Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05345)
*Yaobo Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PJ-RoPE is a unified Fourier-Jet-Affine relative-position formulation that subsumes RoPE, Jordan-RoPE, and ALiBi; it provides sector-aware analysis, a finite-difference-jet algebra, and experimental evidence of stability and boundary behavior across language and music-token tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify disparate relative-position encodings into a single learnable space, understand which regions tasks select, and analyze trade-offs between affine/recency and high-order jets.

Method: Define PJ-RoPE as a Fourier-Jet-Affine formulation; interpret algebraically as a finite constant-coefficient difference module; derive Poincare-type affine completion; separate scalar PJ-bias kernels from PJ-rotary transforms; introduce adaptive sector diagnostics; use LC/rapidity coordinates; employ controlled probes to test sector containment/selection; conduct experiments on small language tasks and music-token streams to probe affine/recency boundaries and high-order corrections.

Result: Demonstrates sector containment and selection via controlled probes; small-language runs reveal an affine/recency boundary; music-token streams show LC/affine variants remain strong while carrying measurable high-order corrections; LC diagnostics indicate a scale-stability gain coupled with phase-resolution loss.

Conclusion: PJ-RoPE provides a unified, flexible relative-position space that generalizes existing schemes, with adaptive sector diagnostics and a stability-aware behavior across tasks. It clarifies how sector regions are exploited by different tasks and reveals trade-offs between scale stability and phase resolution, informing design of future relative-position encodings.

Abstract: We unify RoPE's Fourier phase, Jordan-RoPE's finite jets, and ALiBi's affine recency into a single learnable relative-position space, and study which regions of this space are selected by different tasks. PJ-RoPE is a Fourier-Jet-Affine formulation for relative attention, with an optional Poincare-type reading as the affine completion of a homogeneous Fourier-jet positional representation. Algebraically, the same primitives form a finite constant-coefficient difference module: simple roots of the lag-shift operator give Fourier/RoPE characters, repeated nonzero roots give Jordan/Fourier jets, and the repeated unit root gives ALiBi-like affine recency.
  The framework separates scalar PJ-bias kernels from exact PJ-rotary feature transforms, introduces adaptive sector diagnostics, and uses LC/rapidity coordinates to stabilize high-order jets. Controlled probes verify sector containment and selection; small language runs expose an affine/recency boundary; music-token streams provide the clearest case where LC/affine variants remain strong while carrying measurable high-order corrections; and LC diagnostics show a scale-stability gain coupled to phase-resolution loss.

</details>


### [287] [Mamba-Assisted Non-Markovian Closure for Reduced-Order Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05371)
*Zhi-Feng Wei,Saad Qadeer,Panos Stinis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAC uses a Mamba-based sequence model to learn a non-Markovian closure for ROMs, coupling it to reduced dynamics; it achieves improved long-time accuracy and stability with efficient per-step inference on Burgers' equation and Lorenz-96.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-Markovian closure terms impede ROM predictive skill. The Mori–Zwanzig memory functional suggests closures depend on the resolved trajectory. Recasting closure modeling as sequence forecasting enables data-driven, scalable memory representations; the dual state-space (sequence-to-sequence during training, autoregressive rollout during deployment) enables efficient long trajectories.

Method: Train a Mamba-based sequence model to predict the closure from the resolved trajectory (memory term) within a reduced-order system. Use seq-to-seq convolutional training and deploy in step-by-step autoregressive rollout via a recurrent form, coupled with a ROM integrator. Evaluate on viscous Burgers' equation and Lorenz 96 to demonstrate memory-closure performance.

Result: MAC substantially outperforms a Markovian ROM, a GRU-based sequence model, and Wilks’ method in both predictive accuracy and long-time rollout stability across tested systems.

Conclusion: A Mori–Zwanzig–inspired, Mamba-based memory-closure learned as a sequence model yields accurate, stable long-time ROM predictions with efficient inference, demonstrating strong potential for non-Markovian closures in complex dynamical systems.

Abstract: Reduced-order modeling of high-dimensional dynamical systems is often hindered by the non-Markovian closure term that represents the effect of unresolved variables on the resolved dynamics. Inspired by the Mori--Zwanzig formalism, in which the closure takes the form of a memory functional of the resolved trajectory, we recast closure modeling as a sequence modeling problem and propose the Mamba-Assisted Closure (MAC) framework: a Mamba-based sequence model, trained to predict the closure from the resolved trajectory, is coupled with the reduced-order governing equations through a numerical integrator to advance the resolved variables in time. A key feature of the framework is its exploitation of the dual representation of state-space models -- the model is trained in a sequence-to-sequence fashion via the convolutional form, and deployed for step-by-step autoregressive rollout via the recurrent form, yielding both efficient long-trajectory training and constant per-step inference cost. On the viscous Burgers' equation and the chaotic two-scale Lorenz '96 system, the MAC model substantially outperforms the Markovian reduced-order model, the GRU-based sequence model, and the Wilks method in predictive accuracy and long-time rollout stability.

</details>


### [288] [Evidence-Guided Neural Architecture Selection under Uncertainty for Subject-Specific Blood Glucose Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05373)
*Md Azharul Islam,Dwyer Deighan,Tarunraj Singha,Danial Faghihi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Bayesian, evidence-based neural architecture search framework (EVIDENT) for time-series forecasting under data scarcity and heterogeneity, selecting the smallest architecture that meets a validation criterion and using plausible ensembles when multiple models are competitive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In time-series forecasting with limited, noisy, and heterogeneous data, standard neural-architecture design/validation often yields unreliable predictions. There is a need to identify low-capacity architectures that generalize well and to quantify uncertainty during selection.

Method: Explore a candidate architecture pool using Bayesian training and evidence-based ranking under uncertainty. Identify the lowest-capacity model that satisfies a prescribed validation criterion. If multiple architectures are competitive, form plausibility-weighted ensembles. Demonstrate with temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) for individualized blood glucose forecasting in type 1 diabetes patients. Compare against random-search baselines.

Result: EVIDENT tends to reject both under- and over-parameterized TCNs on population-level diabetes data and identifies models that generalize to unseen patients. When architectures are competitive, plausibility-weighted ensembles improve predictive performance. Relative to random search, EVIDENT finds smaller architectures with more consistent forecasting on unseen patients.

Conclusion: EVIDENT provides a strategy for neural architecture discovery in data-limited, heterogeneous settings, enabling reliable model selection for high-consequence forecasting and enabling ensemble predictions when multiple architectures are plausible.

Abstract: Reliable neural architecture selection is an open challenge in time-series forecasting under limited, noisy, and heterogeneous data, where standard heuristic architecture design and validation approaches fail to ensure accurate and reliable prediction and generalization. We propose EVIDENT (EVidence-based IDEntification of Neural archiTectures), a framework for architecture selection that integrates Bayesian training, evidence-based ranking, and task-specific validation under uncertainty. The framework explores the candidate architecture pool and identifies the lowest-capacity model that satisfies a prescribed validation criterion. We demonstrate this method using temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) for individualized blood glucose forecasting in type 1 diabetes patients. The results show that EVIDENT systematically rejects both under- and over-parameterized TCN architectures on population-level diabetes data, while identifying models that generalize reliably to unseen patients. When multiple architectures are competitive, the framework further supports plausibility-weighted ensemble predictions that enhance predictive performance. Compared with a random-search baseline, EVIDENT identified smaller architectures with more consistent forecasting performance on unseen patients. These findings establish EVIDENT as a strategy to neural architecture discovery, enabling reliable model selection for high-consequence forecasting in data-limited and heterogeneous settings.

</details>


### [289] [SHALA-LLM: Smartly Handling Ambiguous Labels in Aligning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05376)
*Jingyao Wu,Ashley Wang,Keane Ong,Paul Pu Liang,Rosalind Picard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHALA-LLM introduces a reinforcement learning framework to learn from annotator distributions, prioritizing ambiguous samples, to better align LLMs with human judgments in NLI and ER, achieving improved distributional agreement and classification performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Label ambiguity and annotator disagreement are intrinsic to tasks like NLI and emotion recognition; treating disagreement as informative rather than noise can improve model alignment and robustness. Existing approaches typically optimize for a single label, neglecting the distribution of human judgments.

Method: A reinforcement learning algorithm (SHALA-LLM) that learns from annotator distributions and dynamically prioritizes highly ambiguous samples during optimization, enabling the LLM to reflect the full distribution of human judgments in its outputs.

Result: Empirical evaluation on ChaosNLI, GoEmotions, MSP-Podcast shows SHALA-LLM reduces Jensen–Shannon distance to annotator distributions by as much as 62.1% and improves F1 by up to 16.7%, indicating better agreement with human judgments and stronger classification performance.

Conclusion: Modeling annotator disagreement as informative can enhance both distributional alignment and task performance; SHALA-LLM demonstrates a viable RL-based approach to incorporate label uncertainty in LLM alignment.

Abstract: Many human-centered tasks, including natural language inference (NLI) and emotion recognition (ER), have multiple plausible interpretations, leading to label ambiguity and challenging disagreements across human annotators. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in real-world settings, faithfully modeling such ambiguity is essential to identify contested inputs, preserve variability in ambiguous cases, and capture the full distribution of human judgments. Yet, existing LLM alignment approaches have predominantly assumed a single correct label, excluding annotator disagreement during optimization. Instead of treating this ambiguity as noise, we show how to treat it as information that improves model behavior through a new algorithm called SMARTLY HANDLING AMBIGUOUS LABELS IN ALIGNING LLMS (SHALA-LLM). This reinforcement learning framework provides a new way for LLMs to learn directly from annotator distributions while dynamically prioritizing highly ambiguous samples during optimization. Experiments on ambiguity-sensitive NLI and ER benchmarks, including ChaosNLI, GoEmotions, and MSP-Podcast, demonstrate that SHALA-LLM improves agreement with annotator label distributions, e.g. on ChaosNLI, it reduces Jensen-Shannon Distance by up to 62.1%. At the same time, SHALA-LLM improves F1 by up to 16.7%, showing that modeling annotator disagreement can also strengthen classification performance.

</details>


### [290] [Pattern Selectivity is Not Task-Causal Structure: A Cross-Architecture Mechanistic Study of Composed-Task Circuits in 1B-Class Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05378)
*Yongzhong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A single screen-and-ablate protocol yields non-reproducible primary causal screens across model families for the same tasks, highlighting model- and pattern-specific circuitry rather than universal ones; introduces a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy, and a falsifiable MoE substrate hypothesis with predictions for other MoE LMs; methodology remains useful but requires per-model causal verification for robust mechanistic claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether a universal, single mechanistic probe can reliably identify attention-head circuits across diverse model families and pipelines, and evaluate the portability and falsifiability of such mechanistic claims in composed tasks and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models.

Method: Apply a single screen-and-ablate recipe: identify attention-head circuits via task-pattern selectivity, then verify causally against a matched-random null. Port across three model families (1B-class) and four tasks, with ten seeds per cell, yielding 12 task–model cells. Introduce a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy with quantitative thresholds, combine with spectral participation-ratio signal from companion work, and test for cross-model generality and a falsifiable MoE substrate hypothesis.

Result: No two task–model cells share the same primary causal screen at comparable effect sizes; the same task's behavioral capacity is implemented via different attention-pattern types across models. All five screen-outcome categories appear in the data. For OLMoE 1B-7B, the prev-token positional substrate is the strongest ablation in 3 of 4 tasks, with the IOI exception aligning with a distinct pattern. The authors offer falsifiable predictions for other MoE LMs.

Conclusion: A single-screen approach yields highly task- and model-specific causal mappings rather than universal circuits; while the methodology and taxonomy enhance falsifiability, cross-model portability of mechanistic claims is limited and requires explicit per-model causal verification. The MoE substrate hypothesis provides testable predictions for future work.

Abstract: We test whether a single screen-and-ablate recipe -- identify attention-head circuits by task-pattern selectivity, then verify by causal ablation against a matched-random null -- produces consistent mechanistic claims across model families. The recipe ports across pipelines; the specific circuit it identifies does not. Across four composed tasks (indirect-object identification, greater-than, successor sequences, variable binding) and three 1B-class language models from distinct training pipelines (Pythia 1B / Pile / dense; OLMo 1B / DCLM / dense; OLMoE 1B-7B / DCLM / mixture-of-experts), we run a unified protocol with the matched-random null sampled across ten seeds per cell. The resulting 12 (task, model) cells contain no two that share the same primary causal screen at comparable effect size: the same task, with the same behavioral capability, is implemented through different attention-pattern types across models.
  We introduce a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy -- primary cause, secondary cause, correlate, interferer, null -- with quantitative thresholds, and show that all five outcomes appear in the panel. We propose a falsifiable hypothesis: the MoE model in our panel builds composed-task circuits on top of a foundational previous-token positional substrate (the prev-token-circuit ablation is the strongest causal screen on 3 of 4 tasks for OLMoE 1B-7B), with the IOI exception consistent with IOI being a final-position name-copying task whose structure directly probes a different pattern. The hypothesis comes with explicit predictions for other MoE language models.
  We frame the methodology honestly: the spectral participation-ratio signal from the companion methodology paper is a general indicator of specialized computation; what makes a finding task-specific is the task-pattern screen plus a per-model causal verification.

</details>


### [291] [Generalized TV--$\ell_p$ Structured Priors for Bayesian $T_1$ Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05381)
*Disi Lin,Martin Berggren,Tommy Löfstedt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a TV-ℓp prior for Bayesian T1 mapping, proving propriety and yielding improved spatial coherence and uncertainty quantification; outperforms ML and various Bayesian priors on synthetic and in-vivo datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enforce spatial consistency and quantify uncertainty in T1 mapping by using a structured prior that combines total variation with ℓp norms.

Method: Develop an extended family of TV-ℓp priors, prove they form a proper prior distribution, embed them in a Bayesian regression model, and perform posterior inference with No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS). Compare against ML and Bayesian priors (uniform, Gamma, bounded TV) on synthetic brain/cardic T1 maps and real breast T1 data.

Result: TV-ℓp priors produce more concentrated posterior densities (reduced uncertainty), lower variance, and smaller negative bias, yielding more reliable T1 estimates and better spatial coherence.

Conclusion: The TV-ℓp prior provides a robust Bayesian framework for T1 mapping with quantified uncertainty, improving spatial coherence and reliability of the estimated maps.

Abstract: We propose an extended family of structured spatial priors that incorporates the total variation (TV) function with $\ell_p$ norms. The prior is proven to be proper and incorporated into a Bayesian regression framework to enable uncertainty quantification in $T_1$ mapping, with posterior inference performed using the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS). This TV--$\ell_p$ construction is proven to constitute a well-defined family of prior distributions, and it naturally enforces spatial consistency and smooth variations in the estimated parameter maps. The method was evaluated in comparison to maximum-likelihood estimation and several Bayesian alternative priors based on the uniform, Gamma, and bounded TV priors. The evaluation includes experiments on synthetic brain and cardiac $T_1$ mapping datasets, as well as a real in-vivo breast $T_1$ mapping dataset. The results show that the TV--$\ell_p$ prior yields more concentrated posterior densities, indicating reduced uncertainty. It also consistently achieves lower variance and smaller (negative) bias, leading to more reliable estimates. Overall, embedding a TV-based structured penalty along with $\ell_p$ norms in a prior in a Bayesian model improves spatial coherence in $T_1$ maps and enhances uncertainty quantification, offering a robust approach for $T_1$ mapping with uncertainties.

</details>


### [292] [Trust, but Don't Verify: Epistemic Blind Spots in LLM Source Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05403)
*Rohan N. Pradhan,Steve Goley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LMs can detect fabricated statistics in isolation but fail to recruit this check during multi-source synthesis, relying on a methodology-register cue rather than actual numeric validity; this leads to epistemic alignment where deployment trust is based on perceived analytic credibility, not factual correctness. Cross-domain, mechanistic analyses show a transferable methodology representation drives synthesis, while numeric validity cues are suppressed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether language models evaluate evidence quality or merely aggregate signals when synthesizing information from multiple sources, and to identify the underlying mechanisms and limitations of such evaluation in multi-source reasoning.

Method: Empirical evaluation across five models from three families (Claude, Qwen, OLMo) and three professional domains. Tests include: detection of fabricated statistics in isolation (methodology statistics), assessment within multi-source synthesis, analysis of source influence via a methodology-register gate, mechanistic analyses (causal tracing, linear probes, component-level attribution), and prompting-based mitigation attempts (including an oracle checklist).

Result: Models show high accuracy in identifying fabricated methodology statistics in isolation (0.76–1.00) but do not apply this discrimination during multi-source synthesis; numeric validity signals are not used and statistically impossible confidence intervals receive similar weight as valid ones. A methodology-register gate influenced by the distributional text rather than numeric truth governs source influence. Probes indicate a transferable methodology representation (AUC 0.83–0.92) that supports cross-domain transfer, while numeric-validity cues decodable in isolation are suppressed during synthesis. Prompting mitigations yield blanket skepticism rather than selective verification, and training pipelines reinforce reliance on stylistic cues rather than numeric verification.

Conclusion: The core issue is epistemic alignment: deployment of evidence evaluation depends on whether a source appears analytically credible rather than on internal consistency or numeric correctness. This is a failure of deployment rather than capability, with evidence not necessarily being more or less accurate, but being treated as credible based on surface Analytical cues. The phenomenon is robust across models and domains and resists simple mitigation strategies.

Abstract: Language models increasingly act as epistemic proxies, synthesizing evidence from multiple sources to inform decisions. Whether they evaluate the quality of that evidence, or merely aggregate it based on surface presentation, remains poorly understood. We show that models possess the capability to detect fabricated statistics (correct identification rates of 0.76-1.00 for methodology in isolation) but do not recruit this capability during multi-source synthesis, producing similar numeric estimates whether the statistics are fabricated or valid. Specifically, source influence is governed by a methodology-register gate that responds to the distributional register of analytical text but not to numeric validity: for example, statistically impossible confidence intervals receive the same weight as valid ones. The behavioral dissociation replicates across five models from three families (Claude, Qwen, OLMo) and three professional domains. Mechanistic analyses, including causal tracing, linear probes, and component-level attribution, converge on the same account: the model encodes and causally uses a methodology-register representation that transfers across domains (probe AUC 0.83-0.92), while numeric-validity signals, decodable in isolation, are suppressed to chance during multi-source synthesis. Prompting-based mitigations, even an oracle checklist naming the exact statistical checks, produce blanket skepticism rather than selective discernment, and the post-training pipelines we examine reinforce the stylistic shortcut without building numeric verification. Unlike sycophancy, which tracks user preference, this failure tracks whether a source presents as analytically credible, not whether its claims are internally consistent. We term this epistemic alignment: like preference and safety alignment, the question is not capability but deployment.

</details>


### [293] [CausalPOI: Spatio-Temporal Graph-Based Causal Modeling for Cold-Start POI Check-in Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05413)
*Zhaoqi Zhang,Miao Xie,Yi Li,Linyou Cai,Siqiang Luo,Gao Cong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We propose CausalPOI for cold-start POI check-in forecasting using a spatio-temporal functional interaction graph and aligned treatment-control graphs to capture causal effects, achieving state-of-the-art results on SafeGraph datasets; code available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing spatio-temporal POI forecasting relies on proximity-based graphs and correlation, neglecting functional dependencies among POIs and the causal impact of urban interventions; cold-start POIs lack historical data, hindering accurate forecasting and intervention analysis.

Method: Introduce CausalPOI, a spatio-temporal graph-based causal representation learning framework. It uses a Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph to model semantic and spatial relationships between POIs and constructs structurally aligned treatment and control graphs to simulate factual and counterfactual scenarios for causal estimation and forecasting.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real-world SafeGraph datasets shows CausalPOI significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in spatio-temporal forecasting, semantic interaction modeling, and causal effect estimation, with improved interpretability for urban intervention analysis.

Conclusion: CausalPOI provides an interpretable and actionable foundation for urban intervention analysis and data-driven urban planning decisions; source code is available on GitHub.

Abstract: As urban environments continue to evolve rapidly, accurately modeling the dynamic behaviour of Points of Interest is essential for supporting data-driven urban planning and commercial decision-making. While recent advancements in spatio-temporal graph learning have improved POI forecasting, most methods rely on proximity-based graphs and correlation-driven modeling, which overlook the functional dependencies between POIs and fail to capture the causal effects of urban interventions. In this paper, we introduce a novel research problem -- cold-start POI check-in forecasting, which aims to predict the future check-in pattern of a newly introduced POI, by modeling its temporal evolution and functional interactions with nearby POIs in a structured urban spatial context. To address these challenges, we propose CausalPOI, a spatio-temporal graph-based causal representation learning framework. CausalPOI leverages Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph to model semantic and spatial relationships between POIs, and constructs structurally aligned treatment and control graphs to simulate factual and counterfactual scenarios. Extensive experiments on real-world SafeGraph datasets demonstrate that CausalPOI significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across the board, validating its effectiveness in spatio-temporal forecasting, semantic interaction modeling, and causal effect estimation, providing a more interpretable and actionable foundation for urban intervention analysis. Source code is available at Github.

</details>


### [294] [Selective-Advantage Entropy-Adaptive Horizon GRPO: Asymmetric Token-Level Discounting for Efficient Reinforcement Learning of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05434)
*Chirag Chawla,Rohan Charudatt Salvi,Madhav S. Baidya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive-Horizon and Selective-Advantage AH-GRPO improve stability and accuracy on GSM8K reasoning, outperforming baseline GRPO, with notable gains at multiple model scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO's symmetric token-level treatment and rollout sampling can cause inefficiencies under model uncertainty and entropy collapse. The authors propose asymmetric, horizon-adaptive discounting to reduce the effective horizon when uncertain and selective discounting to preserve positive-advantage signals, aiming for stable, verifiable rewards on structured generation tasks.

Method: Introduce Adaptive-Horizon GRPO (AH-GRPO) with an entropy-based cumulative discount alpha that shortens the horizon under uncertainty. Introduce Selective-Advantage AH-GRPO (SA-AH-GRPO) that applies discounting only to negative-advantage rollouts. Evaluate GRPO (alpha=0), AH-GRPO (alpha=0.5), and SA-AH-GRPO (alpha=0.5) on GSM8K using Qwen 2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen 2.5-3B-Instruct with LoRA fine-tuning.

Result: On the 3B model, SA-AH-GRPO peaks at Pass@1=0.858 (step 30) and maintains 0.846 at 180 steps, with training variance 0.0246 (3.6x lower than GRPO) while matching peak accuracy. On the 1.5B model, SA-AH-GRPO peaks at Pass@1=0.686, above zero-shot baseline 0.637.

Conclusion: Asymmetric discounting preserves the gradient signal on correct solutions, prevents entropy collapse, and stabilizes training. The results suggest a principled inductive bias for RL with verifiable rewards on structured generation tasks.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) has emerged as an effective reinforcement-learning algorithm for aligning language models on reasoning tasks, but it treats every token position and every sampled rollout symmetrically. We introduce two complementary extensions: (i) Adaptive-Horizon GRPO (AH-GRPO), which weights each token's policy gradient using a cumulative entropy-based discount that reduces the effective horizon when the model is uncertain, and (ii) Selective-Advantage AH-GRPO (SA-AH-GRPO), which applies this discounting only to negative-advantage rollouts, leaving positive-advantage, successful trajectories unattenuated. We evaluate standard GRPO with alpha = 0, AH-GRPO with alpha = 0.5, and SA-AH-GRPO with alpha = 0.5 on the GSM8K mathematical reasoning benchmark using both Qwen 2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen 2.5-3B-Instruct fine-tuned with LoRA. On the 3B model, SA-AH-GRPO achieves Pass@1 = 0.858 at its peak at step 30 and maintains 0.846 at 180 steps, with training variance reduced to 0.0246, a 3.6 times reduction relative to GRPO while matching its peak accuracy. On the 1.5B model, SA-AH-GRPO achieves a peak Pass@1 of 0.686, improving over the zero-shot baseline of 0.637. Our analysis shows that asymmetric discounting preserves the full gradient signal on correct solutions, prevents entropy collapse, and substantially stabilises training, suggesting a principled inductive bias for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards on structured generation tasks.

</details>


### [295] [DP-MacAdam: Differentially Private Mechanism with Adaptive Clipping and Adaptive Momentum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05435)
*Naima Tasnim,Lalitha Sankar,Oliver Kosut*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DP-MacAdam unifies adaptive clipping and adaptive momentum in DP training, achieving unbiased gradient variance estimates and improved utility without manual clipping tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle practical limitations of fixed gradient clipping in DP-SGD by enabling adaptive clipping while also leveraging gradient mean/variance for momentum to accelerate training.

Method: Integrates AdaClip-style adaptive clipping (shift/scale based on empirical mean/variance) with DP-Adam-like momentum updates that also use the same mean/variance estimates; provides bias-free estimation of gradient variances.

Result: Theoretical analysis demonstrates unbiased gradient variance estimation; empirical tests show DP-MacAdam improves model utility compared to DP-SGD, AdaClip, and DP-Adam baselines, without needing manual clipping threshold tuning.

Conclusion: DP-MacAdam offers improved privacy-utility trade-offs and reduces hyperparameter tuning burden; it demonstrates the viability of combining adaptive clipping with adaptive momentum using shared estimates, suggesting further exploration across datasets and privacy budgets.

Abstract: Differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) has become the standard framework for privacy-preserving machine learning, yet its reliance on a fixed gradient clipping threshold to limit sensitivity remains a significant practical limitation. Adaptive clipping algorithms such as AdaClip shift and scale the gradient prior to clipping and adding noise so that the clipped gradient yields a more informative descent direction. The shift and scaling parameters are selected adaptively based on the empirical mean and variance. However, in existing adaptive clipping algorithms, these empirical estimates have not been also used for momentum to accelerate training itself. On the other hand, DP-Adam is an algorithm that exploits Adam-like momentum updates based on the gradient mean and variance to accelerate training, but does not exploit these estimates for adaptive clipping. In this work, we propose Differentially Private Mechanism with Adaptive Clipping and Adaptive Momentum (DP-MacAdam), a novel algorithm that combines these two approaches so as to use the same mean and variance estimates for both clipping and momentum. We perform an analysis showing that DP-MacAdam estimates the gradient variances in a bias-free manner. In addition, we empirically evaluate the privacy and accuracy of DP-MacAdam, demonstrating that it achieves improved model utility compared to DP-SGD, AdaClip, and DP-Adam baselines, without requiring manual tuning of the clipping threshold.

</details>


### [296] [Sharp First-Order Lower Bounds for Higher-Order Smooth Nonconvex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05438)
*Dongruo Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proves dimension-free first-order lower bounds for ε-stationary point finding in smooth nonconvex optimization with higher-order smoothness, matching known accelerated upper bounds: Ω(ε^{-7/4}) for Hessian-Lipschitz and Ω(ε^{-5/3}) for third-derivative-Lipschitz. Introduces a block-chain hard instance enabling blockwise oracle revelation while preserving smoothness; the bound holds for any finite smoothness order.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Close the gap between established accelerated first-order upper bounds under higher-order smoothness and the lack of matching lower bounds, providing dimension-free lower bounds valid for all finite smoothness orders to guide oracle complexity in nonconvex optimization.

Method: Construct a novel hard instance based on a block-chain mechanism that enforces blockwise oracle revelation while maintaining the required higher-order smoothness. The design reduces to a scalar hard instance and yields a dimension-free lower bound; the proof covers both Hessian-Lipschitz (Ω(ε^{-7/4})) and third-derivative-Lipschitz (Ω(ε^{-5/3})) regimes.

Result: Established matching lower bounds: Ω(ε^{-7/4}) in the Hessian-Lipschitz regime and Ω(ε^{-5/3}) in the third-order-smooth regime, valid for deterministic first-order oracle complexity; the lower bound is dimension-free and holds for every finite smoothness order.

Conclusion: Resolves the long-standing gap by providing dimension-free lower bounds that match known upper bounds for higher-order smooth nonconvex optimization; introduces a block-chain construction for the hard instance; AI-assisted development acknowledged and verified by authors.

Abstract: We study the deterministic first-order oracle complexity of finding \(ε\)-stationary points in smooth nonconvex optimization when the objective satisfies higher-order smoothness assumptions. While the classical \(ε^{-2}\) rate is optimal under only Lipschitz gradients, higher-order smoothness leads to accelerated first-order upper bounds, most notably the \(ε^{-7/4}\) rate under Lipschitz Hessians and the \(ε^{-5/3}\) rate under Lipschitz third derivatives. The matching lower bounds, however, have remained open. We resolve this gap by proving a new dimension-free first-order lower bound for higher-order smooth nonconvex functions, valid for every finite smoothness order. In particular, our construction gives a matching \(Ω(ε^{-7/4})\) lower bound in the Hessian-Lipschitz case and a matching \(Ω(ε^{-5/3})\) lower bound in the third-order-smooth regime. The hard instance is based on a \emph{block-chain} mechanism that enforces blockwise oracle revelation while preserving the smoothness structure needed for the scalar hard instance. The lower-bound construction was discovered with the assistance of ChatGPT 5.5 Pro and subsequently verified by the authors.

</details>


### [297] [GOTabPFN: From Feature Ordering to Compact Tokenization for Tabular Foundation Models on High-Dimensional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05441)
*Al Zadid Sultan Bin Habib,Md Younus Ahamed,Prashnna Kumar Gyawali,Gianfranco Doretto,Donald A. Adjeroh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces GO-LR and GOTabPFN to enable HDLSS tabular prediction without retraining large backbones, using graph-guided feature ordering with refinement, equivalence to weighted Min Linear Arrangement, and a neuro-inspired subunit compression (NSC) to produce compact meta-features; yields improved stability and accuracy under tight token budgets across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HDLSS tabular prediction faces instability and inefficiency when relying on large pretrained backbones. The work aims to enable effective predictions with small foundation models by reordering and aggregating features to form compact representations without retraining.

Method: Proposes Graph-guided Ordering with Local Refinement (GO-LR), shows its equivalence to weighted Minimum Linear Arrangement and interprets the practical solver as a TSP-path-style surrogate. Builds GOTabPFN on GO-LR and introduces a Neuro-Inspired Subunit Compression (NSC) unit to pool locally adjacent ordered features into meta-features, creating a compact representation suitable for TabPFN-style predictions in HDLSS settings.

Result: Empirical evaluation across tabular benchmarks shows that GOTabPFN improves stability and accuracy under tight token budgets, enabling practical HDLSS TabPFN-style predictions without retraining large backbones.

Conclusion: GO-LR provides a principled feature ordering mechanism closely tied to a classic combinatorial objective, and the NSC-based compression yields a compact representation that preserves predictive power, making TabPFN-like capabilities viable in HDLSS regimes.

Abstract: We investigate how to make small tabular foundation models effective for High-Dimensional, Low-Sample Size (HDLSS) tabular prediction without retraining large backbones. We introduce Graph-guided Ordering with Local Refinement (GO-LR), show its equivalence to weighted Minimum Linear Arrangement, and interpret the practical solver as a TSP-path-style surrogate. We propose GOTabPFN,which builds on GO-LR, and a Neuro-Inspired Subunit Compression (NSC) unit to pool locally adjacent ordered features into meta-features, yielding a compact representation that makes TabPFN-style prediction practical in HDLSS regimes. Across tabular benchmarks, GOTabPFN improves stability and accuracy under tight token budgets.

</details>


### [298] [Towards Unified and Data-Efficient Prognostics and Health Management with Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05481)
*Raffael Theiler,Lev Telyatnikov,Leandro Von Krannichfeldt,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tabular foundation models with in-context learning applied to industrial time series, achieved by converting unit-level signals into tabular rows, provide a data-efficient, versatile framework forPHM tasks that yields strong prognostics and diagnostics performance and preserves temporal context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial PHM data are often fragmented, partially observed, and poorly labeled, hindering supervised learning. A reusable, data-efficient interface is needed to handle heterogeneous PHM tasks across varying data regimes.

Method: Transform raw unit-level signals into tabular rows and apply PFN-based tabular foundation models with in-context learning. Evaluate on prognostics and diagnostics tasks under a common protocol, comparing against sequence models, transformer baselines, and gradient-boosted trees, and analyze data efficiency and subsampling effects.

Result: PFN-based tabular models achieve the best average ranks across prognostic and diagnostic tasks, are competitive in low-data regimes, preserve temporal context within the tabular representation, and show performance that depends on representative context construction under subsampling.

Conclusion: Tabular foundation models offer a practical and general interface for heterogeneous PHM problems, delivering data-efficient, robust prognostics and diagnostics across industrial time series.

Abstract: Data-driven Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) uses time-varying condition-monitoring data to diagnose system states and estimate remaining useful life in engineered assets. These tasks are central to maintenance planning, but industrial PHM data are often fragmented, partially observed, and poorly labeled, which hinders supervised learning. Foundation models offer a route toward reusable predictive systems, yet most time-series foundation models are designed for forecasting and assume long, coherent, regularly sampled sequences. To address this gap, we propose a framework for applying Tabular Foundation Models to industrial time series using in-context learning, and we evaluate them on a variety of PHM tasks. By converting raw unit-level signals into tabular rows, we show that these models perform well across multiple tasks - including prognostics, and diagnostics - and are highly data efficient. We compare them directly with sequence models, transformer baselines, and gradient-boosted trees under a common evaluation protocol. The results indicate that tabular foundation models achieve the best average ranks across prognostic and diagnostic tasks. Our findings further show that PFN-based models are competitive in low-data regimes, that temporal context can be preserved in the tabular representation, and that performance depends on representative context construction under subsampling. These results demonstrate that tabular foundation models provide a practical and general interface for heterogeneous PHM problems.

</details>


### [299] [Learned Subspace Compression for Communication-Efficient Pipeline Parallelism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05484)
*Paul Janson,Edouard Oyallon,Eugene Belilovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAPL introduces a manifold-aware approach to learn orthogonal activation projections per pipeline stage for inter-stage compression, with per-stage anchor embeddings and residual vector quantization, achieving high compression with negligible accuracy loss across LLaMA models (150M–1B) and outperforming fixed-projection baselines like Subspace Networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pipeline-parallel training suffers from inter-stage activation communication bottlenecks on low-bandwidth networks. Fixed orthogonal projections degrade performance and require non-standard adaptations. A learnable, orthogonal, per-stage compression method could better balance communication cost and model accuracy.

Method: Treat inter-stage compression as learning an orthogonal projection constrained to the Stiefel manifold (orthogonal matrices) using manifold-constrained steepest descent. Allow each stage to discover its own optimal compression subspace. Introduce per-stage factorized anchor embeddings to recover signals at stage boundaries. Optionally apply residual vector quantization with a streaming codebook synchronization protocol to amortize codebook communication.

Result: Empirical evaluation across LLaMA models from 150M to 1B parameters shows MAPL is easily applicable to existing pipeline setups and achieves high activation compression with negligible performance degradation, yielding a significantly better performance-vs-compression trade-off than Subspace Networks.

Conclusion: MAPL provides a scalable, stage-specific, orthogonality-constrained compression framework for pipeline parallelism, with efficient reconstruction via anchor embeddings and communication-efficient codebook synchronization, enabling effective training of very large language models on bandwidth-constrained interconnects.

Abstract: Pipeline parallelism enables training of large language models that exceed single-device memory, yet inter-stage activation communication becomes the dominant bottleneck when trained on low-bandwidth networks. Recent work in this area has proposed using fixed orthogonal projections to compress activations. However, this still results in a significant performance degradation and requires a number of non-standard adaptations to constrain the optimization. A natural alternative is to learn a low rank projection for each pipeline stage, however maintaining the necessary orthogonality of these projectors during training remains a challenge. We present Manifold Aware Projection Learning (MAPL), a method that treats inter-stage compression as a learnable orthogonal projection under explicit Stiefel manifold (orthogonal matrices) constraints. Rather than prescribing a fixed global subspace, MAPL lets each pipeline stage discover and continuously adapt its own task-optimal compression subspace via manifold-constrained steepest descent. To recover token-specific signals at stage boundaries, we introduce per-stage factorized anchor embeddings that allow for full-rank activation reconstruction with negligible communication overhead. We further show that we can incorporate residual vector quantization after projection with a streaming codebook synchronization protocol that amortizes dictionary communication. Across LLaMA models from 150M to 1B parameters we show that MAPL can be easily applied to the existing pipeline and can achieve high compression with neglibile performance degradation with a drastically improved tradeoffs in performance vs. compression compared to Subspace Networks.

</details>


### [300] [LEVANTE-bench: Multi-Scale Comparison of VLMs to Children Using Cognitive Tasks (or, "Is Your VLM Smarter Than a 5th Grader?")](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05497)
*Alvin Wei Ming Tan,David Cardinal,Tania Lorido-Botran,Laura Bravo-Sanchez,Sunny Yu,Michael C. Frank*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEVANTE-bench evaluates vision-language models against children's cognition (ages 5-12) using six LEVANTE tasks across three countries; results show partial alignment, with better alignment for higher-capability models at task/item level, but human error distributions vary; smaller models can better match some younger children's errors; top models still struggle on matrix reasoning and mental rotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to provide a cross-cultural, developmentally grounded benchmark for assessing how well vision-language models capture human cognitive development and errors, enabling comparison across ages, tasks, and populations using open-source LEVANTE data.

Method: six tasks from LEVANTE, open-source data on children's cognition across languages/cultures; cross-cultural sample n=1547 children aged 5-12 in three countries; evaluate multiple VLMs across scales: overall accuracy, task- and item-level alignment with children, and trial-level error distributions; compare across model sizes; analyze heterogeneity of alignment.

Result: alignment is heterogeneous; more capable models align better at task/item level; match to children's error distributions varies by task; smaller models sometimes match younger children's errors better; even best VLMs struggle on matrix reasoning and mental rotation; overall VLM architectures only partially align with children's cognitive abilities.

Conclusion: LEVANTE-bench is a useful benchmark for evaluating developmentally grounded alignment of VLMs with child cognition; results indicate limits of current architectures and the need for improved reasoning abilities and cross-cultural evaluation.

Abstract: Given the inherently multimodal nature of human experience, vision-language models (VLMs) hold substantial promise for modeling human cognition as it grows and develops with experience. Realizing their potential requires tools for comparing VLMs with human cognitive development across tasks, ages, and populations. We present LEVANTE-bench, a benchmark based on tasks and data from the Learning Variability Network (LEVANTE), which distributes open-source tasks and data measuring children's cognition across languages and cultures. In LEVANTE-bench, we systematically assess VLMs on six tasks, comparing their alignment with children aged 5-12 ($N$ = 1547) across three countries. We compare models at multiple scales, assessing their overall accuracy, their task- and item-level alignment with children, and how well they match children's trial-level error distributions. Alignment was heterogeneous across scales: at the level of tasks and items, more capable models aligned better with humans. However, match to human error distributions varied widely across tasks, and for several tasks, smaller models matched younger children's errors better. In addition, even the best-performing VLMs struggled on matrix reasoning and mental rotation tasks. Thus, current VLM architectures align only partially with the cognitive abilities of children.

</details>


### [301] [Dominant-Layer ZO: A Single Layer Dominates Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05516)
*Wanhao Yu,Ziyan Wang,Zheng Wang,Abeer Matar Almalky,Yihang Zuo,Shuteng Niu,Sen Lin,Adnan Siraj Rakin,Deliang Fan,Li Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZO fine-tuning is dominated by a single decoding layer across LLMs and tasks; this dominant layer can be identified via activation-outlier analysis before training; updating only this layer matches or surpasses full-model ZO fine-tuning and offers substantial speedups (up to 4.52x) in experiments with LLaMA2-7B and Qwen3-8B across nine benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how adaptation signals distribute in zeroth-order (ZO) fine-tuning of large language models and to improve training efficiency by identifying whether a subset of layers suffices for effective adaptation.

Method: Empirical study across multiple decoder-only LLMs (e.g., LLaMA2-7B, Qwen3-8B). Evaluate ZO fine-tuning on various downstream tasks. Develop an inference-only activation-outlier analysis to identify a dominant decoding layer before training. Compare dominant-layer ZO fine-tuning against full-model MeZO and LoRA-based ZO fine-tuning. Analyze perturbation propagation under ZO optimization to explain the dominance. Report average performance, task coverage across benchmarks, and training speedups.

Result: Found a single dominant decoding layer that consistently yields performance at least as good as full-model ZO fine-tuning across tasks; dominant layer is model-specific but task-agnostic within a model; identified by the first activation-outlier layer in the pre-trained model. Perturbation analysis shows the dominant layer has high sensitivity and early residual placement, enabling effects to propagate through subsequent layers. On LLaMA2-7B and Qwen3-8B across nine benchmarks, dominant-layer ZO fine-tuning improves average performance over full-model MeZO and LoRA-based ZO fine-tuning and achieves up to 4.52x training speedup.

Conclusion: The dominant-layer phenomenon reveals that ZO fine-tuning concentrates optimization signals into one strategically placed layer, driven by perturbation sensitivity and residual geometry. This yields practical gains in training efficiency and performance, suggesting a simple, layer-focused approach for ZO adaptation. Practitioners can leverage pre-training activation-outlier analysis to select the target layer before training.

Abstract: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization enables memory-efficient fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) using only forward passes, but it remains unclear how useful adaptation is distributed across layers. In this work, we reveal a surprising phenomenon: ZO fine-tuning is sharply dominated by a single decoding layer. Across multiple LLM families and downstream tasks, fine-tuning this dominant layer alone consistently matches or even exceeds full-model ZO fine-tuning. We further show that the dominant layer is task-agnostic but model-specific, and can be identified before training through a simple inference-only analysis of activation outliers. Specifically, the dominant layer consistently aligns with the first activation-outlier layer in the pre-trained model. To explain this phenomenon, we analyze how perturbation effects propagate under ZO optimization. We find that the dominant layer combines two key properties: high perturbation sensitivity and early placement in the residual stream, allowing perturbation-induced effects to propagate and accumulate through remaining subsequent decoding layers. As a result, this layer produces disproportionately strong and stable optimization signals under forward-only updates. Extensive experiments on LLaMA2-7B and Qwen3-8B across nine benchmarks show that dominant-layer ZO fine-tuning improves average performance over full-model MeZO and LoRA-based ZO fine-tuning while achieving up to 4.52$\times$ training speedup.

</details>


### [302] [What Objects Enable, Not What They Are: Functional Latent Spaces for Affordance Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05533)
*Rohan Siva,Neel P. Bhatt,Yunhao Yang,Seoyoung Lee,Nishant Gadde,Christian Ellis,Alvaro Velasquez,Zhangyang Wang,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A4D introduces an affordance-centric latent space and a discovery mechanism to enable planning based on task-relevant object functionalities, improving generalization to unseen interactions and achieving fast, data-efficient inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Appearance-based latent representations capture visual similarity but miss functional properties (affordances) needed for planning. This limits generalization to new robot-object interactions; a functional reasoning framework is needed.

Method: A4D maps visual observations into a shared latent space structured around affordances (e.g., movable). Proximity to affordances in this space infers object functionalities. An affordance discovery mechanism expands the latent space to handle unseen scenarios, with uncertainty quantified by proximity to affordances to trigger discovery. Evaluations span planning tasks with diverse and unseen affordances.

Result: A4D achieves 94% inference accuracy on existing affordances, outperforming state-of-the-art by over 15 percentage points. New-affordance inference accuracy rises from 70% to over 90% using less than 10% of the original training data. Inference is reported to be 100x faster.

Conclusion: A4D improves generalization and data efficiency for affordance-based planning by aligning perception with functional reasoning and enabling adaptive expansion of the latent space; it supports rapid inference and better handling of unseen affordances. Code and data are publicly available.

Abstract: Existing robot planning systems rely on appearance-based reasoning, where visual observations are encoded into latent spaces organized around object appearances (e.g., recognizing a "cart" based on how it looks). However, planning requires reasoning about task-relevant functionalities of objects (e.g., whether an object is "movable"), which appearance-based latent spaces do not capture. As a result, existing approaches struggle to generalize to novel robot-object interactions. We address this limited generalizability through affordance reasoning, enabling planning based on task-relevant object functionalities instead of appearance alone. We introduce A4D, which maps visual observations into a shared latent space structured around affordances (e.g., "movable"). By projecting visual observations into this functional latent space and measuring their proximity to affordances, A4D infers functionalities relevant to the observed object. Furthermore, we introduce an affordance discovery mechanism that expands the latent space to handle unseen scenarios where existing affordances are insufficient. A4D uses proximity in the functional latent space to quantify uncertainty in affordance inference and selectively triggers affordance discovery. We evaluate A4D across several planning tasks involving diverse and unseen affordances. A4D achieves 94% inference accuracy on existing affordances outperforming state-of-the-art approaches by over 15% points, improves new-affordance inference accuracy from 70% to over 90% with fewer than 10% of the original training data, and enables 100x faster inference. Code, videos, and data available at: https://A4Dance-reasoning.github.io.

</details>


### [303] [Less is MoE: Trimming Experts in Domain-Specialist Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05538)
*Haoze He,Xinkai Zou,Xuan Jiang,Xingyuan Ding,Ao Qu,Juncheng Billy Li,Heather Miller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fisher-MoE uses Fisher information to identify and prune a tiny subset of intermediate FFN dimensions in Mixture-of-Experts models, enabling 50% MoE compression with minimal performance loss, reducing weight memory ~45% and boosting throughput ~21%. It addresses prior coarse-grained compression failures by targeting dimension-level granularity where capability concentrates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models deliver strong performance but incur a large parameter footprint. Previous compression methods fail on general benchmarks because important capabilities are distributed across many experts but concentrated in specific FFN intermediate dimensions. A fine-grained unit is needed to preserve capabilities during compression.

Method: Within the FFN of MoE layers, compute Fisher information to rank intermediate dimensions by task relevance. Remove (prune) low-importance dimensions, using Fisher importance as the criterion. Compare against activation-, router-score-, and magnitude-based baselines. Evaluate on Qwen1.5-MoE and GSM8K, plus factual-knowledge tasks, to assess impact on general benchmarks.

Result: At a 50% MoE compression ratio, Fisher-MoE preserves model capability, while reducing weight memory by ~45% and increasing inference throughput by ~21%. The study shows that a tiny set of task-critical intermediate dimensions governs key capabilities, and removing only a small fraction (e.g., 12 of 1.35M) can dramatically degrade GSM8K performance, highlighting the precision of Fisher-based ranking.

Conclusion: Intermediate FFN dimension granularity is an effective unit for both compression and capability ranking in MoE models. Fisher information provides a robust criterion to identify these critical dimensions, enabling substantial compression without sacrificing broad capabilities beyond commonsense tasks.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models achieve strong performance through conditional computation, but their large parameter footprint poses deployment challenges. Prior MoE compression approaches catastrophically fail when evaluated on general-purpose benchmarks beyond commonsense reasoning. We trace this failure to the granularity of compression: important capabilities are distributed across experts but concentrated in FFN sparse intermediate dimensions. To identify these dimensions, we use Fisher importance which outperforms activation-, router-score-, and magnitude-based alternatives, and identifies tiny sets of task-critical dimensions: in Qwen1.5-MoE, removing as few as 12 of 1.35M routed-FFN intermediate dimensions collapses GSM8K accuracy while largely preserving factual-knowledge performance. Building on this, we propose Fisher-MoE, which operates within FFN to remove intermediate dimensions ranked by Fisher importance. At the same 50% MoE compression ratio, Fisher-MoE preserves model capability, while reducing weight memory by ~45% and improving inference throughput by 21%. These findings suggest intermediate dimension granularity is an effective unit for both compression and ranking where capability concentrates in MoE models.

</details>


### [304] [Balancing Image Compression and Generation with Bootstrapped Tokenization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05552)
*Haozhe Chi,Jinghan Li,Hao Jiang,Wu Sheng,Yi Ma,Jing Wang,Yadong Mu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SelfBootTok splits image tokens into global and local groups and trains a tokenizer to provide global tokens while a self-bootstrapped objective predicts local details from these global tokens, enabling a generator that uses only global tokens and reduces computation by ~40% while achieving state-of-the-art gFID (1.56) with 64 tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Redundant information across granularities within tokens leads to inefficiency and training difficulty for generators. A clean separation of global structure and local detail could improve efficiency and scalability.

Method: Decompose tokens into global and local groups. Use self-bootstrapped learning where the tokenizer provides global tokens and the model predicts local details from these tokens. The generator consumes only global tokens. Train with reconstruction and generation objectives; scale via additional data/parameters to enhance local representation learning.

Result: Generator computation reduced by ~40%; improved reconstruction and generation quality; achieves a new state-of-the-art gFID score of 1.56 using only 64 tokens.

Conclusion: Explicitly separating granularity in image tokenization yields more efficient, scalable generation with strong empirical gains; self-supervised local representation learning benefits from more data or parameters.

Abstract: Despite progress in image tokenization, standard methods encode redundant information by mixing all granularities within each token, thus redundancy persists between tokens. The mix of information of different granularity also complicates the training of generators. This paper introduces SelfBootTok, a method that resolves this by cleanly decomposing information into global and local token groups. Through self-bootstrapped learning, the model predicts local details exclusively from global tokens, shifting the burden of visual details from the generator to the tokenizer. Consequently, our generator is far more efficient, requiring only global tokens and reducing computation by approximately 40%, while delivering superior reconstruction and generation. Moreover, this paradigm scales elegantly: by leveraging more data or parameters to self-supervise local representation learning, SelfBootTok achieves a new state-of-the-art gFID score of 1.56 using only 64 tokens.

</details>


### [305] [Representation Learning Enables Scalable Multitask Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05555)
*Johan Obando-Ceron,Lu Li,Scott Fujimoto,Pierre-Luc Bacon,Aaron Courville,Pablo Samuel Castro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Predictive representation learning with MR.Q in a model-free, high-capacity actor-critic yields scalable multitask RL without planning, outperforming model-based methods and baselines while reducing computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalability in multitask reinforcement learning is hindered by planning complexity and training pipelines; it is unclear which components are essential for scalable performance.

Method: A simple model-free algorithm MR.Q augmented with auxiliary predictive objectives within a scalable actor-critic architecture, using predictive, model-based representations but no planning.

Result: MR.Q with predictive representations outperforms a recent world-model-based method and various deep RL baselines across diverse multitask continuous-control tasks, with lower computational overhead and better wall-clock efficiency; performance improves with greater model capacity; ablations show predictive representation learning is crucial.
