Conclusion: The key to scalable multitask RL is predictive representation learning rather than model-based control/planning; combining predictive representations with high-capacity value functions suffices for strong performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Scaling reinforcement learning (RL) to diverse multitask settings remains a central challenge. While recent advances in model-based RL achieve strong performance, they rely on planning and complex training pipelines, making it unclear which components are essential for scalability. We revisit this question and argue that the primary driver of scalable multitask RL is not model-based control, but \emph{representation learning}. In particular, we show that combining predictive, model-based representations with high-capacity value function approximation is sufficient to achieve strong performance, even without planning. We evaluate a simple model-free algorithm, MR.Q, coupled with auxiliary predictive objectives into a scalable actor-critic architecture. This approach outperforms a recent world-model-based method and a range of deep RL baselines across a diverse suite of multitask continuous control tasks, while significantly reducing computational overhead and improving wall-clock efficiency. We observe consistent improvements with increased model capacity and show through ablations that predictive representation learning is critical for performance.

</details>


### [306] [Field Validation of a Multi-Resolution ConvLSTM Framework for Retaining Wall Deformation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05556)
*Jihoon Kim,Heejung Youn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-resolution ConvLSTM ensemble for predicting retaining-wall deformation during staged excavation; trained on Gaussian-noise-augmented simulations; validated with field data from 34 inclinometer sensors across 11 sites in South Korea; MAE 1.4 mm, R^2 0.93; demonstrates effective transfer to real conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance predictive accuracy and generalization for retaining-wall deformation in staged excavation by leveraging spatiotemporal deep learning and ensemble fusion, while addressing limited real data through simulated augmentation.

Method: Train ConvLSTM models at multiple temporal resolutions and fuse via a stacking ensemble; use Gaussian-noise-augmented numerical simulations for training; validate on field monitoring data from 34 inclinometer sensors across 11 sites; analyze effects of temporal deformation irregularity and spatiotemporal prediction characteristics on performance.

Result: Average MAE ≈ 1.4 mm and R^2 ≈ 0.93 across sites for up to 5.0 m of additional excavation; framework generalizes from simulated/augmented data to diverse field conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-resolution ConvLSTM ensemble provides a reliable, field-validated framework for retaining-wall deformation prediction in practical excavation scenarios, despite training solely on simulated data.

Abstract: This study presents a comprehensive field validation of a multi-resolution Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory (ConvLSTM) framework for predicting retaining wall deformation during staged excavation. The framework is trained on Gaussian noise-augmented numerical simulations and integrates ConvLSTM models operating at different temporal resolutions through a stacking ensemble strategy. The proposed framework is validated using field monitoring data from 34 inclinometers across 11 excavation sites in South Korea. Site-wise prediction performance is systematically evaluated using multiple evaluation metrics, with analyses of the influence of temporal deformation irregularity and spatiotemporal prediction characteristics on model performance. The results demonstrate that the framework predicts retaining wall deformation associated with up to 5.0 m of additional excavation with an average mean absolute error of 1.4 mm and a coefficient of determination of 0.93 across the excavation sites. These results indicate that the framework, although trained exclusively on numerically simulated and augmented database, can be effectively applied to diverse field excavation conditions and achieve a reliable level of prediction accuracy in practical retaining wall deformation prediction.

</details>


### [307] [Autoregressive Diffusion World Models for Off-Policy Evaluation of LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05558)
*Kaixuan Liu,Guojun Xiong,Weinan Zhang,Shengpu Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline evaluation of LLM agents via ADWM, a latent diffusion world model that simulates environment responses to a policy without real interaction; enables step-by-step rollouts with a policy-conditioned diffusion process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce cost and risk of evaluating LLM agents in live, multi-turn environments by enabling reliable offline evaluation from pre-collected trajectories.

Method: Train a latent diffusion world model that predicts environment dynamics as sequential denoising steps. Treat each transition as independent denoising to avoid compounding errors. The LLM agent’s policy guides the diffusion generation at each step through a policy-conditioned score function, integrating the agent’s decision-making. The environment and agent alternate in causal order during simulated rollouts, and actions (text) are sampled from the policy after observing the environment.

Result: ADWM achieves accurate value estimates and reliable evaluation across diverse multi-turn agent tasks in offline settings.

Conclusion: ADWM offers a practical offline evaluation framework for LLM agents, reducing reliance on costly and risky online testing while preserving fidelity to the agent’s decision process.

Abstract: Evaluating large language model (LLM) agents in multi-turn interactive environments is expensive and risky, as it requires online environment interaction. We propose ADWM (Autoregressive Diffusion World Model), an evaluation framework that estimates the performance of a new LLM agent policy purely from pre-collected trajectories. The core idea is to learn a latent diffusion world model that simulates how the environment responds to the evaluation policy, without ever executing it in the real environment. Existing diffusion-based OPE methods guide full trajectories in a single pass by jointly diffusing states and actions, an assumption that breaks down for LLM agents whose actions are discrete text that must be sampled from the policy after observing the environment. Unlike autoregressive world models that suffer from compounding errors, ADWM models each transition as an independent denoising process, enabling reliable step-by-step rollouts where the world model and agent alternate in causal order. Crucially, the LLM agent under evaluation directly guides the diffusion generation at each step via a policy-conditioned score function, ensuring that simulated trajectories accurately reflect its decision-making patterns. Empirically, ADWM achieves accurate value estimates and evaluation reliability across diverse multi-turn agent tasks, demonstrating its promise as a practical framework for offline LLM agent evaluation.

</details>


### [308] [CLaaS: Continual learning as a service for sample efficient online learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05559)
*Kion Fallah,Silen Naihin,Barak Widawsky,Qingqing Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces CLaaS for experiential online continual learning of LLM agents under distribution shift; uses replay buffer for gradient reuse; shows parametric updates outperform in-context learning with replay for sample efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In dynamic, non-resettable environments, agents must adapt from ongoing experiences without forgetting prior capabilities; traditional one-shot or in-context methods are insufficient.

Method: Proposes continual learning as-a-service (CLaaS) with a chat API; stores collected rollouts in an experience replay buffer; performs asynchronous gradient-based updates; evaluates on an adversarial task; compares parametric updates to in-context learning; emphasizes replay as critical for sample efficiency.

Result: Parametric updates achieve better forward transfer and less forgetting than in-context learning; experience replay substantially improves sample efficiency; replay choice is a key factor.

Conclusion: CLaaS enables ongoing improvement during deployment, balancing adaptation and retention through replay-enabled gradient updates.

Abstract: Deployed large language model agents must adapt to distribution shift in dynamic environments. Ideally, adaptation can be performed from accumulated agent experiences and retain prior capabilities while transferring to future tasks. However, agent actions and environmental transitions can only be sampled once per scenario, as real-world environments cannot be trivially reset. To this end, we investigate an experiential and online continual learning setting in which agents learn from a stream of scenarios. We propose continual learning as-a-service (CLaaS), a system which enables agents to improve during deployment, abstracted behind a chat API. To increase sample efficiency, CLaaS stores rollouts in an experience replay buffer for gradient reuse during asynchronous training. We evaluate CLaaS on an adversarial task, demonstrating that parametric updates lead to superior forward transfer and less forgetting than in-context learning, with replay being a critical choice for sample efficiency.

</details>


### [309] [AsyncWebRL: Efficient Multi-Step RL for Visual Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05597)
*Hao Bai,Rui Yang,Chenlu Ye,Spencer Whitehead,Aviral Kumar,Tong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AsyncWebRL achieves up to 2.9x end-to-end training throughput over the fastest open synchronous WebGym pipeline, via asynchronous rollout/gradient/policy refresh and domain-specific adaptations; it also fixes trajectory/token inefficiency by using a constant normalization 1/k instead of 1/|τ_i|, yielding state-of-the-art open-source results on WebGym's out-of-distribution split (+5.8% relative over prior best), with pronounced gains on Medium and Hard slices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce compute waste in training vision-language web agents by tackling two inefficiency sources: (i) idle GPUs due to synchronous rollouts, and (ii) too-long, over-tokenized trajectories that bias gradients.

Method: Introduce AsyncWebRL with an asynchronous architecture that overlaps rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh; add an everlasting rollout pool and lightweight screenshot handling; replace per-trajectory normalizer 1/|τ_i| with a constant 1/k to contract trajectories while preserving aggregate success.

Result: End-to-end throughput up to 2.9x higher than WebGym; open-source state-of-the-art on WebGym OOD test with +5.8% relative improvement over the prior best (42.9%); gains especially large on harder slices (+42% on Medium, +48% on Hard).

Conclusion: Asynchronous system design and a simple normalization fix together yield substantial efficiency and performance gains, establishing a new open-source benchmark state-of-the-art for WebGym.

Abstract: Training vision-language web agents with multi-step RL is compute-intensive, with two dominant forms of inefficiency: idle GPUs in synchronous RL, and trajectories that use more steps and tokens than necessary. We present AsyncWebRL, which addresses both. On the system side, an asynchronous design overlaps rollout, gradient update, and policy refresh across iterations, paired with two web-agent-specific adaptations, namely an everlasting rollout pool and lightweight screenshot handling, that together deliver up to a $2.9\times$ end-to-end training-throughput speedup over the previously fastest open synchronous pipeline (WebGym). On the algorithmic side, we identify the per-trajectory normalizer $1/|τ_i|$ in multi-step GRPO as the root cause of trajectory-level and token-level inefficiency: because failures are systematically longer than successes, it down-weights the negative gradient on failed tokens, so the policy keeps producing verbose memory schemas. Replacing $1/|τ_i|$ with a constant $1/k$ breaks this coupling, contracting trajectories while preserving aggregate success. Together, these contributions set a new open-source state of the art on the WebGym out-of-distribution test split (+5.8% relative over the 42.9% prior best), with the largest gains on the harder slices (+42% relative on Medium, +48% relative on Hard).

</details>


### [310] [Mitigating the Curse of Dimensionality in Uniform Convergence of Deep Neural Networks via Smooth Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05599)
*Yizhe Ding,Runze Li,Jia Liu,Lingzhou Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Smoothly activated deep nets offer non-asymptotic uniform convergence guarantees that counteract the curse of dimensionality in comparison to ReLU nets, via pseudo-dimension and Hölder-norm bounds, enabling uniform rates across regression tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by the need for reliable uniform guarantees in downstream tasks and worst-case reliability, addressing the observed curse of dimensionality in uniform convergence for least-squares ReLU estimators; leverages smooth activations to exploit low-dimensional hierarchical structure.

Method: Develop a theoretical framework for uniform convergence of smooth DNN estimators (including feedforward and residual networks); derive pseudo-dimension bounds, non-asymptotic approximation guarantees, and Hölder-norm bounds; prove non-asymptotic uniform convergence rates across Huber, least-squares, quantile, and logistic regression; demonstrate adaptivity to low-dimensional hierarchical composition of the target function.

Result: Establish non-asymptotic uniform convergence rates for smooth DNN estimators; show that smooth activations mitigate the curse of dimensionality by leveraging the target’s hierarchical composition structure; provide a lower bound showing ReLU LS estimators can suffer from the curse; supported by simulations and a real-world application.

Conclusion: Smooth DNNs are theoretically grounded and practically viable alternatives to ReLU networks for tasks requiring uniform guarantees, offering reliable performance in worst-case scenarios and broader applicability across regression and classification tasks.

Abstract: This paper establishes a theoretical framework for the uniform convergence of smoothly activated deep neural network (DNN) estimators. While standard ReLU networks achieve minimax-optimal rates in the $L^2(P)$ norm for various nonparametric regression tasks, we establish a theoretical lower bound demonstrating that least-squares ReLU estimators can suffer from the curse of dimensionality in their uniform convergence behavior. Motivated by the need for reliable uniform guarantees in downstream tasks requiring worst-case reliability, we address this limitation by analyzing smoothly activated DNNs (smooth DNNs), encompassing both feedforward and residual structures. We establish novel pseudo-dimension bounds, non-asymptotic approximation guarantees, and Hölder-norm bounds for the approximators of these models. Leveraging these results, we derive non-asymptotic uniform convergence rates for smooth DNN estimators across multiple statistical contexts, including Huber, least-squares, quantile, and logistic regression. We prove that smooth DNNs can mitigate the {curse of dimensionality} in uniform convergence by adaptively exploiting the low-dimensional hierarchical composition structure of the target function. Supported by both simulation studies and a real-world application, our results position smooth DNNs as a theoretically grounded and practically viable alternative to ReLU networks for statistical learning tasks requiring uniform guarantees.

</details>


### [311] [From Prediction to Self: Developmental Conditions for Agency in Minimal Neural Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05605)
*Evan Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A minimal 192-d GRU undergoes 40 developmental experiments to learn self-agency by distinguishing self-caused vs world-caused changes; four required conditions emerge in order; agency gain metric; self-aware predictor outperforms self-blind; forward-sampled action selection essential; predictive coding alone insufficient; 12 hypotheses falsified.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how a predictive system develops a sense of agency and distinguishes internal versus external causes, using a controlled developmental sequence.

Method: 40 experiments on a 192-dimensional GRU with components added stepwise; test environments include sinusoidal and Lorenz chaotic dynamics; measure Err_world vs Err_self, compute agency gain; perform ablations; compare forward sampling against gradient-based action selection.

Result: Four conditions must be met in sequence for the system to distinguish self from world causation; the self-aware predictor consistently outperforms the self-blind predictor across tested environments and persists under ablations; meaningful agency gain requires forward-sampled action selection; two gradient-based alternatives fail; predictive coding alone does not suffice; 12 falsified hypotheses map where development stalls.

Conclusion: Agency emerges only when a specific ordered developmental sequence is satisfied, with explicit proprioceptive feedback and asynchronous learning being crucial; the agency gain metric provides a robust measure across environments and ablations; results constrain theories of self-modeling in predictive systems.

Abstract: How does a system that merely predicts the world come to distinguish its own causal influence from everything else? We trace this transition in a minimal 192-dimensional GRU through 40 controlled experiments arranged as a developmental sequence, adding components one at a time and tracking whether the system can distinguish self-caused from world-caused changes.
  The developmental path reveals four conditions that must be satisfied in strict order: (1) persistent state forming stable attractors, (2) a causal action loop linking output to input, (3) proprioceptive feedback that makes implicit causal knowledge explicit, and (4) asynchronous awakening - perceptual learning must consolidate before action learning begins. We propose agency gain (A = Err_world - Err_self), the predictive advantage of knowing one's own action, as a metric to track this process. The self-aware predictor consistently outperforms the self-blind predictor across periodic (sinusoidal) and chaotic (Lorenz) environments, and the metric survives ablation of all auxiliary components. Only forward-sampled action selection produces meaningful agency gain; two gradient-based alternatives degenerate.
  Equally significant are 12 falsified hypotheses mapping where development stalls: predictive coding alone does not produce self-represent

</details>


### [312] [Cross-Epoch Adaptive Rollout Optimization for RL Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05606)
*Yiming Zong,Yige Wang,Jiashuo Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive rollout budgeting for LLM RLHF using a Bayesian, concave utility with Fenchel-dual optimization improves sample efficiency and has provable regret bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed rollout budgets per prompt ignore heterogeneity in training signal across prompts. An adaptive allocation under a global budget can improve efficiency.

Method: Maintain a Beta posterior per prompt for its success probability; use posterior Bernoulli variance as a Bayesian estimate of the value of extra rollouts; define a concave, saturating utility over cumulative allocations; formulate a nonseparable objective coupled by the global budget; apply Fenchel-dual reformulation; update prompt-level and budget-level dual variables via projected online gradient descent.

Result: CERO outperforms GRPO across multiple open-weight LLMs on mathematical-reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating improved sample efficiency under a fixed global rollout budget.

Conclusion: Adaptive rollout budgeting with online dual optimization yields both theoretical regret guarantees (O(sqrt(K))) and practical gains in LLM RLHF settings.

Abstract: LLM post-training often relies on reinforcement learning methods that sample multiple rollouts per prompt, yet most existing approaches use a fixed rollout budget for every prompt, despite large differences in the training signal different prompts provide. In this paper, we study adaptive rollout allocation under a fixed global budget and formulate the problem as online resource allocation with prompt-level diminishing returns. Our method, CERO, maintains a Beta posterior over each prompt's success probability and uses the posterior expected Bernoulli variance as a Bayesian estimate of the value of additional rollouts. We use this estimate to construct a concave, saturating utility over cumulative allocations, yielding an objective in which decisions across prompts and epochs are coupled by the global budget. Since the resulting objective is temporally nonseparable, we derive a Fenchel-dual reformulation and update both prompt-level and budget-level dual variables via projected online gradient descent. Under fixed prompt utilities, we prove an $O(\sqrt{K})$ regret bound against the offline allocation benchmark. Experiments on mathematical-reasoning problems show that CERO consistently outperforms GRPO across multiple open-weight LLMs and benchmarks, demonstrating that adaptive rollout budgeting can improve sample efficiency.

</details>


### [313] [StableRCA: Robust Graph-Agnostic Mechanism-Level Root Cause Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05636)
*Xiaoyu Lin,Nicholas Tagliapietra,Kehan Li,Lavdim Halilaj,Juergen Luettin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StableRCA is a local mechanism-level RCA method that identifies intervention targets by estimating local Markov boundaries and detecting conditional distribution shifts, avoiding global graph discovery and proving robustness and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph-based causal methods require a known or estimated causal graph; graph-free approaches either miss structural causes or rely on restrictive assumptions. There is a need for a scalable, graph-free RCA that targets local mechanisms.

Method: Local mechanism-level RCA framework (StableRCA) estimating local Markov boundaries and detecting conditional distribution shifts within them; relies on Independent Causal Mechanism; identifies intervention targets with probability converging exponentially in sample size under faithful boundary recovery and non-degenerate shifts.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and five real-world datasets show robustness to graph misspecification, effectiveness with multiple interventions, scalability to large systems, and reliability across diverse domains.

Conclusion: StableRCA provides a graph-free, scalable, mechanism-level RCA approach with strong theoretical and empirical support, widening applicability of RCA in complex systems; code available at the provided URL.

Abstract: Root-Cause Analysis (RCA) seeks to identify the variables responsible for abnormal system behavior in complex domains such as manufacturing, cloud computing, and healthcare. Existing approaches face a critical bottleneck: graph-based causal methods can identify intervention targets but typically require a known or accurately estimated causal graph, while graph-free statistical methods either localize marginal anomalies rather than structural causes, or rely on restrictive assumptions about graph structure or functional form. We propose StableRCA, a local mechanism-level RCA framework that avoids global graph discovery by estimating local Markov boundaries and detecting conditional distribution shifts within them. Leveraging the Independent Causal Mechanism principle, we show that intervention targets can be identified with probability converging exponentially in sample size under faithful Markov boundary recovery and non-degenerate mechanism shifts. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and five real-world datasets demonstrate that StableRCA is robust to graph misspecification, effective under multiple intervention targets, scalable to large systems, and reliable across diverse application domains. Code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/StableRCA-E362

</details>


### [314] [Q-GNN: Query-Conditioned Graph Neural Networks with Type Awareness for Knowledge Graph Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05639)
*Dongxiao He,Ruqiong Zhang,Zhizhi Yu,Ling Ding,Di Jin,Guangquan Xu,Zhiyong Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-GNN improves Knowledge Graph Completion by leveraging both the query entity's structural context and a semantic type provided by a language model, in addition to the query relation, to guide GNN-based reasoning over local subgraphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNN-based KGC methods primarily use the query relation as the guiding signal and ignore the rich information encoded in the query entity's neighborhood and semantics. This limits reasoning capability, especially for entities with distinctive type or local graph patterns.

Method: Introduce a dedicated structural context encoder to capture neighboring structure and relation patterns around the query entity and modulate message passing. Infer the entity's semantic type with a large language model and integrate this type information into attention mechanisms and the final scoring to impose type-level priors. The model jointly reasons with both the query relation and the enriched query entity cues.

Result: Experimental results on standard benchmark datasets show that incorporating query entity information via the structural context and LLM-derived semantic types improves performance over baselines, validating the effectiveness of the proposed Q-GNN and ablations confirm the contribution of each component.

Conclusion: Incorporating query entity information—both structural context and semantic type—into GNN-based KGC provides a more informative guiding signal for reasoning, leading to improved link prediction accuracy.

Abstract: Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) aims at predicting missing triplets from incomplete knowledge graphs, which is crucial for downstream applications. Recently, Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based methods have achieved remarkable success by performing message passing over query-centered local subgraphs. However, in practice, a query is jointly defined by both the entity and the relation, with both carrying information indispensable for reasoning, yet these methods rely solely on the query relation as the guiding signal, while the information inherent in the query entity is not leveraged to guide inference - the entity serves merely as a structural anchor for subgraph extraction. To this end, we incorporate query entity information into the reasoning process from two perspectives: the first is structural context, i.e., the neighboring structure and relation patterns around the entity, which is encoded by a dedicated context encoder and used to modulate messages; the second is semantic type of the entity, inferred by a large language model, which is incorporated into attention computation and final scoring to provide type-level prior constraints. Together, these two sources of information enable the reasoning process to be guided by both the query relation and the query entity. Experimental results on standard benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed Q-GNN.

</details>


### [315] [Two-Way Is Better Than One: Bidirectional Alignment with Cycle Consistency for Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05675)
*Hongye Xu,Bartosz Krawczyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BiCyc introduces bidirectional projection alignment with a cycle-consistency objective to address prototype drift in exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In EFCIL, past data cannot be stored, so prototypes drift as the embedding space evolves, causing old-class misclassification. One-directional projection methods introduce bias and cycle inconsistencies; a cycle-consistent, bidirectional approach is needed to co-evolve representations and projections.

Method: BiCyc jointly optimizes two transport maps (old-to-new and new-to-old) with stop-gradient gating, trained with a cycle-consistency loss. The approach yields a contracted singular spectrum toward unity in whitened space and improves transport of class means and covariances, reducing perturbations to log-odds and preserving old-class decisions.

Result: Empirical evaluation on standard EFCIL benchmarks shows BiCyc substantially reduces forgetting and improves accuracy from scratch, while remaining competitive in pretrained fine-grained settings.

Conclusion: A cycle-consistent, bidirectional projection framework effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in EFCIL by ensuring consistent transport and representation updates, outperforming one-directional methods and aligning with strong baselines.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) seeks models that acquire new skills without erasing prior knowledge. In exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL), this challenge is amplified because past data cannot be stored, making representation drift for old classes particularly harmful. Prototype-based EFCIL is attractive for its efficiency, yet prototypes drift as the embedding space evolves; therefore, projection-based drift compensation has become a popular remedy. We show, however, that existing one-directional projections introduce systematic bias: they either retroactively distort the current feature geometry or align past classes only locally, leaving cycle inconsistencies that accumulate across tasks. We introduce BiCyc, a bidirectional projector alignment approach with a cycle-consistency objective. BiCyc jointly optimizes two maps, old-to-new and new-to-old, with stop-gradient gating so that transport and representation co-evolve. Analytically, we show that the cycle loss contracts the singular spectrum toward unity in whitened space, and that improved transport of class means and covariances yields smaller perturbations of classification log-odds, preserving old-class decisions and mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Empirically, across standard EFCIL benchmarks, BiCyc substantially reduces forgetting and improves accuracy in from-scratch settings, while remaining competitive in the pretrained fine-grained regime.

</details>


### [316] [Causal Modeling of Selection in Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05689)
*Haoyue Dai,Zeyu Tang,Peter Spirtes,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distinguishes static (one-shot) vs evolutionary (generational) selection in data, shows that standard selection models fail for evolutionary data, and introduces a dedicated evolutionary-selection model with a complete identifiability procedure across environments, validated by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current causal-discovery methods assume a single, static selection mechanism; this conflation yields false discoveries when data reflect evolutionary processes such as immune adaptation or antibiotic resistance. A model that captures evolutionary selection is needed for reliable inference across generations or environments.

Method: Proposes a new graphical/modeling framework tailored to evolutionary selection, and presents a sound and complete identifiability procedure to identify such models from multi-generational or multi-environment data.

Result: Empirical results demonstrate the method can uncover the mechanisms driving evolution from data, validating the approach."

Conclusion: Distinguishing static and evolutionary selection yields correct identifiability and improves causal inference in evolutionary settings; the proposed model addresses a gap in current methods and enables discovery across generations/environments.

Abstract: Understanding potential selection in data is crucial for causal discovery; we argue that "selection" in common narratives takes two forms, which we term static and evolutionary selection, respectively. Static selection refers to a one-shot filtering process where observed data consist of a subset of the population of interest, as in survey volunteer bias. Evolutionary selection, in contrast, operates through repeated rounds of differential fitness in reproduction, where observed data constitute the latest generation shaped by a historical trajectory, as in immune adaptation, antibiotic resistance, and social norm emergence. Existing methods largely conflate these two forms and rely on an identical graphical model of selection. We show that this model is valid for static settings but fails to characterize data under evolution, yielding false discovery results. To address this, we introduce a new model that specifically characterizes evolutionary selection, and develop a sound and complete procedure for identifying such models from data across one or multiple environments or generations. Experimental results validate the method's ability to uncover the relevant mechanisms underlying evolution from data.

</details>


### [317] [Benchmarking Counterfactual Prediction in Epidemic Time Series with Time-Varying Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05692)
*Wenhao Mu,Facundo Yan,Anik Mumssen,Marisa Eisenberg,Alexander Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A large-scale benchmark for counterfactual prediction in epidemic time series using a calibrated agent-based model to generate ground-truth counterfactuals across 150+ U.S. counties, enabling evaluation of static/time-varying and single/multi-policy interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of benchmarks with observable counterfactual outcomes in time-series causal inference and enable robust evaluation across diverse causal scenarios.

Method: Calibrated agent-based model built from real-world demographic, mobility, epidemiological, and policy data to generate realistic counterfactual trajectories; supports static and time-varying treatments and single/multi-policy settings; applied to >150 counties.

Result: The benchmark reveals substantial performance differences among widely used and state-of-the-art causal inference methods, highlighting the challenges of realistic time-series causal reasoning.

Conclusion: The benchmark fills a critical gap by providing a flexible, large-scale evaluation platform for time-series counterfactual inference under dynamic interventions, facilitating rigorous method comparison and progress in the field.

Abstract: Deep learning has enabled significant advances in time-series causal inference, yet progress remains constrained by the lack of realistic benchmarks with observable counterfactual outcomes. Existing datasets either rely on real-world observations without ground-truth counterfactuals or on simplified simulations that fail to capture complex causal dynamics. To address this gap, we develop a large-scale benchmark for counterfactual prediction in epidemic time series under dynamic interventions. Unlike existing benchmarks, it supports static and time-varying treatments, as well as both single-policy and multi-policy intervention settings, enabling evaluation of causal inference methods across a broad range of causal inference scenarios. Leveraging a calibrated agent-based model grounded in real-world demographic, mobility, epidemiological, and policy data, we generate realistic counterfactual trajectories across more than 150 U.S. counties. Using this benchmark, we evaluate widely used and state-of-the-art causal inference methods, revealing substantial performance differences and highlighting the challenges of realistic time-series causal reasoning.

</details>


### [318] [MolE-RAG: Molecular Structure-Enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Chemistry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05693)
*Joey Chan,Wonbin Kweon,Ashley Shin,Niharika Bhattacharjee,Pengcheng Jiang,Yue Guo,Jiawei Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MolE-RAG is a training-free, retrieval-augmented generation framework that uses literature, molecule-specific context, and structurally similar molecules to enhance LLM-based molecular property prediction, yielding significant improvements over SMILES-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are trained on natural language and struggle to reason over chemical structures; bridging semantic/chemical knowledge gaps is essential for accurate molecular property prediction without fine-tuning.

Method: At inference time, MolE-RAG retrieves three sources: (1) chemistry literature; (2) molecule-specific context (synonyms, identifiers, functional groups, descriptors); (3) structurally similar molecules from the training set. It leverages LLMs in a training-free, molecule-centric retrieval-augmented generation framework and evaluates across nine tasks with various LLMs.

Result: On general-purpose LLMs, ROC-AUC improved by up to 28 percentage points for classification and RMSE reduced by up to 67% for regression, compared with SMILES-only baselines. The usefulness of each context source varies by model and task, indicating a flexible, source-dependent benefit without model fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Molecule-centric retrieval is a viable, flexible approach to boost LLM-based molecular property prediction without fine-tuning, enabling integration of heterogeneous chemical knowledge at inference time and offering insights into which context sources are most beneficial per model and task.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise for molecular property prediction, but their ability to reason over chemical structures remains limited, as molecular representations such as SMILES differ substantially from the natural language on which LLMs are primarily trained. To bridge this semantic and chemical knowledge gap, we propose MolE-RAG, a training-free, molecule-centric retrieval-augmented generation framework for LLM-based molecular property prediction. MolE-RAG augments each prediction with three complementary sources of inference-time context: retrieved chemistry literature, molecule-specific information including compound synonyms, identifiers, functional group annotations, and physicochemical descriptors, and structurally similar molecules retrieved from the training set. We evaluate MolE-RAG across nine molecular property prediction tasks using proprietary, chemistry-specialized, and open-source LLMs. Across general-purpose LLMs, MolE-RAG improves ROC-AUC by up to 28 percentage points on classification tasks and reduces regression RMSE by up to 67% relative to a SMILES-only baseline. We further find that the utility of each context source varies across models and tasks, with different models benefiting most from textual retrieval, molecular context, or structural retrieval. These results suggest that molecule-centric retrieval can improve LLM-based molecular property prediction without model fine-tuning while providing a flexible framework for integrating heterogeneous chemical knowledge at inference time.

</details>


### [319] [Revisiting Prototype Rehearsal for Exemplar-Free Continual Learning: Manifold-Aware Boundary Sampling with Adaptive Class-Balanced Loss](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05695)
*Hongye Xu,Bartosz Krawczyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Manifold-aware upgrade of prototype rehearsal for exemplar-free class-incremental learning using Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling and Adaptive Class-Balanced loss; closes the gap with drift-compensation methods and achieves state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prototype-based rehearsal in EFCIL struggles due to ignoring information from nearby non-target classes (enemy classes) and severe old-vs-new data imbalance. Aims to leverage data manifold structure and temporal class balance to mitigate forgetting.

Method: 1) Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling: interpolate old-class prototypes toward nearest new-class features to create boundary-aware samples that better trace the data manifold while preserving inter-class separation. 2) Adaptive Class-Balanced loss: time-based weighting that amplifies gradients from old prototypes early and gradually reduces their influence as new-task supervision accumulates.

Result: The approach closes or reverses the performance gap with drift-compensation methods and achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple EFCIL benchmarks.

Conclusion: Prototype rehearsal becomes drift-resilient and imbalance-aware when instantiated with manifold-aware sampling and time-adaptive balancing, effectively bridging the gap to drift-compensation approaches in EFCIL.

Abstract: Exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL) aims to acquire new classes over time without storing raw data. Historically, prototype rehearsal, which samples around stored class prototypes and mixes them with current-task data, has been a popular strategy to reduce catastrophic forgetting. However, recent drift-compensation methods that explicitly realign prototypes in the evolving feature space consistently outperform prototype-based rehearsal, raising the question of whether rehearsal itself is fundamentally limited. We argue that the performance gap stems not from the idea of prototype rehearsal per se, but from how it is typically instantiated: existing approaches treat prototypes as isolated class summaries that ignore information from nearby enemy classes, and fail to correct the emerging class imbalance between a handful of synthetic old-class samples and hundreds of real instances from newly introduced classes. Building on this hypothesis, we revisit prototype rehearsal and propose a manifold-aware variant that restores its competitiveness in EFCIL. First, we introduce Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling, which interpolates each old-class prototype toward its nearest enemy features from new classes, generating boundary-aware rehearsal samples that better follow the underlying data manifold while preserving inter-class separation. Second, we design an Adaptive Class-Balanced loss that performs time-based class weighting, amplifying gradients from older prototypes when they are most informative and gradually annealing their influence as richer supervision from later tasks accumulates. Together, these components turn prototype rehearsal into a drift-resilient, imbalance-aware mechanism that closes, and often reverses, the gap to recent drift-compensation methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple EFCIL benchmarks.

</details>


### [320] [Intercomparison of Machine Learning Algorithms for Remote Sensing-based In-season Crop Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05731)
*August Posch,Jitendra Kumar,Forrest M. Hoffman,Auroop R. Ganguly*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pre-harvest crop-type mapping using Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel imagery with extensive ML model comparison; SVM delivers best performance (~0.74 F1) for almonds in CA and corn in IA by early June; interannual variability remains a major uncertainty; ensemble methods and ancillary data could improve results; future work includes multiclass, CONUS-wide maps, and yield forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable near real-time or pre-harvest crop-type maps to support emergency management and food security, addressing the limitations of the CDL which maps post-harvest with delays.

Method: Combine Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel surface reflectance time series with crop rotation history; evaluate thousands of model configurations across ten machine learning algorithms; use year-wise cross-validation and multiple metrics; conduct hyperparameter searches; quantify uncertainty due to phenology and distribution.

Result: SVM emerged as the most successful algorithm overall, achieving a mean F1 score of about 0.74 (0.59) across five unseen validation years for almonds in California and corn in Iowa by early June; interannual variation is a large source of uncertainty; ensemble approaches or ancillary data have potential to further improve performance.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of accurate 30 m-resolution pre-harvest crop-type mapping by early June; uncertainty largely driven by phenology and crop distribution; outlines paths for extending to multiclass maps, CONUS-wide coverage, and in-season yield forecasting.

Abstract: In-season crop type mapping is critical for food security in the face of increasingly extreme climate-related threats to crops. Currently, the USDA Cropland Data Layer provides crop type labels at 30m resolution and is available the February after harvest, but no product exists that maps crop types before harvest with satisfactory accuracy that would allow emergency managers to respond to crop threats in near real time. Furthermore, the relative advantages of a wide range of algorithms have not been evaluated in a way that accounts for interannual variability, until this study. Here, Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel surface reflectance imagery time series and crop rotation history information are combined to map corn in Iowa and almonds in California at 30m resolution accurately by early June in unseen years, with robust quantification of uncertainty due to phenology and crop distribution. Thousands of model configurations across ten machine learning algorithms were compared using a year-wise cross-validation and a suite of metrics. Hyperparameter search revealed Support Vector Machines to be the most successful algorithm overall, with a mean F1 score of 0.74 (0.59) across five unseen validation years for almonds by early June in California (corn by early June in Iowa). Interannual variation was a large source of uncertainty, but patterns showed the potential to further improve performance with ensemble approaches or ancillary data. Future work may extend these methods to include multiclass maps of all crop types, CONUS-wide maps, and in-season crop yield forecasting.

</details>


### [321] [Zero-Copy Semantic Contagion: An In-Memory Streaming Architecture for Evolving Attention Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05733)
*Kabir Murjani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming architecture enables cross-company signal propagation in financial time-series via a continuous-time graph and neural Hawkes process, delivering low latency and precision gains; graph topology is essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-asset models fail to capture cross-company propagation (e.g., disruptions in one firm affecting others). A real-time cross-entity signal extractor with scalable inference is needed.

Method: Ingestion: zero-copy Rust edge parses news in ~100 ns and scans the target equity universe in ~1.2 μs. Inference: multivariate Neural Hawkes Process with per-node continuous-time LSTM states and bilinear latent projection; adaptive pruning bounds dynamic neighborhood updates. End-to-end latency: ~13 ms per incoming news record on a commodity CPU.

Result: On a one-month holdout of the FNSPID corpus (638 articles, 47 tickers): ~1.70x precision lift over random at the 90th-percentile next-day return threshold; ~3.36x over a same-sector baseline. Removing the graph topology collapses precision to zero, indicating the graph-driven cross-company signal is essential.

Conclusion: Dynamic attention network is the primary driver of cross-company signal in this architecture; the system achieves low latency and measurable precision gains by modeling cross-asset interactions as a continuous-time graph with adaptive pruning.

Abstract: Per-ticker forecasting models dominate financial time-series work yet remain blind to cross-company propagation: a foundry disruption in Taiwan does not register in a single-asset model until Apple's own price has already moved. To address this limitation, we introduce a heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming architecture that maps cross-company attention as a continuous-time graph driven directly from text. We show that on the ingestion side, a zero-copy Rust edge parses news records in $\sim$100 ns and scans the target equity universe in $\sim$1.2 $μ$s. On the inference end, a multivariate Neural Hawkes Process featuring per-node continuous-time LSTM states and a bilinear latent projection propagates directed excitation, while an adaptive pruning rule bounds the computational cost of dynamic neighborhood updates. Combining these stages, we demonstrate an end-to-end processing latency of $\sim$13 ms per incoming news record on a single commodity CPU. Evaluated on a one-month temporal holdout of the FNSPID corpus (638 articles across 47 tickers), the system delivers a $1.70\times$ precision lift over random at the 90th-percentile next-day return threshold, and $3.36\times$ over a same-sector baseline. Crucially, removing the graph topology collapses precision to zero, confirming that the dynamic attention network is the sole driver of cross-company signal in this architecture.

</details>


### [322] [Beyond Soft Masks: Hard-Perturbation Mixup Explainer for Robust GNN Explainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05756)
*Jialiang Yin,Zheng Zhao,Linsey Pang,Bo Dong,Bin Shi,Jiaxing Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hard-perturbation mixup explanation (HPME) uses graph pooling to extract discrete, informative subgraphs and applies a structure-level mixup to generate in-distribution explanations, addressing the OOD issue and improving explanation fidelity over soft-mask methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability for GNNs is hampered by soft masks that leak label-irrelevant information and fail to fully address OOD when explanations (subgraphs) are used for prediction. A discrete, information-bottlenecked approach with hard perturbations could yield more faithful explanations and mitigate distribution shift.

Method: Introduce HPME, a framework based on a generalized Graph Information Bottleneck. It uses graph pooling to produce discrete explanatory subgraphs and derives an information-capacity bound to compress label-irrelevant parts. It also employs a new mixup strategy via structure-level replacement to generate in-distribution explanations.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that HPME achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating robust, interpretable explanations, with improved fidelity and reduced distribution shift compared to existing post-hoc explainers that rely on soft masks.

Conclusion: Hard perturbations combined with a graph-information-bottleneck-driven subgraph extraction and a structure-level mixup provide robust and faithful explanations for GNNs, addressing key limitations of soft-mask approaches and improving OOD resilience.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across a range of applications involving graph-structured data, particularly in high-stakes domains. However, the opaque nature of their decision-making processes limits their trustworthiness and broader adoption. Existing post-hoc explanation methods aim to improve explainability by identifying subgraphs that influence GNN predictions and adopt mixup strategies to alleviate the out-of-distribution (OOD) issue caused by using subgraphs for prediction. Yet, these approaches typically rely on soft masks, which are inherently unable to fully eliminate label-irrelevant information, allowing redundant structures to leak into the mixup process and hindering the resolution of the OOD problem, thereby degrading explanation fidelity. In this work, we propose HPME, a Hard-Perturbation Mixup Explanation framework grounded in a generalized Graph Information Bottleneck, which leverages graph pooling to extract discrete explanatory subgraphs and to yield an information-capacity bound to thoroughly compress label-irrelevant components. Furthermore, we introduce a novel mixup strategy built upon structure-level replacement, generating in-distribution explanations to effectively mitigate the distribution shift. Extensive experiments on diverse tasks demonstrate that HPME achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating robust and interpretable explanations across both synthetic and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [323] [Domain-Adapted Small Language Models with Hybrid Post-Processing: Achieving Cost-Efficient, Low-Latency Multi-Label Structured Prediction via LoRA Fine-Tuning on Scarce Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05781)
*Srinivasan Manoharan,Dilipkumar Nallusamy,Sachin Kumar,Haifeng Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A domain-adapted small LLM with LoRA and a rule-based post-processing layer can match frontier-model accuracy for multi-label, structured compliance evaluation with significantly lower cost, latency, and privacy risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce latency, cost, and data/privacy overhead of using frontier large language models for domain-specific structured evaluation tasks by leveraging a small, domain-adapted model and deterministic post-processing.

Method: Fine-tune a small LLM (LLaMA 3.1 8B) using LoRA to 2.05% trainable parameters, trained on 219 curated examples. Apply to 18 heterogeneous output fields for multi-label compliance evaluation of transcripts. Introduce a deterministic post-processing layer and targeted hard-negative augmentation. Evaluate on 53 unseen production transcripts. Run inference on an NVIDIA A100 GPU.

Result: Achieves 100% JSON structural validity and 83.0% human-validated overall accuracy, with 100% accuracy on the most critical field. Inference ~2 seconds, 2-5x faster than frontier-model APIs. Cost per evaluation $0.013 vs $0.025-$0.055 for alternatives, yielding 46-76% cost savings.

Conclusion: Domain-adapted small LMs with deterministic post-processing can match frontier-model accuracy for structured compliance evaluation while substantially reducing latency, cost, and privacy risk.

Abstract: Deploying frontier large language models (LLMs) for domain-specific structured evaluation tasks often incurs substantial latency, cost, and data privacy overhead. We present a hybrid framework that combines a fine-tuned small language model (LLaMA 3.1 8B, with only 2.05% trainable parameters via LoRA) and a deterministic rule-based post-processing layer. Trained on just 219 curated examples, the system is applied to multi-label compliance evaluation of conversational transcripts spanning 18 heterogeneous output fields. In blind evaluation on 53 previously unseen production transcripts, it achieves 100% JSON structural validity, 83.0% human-validated overall accuracy, and 100% accuracy on the most critical classification field. The proposed approach formalizes a hybrid neural-symbolic decomposition and introduces targeted hard-negative augmentation to improve performance on critical decision boundaries. Running on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, inference completes in approximately 2 seconds, which is 2-5x faster than frontier-model APIs. The system costs only $0.013 per evaluation compared with $0.025-$0.055 for proprietary alternatives, resulting in 46-76% cost savings. These results demonstrate that domain-adapted small language models, when combined with deterministic post-processing, can match frontier-model accuracy for structured compliance evaluation while substantially reducing operational cost, latency, and privacy risk.
  Keywords: small language models, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, LoRA, domain adaptation, hybrid inference, compliance evaluation, structured output.

</details>


### [324] [Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks for Counterfactual Outcome Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05797)
*Amirhossein Zare,Amirhessam Zare,Herlock Rahimi,Reza Salarikia,Mohammad Kashkooli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A pretrained, frozen, in-context predictor (CausalLongPFN) for longitudinal causal prediction trained on synthetic episodes from broad temporal causal models; at test time it uses support histories and a proposed future treatment sequence to output a distribution over future outcomes without further training, enabling multi-step predictions by recursion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address time-varying confounding, latent heterogeneity, and data scarcity in longitudinal causal inference without per-cohort retraining or propensity model fitting.

Method: Pretrain a predictor on synthetic episodes drawn from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models (including treatment-confounder feedback, delayed effects, nonlinear dynamics). At test time, freeze the model, condition on support trajectories, query history, and a planned future treatment sequence, and produce a predictive distribution for future outcomes; generate multi-step forecasts by recursive one-step predictions.

Result: On benchmarks of branchable cancer, HIV, and warfarin with ground-truth counterfactuals, CausalLongPFN is competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines; on factual MIMIC-III trajectory prediction, it performs strongly, suggesting synthetic causal pretraining can be a practical frozen alternative when domain-specific retraining is costly.

Conclusion: Broad synthetic causal pretraining can yield a useful frozen predictor for longitudinal causal tasks, offering competitive performance on counterfactual benchmarks and strong factual forecasting without ongoing gradient updates or domain-specific training.

Abstract: Longitudinal treatment decisions require predicting potential outcomes under future treatment sequences in the presence of time-varying confounding, heterogeneous patient dynamics, and limited domain-specific data. Existing longitudinal causal estimators typically train a new model for each cohort or simulator. We introduce Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks (CausalLongPFN), a prior-fitted in-context predictor for longitudinal causal prediction. The model is pretrained entirely on synthetic episodes sampled from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models, exposing it to treatment-confounder feedback, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear state evolution, delayed effects, and cumulative treatment responses. At test time, CausalLongPFN is frozen: it conditions on support trajectories, a query history, and a proposed future treatment sequence, and returns a predictive distribution over future outcomes without gradient updates or propensity-model fitting. Multi-step predictions are obtained by recursively applying the one-step predictor under the specified treatment sequence. We evaluate on branchable cancer, HIV, and warfarin benchmarks with ground-truth counterfactual labels, and on factual-only rolling-origin prediction in MIMIC-III ICU trajectories. CausalLongPFN is competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines on counterfactual benchmarks and performs strongly on factual MIMIC-III prediction, suggesting that broad synthetic causal pretraining can provide a useful frozen alternative when repeated domain-specific training is costly or impractical.

</details>


### [325] [CaliDist: Calibrating Large Language Models via Behavioral Robustness to Distraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05799)
*Mohammad Anas Jawad,Cornelia Caragea*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CaliDist is a post-hoc calibration method that uses a model's sensitivity to distractor perturbations to adjust its confidence, improving calibration metrics across multiple benchmarks and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Trustworthiness in LLMs should reflect behavioral robustness: true confidence should be stable under irrelevant or misleading cues. Current calibration methods ignore this stability, potentially misrepresenting model certainty.

Method: Apply semantic distractors to input prompts and measure how predictions and uncertainties shift; derive a stability signal from this perturbation response; use this signal to adaptively scale the model's original confidence score in a post-hoc calibration step.

Result: CaliDist consistently achieves better calibration (lower ECE and Brier Score) than strong baselines across seven NLU benchmarks and six LLMs; average ECE reduction from 23% to 7% (≈70% relative improvement).

Conclusion: Behavioral stability is a valuable calibration signal for LLMs; CaliDist demonstrates that incorporating robustness to distraction yields significantly improved calibration and reliable uncertainty estimates. Code and datasets are released.

Abstract: Existing calibration methods for Large Language Models (LLMs) often overlook a critical dimension of trustworthiness: a model's {\em behavioral robustness} to irrelevant or misleading information. In this paper, we argue that a model's true confidence should reflect its stability under cognitive pressure. We introduce \textsc{CaliDist}, a novel post-hoc calibration approach that directly measures and penalizes a model's susceptibility to distraction. \textsc{CaliDist} quantifies how an LLM's predictions and uncertainty change when its input prompt is perturbed with semantic \textit{distractors}. This stability (or lack thereof) signal is then used to adaptively scale the model's initial confidence score. Our extensive experiments on seven Natural Language Understanding classification benchmarks using six distinct LLMs show that \textsc{CaliDist} consistently achieves lower Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Brier Score compared with strong baselines. Remarkably, our method reduces the ECE from 23\% to 7\% on average--a relative improvement of 70\%--demonstrating that behavioral stability is a powerful signal for calibration. We make our code and datasets available at github.com/m-anas-j/CaliDist.

</details>


### [326] [SALT: When More Rollouts Don't Help in Group-Based Policy Optimization and How to Make Them Matter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05800)
*Powei Chang,Jinpeng Zhang,Chaoqun Sun,MiniWell Tsao,Lianrui Li,Jianxiang Xiang,Chenyu Wang,Yukang Gao,Dongying Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SALT mitigates cancellation in GRPO-style RL updates by a plug-in, subspace-adaptive reweighting of group-relative coefficients, derived from mini-batch Gram geometry, improving learning signals without altering sampling or rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under GRPO-style group normalization, learning signals from multiple rollouts can cancel due to low-rank, signed geometry, weakening updates. A post-hoc mechanism is needed to preserve and amplify informative components.

Method: SALT learns a dominant shared subspace from the mini-batch Gram geometry, decomposes group-relative coefficients into shared and residual channels, and adaptively amplifies residual channels when signed cancellation is severe. It acts as a plug-in that does not modify the reward model or rollout procedure.

Result: Across diverse reasoning-oriented RLVR benchmarks and model scales, SALT improves the quality of the gradient geometry and empirical performance.

Conclusion: SALT provides an effective, model-agnostic boost to RLVR updates by aligning gradient geometry with the data-sourced subspace, without requiring changes to rewards or rollout sampling.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) often adopts GRPO-style group-relative updates, sampling multiple rollouts per prompt to construct normalized learning signals. However, merely increasing the number of rollouts does not reliably strengthen learning: under GRPO-style group normalization, per-rollout policy-gradient features can concentrate into a low-rank, signed geometry, causing substantial cancellation during aggregation and weakening the effective update. We address this failure mode with SALT, a Subspace-Adaptive geometry pLug-in componenT that uses sample-wise gradient geometry to reweight the coefficients of group-relative updates. SALT estimates a dominant shared subspace from the mini-batch Gram geometry, decomposes group-relative coefficients into shared and residual channels, and adaptively amplifies the residual channel when signed cancellation is severe. Across diverse reasoning-oriented RLVR benchmarks and model scales, SALT improves effective update geometry and performance without modifying the reward model or the rollout sampling procedure

</details>


### [327] [Robust and sparse support vector machine via hybrid truncated loss for supervised classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05814)
*Yuliang Yang,Chen Chen,Yuxiang Liu,Huiru Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a hybrid truncated loss for SVM (L_ht) that yields a sparse and bounded objective, with an efficient ADMM-based solver; extends to multi-view learning (MvL_ht-SVM) with structural view weighting under consensus and complementarity; demonstrates improved accuracy and robustness with fewer support vectors across single- and multi-view datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outlier sensitivity of convex losses (hinge/L2) and high cost of bounded non-convex losses motivate a loss that is both robust and computationally tractable while promoting sparsity; extendable to multi-view learning with structured information.

Method: Introduce L_ht as a sparse, bounded surrogate; derive P-stationary point and first-order necessary and sufficient optimality conditions; develop an alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) with a working-set strategy for optimization and convergence guarantees; extend to multi-view through structural information and view weights, enforcing consensus and complementarity.

Result: L_ht-SVM achieves higher accuracy with fewer support vectors and better noise robustness than five single-view baselines; MvL_ht-SVM outperforms six multi-view methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 on synthetic, real-world, and image datasets.

Conclusion: The hybrid truncated loss framework provides robust, sparse, and scalable SVM training with strong empirical performance; the multi-view extension yields improved predictive metrics by leveraging consensus and complementary information across views; potential for broader application and theoretical refinement.

Abstract: The support vector machine (SVM) is a widely used classifier, but choosing an appropriate loss function remains difficult. Convex losses such as the hinge loss and least-squares loss are sensitive to outliers, while bounded non-convex losses often lead to high computational cost. To address this, we propose a hybrid truncated loss function ($L_{\mathrm{ht}}$) that is both sparse and bounded, and build the $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM model for single-view classification. We introduce the P-stationary point and use it to establish the first-order necessary and sufficient optimality conditions. Based on these conditions, we design an alternating direction method of multipliers with a working-set strategy that reduces computational cost and achieves global convergence. We further extend $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM to multi-view learning by adding structural information and view weights, resulting in Mv$L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM, which follows both the consensus and complementarity principles. Experiments on synthetic, real-world, and image datasets show that $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM achieves higher accuracy with fewer support vectors and better noise robustness than five single-view methods, while Mv$L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM outperforms six multi-view methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

</details>


### [328] [Consistency Training Along the Transformer Stack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05817)
*Sukrati Gautam,Neil Shah,Arav Dhoot,Bryan Maruyama,Caroline Wei,Rohan Kapoor,Robert Sidey,Prakhar Gupta,Zi Cheng Huang,David Demitri Africa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces MLPCT and AttCT as internal consistency targets; applies consistency training to four safety threats; finds reduced misalignment beyond prior settings; shows cross-threat generalization and a shared residual-stream mechanism across ACT, MLPCT, and AttCT; BCT is mechanistically distinct; frames consistency training as a flexible, extensible defense framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Broaden the scope of consistency-based alignment methods beyond limited contexts (e.g., sycophancy, jailbreak) by leveraging internal representations (MLP states, per-head attention) and by evaluating across diverse threat modalities to improve robustness.

Method: Define two internal consistency targets: MLPCT (matches post-activation MLP states) and AttCT (matches per-head attention distributions). Apply consistency training across four safety threats—persona in-context learning attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment—evaluating across multiple models. Analyze whether a shared residual-stream mechanism underpins ACT, MLPCT, and AttCT, and compare with BCT.

Result: Consistency training reduces misalignment more effectively than prior work limited to sycophancy/jailbreak contexts; evidence of cross-threat generalization where training against one failure mode improves robustness to another; identification of a shared residual-stream mechanism underpinning ACT, MLPCT, and AttCT, with BCT treated as mechanistically distinct.

Conclusion: Consistency training is a flexible and extensible alignment framework capable of unifying defenses against a broad class of model pathologies.

Abstract: Consistency training encourages models to behave similarly across different contexts, and has shown promise for reducing misalignment. We broaden the scope of consistency training in two ways. First, we introduce two new internal consistency targets: MLP Consistency Training (MLPCT), which matches post-activation MLP states, and Attention Consistency Training (AttCT), which matches per-head attention distributions. Second, we apply consistency training to four additional safety threats: persona in-context learning attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment. Across several models and threat settings, we find that consistency training reduces misalignment well beyond the sycophancy and jailbreak settings studied in prior work. We also find cases of cross-threat generalization, where training against one failure mode improves robustness to another, and identify a shared residual-stream mechanism underlying ACT, MLPCT, and AttCT, while distinguishing BCT as mechanistically distinct. Our results suggest that consistency training is a flexible and extensible framework for alignment, capable of unifying defenses against a broader class of model pathologies.

</details>


### [329] [GenAutoML: An Agentic Framework for Dynamic Architecture Generation and Optimization in Time-Series Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05860)
*Oleeviya Babu Poikarayil,Cédric Schockaert,Abdulrahman Nahhas,Christian Daase,Mursal Dawodi,Jawid Ahmad Baktash*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GenAutoML uses LLMs to autonomously generate task-specific neural architectures for time-series forecasting and anomaly detection, with safeguards and a robustness wrapper, achieving ultra-light models like WaveInterferenceNet for edge-ready deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automating neural-architecture design for time-series is resource-intensive and inflexible; static AutoML search spaces fail to adapt to diverse data patterns and non-stationarity.

Method: An agentic GenAutoML framework using LLMs to generate PyTorch implementations; includes Sandboxed Reflection Loop for autonomous code refinement; Signature-Aware Runtime to enforce architecture consistency and safety; Dyn-RevIN for non-stationary data; evaluation on ETTh1, ETThm1, Weather benchmarks; development of WaveInterferenceNet.

Result: GenAutoML can dynamically generate task-specific architectures; WaveInterferenceNet achieves sub-0.01 ms per-sample latency with competitive predictive accuracy; demonstrates robustness and efficiency for edge deployments.

Conclusion: Focuses on computational efficiency, adaptability, and stable optimization to enable ultra-lightweight networks suitable for edge AI.

Abstract: Designing neural architectures for time-series forecasting and anomaly detection remains a resource-intensive task that often requires substantial domain expertise. Traditional Automated Machine Learning (AutoML) systems typically rely on static, predefined search spaces, limiting their ability to adapt to diverse data characteristics. We present GenAutoML, an agentic framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) as neural architects to bridge natural-language requirements and executable PyTorch implementations. The framework incorporates a Sandboxed Reflection Loop for autonomous code refinement and a Signature-Aware Runtime that enforces architectural consistency and execution safety. To improve robustness under non-stationary conditions, we further introduce a Dynamic Reversible Instance Normalization (Dyn-RevIN) wrapper. Experiments on the ETTh1, ETTm1, and Weather benchmarks demonstrate that GenAutoML can dynamically generate task-specific neural architectures tailored to dataset characteristics. Among the generated models, WaveInterferenceNet achieves inference latency below 0.01 ms per sample while maintaining competitive predictive performance. By emphasizing computational efficiency, architectural adaptability, and stable optimization behavior, GenAutoML enables the creation of ultra-lightweight neural networks suitable for resource-constrained and latency-sensitive Edge AI deployments.

</details>


### [330] [Deciphering Two Training Clocks in Grokking via Deep Linear Network Theory with Conditional ReLU Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05863)
*Hu Tan,Kuo Gai,Shihua Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Grokking is explained as two distinct training clocks: a fast fitting of the training data (loss decay) and a slower simplification of the learned representation. In deep linear nets, under gap-growth or tail-contraction conditions, cross-entropy can be driven to epsilon on a logarithmic time scale; with layerwise weight decay, a Schatten-type penalty induces polynomial-time simplification via a Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail. The same mechanism appears in ReLU networks: in fixed activation regions, the network is linear on active coordinates; in a two-layer ReLU embedding model, the classifier head can receive larger gradients than the embedding block, suggesting a two-stage learning where the classifier fits first and the representation simplifies later. Experiments focus on modular addition. The deep linear theory provides a rigorous core; ReLU results are conditional reductions rather than global proofs for nonlinear training dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formalize the grokking phenomenon by decoupling fast loss minimization from slow representation learning, and bridge deep linear theory with nonlinear ReLU networks to explain two-stage training dynamics.

Method: Theoretical analysis of deep linear networks with conditions such as post-margin gap-growth or one-step tail-contraction; incorporation of layerwise weight decay leading to a Schatten-type penalty; application of Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail arguments for late-time dynamics; extension to ReLU MLPs via regions of fixed activation patterns and chain-rule gradient estimates in a two-layer embedding model; empirical validation using modular addition as a testbed; results are framed as conditional reductions rather than global nonlinear proofs.

Result: Identification of two training clocks separating fitting from representation learning. For deep linear nets, loss reaches epsilon on a logarithmic time scale under specified conditions; weight decay plus structural energy yields polynomial-time convergence due to a Schatten penalty and KL-tail; in ReLU networks, the mechanism is supported by regional linearization and gradient dynamics, enabling a two-stage learning where the classifier fits before the representation simplifies; modular addition experiments illustrate the phenomenon, with the deep-linear theory providing a rigorous core and the ReLU analysis offering conditional reductions.

Conclusion: Grokking can be understood as a two-stage optimization phenomenon arising from distinct timescales for fitting and representation simplification. The work provides a rigorous foundation for linear models and a plausible, conditional extension to nonlinear ReLU networks, with empirical validation on modular addition. Limitations include reliance on strong assumptions and conditional results for nonlinear dynamics, and scope limited to specific settings rather than general architectures.

Abstract: Grokking suggests that fitting the training data and learning a simple underlying rule may occur on different time scales. We formalize this phenomenon by separating the fast decay of the classification loss from the slower simplification of the learned representation, and we call the resulting pair of stopping times two training clocks. For deep linear networks, we show that a post-margin gap-growth or one-step tail-contraction condition reduces the cross-entropy loss to level epsilon on a logarithmic time scale. In contrast, when layerwise weight decay is present, the induced regularization on the end-to-end map can be expressed as a Schatten-type penalty; under a sharp late-time Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail, this structural energy closes on a polynomial time scale. The two clocks, therefore, separate fitting from representation simplification. We then explain how the same mechanism can appear in ReLU MLPs. In regions where the activation patterns on the training set remain fixed, the network reduces to a linear model in the active coordinates. In a two-layer ReLU embedding model, chain-rule estimates further show that the classifier head can receive larger effective gradients than the embedding block under controlled downstream norms. This supports a two-stage mechanism in which the classifier fits first, while the representation continues to simplify later. We use modular addition as the main experimental setting. The deep linear theory provides the rigorous core of the analysis. But the ReLU results are formulated as conditional reductions that account for empirical behavior without claiming a global proof for nonlinear training dynamics.

</details>


### [331] [TS-ICL: A Flexible Time-Indexed Foundation Model for Time Series via In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05878)
*Etienne Le Naour,Tahar Nabil,Adrien Petralia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TS-ICL is a probabilistic In-Context Learning encoder–regressor Transformer that unifies forecasting and imputation for irregular, partially observed time series by treating tasks as timestamp-aligned regression and incorporating covariates via a novel causal data prior; it achieves state-of-the-art imputation and competitive forecasting, especially with partially observed look-back windows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models promise general-purpose zero-shot time series capabilities, but real-world data are often irregular and partially observed. There is a need for models that jointly forecast and impute, rather than focusing solely on forecasting.
