Method: Introduce TS-ICL, a probabilistic In-Context Learning (ICL) encoder–regressor Transformer. Formulate tasks as timestamp-aligned regression. Incorporate covariates by training on synthetic dependency structures generated from a novel causal data prior. The model is designed to jointly forecast and impute under degraded sampling conditions.

Result: TS-ICL achieves state-of-the-art performance in time-series imputation and is competitive with leading forecasting foundation models on both univariate and covariate-aware benchmarks. It particularly excels in forecasting with partially observed look-back windows.

Conclusion: TS-ICL demonstrates that a unified probabilistic ICL-based encoder–regressor framework can jointly handle forecasting and imputation under irregular sampling, leveraging causal data priors to better capture dependencies. This supports a broader foundation-model-like approach for time-series tasks.

Abstract: Foundation models mark a profound paradigm shift in time series modeling, with task-specific models being superseded by general-purpose zero-shot models. Yet, current approaches primarily focus on forecasting, while real-world time series are often irregularly and partially observed, requiring models that can jointly forecast, impute missing values, and handle degraded sampling conditions. To address these challenges, we introduce TS-ICL, a novel probabilistic In-Context Learning encoder--regressor Transformer that unifies forecasting and imputation. TS-ICL formulates time series tasks as timestamp-aligned regression and naturally incorporates covariates by training on synthetic dependency structures generated from a novel causal data prior. Empirically, TS-ICL achieves a new state-of-the-art in imputation, while remaining competitive with leading forecasting foundation models across both univariate and covariate-aware benchmarks. It shows particularly strong performance in forecasting with partially observed look-back windows.

</details>


### [332] [When Denser Credit Is Not Enough: Evidence-Calibrated Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon LLM Agent Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05885)
*Yuanfan Li,Qi Zhou,Wenjing Duan,Lu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ECPO introduces critic-free, evidence-calibrated policy optimization to improve long-horizon RL for LLM agents by addressing unreliable dense credit from anchor states. It combines two calibrations—Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage and Variance-Gated Credit Weighting—to stabilize learning with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon tasks with sparse/delayed rewards suffer from unreliable step-level credit when using dense advantages. Existing group-based methods like GiGPO can overfit to rare lucky actions due to limited rollouts, causing anchor bias and training oscillations.

Method: ECPO decomposes credit using: (1) Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage, which groups rollouts by canonical actions and shrinks low-count estimates; (2) Variance-Gated Credit Weighting, which downweights anchor states where within-action noise dominates. The approach is critic-free, avoiding value-function estimation.

Result: On ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B, ECPO consistently outperforms strong baselines, adding +5.2 and +7.3 success points over GiGPO, respectively, with only ~0.1% extra overhead for advantage computation.

Conclusion: ECPO provides more stable and reliable credit assignment for long-horizon RL in LLM agents, achieving better performance than GiGPO with minimal computational cost.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents require reinforcement learning methods that can assign credit to intermediate decisions under sparse and delayed rewards. Recent group-based methods such as GiGPO improve over GRPO by constructing step-level advantages at repeated anchor states. However, we show that such dense credit can be statistically unreliable: under limited rollouts, rare but lucky actions may receive overly large advantages, producing divergent anchor bias and late-stage training oscillation. We propose Evidence-Calibrated Policy Optimization (ECPO), a critic-free policy optimization algorithm that calibrates step-level credit before policy updates. ECPO combines Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage, which groups rollouts by canonical actions and shrinks low-count estimates, with Variance-Gated Credit Weighting, which suppresses anchor states dominated by within-action noise. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B show that ECPO consistently outperforms strong baselines, improving GiGPO by +5.2/+7.3 success points on ALFWorld/WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B while adding only 0.1% additional advantage-computation overhead.

</details>


### [333] [High-Dimensional Theory of LoRA Fine-Tuning in a Solvable Attention Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05899)
*O. Duranthon,F. Boncoraglio,L. Zdeborová*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Solvable high-dimensional theory of LoRA in attention: a rank-one, post-pretraining adaptation of a single-head layer yields explicit order-parameter predictions for test error and representation alignment; pretraining acts as an effective noise term and informs optimal pretraining and active fine-tuning strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how pre-training and low-rank adaptation interact in high dimensions, and to predict test performance and representation quality to guide pretraining and active fine-tuning.

Method: A tractable framework where a single-head attention layer is pre-trained on a data-rich task and then adapted with a rank-one LoRA update using limited data. In the high-dimensional limit, the dynamics reduce to a finite set of order parameters, enabling closed-form predictions for test error and representation alignment; the pre-training influence is captured as an effective noise term.

Result: Provides explicit asymptotic predictions for test error and representation alignment, yields prescriptions for optimal pre-training, reveals regimes where test error and representation quality diverge, and shows how the theory can inform active fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The framework clarifies when and how pre-training benefits LoRA in high dimensions, offering practical guidelines for pretraining design and a pathway to active fine-tuning, while highlighting simplifications (single head, rank-one LoRA) and potential extensions.

Abstract: We develop a high-dimensional statistical theory of low-rank adaptation (LoRA) in attention models, capturing the interplay between pre-training and fine-tuning. We introduce a solvable framework in which a single-head attention layer is first pre-trained on a data-abundant task and subsequently adapted via a rank-one LoRA update on limited data. In the high-dimensional limit, both stages admit a sharp asymptotic characterization in terms of a finite set of order parameters, yielding explicit predictions for test errors and representation alignment. Our analysis shows that the impact of pre-training on LoRA is summarized by an effective noise term, from which we derive prescriptions for the optimal pre-training procedure. We also demonstrate a regime with a mismatch between the value of the test error and representation quality, and propose an application of our theory to active fine-tuning.

</details>


### [334] [Addressing Imbalance in Multi-Label Data via Label-Specific Distance-based Oversampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05927)
*Bin Liu,Jun Wu,Haoyu Peng,Ao Zhou,Jin Wang,QiaoSong Chen,Grigorios Tsoumakas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A label-specific, distance-based oversampling method (LSDMLO) improves multi-label data balancing by generating better-labeled synthetic samples using label-aware distances in a weighted feature space, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imbalanced multi-label datasets cause classifiers to be biased toward majority and high-frequency labels; existing oversampling relies on global Euclidean neighborhoods and can produce label-inconsistent, overfitted synthetic instances.

Method: Introduce LSDMLO, which computes label-specific distances to identify label-consistent neighbors within a weighted feature space; selects seed instances expressing stronger label correlations in boundary regions and generates synthetic samples that reflect the original label distribution.

Result: Comprehensive experiments show LSDMLO outperforms state-of-the-art multi-label sampling approaches across various base classifiers.

Conclusion: LSDMLO effectively addresses imbalance and label inconsistency in multi-label oversampling, improving generalization by producing better-labeled synthetic data and demonstrating the potential of label-aware distances in this domain.

Abstract: The complex imbalanced label distribution poses a crucial challenge to multi-label classification, as most classifiers are biased towards the majority class and high-frequent labels. Oversampling is an efficient and flexible solution that augments instances to provide a more balanced training dataset for multi-label classifiers. Most existing oversampling methods create synthetic instances in a heuristic way that essentially relies on neighborhood information retrieved using Euclidean distance within the entire feature space. However, they fail to consider the varying semantic relevance of features to different labels, leading to label inconsistency among proximate neighbors and further introducing label confusion and overfitting to synthetic instances. To overcome the above issue, we propose a novel sampling approach called Label-Specific Distance-based Multi-Label Oversampling (LSDMLO) that creates more useful and well-labeled synthetic instances to address the imbalance in multi-label datasets. LSDMLO derives the label-specific distance to identify label-consistent neighbors based on the weighted pertinent feature space, which facilitates selecting seed instances that express more label correlations in boundary areas and generating synthetic instances aligned with the label distribution of original data. The comprehensive experiments verify that the proposed LSDMLO outperforms the state-of-the-art multi-label sampling approaches under various base classifiers.

</details>


### [335] [Short paper: Models in the dark -- Rectification and erasure under GDPR in ML supply chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05946)
*Henrik Graßhoff,Malte Hansen,Meiko Jensen,Sara Ramezanian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A holistic, interdisciplinary survey shows that enforcing GDPR rights to rectification and erasure in ML is currently limited by technical infeasibility and opaque ML supply chains, and introduces 'models in the dark' as a key risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between legal data-protection rights and technical ML implementation across the end-to-end development and deployment supply chain, ensuring trustworthy AI.

Method: Systematic review of academic literature and data-protection authority guidance; synthesis across legal and technical perspectives; introduction of the 'models in the dark' concept to frame opaque downstream model origins.

Result: Many GDPR requirements cannot yet be technically met in practice; ML supply chains have insufficiently addressed issues in research; the 'models in the dark' phenomenon highlights urgent transparency and traceability challenges.

Conclusion: An interdisciplinary approach is needed to align legal rights with technical capabilities, advancing trustworthy AI and guiding future research toward practical, transparent ML supply-chain governance.

Abstract: The rights to rectification and erasure, as established under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), are central to protecting individuals' privacy. However, their effective enforcement in machine learning (ML) systems remains challenging. Existing work has largely addressed these rights from either a legal or a technical perspective in isolation and disregards the fact that models are produced in complex supply chains involving multiple actors across development, distribution, and deployment. This paper presents a holistic survey of challenges in implementing the rights to rectification and erasure in ML models. Drawing on academic literature and guidance from data protection authorities, we find that many GDPR requirements cannot yet be technically met in practice. Our findings further suggest that issues arising in ML supply chains are insufficiently addressed in research. To tackle this gap, we introduce the notion of models in the dark -- derived models created further downstream in an ML chain without sufficient transparency or traceability -- and analyse the urgent challenges posed by this phenomenon. By adopting an interdisciplinary perspective, this work contributes to bridging the gap between legal requirements and the technical implementation of data subject rights in ML, ultimately supporting the development of trustworthy artificial intelligence.

</details>


### [336] [Dead Directions: Geometric Singular Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05957)
*Tejas Pradeep Shirodkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bridge singular learning theory and information geometry by identifying the dead direction where the Fisher metric degenerates, recover KL order from the decay of directional curvature, and extend to multi-component singularities; apply via deep-network K-FAC factorization, gauge quotient, and a novel Adam variant to read Watanabe's invariants from a single checkpoint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two established frameworks (singular learning theory and information geometry) use different coordinates and assumptions. The paper aims to unify them around a primitive dead direction, enabling calculable KL-order invariants (λ, m, ν) and practical training dynamics predictions in modern networks.

Method: Define a dead direction as a unit vector tangent to the analytic singular set with a defined KL order. Relate KL order to the decay rate of directional Fisher curvature in original coordinates without resolution. Generalize to multi-component crossings (multiplicity m) and singular fluctuation ν. Translate KL rate to Watanabe's RLCT contributions via a selection rule on smooth fibres. Develop deep-network instantiations via a multi-layer K-FAC factorization, linking activation- and gradient-side rates. Prove a quotient theorem for gradient flow on a G-invariant metric, enabling a gauge quotient Θ/G; show SGD preserves it but standard Adam does not. Propose a G-equivariant Adam variant (DDCAdam). Show practical readouts: per-architecture predictions, and trajectory-rate readout of (λ, m, ν) from one forward/backward pass without sampling.

Result: A unified framework yielding per-architecture predictions and practical readouts of singularity invariants. A translation from KL-order to RLCT components, extension to multiple singular components, and a practical toolkit (DDCAdam) for training with gauge invariance. Demonstration that singular geometry informs training dynamics and can be inferred from a single checkpoint through forward/backward rates.

Conclusion: The bridge furnishes a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry and yields explicit, architecture-aware predictions. It enables immediate extraction of Watanabe invariants from a single checkpoint and connects deep-network training (via K-FAC and DDCAdam) to the underlying singular structure without posterior sampling.

Abstract: Singular learning theory and information geometry have studied the same parameter spaces in mostly separate vocabularies: the former computes Bayesian invariants in resolved coordinates, the latter works in original coordinates under a non-degeneracy assumption that overparameterised models routinely violate. We bridge them through one primitive, the dead direction: a unit vector along which the Fisher metric degenerates, equivalently a tangent to the analytic singular set with a definite KL order, set by how fast the KL divergence vanishes. The two readings name the same vector; our central move shows its KL order is recoverable as the decay rate of the directional Fisher curvature approaching the singularity, in original parameter coordinates and without a Hironaka resolution. A selection rule on smooth fibres translates this rate into Watanabe's single-direction contribution to the real log canonical threshold, and we extend the recovery to multi-component crossings, multiplicity $m$, the singular fluctuation $ν$ (universal in the KL order for 1D directions), prior-RLCT shifts, and tempered posteriors. We then lift this rate to a deep network: a multi-layer K-FAC factorisation writes each Fisher block as a product of activation- and gradient-side rates with a duality between them, instantiated at modern-network primitives (residual streams, layer normalisation, attention). A quotient theorem carries the rate to the gauge quotient $Θ/G$ under gradient flow on a $G$-invariant metric; SGD qualifies, standard Adam does not, and we construct a $G$-equivariant Adam-family preconditioner (DDCAdam) that does. The bridge yields a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry, closed-form per-architecture predictions, and a trajectory-rate readout of Watanabe's triple $(λ, m, ν)$ from one checkpoint's forward and backward passes, without posterior sampling.

</details>


### [337] [Steering Vectors are an Adversarial Attack Surface](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05958)
*Abzal Aidakhmetov,Donato Crisostomi,Tommaso Mencattini,Adrian Robert Minut,Iacopo Masi,Emanuele Rodolà*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Poisoning attack on activation steering: attacker subtly substitutes 4-6% of tokens to align steering vectors with an anti-refusal direction, enabling jailbreaks while preserving benign steering; defense via refusal-direction orthogonalization can recover ~82% of the ASR gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess security of plug-and-play activation steering for LLMs and potential stealth data poisoning attacks.

Method: Replace 4-6% of tokens in steering dataset; craft payload vectors; evaluate on two open-weight model families across eight attribute combinations; measure absolute ASR and improvement over clean; test a defense by orthogonalizing with respect to refusal direction.

Result: Poisoned vectors achieve 20-55% absolute ASR; 19-51 percentage-point increase over clean; defense recovers ~82% of the ASR gap without harming benign prompts.

Conclusion: Activation steering is vulnerable to stealth data poisoning; a simple orthogonalization defense mitigates much of the risk; further work needed to close remaining gap and harden pipelines.

Abstract: Activation steering has become a popular way to control Large Language Model (LLM) behavior without fine-tuning. Since the technique is plug-and-play, users share datasets and precomputed vectors to steer model activations. However, we show that a \emph{stealth data poisoning attack} silently compromises this pipeline. By substituting $4{-}6\%$ of tokens in the steering dataset, an attacker can silently align the resulting vector with an anti-refusal direction. This jailbreaks the target model while preserving the intended steering effect on benign prompts. Under this threat model, a malicious actor can distribute an apparently safe bundle containing texts, vectors, and weights, alongside an equivalence certificate that the end-user can verify. We test the attack on two open-weight model families and eight model-attribute combinations, observing that poisoned vectors reach an absolute attack success rate (ASR) of $20{-}55\%$, $+19\%$ to $+51\%$ over a clean reference. Finally, we find that a refusal-direction orthogonalization defense can recover ${\approx}82\%$ of the ASR gap without harming benign behavior.

</details>


### [338] [LLM Explainability with Counterfactual Chains and Causal Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05972)
*Nirit Nussbaum-Hoffer,Nitay Calderon,Liat Ein-Dor,Roi Reichart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes modeling LLM inference as a causal graph over concept states, using a four-phase pipeline to identify discriminative concepts and map inputs to LLM-perceived concepts, aided by an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation (σ-CG) for robust causal discovery; evaluated on disease diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and LLM-as-a-judge tasks, showing interpretable, predictive graphs and stable inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide transparent, concept-level explainability of LLM reasoning by making the model's internal inference process observable as a causal graph, addressing the opacity of large language models.

Method: Four-phase pipeline: (1) discover class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts; (2) map each input to LLM-perceived concept states; (3) apply MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation to generate chains of counterfactuals, expanding sparse data; (4) perform causal discovery with σ-CG to yield informative, interpretable graphs.

Result: Learned graphs exhibit meaningful dependencies aligned with LLM reasoning; the counterfactual augmentation improves convergence and downstream utility; across three tasks, graphs achieve predictive fidelity and structural stability.

Conclusion: The work establishes concept-level explainability for LLMs via causal graphs of the inference process, providing a foundation for transparent interpretation of how LLMs organize high-level concepts to reach predictions.

Abstract: Causal graphs provide a high-level language for making mechanisms transparent. Recent work uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to recover causal graphs of external-world processes. Instead, in this paper, we use causal graphs to model LLM inference itself, providing stakeholders with a transparent view of how the model perceives and organizes high-level concepts to produce a prediction. We propose a four-phase method for constructing such graphs. Given a target LLM and a set of textual examples, our method discovers class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts and maps each input to LLM-perceived concept states. We then introduce an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation procedure that expands the sparse observational data through chains of counterfactuals. This enables stable causal discovery with $σ$-CG, yielding informative, interpretable graphs. We apply our method to three LLMs across disease diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and LLM-as-a-judge classification tasks. We evaluate the learned graphs for predictive fidelity and structural stability, and the MCMC-inspired augmentation for convergence and downstream utility. Our results show that the discovered causal graphs capture meaningful dependencies consistent with LLMs' reasoning. Together, this paper provides a foundation for concept-level explainability of LLMs.

</details>


### [339] [Compress-Distill: Reasoning Trace Compression for Efficient Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05988)
*Maxime Griot,Paul Steven Scotti,Tanishq Mathew Abraham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc compression of reasoning traces (chain-of-thought) for distillation yields substantial efficiency gains but does not surpass raw traces in accuracy; compression offers an accuracy-efficiency trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long chain-of-thought traces during reasoning are costly to distill and produce verbose outputs. Reducing trace length post-hoc aims to maintain performance while improving training efficiency and inference latency.

Method: Two large-language-model teachers (Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B) generate ~283k correct traces each. Students (instruction-tuned) compress traces to 8.6–21.0% of their original length. A broad evaluation: 48-grid main experiments plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations. Comparisons are made between compressed traces, raw traces, and length-matched truncations.

Result: Compression reduces training tokens to 12–30% of raw, speeds training 2.0–7.6x, and shortens inference outputs by 3–19x. Raw traces yield the highest downstream accuracy at all scales and for both teachers. A length-matched truncation shows compression benefits extend beyond budget effects: model-compressed traces often beat or match naive truncation, notably for smaller students. Up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy is retained, with up to 18x per-token efficiency gains. At 0.8B scale with LoRA, compressed traces narrow the raw-vs-compressed gap but do not exceed raw.

Conclusion: Reasoning-trace compression provides a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off rather than a blanket improvement: significant efficiency gains come with modest accuracy loss. Depending on scale and method (teacher, compression level), compressed traces can approach but not surpass raw traces; decisions should weigh desired throughput versus accuracy targets.

Abstract: Reasoning models produce long chain-of-thought traces that are costly to distill and encourage verbose student outputs. We study post-hoc compression of such traces before knowledge distillation. Two teachers, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B, generate about 283k correct traces each; two instruction-tuned models then compress them to 8.6-21.0% of their original character length. Across a 48-run main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, compressed traces reduce training tokens to 12-30% of raw, speed up training by 2.0-7.6x, and shorten inference outputs by 3-19x with smaller reductions under the shorter gpt-oss teacher. However, raw traces retain the highest downstream accuracy at every scale and for both teachers. A length-matched raw-trace truncation ablation shows that compression is not merely benefiting from a smaller token budget: model-compressed traces usually beat or match naive truncation, especially for smaller students, while maintaining shorter inference outputs. Overall, reasoning-trace compression offers an accuracy-efficiency trade-off rather than a free improvement: students retain up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy while gaining up to 18x higher per-token efficiency, and at the 0.8B scale under LoRA compressed traces narrow the raw-vs-compressed gap but do not exceed raw.

</details>


### [340] [HoT-SSM:Higher-order Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning with State Space Models for Health Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05994)
*Thummaluru Siddartha Reddy,Vempalli Naga Sai Saketh,Yash Punjabi,Mahesh Chandran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HoT-SSM introduces a higher-order temporal graph approach using per-visit hypergraphs and a dynamic state-space model to capture higher-order clinical interactions and long-range temporal dependencies in MKGs for EHR prediction, achieving state-of-the-art results on MIMIC datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MKGs typically model only pairwise relations and often fail to preserve temporal dynamics across visits, limiting predictive performance for tasks like mortality prediction. There is a need to model higher-order interactions and long-range temporal dependencies while maintaining efficiency.

Method: Per visit, construct hypergraphs by grouping clinically related concepts into hyperedges using domain knowledge; develop a dynamic hypergraph-based state-space model to capture latent state evolution over time with long-range information; optimize for downstream prediction with parameter-efficient architecture.

Result: Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV show significant improvements over state-of-the-art models, validating that modeling higher-order interactions and long-range temporal dependencies enhances predictive performance.

Conclusion: Joint higher-order clinical interactions and long-range temporal reasoning yield more expressive, accurate MKG-based representations for EHR prediction and reasoning; the approach has potential for broader clinical tasks.

Abstract: Medical knowledge graphs (MKGs) infused with clinical knowledge have been increasingly used to model electronic health records (EHRs) to support interpretable predictions in healthcare domain. However, existing MKG-based approaches are limited in capturing pairwise relations between clinical concepts (e.g., conditions, procedures, and medications), and restricts their ability to model higher-order interactions among co-occurring or semantically related concepts. In addition, most representation learning methods that leverage MKGs either collapse temporal information across visits or lack an explicit mechanism for modeling long-range temporal dependencies, which is critical for clinical tasks such as mortality prediction. To mitigate these limitations, we propose HoT-SSM, a parameter efficient and higher-order temporal graph reasoning with state space models. For each visit, HoT-SSM constructs hypergraphs by grouping semantically related clinical concepts into hyperedges using domain knowledge, thereby preserving visit-level clinical context. Further, to model the temporal dynamics while learning the representations, we introduce a novel dynamic hypergraph-based state space model that explicitly captures patients latent state evolution over time while preserving long-range information. The learned representations are used for downstream clinical prediction and reasoning. Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV datasets shows significant performance improvement over the current state-of-the-art models, demonstrating the effectiveness of jointly modeling higher-order clinical interactions and long-range temporal dependencies.

</details>


### [341] [Diffusion Models for Adaptive Sequential Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06007)
*Haoyang Cao,Minshuo Chen,Yinbin Han,Renyuan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Sequential Forward-Backward Diffusion (SFBD) for adapted time-series generation, addressing temporal dependence without peeking future information; proposes a novel score-matching objective and provides theoretical guarantees plus empirical validation on ARMA, Gaussian processes, and portfolio optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To generate realistic sequential data with diffusion models that respect temporal structure and do not rely on future information, overcoming limitations of static or non-adapted sequential diffusion methods.

Method: Proposes a sequential forward-backward diffusion process that progressively injects and removes noise along the sequence, conditioning on previously generated history to ensure adaptiveness. Introduces a novel score-matching objective enabling efficient parallel training. Develops a generic statistical framework with theoretical guarantees; implements ReLU networks for score, estimation, and distribution estimation. Empirically validates on synthetic data (ARMA, Gaussian processes) and applies to mean-variance optimal portfolio construction.

Result: Rigorous statistical guarantees for score approximation, score estimation, and distribution estimation within the generic framework. Empirical validation demonstrates effective generation of sequential data and practical utility in portfolio optimization.

Conclusion: The framework advances diffusion-based sequential data generation by ensuring adaptiveness and non-anticipation, backed by theory and empirical results, with potential impact on risk assessment and data-driven decision-making in temporally structured domains.

Abstract: Generating realistic synthetic sequential data is critical in real-world applications across operations research, finance, healthcare, energy systems, and scientific computing, where time-indexed observations are used for prediction, simulation, risk assessment, and data-driven decision-making. While diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in generating static data, their direct extensions to sequential settings often fail to capture temporal dependence and information structure. Designing diffusion models that can simulate sequential data in an adapted manner, and hence without anticipation of future information, therefore remains an open challenge.
  In this work, we propose a sequential forward-backward diffusion framework for adapted time series generation. Our approach progressively injects and removes noise along the sequence, conditioning on the previously generated history to ensure adaptiveness. A novel score-matching objective is introduced for efficient parallel training. We derive rigorous statistical guarantees under a generic framework, then establish score approximation, score estimation, and distribution estimation results with ReLU networks serving as a concrete instance. Empirically, we validate our method on synthetic data, including ARMA models and Gaussian processes, and demonstrate its effectiveness in constructing mean-variance optimal portfolios.

</details>


### [342] [Adaptive Oscillatory-State Alignment for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06010)
*Zhangyao Song,Ziqiong Li,Xiangfei Qiu,Chao Zha,Yinfei Xu,Tao Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AOSNET introduces a Hilbert-guided, adaptive oscillatory-state alignment for long-term forecasting, replacing fixed periodic templates with a learnable global oscillatory prior and a descriptor-conditioned gate to align observed sequence states. It achieves state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy with fast inference on eight benchmarks, and its advantage grows with non-stationarity (amplitude modulation, phase drift, local frequency variation).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world temporal dynamics are often non-stationary oscillations with amplitude modulation, phase drift, and varying local frequency. Fixed-template periodic models struggle to adapt, leading to mismatches and degraded long-horizon forecasts. An adaptive, signal-based alignment can better capture these evolving structures while preserving efficient inference.

Method: The framework extracts analytic-signal descriptors (via Hilbert transform) from both the observed sequence and a learnable global oscillatory prior. It then adaptively aligns local states through a descriptor-conditioned gate that preserves reliable observations and softly corrects mismatches. The prior acts as a flexible oscillatory reference interpreted through local state dynamics, rather than a fixed template.

Result: Empirical evaluation on eight benchmarks shows state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy with fast inference. Controlled synthetic studies isolate amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation, confirming that the advantage of oscillatory-state alignment increases as non-stationarity intensifies.

Conclusion: AOSNET presents a Hilbert-guided, adaptive approach to periodic forecasting that replaces rigid templates with flexible oscillatory-state alignment. It delivers strong empirical performance and provides insights into handling non-stationary oscillatory dynamics in long-term forecasting.

Abstract: Long-term time series forecasting benefits from inductive biases that expose recurring temporal structure. Existing periodic forecasting methods typically model recurrence through predefined periods, global spectral components, or fixed learnable templates. However, real-world temporal dynamics are rarely rigidly periodic: oscillatory behavior often evolves through amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation. Under these conditions, fixed-template periodic modeling can become fundamentally mismatched to the underlying temporal states. We propose AOSNET, a Hilbert-guided forecasting framework that reformulates periodic forecasting from fixed template matching to adaptive oscillatory-state alignment. AOSNET extracts analytic-signal descriptors from both the observed sequence and a learnable global oscillatory prior, then adaptively aligns local states through a descriptor-conditioned gate that selectively preserves reliable observations while softly correcting mismatched regions. The learned prior serves not as a rigid repeated template but as a flexible oscillatory reference interpreted through local state dynamics. Experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy with fast inference speed. Controlled synthetic studies isolating amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation confirm that the advantage of oscillatory-state alignment consistently increases as non-stationarity intensifies.

</details>


### [343] [OPRD: On-Policy Representation Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06021)
*Shenzhi Yang,Guangcheng Zhu,Bowen Song,Haobo Wang,Mingxuan Xia,Xing Zheng,Yingfan Ma,Zhongqi Chen,Weiqiang Wang,Gang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPRD (On-Policy Representation Distillation) aligns student and teacher hidden representations across selected layers during the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head, to reduce sampling variance and improve efficiency, outperforming output-only OPD on AIME/AIMO benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPD's output-only supervision incurs sampling variance from Monte Carlo KL estimates with large vocabularies and treats the teacher as a black box, discarding intermediate hidden states.

Method: Align student and teacher representations across chosen layers on the same rollouts in hidden-state space, without using the LM head. This provides per-layer structural supervision and theoretically eliminates sampling variance.

Result: OPRD closes the student–teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO; OPD baselines plateau below the teacher. Training is 1.44x faster and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD.

Conclusion: OPRD provides richer, per-layer supervision in hidden-state space, eliminates sampling variance, and improves efficiency and performance versus output-only distillation.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) supervises the student only in output space by matching next-token probabilities. This output-only paradigm has two limits: (1) sampling variance from Monte Carlo KL estimates over large vocabularies (e.g., Qwen's ~150k tokens) persists throughout training, and (2) it treats the teacher as a black-box, discarding all intermediate hidden states after the LM head. We propose On-Policy Representation Distillation (OPRD), which lifts distillation into hidden-state space by aligning student and teacher representations across selected layers on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head entirely. Theoretically, OPRD eliminates sampling variance and provides richer per-layer structural information. Empirically, OPRD closes the student-teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO, while output-space OPD baselines plateau below the teacher. OPRD also trains 1.44x faster and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD. Code: https://github.com/ShenzhiYang2000/OPRD.

</details>


### [344] [Catastrophic Forgetting as Accessibility Collapse: A Three-Level Framework for Knowledge Persistence in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06032)
*Ayushman Trivedi,Bhavika Melwani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This work challenges catastrophic forgetting as irreversible erasure and reframes it as an accessibility failure in learned representations. It introduces a three-level framework (storage, representation, accessibility) and uses sequential CIFAR-100 classification with ResNet-18 to dissect forgetting via checkpoint persistence, probing, geometry, and layer-wise recoverability. Findings show complete behavioral forgetting despite substantial retained representational information, with final classifier retraining recovering most performance and early/intermediate layers retaining recoverable information, implying high-dimensional distributed representations underlie knowledge retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reassess the dominant view that catastrophic forgetting erases knowledge entirely during sequential learning, by distinguishing storage, representation, and accessibility, and to determine whether preserved information remains accessible under task interference.

Method: Three-level framework applied to continual learning: analyze knowledge storage, representations, and accessibility. Empirical evaluation on sequential CIFAR-100 classification using ResNet-18, employing checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation geometry analysis, classifier-reset recovery experiments, and layer-wise recoverability assessments. Also uses projection-energy and principal-angle analyses to characterize the geometry of retained knowledge.

Result: Behavioral forgetting occurs: earlier task accuracy collapses from 54.8% to 0%. Yet linear probes retain ~76% of original representational information. Retraining only the final classifier recovers 75.7% of the original task performance without touching the backbone. Layer-wise analysis shows early/intermediate layers retain highly recoverable information despite degradation in later stages. Projection-energy and principal-angle analyses indicate retained knowledge exists in distributed high-dimensional representations rather than a single dominant subspace.

Conclusion: Catastrophic forgetting is better described as an accessibility problem than an erasure of representations. A substantial portion of task-relevant information remains embedded in neural representations even after functional forgetting, suggesting new directions for mitigating forgetting by improving accessibility rather than solely preserving representations.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is commonly interpreted as the irreversible erasure of previously acquired knowledge during sequential learning. In this work, we investigate an alternative perspective: that forgetting may arise not from complete destruction of task representations but from a loss of accessibility to preserved information. We introduce a three-level framework separating knowledge storage, representation, and accessibility, and evaluate each component through a series of continual-learning experiments on sequential CIFAR-100 classification using ResNet-18. Our analysis combines checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation geometry, classifier-reset recovery, and layer-wise recoverability experiments. We observe complete behavioral forgetting of earlier tasks, with task accuracy collapsing from 54.8% to 0%, while linear probe performance retains approximately 76% of the original representational information. Furthermore, retraining only the final classifier restores 75.7% of the original task performance without modifying the backbone network. Layer-wise analysis reveals that early and intermediate layers preserve highly recoverable task information despite severe degradation at later stages. Projection-energy and principal-angle analyses indicate that retained knowledge persists as distributed high-dimensional representations rather than through preservation of a small dominant subspace. These findings suggest that catastrophic forgetting is better characterized as an accessibility failure than complete representational erasure, and that substantial task-relevant information remains embedded within neural representations even after functional forgetting has occurred.

</details>


### [345] [When Good Enough Is Optimal: Multiplication-Only Matrix Inversion Approximation for Quantized Gated DeltaNet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06034)
*Luoming Zhang,Yuwei Ren,Kui Zhang,Tian Liu,Lingjuan Ge,Denghao Li,Matthew Harper Langston,Yin Huang,Weiliang Will Zeng,Liang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fast, hardware-friendly matrix-inversion method for chunk-wise linear attention using a MatMul-based truncated Neumann expansion with masking and residual correction; extends to low-bit INT with dynamic-range mitigation; achieves up to 5x kernel speedup and 20% decode-layer overhead reduction on Qwen3.5 models while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context modeling with transformer architectures is bottlenecked by computing inverses in the attention mechanism, particularly on NPUs where forward-substitution limits parallelism. The chunk-wise linear attention setting yields strictly lower-triangular matrices, motivating a parallelizable, hardware-efficient inversion.

Method: Introduce a MatMul-based algorithm for strictly lower-triangular matrices in chunk-wise linear attention. Use a truncated Neumann expansion leveraging diagonal concentration of the inverse, with structural masking and parallel residual correction to remove sequential dependencies. Extend to low-bits INT by controlling dynamic range from repeated matrix powers, and adapt the approximation order and residual steps to the chunk size to balance cost and accuracy.

Result: Experiments on Qwen3.5-family models show up to 5× kernel-level speedup and ~20% reduction in decode-layer overhead, with preserved accuracy under both floating-point and low-precision inference.

Conclusion: Offers an efficient, hardware-friendly solution for scalable linear attention suitable for long-context modeling on NPUs, combining fast MatMul-based inversion with adaptive-precision strategies and residual correction to maintain accuracy.

Abstract: Matrix inversion in chunk-wise parallel linear attention is a major bottleneck for long-context modeling, particularly on NPUs, where forward-substitution-based methods exhibit limited parallelism and poor hardware utilization. We propose a fast, Matrix Multiplication (MatMul)-based algorithm tailored for strictly lower-triangular matrices arising in chunk-wise linear attention. Motivated by the rapid growth of Neumann-series terms and the diagonal concentration of the inverse matrix, we employ a truncated Neumann expansion with structural masking and parallel residual correction to eliminate sequential dependencies. We further extend our method to low-bits INT by mitigating the dynamic range expansion arising from repeated matrix power operations, and adapt the approximation order and residual step to the chunk size to minimize computational cost while preserving the model's accuracy. Experiments on Qwen3.5-family models demonstrate up to 5$\times$ kernel-level speedup and a 20% reduction in decode-layer overhead, while preserving accuracy under both floating-point and low-precision inference. Our method offers an efficient and hardware-friendly solution for scalable linear attention.

</details>


### [346] [Online KL-Regularized Reinforcement Learning with Function Approximation under Misspecification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06053)
*Haoyang Hong,Zichen Wang,Quanquan Gu,Huazheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces KL misspecification frameworks for contextual bandits and episodic RL with Gibbs policy updates; provides high-probability KL-regret bounds that account for misspecification, generalizing realizable KL-regularized results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world RL/CB often relies on function approximation; misspecification breaks realizability; robust regret guarantees under misspecification are needed.

Method: Defines KL misspecification formulations for contextual bandits and episodic RL; analyzes regression-based algorithms with Gibbs policy updates; derives high-probability KL-regret bounds with explicit misspec terms; connects to realizable KL-regularized theory.

Result: High-probability KL-regret bounds with explicit misspecification terms; the results reduce to standard realizable KL-regularized bounds when misspecification is absent.

Conclusion: KL-based RL/CB can tolerate model misspecification while maintaining regret guarantees, unifying misspecified and realizable settings and informing algorithm design under approximate models.

Abstract: We study KL-regularized contextual bandits and episodic reinforcement learning (RL) under general function approximation with model misspecification. Existing guarantees rely on realizability and therefore do not extend to misspecified models, where classical regret bounds may fail. This work introduces KL misspecification formulations for contextual bandits and episodic RL and analyzes regression-based algorithms with Gibbs policy updates. High-probability KL-regret guarantees with explicit misspecification terms are established, recovering the standard realizable KL-regularized setting as a special case.

</details>


### [347] [MDP-GRPO: Stabilized Group Relative Policy Optimization for Multi-Constraint Instruction Following](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06058)
*Mohammad Mahdi Salmani-Zarchi,Zahra Rahimi,Heshaam Faili,Mohammad Javad Dousti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MDP-GRPO stabilizes GRPO under discrete, low-dispersion rewards by addressing z-score normalization pathologies with multi-temperature sampling, dual-anchor advantages, prospect-theoretic shaping, and asymmetric KL, achieving up to 5% gain in strict constraint satisfaction on Llama-3.2-3B and robust convergence with small groups while maintaining capabilities on benchmarks like MMLU/ARC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) becomes unstable when within-group reward distributions are homogeneous (low dispersion). The paper formalizes three z-score normalization pathologies—low-variance amplification, mean-centering blindness, and zero-variance collapse—and seeks a stable learning framework for multi-constraint instruction following.

Method: MDP-GRPO framework with four components: (1) multi-temperature sampling to increase reward dispersion; (2) dual-anchor advantages to restore gradients in homogeneous groups and counter act mean-centering blindness; (3) prospect-theoretic shaping to bound updates and penalize violations following Kahneman–Tversky theory; (4) asymmetric KL regularization to stabilize policy updates.

Result: Empirical evaluation on FollowBench, IFEval, and a curated multi-constraint dataset shows that MDP-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO, achieving up to 5.0% improvement in strict constraint satisfaction on Llama-3.2-3B, with stable convergence for small group sizes and preserved general capabilities on MMLU and ARC.

Conclusion: MDP-GRPO effectively stabilizes learning under discrete, low-dispersion reward regimes and extends GRPO to challenging multi-constraint instruction-following tasks without sacrificing generalization.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is ideal for multi-constraint instruction following, yet standard group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) becomes unstable under discrete, low-dispersion rewards, where within-group reward distributions are frequently homogeneous. We identify and formalize three pathologies of z-score group normalization in this regime: low-variance amplification, mean-centering blindness, and zero-variance collapse. To address them, we propose MDP-GRPO, which stabilizes learning through (1) multi-temperature sampling to increase reward dispersion, (2) dual-anchor advantages to restore gradients in homogeneous groups and stop mean-centering blindness, (3) prospect-theoretic shaping to bound updates and penalize violations based on Kahneman and Tversky's theory, and (4) asymmetric KL regularization. Evaluated on FollowBench, IFEval, and a curated multi-constraint dataset, MDP-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO, improving strict constraint satisfaction by up to 5.0% on Llama-3.2-3B. Our method also enables stable convergence with small group sizes while preserving general capabilities on MMLU and ARC.

</details>


### [348] [On Advantage Estimates for Max@K Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06080)
*Shota Takashiro,Soichiro Nishimori,Paavo Parmas,Yongmin Kim,Kohsei Matsutani,Gouki Minegishi,Yusuke Iwasawa,Takeshi Kojima,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MaxPO introduces a Leave-Two-Out (L2O) baseline to center advantages for max@K objectives in RL-based LLM post-training. It preserves policy-gradient unbiasedness while centering realized batch advantages, enabling a quadratic-time implementation and improved variance reduction. It unifies finite-batch advantage notions for max@K and empirically outperforms non-centered estimators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse outcome rewards hinder exploration in RL-based post-training for LLMs; directly optimizing inference-time objectives (pass@K, max@K) requires stable, unbiased gradient estimators and a clear relationship among existing estimators.

Method: Starting from a leading advantage estimator, show policy-gradient unbiased but non-centered; introduce Leave-Two-Out baseline that preserves unbiasedness while centering batch advantages; propose MaxPO with quadratic-time implementation; integrate with group-based RL for LLM post-training; derive canonical finite-batch advantage for max@K to unify estimators.

Result: L2O baseline reduces gradient variance and outperforms non-centered alternatives in empirical evaluations; MaxPO is efficient and integrates into group-based RL for LLM post-training.

Conclusion: An unbiased, variance-reducing, centered advantage estimator (L2O) enables efficient MaxPO optimization for max@K in LLM post-training, providing a unified finite-batch perspective on existing estimators.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is widely used for post-training reasoning models, but sparse outcome rewards make exploration difficult. A complementary approach is to optimize inference-time objectives such as pass@K and max@K directly, yet existing policy-gradient estimators for these objectives use different signals, baselines, and normalizations, making their relationships unclear. We study this issue through baseline design and advantage centering. Starting from the advantage estimator of a leading method in the field, we show that it is policy-gradient unbiased but yields a non-centered advantage. We then introduce a Leave-Two-Out baseline that preserves policy-gradient unbiasedness while making realized batch advantages exactly centered. The resulting method, MaxPO, has an efficient quadratic-time implementation and integrates naturally into group-based RL for LLM post-training. We further derive the canonical finite-batch advantage for max@K, providing a unified view of existing advantage estimators. Empirically, we verify that the L2O baseline reduces gradient variance and outperforms non-centered alternatives.

</details>


### [349] [OrderGrad: Optimizing Beyond the Mean with Order-Statistic Policy Gradient Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06096)
*Paavo Parmas,Yongmin Kim,Kohsei Matsutani,Shota Takashiro,Soichiro Nishimori,Takeshi Kojima,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrderGrad provides unbiased gradient estimators for finite-sample order-statistic objectives (VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, top-K) using likelihood-ratio and reparameterization methods; it transforms rewards to enable standard policy-gradient or reparameterized updates, enabling risk-averse, robust, and exploratory learning with a unified, plug-and-play approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In many real-world tasks, optimizing the mean return is insufficient; practitioners care about distributional properties like tail risk, outliers, or best-of-K performance. There is a need for unbiased, scalable gradient estimators for order-statistics under finite samples to align optimization with deployment objectives.

Method: Introduce OrderGrad, a family of likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives. For a fixed sample size and rank-weight vector, it yields unbiased gradient estimates for the corresponding order-statistic objective. Implemented as a reward transformation, it can be integrated with standard policy-gradient or reparameterized updates. Analyzed variance and evaluated on tasks where mean optimization is misaligned with the objective (including LLM math post-training).

Result: Derives unbiased gradient estimators for finite-sample L-statistics and characterizes their variance behavior. Demonstrates applicability to objectives like VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, and top-K criteria across tasks where mean-optimization is suboptimal, showing practical benefits in risk-averse and robust learning scenarios.

Conclusion: OrderGrad offers a unified, plug-and-play framework to optimize order-statistic objectives in reinforcement learning, enabling risk-averse, robust, and exploratory behavior by simply adjusting rank weights; code is available for practical adoption.

Abstract: Policy-gradient methods usually optimize expected return, but many real world applications care about distributional properties of returns: tail risk, outlier robustness, or best-of-K discovery. We introduce OrderGrad, a family of likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives. OrderGrad optimizes finite-sample L-statistics, i.e., weighted averages of sorted rewards or costs, recovering objectives such as VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, and top-m/best-of-K criteria by changing only the rank weights. For any fixed sample size and rank-weight vector, OrderGrad provides an unbiased gradient estimator for the corresponding order-statistic objective. The method is implemented as a simple reward transformation that can then be used in an otherwise standard policy-gradient or reparameterized update. We study the resulting estimator's variance behavior and evaluate it on tasks where mean optimization is mismatched to the deployment objective, including LLM math post-training and other tasks. OrderGrad provides a unified, plug-and-play route to risk-averse, robust, and exploratory learning.
  Code: https://github.com/paavo5/ordergrad

</details>


### [350] [A Sliced-Wasserstein Framework on Correlation Matrices for EEG Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06104)
*Chen Hu,Rui Wang,Jiale Zhou,Jingjun Yi,Shaocheng Jin,Yidong Song,Yefeng Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW) for correlation-based EEG decoding. Instantiates CorSW discrepancies on the manifold of full-rank correlation matrices under Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM), and builds a domain generalization framework. Demonstrates improved cross-domain generalization on three EEG datasets with low training overhead and no inference cost; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EEG covariance descriptors are robust to noise but sensitive to channel-wise scaling. Full-rank correlation matrices offer scale-invariant representations, but comparing distributions on correlation manifolds requires geometry-aware distances. The work extends Sliced Wasserstein to manifolds with Pullback Euclidean Metrics to enable robust, scalable domain-generalizable EEG decoding.

Method: Define PEMS to pull back Euclidean metrics to the data space on correlation manifolds. Develop two Correlation Sliced-Wasserstein (CorSW) discrepancies on the manifold of full-rank correlation matrices using OLM and LSM geometries. Integrate CorSW into a domain generalization framework for EEG decoding, enabling distribution-shift robustness with minimal training overhead and no extra inference cost.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three EEG datasets shows improved generalization under distribution shifts, with low training overhead and no additional inference cost.

Conclusion: PEMSW-based CorSW provides a principled, efficient, scale-invariant approach to EEG decoding with strong cross-domain generalization, and the approach is readily reusable with the provided source code.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) offers noninvasive, millisecond resolution recordings of neuronal activity and is widely used in neuroscience and healthcare. Many EEG decoding pipelines rely on covariance descriptors for their robustness to noise, but such representations are sensitive to channel-wise scaling. Recent studies have therefore advocated full-rank correlation matrices as a scale-invariant alternative for EEG decoding. In this paper, we propose a general framework for Sliced Wasserstein (SW) discrepancies on manifolds endowed with Pullback Euclidean Metrics (PEMs), termed Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW). Within this framework, we instantiate two Correlation Sliced-Wasserstein (CorSW) discrepancies on the manifold of full-rank correlation matrices under two recently introduced correlation geometries, \textit{i.e.}, the Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM). Building on CorSW, we further develop a domain generalization (DG) framework for EEG decoding. Experiments on three EEG datasets demonstrate improved generalization under distribution shifts, with low training overhead and no additional inference cost. The source code is available at https://github.com/ChenHu-ML/CorSW.

</details>


### [351] [Adaptive state-action abstractions via rate-distortion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06123)
*Fernando E. Rosas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled, adaptive abstraction refinement framework for RL that switches granularity based on comparing learning error to abstraction error, via a performance certificate combining Bellman residual and bisimulation error, implemented with rate-distortion-based soft abstractions and validated on tabular tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address lack of general principles for dynamically adjusting abstraction granularity in reinforcement learning to balance learning progress against information compression.

Method: Formalize a performance certificate decomposing value error into learning error bound (Bellman residual) and abstraction error bound (bisimulation metric); develop soft state-action abstractions using rate-distortion principles with tunable resolutions; implement a switching strategy to refine abstractions when learning error approaches abstraction error; validate in tabular experiments.

Result: Near-optimal performance under substantial lossy compression of state and action information across test settings.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a principled, tunable framework for adaptive abstraction in RL, enabling efficient learning with compressed representations and dynamic refinement guided by the proposed error decomposition.

Abstract: When learning to walk, infants seem to address a coarse version of the problem first - stay upright, reach the caregiver - and refine it only when further practice at that resolution stops paying off. Reinforcement learning offers multiple techniques for building simple versions of complex tasks, but lacks general principles for how to dynamically adjust the granularity of these abstractions during learning. This paper proposes one such principle: refine the abstraction as soon as the learning error within it becomes comparable to the error induced by the abstraction itself. Here, we investigate one way of formalising this principle via a performance certificate that decomposes value error into two terms: a learning error bound captured by a Bellman residual, and an abstraction error bound given by a bisimulation metric. The resulting switching strategy is implemented by soft state-action abstractions built from rate-distortion principles, whose resolution along state and action axes can be continuously adjusted. We validate this construction in a range of tabular settings, showing that near-optimal performance can be achieved under substantial lossy compression of state and action information.

</details>


### [352] [Tight list replicability bounds via a novel sphere covering theorem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06148)
*Ari Blondal,Hamed Hatami,Pooya Hatami,Chavdar Lalov,Sivan Tretiak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a topological sphere covering theorem (via Borsuk-Ulam) to obtain sharp list-size vs. accuracy bounds in learning theory. Demonstrates a tight bound for VC classes; for large-margin half-spaces the list size matches the ambient dimension under moderate margins, and achieves the minimal bound ceil(d/2)+1 with a replicable algorithm for very large margins.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how the required list size for replicability in learning theory scales with accuracy and intrinsic complexity measures (e.g., VC dimension, data margin), and to derive sharp, provable bounds.

Method: Develops a novel sphere covering theorem derived from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. Applies the theorem to bound list size-accuracy trade-offs for VC classes. Analyzes large-margin half-spaces, characterizes the impact of margin on the optimal list size, and constructs a replicable algorithm achieving the minimal bound in the very large-margin regime.

Result: Sharp bound on list size vs. accuracy for VC classes. For large-margin half-spaces with moderate margins, the optimal list size equals the ambient dimension. For very large margins, a replicable algorithm achieves the minimal list size of ceil(d/2) + 1.

Conclusion: The work connects topological methods with learning theory to yield tight, regime-specific bounds on replicability. It clarifies how margin and dimension govern reproducibility and suggests a boundary between 'dimension-dominated' and 'margin-dominated' regimes, with potential implications for designing reproducible learning procedures.

Abstract: In recent years, list replicability has emerged as a framework for formalizing reproducibility in learning theory. A central question is how the required list size relates to the accuracy parameter and natural complexity measures of the hypothesis class.
  To achieve sharp bounds on list replicability, we prove a novel topological sphere covering theorem, derived from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. Specifically, if the $d$-sphere is covered by open sets, each of which lies in an open hemisphere, then $d+1$ of these sets must have a common intersection. Using this result, we obtain a sharp bound on the relationship between list size and accuracy for VC classes. We also show that for large-margin half-spaces, provided the margin is not too large, the optimal list size equals the ambient dimension. However, when the margin is taken to be very large, we devise a replicable algorithm achieving the minimal list size of $\lceil d/2 \rceil + 1$.

</details>


### [353] [Trust-Aware Predictive Emissions Monitoring for Gas Turbine Fleets with Limited Labelled Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06156)
*Rebecca Potts,Aiden Durrant,Rick Hackney,Georgios Leontidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Trust-aware probabilistic framework for fleet-level NOx prediction under limited labels, with per-sample trust signals, uncertainty quantification, and confidence-based filtering that improves MAE on high-confidence predictions and detects distributional shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable, scalable NOx prediction for gas-turbine fleets when emission labels are available only for a subset of assets, addressing deployment challenges of PEMS.

Method: Multi-head recurrent predictor with learned confidence; ensemble-based uncertainty quantification; auxiliary feature prediction; feature-space distance analysis; operating-range diagnostics; calibration on labeled data to yield per-sample trust scores for unlabelled units.

Result: Confidence-based filtering reduces MAE from 0.202 (full coverage) to 0.070 for the top 10% highest-confidence predictions; unlabelled and out-of-distribution samples show increased uncertainty and reduced confidence, indicating effective detection of distributional shift; framework provides actionable reliability signals for fleet-wide deployment of PEMS.

Conclusion: A trust-aware framework offers interpretable, per-sample reliability for emissions prediction on unlabelled turbines, enabling more transparent and trustworthy deployment of predictive emissions monitoring across industrial fleets.

Abstract: Machine learning-based predictive emissions monitoring systems offer a practical alternative to direct emissions measurement, but their deployment across gas turbine fleets is challenging when emissions labels are available for only a small subset of assets. In this work, a trust-aware probabilistic framework is proposed for fleet-level gas turbine NOx prediction under limited labelled supervision. The framework combines a multi-head recurrent prediction model with learned confidence estimation, ensemble-based uncertainty quantification, auxiliary feature prediction, feature-space distance analysis, and operating-range diagnostics. These signals are calibrated on labelled data to produce interpretable per-sample trust scores, providing indicators of prediction reliability on unlabelled turbines, supporting the identification of predictions that should be treated with greater caution during fleet-level deployment. Confidence-based filtering reduces MAE from 0.202 at full coverage to 0.070 for the highest-confidence 10\% of predictions, demonstrating that confidence estimates are meaningfully related to prediction error. Unlabelled and out-of-distribution samples exhibit increased uncertainty and reduced confidence, indicating that the framework responds appropriately to distributional shift. The results show that the proposed trust framework provides actionable reliability information for emissions prediction on unlabelled turbines, supporting more transparent and trustworthy deployment of PEMS across industrial fleets.

</details>


### [354] [On the training of physics-informed neural operators for solving parametric partial differential equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06164)
*Nanxi Chen,Chuanjie Cui,Airong Chen,Sifan Wang,Rujin Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINOs learn PDE solution operators via physics-informed supervision, combining neural operator generalization with data efficiency. An empirical study across three backbones (DeepONet, FNO, CViT) on five parametric PDEs finds CViT to be the most robust; optimization pathologies seen (gradient conflicts, causal violations) exist but PINN mitigations help. Physics-informed training can match or exceed data-driven performance under varying data regimes. Provides practical pipeline and code for robust PINO training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the training fragility of physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) and establish a practical, robust workflow that combines architectural choices, optimization strategies, and sampling schemes to achieve reliable operator learning.

Method: Empirical analysis of three operator backbones (DeepONet, Fourier Neural Operator, Continuous Vision Transformer) across five parametric PDEs. Systematic evaluation of architecture design, optimizer, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling. Comparison of physics-informed vs data-driven training under different data regimes. Investigation of optimization pathologies and application of PINN mitigation techniques.

Result: CViT delivers consistently strong and stable performance across benchmarks. Pathologies such as gradient conflicts and causal violations arise in PINOs but mitigation methods from PINNs remain effective. Physics-informed training can match or outperform data-driven operators under certain data regimes. The work offers a practical, systematic pipeline for robust PINO training and confirms the usefulness of PINN-inspired mitigations.

Conclusion: The study provides a comprehensive empirical understanding of PINO training challenges and delivers actionable guidance for building robust physics-informed operator learners, with CViT as a strong default backbone and physics-informed training capable of delivering data-efficient performance.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) aim to learn solution operators for partial differential equations by using the governing physics as supervision, rather than relying solely on paired input-output simulation data. By incorporating physical constraints into the training objective, PINOs combine the cross-instance generalization of neural operators with the data efficiency of physics-informed learning. Despite this promise, how to train PINOs efficiently and robustly remains less well-understood than the training of either data-driven neural operators or physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). To bridge this gap, we examine key components of the PINO training pipeline, including architecture design, optimizer choice, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling strategy. We study three representative operator backbones, Deep Operator Network (DeepONet), Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), and Continuous Vision Transformer (CViT), across five diverse parametric PDE systems. Our results show that CViT provides consistently strong and stable performance across the considered benchmarks. Beyond architecture, we find that several optimization pathologies previously identified in PINN training naturally arise in PINOs, including gradient conflicts and causal violation. We also find that mitigation algorithms developed for PINNs remain effective in the PINO setting. We further compare physics-informed and data-driven training under different data regimes, revealing that a carefully designed physics-informed training pipeline can match, and in some cases, outperform purely data-driven neural operators. Taken together, these findings provide a systematic empirical understanding of the optimization challenges in PINO training and inform a practical pipeline for efficient and robust physics-informed operator learning. Code and data are available at https://github.com/NanxiiChen/PI-CViT.

</details>


### [355] [Learning to model pediatric asthma exacerbation from multiple risk factors: a case study in coastal Virginia](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06174)
*Jonathan Colen,Eric Werner,Maryam Golbazi,Heather Richter,Diana McSpadden,Amy Quinn,Jocel Santos,Mary Jane Darling,Mary Margaret Gleason*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Three modeling approaches (GLM, NN, sparse dictionary learning) were used to predict pediatric asthma exacerbations in Hampton Roads; all frameworks produced consensus on relative risks and helped uncover interpretable nonlinear interactions, with potential to inform public health interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Asthma exacerbations are driven by multiple interacting environmental and socioeconomic factors (air pollution, weather, neighborhood SES). Large spatiotemporal data require disentangling these contributors while maintaining predictive power and interpretability.

Method: Data: ambient air pollution, weather, neighborhood opportunity; outcome: zip-code level acute AE visits to regional pediatric hospital/providers, 2018–2023; models: GLM as baseline, NN as predictive target, sparse dictionary-learning framework to extract parsimonious nonlinear interacting equations, then compare predictive performance and estimate relative risks.

Result: All three approaches yielded consensus in relative risk estimates across input exposures; the dictionary-learning approach provided interpretable nonlinear interactions; the ensemble showed that synergy among exposures influences AE.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates a bridge between traditional statistics and interpretable ML for environmental health, highlighting synergistic interactions in AE and offering a framework to guide public health interventions in coastal Virginia.

Abstract: Childhood asthma is a common illness exacerbated by air pollution as well as meteorological and neighborhood-level socioeconomic factors. Modeling asthma exacerbation (AE) in large spatiotemporal datasets requires disentangling impacts from multiple contributors. In this case study, we compared three techniques that balance predictive power with interpretability to predict AE in Hampton Roads, a coastal Virginia region comprising 7 cities and over 1.5 million people. After collating ambient air pollution measurements, weather data, and measures of neighborhood opportunity, we modeled zip code-level acute AE visits to a regional children's hospital and affiliated providers from 2018-2023. Generalized linear models (GLM) provided a baseline while neural networks (NN) served as a maximally predictive target. To bridge between statistical models and deep learning, we developed a framework based on sparse dictionary learning to identify and interpret parsimonious nonlinear interacting equations. After comparing each model's predictive performance, we estimated relative risks for AE due to input exposure variables and found consensus across frameworks. Our work links statistical and interpretable machine learning models to highlight possible synergistic interactions influencing AE, and may enable future studies to guide public health interventions in coastal Virginia.

</details>


### [356] [Learning to Route LLMs from Implicit Cost-Performance Preferences via Meta-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06178)
*Jiahao Zeng,Ming Tang,Ningning Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MetaRouter: a meta-learning, contextual-bandit framework for personalized LLM routing that learns user cost-performance preferences with minimal interaction, achieving strong performance across distributions and robustness/ scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users exhibit diverse cost–performance preferences and routing systems must adapt efficiently. Existing routing methods struggle to personalize across heterogeneous users, leading to suboptimal trade-offs.

Method: Formulate user preference profiles as a set of tasks in a contextual-bandit setting and introduce MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework that enables preference-aware routing among multiple LLMs.

Result: MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks, learns user preferences efficiently, is robust to changes in routable LLMs, and scales to multi-model routing.

Conclusion: MetaRouter enables efficient, personalized, and robust LLM routing that optimizes the cost-performance trade-off for individual users, adaptable to varying model ecosystems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) present a trade-off between performance and cost, where more powerful models incur greater expense. LLM routing aims to mitigate expenses while maintaining performance by sending queries to the most suitable model. However, existing methods cannot perform well for different user cost-performance preferences. To address this gap, we introduce a novel perceptive LLM routing paradigm for personalized and user-centric cost-performance optimization, which efficiently learns users' implicit preferences through little interaction. To handle the challenge of heterogeneous user needs, we formulate preference profiles as a set of distinct tasks in contextual bandit and propose MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework designed for preference-aware LLM routing. Experimental results show that MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks. Furthermore, it exhibits high efficiency in learning user preferences, robustness to changes in the routable LLMs, and scalability to multi-model routing.

</details>


### [357] [A Machine Learning-Based Framework for Discovering Huntington's Disease Stages: Integrating Graph Representation Learning and clustering to Uncover Progression Dynamics in Longitudinal Enroll-HD Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06196)
