*Lubna M. Abu Zohair,Marta Vallejo,MD Azher Uddin,John R. Woodward,Hind Zantout*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised dynamic graph learning identifies four robust, distinct Huntington's disease progression stages from longitudinal clinical data, improving upon fixed threshold staging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of discrete clinical cut-offs and inter-rater variability by capturing temporal relationships in longitudinal HD measurements, enabling data-driven staging.

Method: Construct dynamic graph representations from longitudinal 44-variable clinical data across 302 individuals; learn latent representations; apply K-means++ clustering; perform clustering stability analysis while increasing k to identify robust, meaningful clusters; operate in a 4D latent space.

Result: Identified four statistically distinct HD stages in a four-dimensional latent space with well-defined boundaries and minimal overlap with existing staging methods; dataset: 302 participants, 1,477 visits, 44 variables, 80% manifest; clustering robust despite limited cohort size.

Conclusion: A data-driven HD staging framework captures natural clinical progression and improves upon traditional threshold-based staging, with potential benefits for patient stratification and personalized care; future work should validate in larger cohorts and explore clinical interpretability of the latent features.

Abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive brain disorder that gradually affects movement, cognitive function, and behavior. Identifying the stage of the disease accurately and consistently is important for understanding its course, grouping patients, personalized care, and discovering treatment. Existing clinical staging frameworks rely primarily on predefined clinical measurement thresholds and clinical expert decisions, yet these discrete cut-offs may obscure meaningful intra-stage variability and remain vulnerable to inter-rater differences, especially in motor and functional assessments. To address these limitations, we developed an unsupervised machine learning framework based on dynamic graph representation learning to capture temporal relationships within and across patients from longitudinal clinical measurements. Using the learned representations, we applied K-means++ clustering to identify well-separated groups. We then iteratively increased the number of clusters (k), using stability analysis to assess robustness and reveal additional meaningful clusters beyond the initial optimal solution. We applied the framework to 302 individuals from the Enroll-HD cohort (1,477 visits, 44 clinical variables per visit; 80% manifest participants), enabling data-driven discovery of HD stages reflecting natural clinical progression. Despite the limited cohort size, the proposed framework achieved robust clustering performance using a four-dimensional latent space, identifying four meaningful and statistically distinct disease stages through clustering stability analysis. Each stage corresponded to well-defined clinical measurement boundaries, with minimal overlap compared to previously established clinical staging methods.

</details>


### [358] [Non-Negative Matrix Factorization for Event Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06205)
*Raphaël Romero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EventNMF: continuous-time non-negative factorization for event-time data using Poisson processes with non-negative B-spline intensity basis; avoids binning and preserves temporal heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bin-based preprocessing in NMF risks erasing fine-grained temporal structure; need a principled, scalable method that operates on raw event times.

Method: Model each entity's events as a Poisson process with intensity decomposed into non-negative B-spline basis functions; estimate non-negative coefficients, yielding interpretable temporal templates shared across entities; special-case connection to binning via degree-zero splines; simple, efficient optimization.

Result: Demonstrates bias-variance tradeoffs; compares to existing methods on synthetic latent-factor data; validates on real-world datasets; shows improved preservation of heterogeneity and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: EventNMF is principled, easy to implement, and efficient; binning corresponds to degree-zero splines; suitable for continuous-time event data across domains.

Abstract: Continuous-time event data, in which entities emit instantaneous events over time, arises naturally across many domains such as neuroscience, seismology, and social networks. Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) is a natural tool to uncover interpretable structure in such data, but it has so far only been applied after binning or smoothing the entity-level counting measures. This preprocessing step comes with the risk of erasing entity-level heterogeneities and fine-grained temporal features. In this paper, we introduce EventNMF, a continuous-time non-negative factorization model that operates directly on event times: each entity's events are modeled as a Poisson process whose intensity factorizes through a non-negative B-spline basis, and a simple estimation procedure recovers interpretable temporal templates shared across entities. The resulting method is mathematically principled, easy to implement, and computationally efficient. We further show that standard binned-count approaches arise as the special case of degree-zero splines, explore bias-variance tradeoffs and compare against existing methods on a synthetic latent factor model, and demonstrate the effectiveness of EventNMF on several real-world applications.

</details>


### [359] [Design a Reliable LLM-Integrated Interface for Mortality Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06235)
*Thi Kim Ngan Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An LLM-assisted interface translates natural language forecasting requests into a deterministic CoMoMo-based mortality forecasting pipeline, improving usability while preserving reproducibility and actuarial validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Make mortality forecasting accessible to non-experts without sacrificing statistical rigor.

Method: Three-phase: (1) implement baseline CoMoMo pipeline to reproduce results; (2) extend to multi-step forecasts with rolling-origin evaluation and MSE; (3) develop a local LLM interface as a constrained orchestration layer to map user input to pipeline configurations.

Result: Shows that LLMs can enhance accessibility without undermining reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in high-stakes workflows.

Conclusion: LLM-driven orchestration can broaden usability of mortality forecasts while maintaining statistical integrity; supports reproducible, transparent, high-stakes analytics.

Abstract: Mortality forecasting plays an important role in actuarial and policy decision-making, but its implementation remains technically complex and inaccessible to non-expert users. This project proposes a reliable large language model (LLM)-integrated interface that improves usability while maintaining statistical power. The LLM is designed as a constrained orchestration layer that translates natural-language inputs into structured configurations for a deterministic forecasting pipeline. A three-phase methodology is employed to ensure accuracy, usability, and transparency. First, a baseline pipeline is implemented using the CoMoMo package, reproducing established mortality forecasting results. Second, the pipeline is extended to generate multi-step forecasts using rolling-origin evaluation and mean squared error (MSE). Third, a prototype interface uses a local LLM to handle users' forecasting requests in plain language. The system demonstrates that LLMs can enhance accessibility without compromising reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in high-stakes analytical workflows.

</details>


### [360] [Tracing the Oracle: Improving Diffusion Timestep Scheduling for 3D CT Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06236)
*Yujia Wu,Zhaoqiang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TrO offers plug-and-play optimized timestep scheduling for diffusion-based 3D CT inverse problems by learning an oracle-guided schedule via dynamic programming, improving accuracy and efficiency under tight step budgets (≤10 steps) when combined with DDS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform timestep schedules fail to capture the non-uniform evolution of the reverse conditional diffusion SDE, leading to truncation errors and high computational cost in ill-posed 3D CT reconstructions with pretrained diffusion models.

Method: Construct a reference oracle from densely sampled numerical trajectories on a small subset; use dynamic programming to minimize the cumulative error between a few-step approximation and the oracle; derive an adaptive schedule that allocates steps to critical evolution stages.

Result: On the AAPM dataset across multiple 3D CT reconstruction tasks, when combined with the state-of-the-art 3D CT method DDS, the optimized timesteps outperform existing heuristic schedules in reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency under a strict budget of no more than 10 sampling steps.

Conclusion: TrO provides a practical, plug-and-play improvement to diffusion-based 3D CT inversion by reducing truncation error through oracle-guided nonuniform timestep allocation, enabling high-quality reconstructions with limited steps.

Abstract: Pretrained diffusion models demonstrate impressive potential in solving highly ill-posed 3D computed tomography (CT) inverse problems, while the inference process suffers from significant computational overhead. Furthermore, existing uniform timestep schedules fail to capture the non-uniform evolution of the reverse conditional diffusion stochastic differential equation, thereby introducing substantial truncation errors. To overcome this limitation, we propose Tracing the Oracle (TrO), a plug-and-play framework for improved timestep scheduling. Specifically, we treat densely sampled numerical integration trajectories on a few samples as the reference oracle. The optimized schedule is extracted by leveraging dynamic programming to globally minimize the cumulative error between the few-step approximation and the oracle. This mechanism precisely allocates the limited sampling steps to critical evolution stages that are highly susceptible to truncation errors. Our extensive experiments on the AAPM dataset across multiple 3D CT reconstruction tasks demonstrate that, when combined with the state-of-the-art 3D CT reconstruction method DDS, our optimized timesteps significantly improve reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency compared to existing heuristic schedules, especially under a strict budget of no more than 10 sampling steps.

</details>


### [361] [Generative Criticality in Large Language Model Temperature Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06238)
*Huajian Ruan,Jinyang Li,Xingyu Guo,Lingxiao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A statistical-field model treats LLM token embeddings as continuous spins on a 1D chain, revealing phase-transition–like behavior as a function of softmax temperature with a susceptibility peak at Tc, order-parameter changes, and a minimal intrinsic dimension near Tc, robust across models and prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify and diagnose the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs using physics-inspired order parameters and correlation measures, potentially informing decoding strategies.

Method: Model token embeddings as continuous spins on a 1D lattice; define susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an ensemble-averaged embedding-field order parameter; sweep softmax temperature T; estimate intrinsic dimension via TwoNN; test robustness across model scales (Qwen3 0.6B–32B) and prompt categories.

Result: Sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic Tc with power-law-like scaling; rapid change in the order parameter; collapse of embeddings onto a single semantic direction below Tc; TwoNN-derived intrinsic dimension minimized near Tc; findings robust across model scales and prompts; non-equilibrium nature noted and warrants further study.

Conclusion: The framework provides quantitative tools to probe collective statistical structure in LLM outputs and suggests a link between decoding strategies and critical phenomena; while reminiscent of a continuous phase transition, non-equilibrium aspects require further investigation to fully interpret the observed phenomenology.

Abstract: We propose a statistical-field framework for text generated by large language models (LLMs), treating token embeddings as continuous spin variables on a one-dimensional chain. Defining a susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an order parameter from the ensemble-averaged embedding field, we vary the \texttt{softmax} temperature $T$ and observe a sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic $T_c$ with power-law-like scaling, a concurrent rapid change in the order parameter, and a collapse onto a single semantic direction below $T_c$. The intrinsic dimension estimated by the two nearest neighbor (TwoNN) method independently corroborates these findings, reaching a minimum near $T_c$. Results are robust across model scales (Qwen3: 0.6B--32B) and prompt categories. While the phenomenology closely resembles a continuous phase transition, the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further investigation. Our framework provides quantitative tools for probing the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena.

</details>


### [362] [Your GFlowNet Secretly Learns an Optimal Transport Plan](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06272)
*Ian Maksimov,Nikita Morozov,Denis Belomestny,Sergey Samsonov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fixing the initial flow in a minimum-flow GFlowNet reduces its objective to a Kantorovich OT problem on a graph with shortest-path costs; the optimum policy encodes an optimal transport plan, and sampling from the minimum-flow GFlowNet recovers the corresponding coupling. This enables applying GFlowNets to OT on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization, with experiments confirming agreement with exact OT solvers and high-quality transport plans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OT on large graphs is computationally challenging; GFlowNets provide a stochastic trajectory framework for sampling structured objects. Establishing a link between non-acyclic GFlowNets and OT offers a theoretical bridge and a practical method to learn transport plans using neural architectures.

Method: The authors prove that fixing the initial flow distribution in a minimum-flow GFlowNet yields a Kantorovich OT problem with graph-induced shortest-path costs. At the optimum, the learned GFlowNet policy encodes an optimal transport plan; sampling trajectories from the minimum-flow GFlowNet recovers the corresponding optimal coupling. They discuss enabling OT on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization and validate with experiments.

Result: Theoretical connection between non-acyclic GFlowNets and OT is established; the minimum-flow objective aligns with Kantorovich OT under graph costs; the optimal policy induces an OT plan, and sampling recovers the optimal coupling. Empirical results show agreement with exact OT solvers and that GFlowNets can learn high-quality transport plans.

Conclusion: This work broadens the GFlowNet framework to OT problems on graphs, enabling scalable learning of transport plans through edge-flow parameterizations and neural networks, with empirical validation against exact OT solvers.

Abstract: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are a framework for sampling structured objects via stochastic trajectories in a directed graph. In this work, we establish a theoretical connection between non-acyclic GFlowNets and optimal transport (OT). We show that fixing the initial flow distribution in a minimum-flow GFlowNet reduces its objective to a Kantorovich OT problem with graph-induced shortest path costs. At the optimum, the learned GFlowNet policy therefore encodes an optimal transport plan from the source distribution to the target distribution: we show that sampling trajectories from the minimum-flow GFlowNet recovers the corresponding optimal coupling. Our formulation enables applying the GFlowNet learning framework to OT problems on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization. Experiments confirm agreement with exact OT solvers and demonstrate that GFlowNets can learn high-quality transport plans.

</details>


### [363] [PAC-Bayesian Adversarially Robust Generalization for Message Passing Graph Neural Networks: A Sensitivity Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06293)
*Ziling Liang,Xinping Yi,Qingsong Wen,Shi Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian bound for MPGNNs using Jacobian-aligned anisotropic Gaussian posteriors, yielding tighter adversarial robustness bounds by replacing width-based terms with dependence on the number of classes (K).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand robust generalization of graph neural networks under adversarial attacks and overcome limitations of isotropic posteriors and full-parameter-space perturbations in existing PAC-Bayesian analyses.

Method: Extend the sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework to message passing GNNs (MPGNNs). Derive output Jacobians with respect to weight parameters; note that in K-class classification these Jacobians have rank at most K. Build Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices and employ anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to tighten the KL-divergence bound. Refine spectral-norm dependence on learned weights and reduce the leading-dimension factor from hidden-width to the number of classes K.

Result: Yields much tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs in the adversarial setting. The bounds scale with K rather than with hidden width, providing guidance for designing MPGNNs to improve adversarial robustness.

Conclusion: The sensitivity-aware, Jacobian-aligned PAC-Bayesian analysis offers tighter, data-dependent robustness bounds for MPGNNs and informs architectural/design choices to enhance adversarial robustness.

Abstract: Whilst the vulnerability of graph neural networks (GNNs) to adversarial attacks poses a critical threat to graph representation learning, the understanding of the robust generalization behavior remains a fundamental challenge in the adversarial setting. Recently, PAC-Bayesian margin-based generalization analysis substantially advances this line of research by providing a flexible and data-dependent analytical framework. However, existing robust analyses often rely on isotropic Gaussian posteriors and control weight perturbations in the full parameter space, which limits the ability to capture heterogeneous parameter sensitivity yet hinges on hidden-width-dependent complexity terms, resulting in not-tight-enough generalization bounds. In this paper, we extend a recently proposed sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework from deep neural networks to message passing GNNs (MPGNNs) and derive a tighter robust generalization bound in the adversarial setting. Specifically, we first quantify how sensitive the perturbations across different parameter blocks are to the network outputs by deriving the output Jacobians with respect to the weight parameters. Exploiting the fact that these Jacobian matrices have rank at most $K$ in $K$-class graph classification, we then construct Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices and use anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to upper bound the KL divergence in a tight way. Notably, by refining the spectral-norm dependence on the learned weights and reducing the leading dimension factor from hidden-width-dependent terms to the number of classes $K$, our analysis yields much tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs, thereby guiding their designs to enhance adversarial robustness.

</details>


### [364] [Reactive Flux Matching: Mechanism Discovery and Adaptive Sampling of Rare Events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06295)
*Rishal Aggarwal,David Ryan Koes,Nicholas M. Boffi,Eric Vanden-Eijnden*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flux Matching learns a current velocity u(z) and a scalar potential h(z) from reactive trajectories, enabling non-Markovian robust reaction coordinates and adaptive interfaces for enhanced sampling, without needing the stationary distribution; validated on molecular systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance interpretability of path sampling data by extracting mechanistic pathways and coordinates directly from reactive ensembles, addressing limitations of committor-based methods, especially under non-Markovian projection.

Method: Data-driven decomposition of the reactive current into a velocity field and a potential via a weighted Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition; optimization of quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble; current velocity streamlines trace dominant pathways; potential h(z) is derived; method does not require knowledge of the underlying dynamics or stationary distribution.

Result: Generated current velocity trajectories and rate constants; demonstrated robustness under projection onto non-Markovian coordinates; level sets of h(z) provide adaptive interfaces for enhanced sampling; validated on molecular systems.

Conclusion: Flux Matching offers a principled, data-driven framework for mechanistic analysis in path sampling, complementing committor-based approaches and enabling trajectory-based learning for improved sampling strategies.

Abstract: Path sampling methods generate ensembles of reactive trajectories connecting metastable states, but extracting mechanistic insight from these data remains nontrivial. We introduce Flux Matching, a framework that learns two complementary objects directly from reactive trajectory data: a current velocity $u(z)$, whose streamlines trace the dominant reaction pathways, and a scalar potential $h(z)$, obtained from a weighted Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition of the reactive current, that serves as a data-driven reaction coordinate. Both minimize quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble, analogous to the flow matching loss in generative modeling, and require no knowledge of the underlying dynamics or stationary distribution. Unlike committor-based methods, $u$ and $h$ remain well-defined under projection onto non-Markovian collective variables, and their level sets in turn provide adaptive interfaces for improved sampling with enhanced sampling methods. Flux Matching is validated through the generation of current velocity trajectories and rate constant calculations on molecular systems.

</details>


### [365] [Tangram: Unlocking Non-Uniform KV Cache for Efficient Multi-turn LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06302)
*Hyungmin Kim,Minsoo Kim,Hongseok Kim,Jungwook Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tangram introduces a non-uniform KV cache management for LLM serving that uses deterministic budgets, head-group page clustering, and ahead-of-time load balancing to eliminate dynamic scheduling overhead and improve throughput (up to 2.6x) while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-uniform KV caches save memory by weighting the importance of K/V pairs for each attention head, but this heterogeneity causes memory fragmentation, scheduling complexity, and poor kernel utilization in existing LLM serving systems.

Method: Three design pillars: (1) Deterministic Budget Allocation assigns a static memory footprint per head to remove dynamic scheduling and prefill stalls; (2) Head Group Page clusters similar retention-demand heads and manages them with independent, vectorized page tables to improve memory reclamation and locality; (3) Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Load Balancing uses static budget profiles to achieve uniform GPU utilization with no runtime overhead.

Result: Experiments report throughput gains up to 2.6x over baselines while fully preserving model accuracy; implementation is open-source at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Tangram shows that practical, high-performance serving is possible for non-uniform KV caches through carefully designed memory budgeting, grouping, and scheduling techniques, enabling efficient large-scale LLM deployment.

Abstract: Multi-turn Large Language Model (LLM) serving is critical for consistent user experiences, yet the linear growth of the Key-Value (KV) cache imposes significant pressure on GPU memory and bandwidth. Non-uniform KV compression effectively preserves more information by considering the individual importance of each KV cache. However, such KV cache heterogeneity introduces various systemic challenges - including memory fragmentation, scheduling complexities, and diminished kernel utilization - which collectively lead to significant inefficiencies in existing LLM serving systems.
  To overcome these challenges, we present Tangram, a novel serving system designed to make Non-uniform KV caches practical. Tangram addresses systemic inefficiencies through three core techniques: (1) Deterministic Budget Allocation assigns a static memory footprint to each head based on its intrinsic pattern, entirely eliminating dynamic scheduling overhead and prefill stalls; (2) Head Group Page clusters attention heads with similar retention demands and manages them with independent, vectorized page tables, thereby maximizing physical memory reclamation; and (3) Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Load Balancing leverages static budget profiles to ensure uniform GPU utilization without runtime overhead. Experimental results show that Tangram improves throughput by up to 2.6x compared to existing baselines, while fully preserving model accuracy. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/TANGRAM.

</details>


### [366] [Plug-and-Play Guidance for Discrete Diffusion Models via Gradient-Informed Logit Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06303)
*Hongkun Dou,Zike Chen,Fengji Li,Hongjue Li,Yue Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GILC introduces a plug-and-play, Jacobian-free gradient-informed logit correction to guide discrete diffusion models without retraining, using the pretrained denoiser as a variational proxy, and supporting both differentiable and non-differentiable rewards. It delivers state-of-the-art results on DNA, protein sequences, and molecular generation without extra training, often exceeding fine-tuning baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Controllable generation with discrete diffusion models often incurs high computational cost and requires retraining; there is a need for an efficient, stable guidance mechanism that leverages pretrained models.

Method: A plug-and-play framework that repurposes the pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy to estimate guidance signals. Introduces a Jacobian-free mechanism to directly correct clean prediction logits, enabling stable guidance in high-dimensional discrete spaces and supporting both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions.

Result: Empirical evaluation across DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks shows state-of-the-art performance without additional training, frequently outperforming fine-tuning approaches.

Conclusion: GILC provides a scalable, stable, and versatile guidance paradigm for controllable generation with discrete diffusion, eliminating the need for retraining while delivering strong performance across diverse domains.

Abstract: Controllable generation with discrete diffusion models is often hindered by high computational overhead or the need for retraining. In this paper, we present \underline{\textbf{G}}radient-\underline{\textbf{I}}nformed \underline{\textbf{L}}ogit \underline{\textbf{C}}orrection (\textbf{GILC}), a plug-and-play framework that efficiently estimates guidance signals by repurposing the pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy. To circumvent the gradient instability inherent in high-dimensional discrete spaces, we introduce a Jacobian-free mechanism that directly corrects the clean prediction logits, facilitating stable and effective guidance. Our method accommodates both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions. Extensive experiments across DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks demonstrate that GILC achieves state-of-the-art performance without additional training, frequently outperforming fine-tuning approaches.

</details>


### [367] [Learning What to Forget: Improving LLM Unlearning via Learned Token-Level Importance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06320)
*Gizem Yüce,Giorgos Nikolaou,Nicolas Flammarion*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Alternating Token-Weighted Unlearning (ATWU) to identify forget-specific tokens and perform unlearning in autoregressive LMs, using a joint optimization over model parameters and token weights with a simple linear scorer on hidden states, achieving state-of-the-art forget-retain trade-offs without external supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Token relevance for forgetting in language models is heterogeneous; existing methods rely on heuristics or auxiliary models and may not capture token-level forget-specificity. There is a need for unsupervised, scalable token-level forgetting signals grounded in model representations.

Method: Formulates unlearning as a joint optimization over model parameters and per-token weights. Defines a forget objective that competes with a retain objective; introduces alternating optimization (ATWU) with a lightweight linear scorer on hidden states to estimate token forget-specificity without token-level supervision. Evaluates on TOFU and RWKU benchmarks, comparing against sample-level methods, probability-based weighting, and auxiliary-model approaches.

Result: ATWU achieves state-of-the-art forget-retain trade-offs on TOFU and RWKU datasets, outperforming baselines including sample-level and heuristic/token-weighted methods as well as auxiliary-model-based approaches. Learned token scores align more closely with ground-truth forget-specific spans, indicating semantically meaningful forgetting signals with minimal computational overhead.

Conclusion: Retain conflicts provide an effective criterion for identifying forget-worthy content. Token forget-specificity can be learned unsupervised from model representations via ATWU, enabling scalable, low-overhead unlearning without external supervision.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model while preserving its general capabilities. For autoregressive language models, not all tokens in a forget sample are equally relevant to forgetting. Existing approaches either ignore this heterogeneity or rely on auxiliary models, heuristics, or external annotations to estimate each token's relevance for forgetting. We instead characterize it through the interaction with the retain objective: a token is forget-specific to the extent that minimizing the forget loss on that token does not conflict with retain optimality. We formalize this perspective as a joint optimization problem over the model parameters and the token weights and show that, under a natural separation condition, the resulting objective recovers the oracle forget-specific token support. Motivated by this formulation, we introduce Alternating Token-Weighted Unlearning (ATWU), a lightweight framework that jointly learns token forget-specificity and model parameters during unlearning using a simple linear scorer over the hidden states, without external token level supervision. Across TOFU and RWKU, ATWU achieves state of the art forget-retain trade-offs, outperforming sample-level methods, probability-based token weighting heuristics, and auxiliary-model-based approaches. Moreover, the learned scores align substantially better with ground truth forget-specific spans, indicating that ATWU identifies semantically meaningful token level forgetting signals. Overall, our results suggest that retain conflict provides an effective criterion for identifying what language models should forget, enabling unsupervised learning of token level forget-specificity directly from model representations with minimal computational overhead.

</details>


### [368] [PAMF: Prior-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Incomplete Time Series Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06328)
*Ziwen Kan,Wugeng Zheng,Tianlong Chen,Song Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PAMF introduces a prior-aware multimodal time-series imputation framework that explicitly handles within-modality and modality-level missingness and couples imputation with prediction via weight-sharing encoders and flow matching, achieving state-of-the-art downstream performance on healthcare benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address two distinct missingness patterns in multimodal healthcare time-series and limitations of existing methods that either ignore instance-specific missing information or separate imputation from prediction.

Method: PAMF initializes flow-matching with type-specific priors for the two missingness patterns, uses architecturally matched encoders with shared weights to couple imputation and downstream classification, and employs prior-aware flow matching to guide imputation toward task-relevant representations.

Result: Empirical evaluations on multiple healthcare time-series benchmarks show PAMF achieves the strongest overall downstream performance across diverse datasets and missing settings relative to baselines.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling missingness with priors and sharing representations between imputation and prediction yields more informative imputations and improved predictive performance in multimodal healthcare time-series tasks.

Abstract: In healthcare, multimodal time series tasks often operate on incomplete observations in practice, for example when ECG segments are lost because electrodes detach or an entire respiratory channel is unavailable during overnight monitoring. Such missingness typically appears in two structurally distinct patterns: within-modality missing, where values are absent within an otherwise observed modality, and modality-level missing, where an entire modality is unavailable. Existing methods typically represent unobserved data implicitly through masks or missing embeddings, without learning instance-specific missing information, and most are designed for only one missingness pattern. A natural approach is to explicitly estimate the missing data; however, existing imputation methods treat missingness uniformly despite their different structural priors, and the imputation process is often isolated from downstream tasks, preventing downstream tasks from guiding imputation toward more informative representations. To address these limitations, we present PAMF, a multimodal time-series framework that explicitly handles different missingness patterns while coupling imputation with downstream prediction through prior-aware flow matching and weight sharing. Specifically, the method initializes the flow-matching source state with type-specific priors to distinguish two missing types. It further connects imputation and classification through architecturally matched encoders with weight sharing, transferring task-relevant representations into the imputation process. Experiments on multiple multimodal healthcare time-series benchmarks show that the proposed method achieves the strongest overall downstream performance across diverse datasets and missing settings compared with existing baselines.

</details>


### [369] [Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders for Effective Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06333)
*Seyed Arshan Dalili,Mehrdad Mahdavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Standard Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) assume 1D latent features, causing a mismatch with the multi-dimensional structure of model features and leading to feature splitting. The paper introduces Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA) with decoder subspaces, block sparsity, and rank adaptation, achieving polynomial sample complexity and reducing splitting while preserving interpretability on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve mechanistic interpretability of large language models by aligning latent feature representations with their intrinsic multi-dimensional geometry, addressing the unintended feature splitting caused by single-direction decoders in conventional SAEs.

Method: Replace single-vector decoders with learned subspace decoders per feature; enforce block sparsity via Top-s group gating; use a nuclear-norm regularizer to adapt the rank of each group. The authors provide a theoretical result: when the group size r >= the intrinsic dimension d_i, a single group can represent the whole feature slice and becomes the global minimizer of the SASA objective. They show the conventional SAE optimization can force exponential growth of required atoms in d_i, driving features toward splitting.

Result: SASA consolidates a feature into a single subspace group when r >= d_i, yielding polynomial (instead of exponential) sample complexity in d_i; empirically, SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability, and matches or exceeds standard SAEs while using about half the token budget on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B.

Conclusion: Subspace-aware decoders with group sparsity and rank adaptation address both geometric and optimization-induced feature splitting in SAEs, enabling faithful, efficiently learned representations of multi-dimensional model features and improving interpretability with favorable data efficiency.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for mechanistic interpretability in large language models, yet their formulation assigns each latent feature a single decoder direction, implicitly assuming features to be one-dimensional. We show that this assumption mismatches with the multi-dimensional structure of model features, provably inducing feature splitting through two distinct mechanisms. Geometrically, reconstructing a feature of intrinsic dimension $d_i \ge 2$ to error $\varepsilon$ with single-direction decoders forces a number of atoms that is exponential in $d_i$. From an end-to-end optimization perspective, this splitting is not merely possible but actively preferred. We prove that there exists a continuous path from the true $d_i$-dimensional basis to a strictly lower risk of the $\ell_1$-regularized SAE objective, whose descent directions drive any trained dictionary into that exponential regime. A single coherent feature is therefore fragmented across many near-collinear latents, producing spurious multiplicity and obscuring the intrinsic geometry. Motivated by this, we introduce Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA), which replace single-vector decoders with learned decoder subspaces, enforce block sparsity via Top-$s$ group gating, and adapt each group's effective rank with a nuclear-norm regularizer. We then show that once the block size satisfies $r \ge d_i$, a single group not only can represent the entire feature slice but is the global minimizer of the SASA objective. This consolidation yields a sample complexity polynomial in $d_i$ rather than exponential -- a decisive advantage given that every training activation costs an LLM forward pass. Empirically, on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B, SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability, and matches or exceeds standard SAEs while training on roughly half the token budget.

</details>


### [370] [Quantifying the Privacy of Counterfactuals by Leveraging Membership Inference Attacks Against Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06334)
*Maryam Babaei,Yingke Wang,Hadrien Lautraite,Heber H. Arcolezi,Ulrich Aivodji,Sebastien Gambs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Counterfactual explanations can leak private training data via membership inference; attacks transfer from synthetic data to counterfactuals and can be executed without model access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counterfactuals resemble realistic substitutes for training data, creating a potential privacy leakage surface; need to evaluate whether membership inference attacks on synthetic data apply to counterfactuals.

Method: Adapt membership inference attacks designed for synthetic data to counterfactual explanations, evaluate across several counterfactual types, and demonstrate attacks using only a set of counterfactuals without querying the model.

Result: Attack success observed across counterfactual types, confirming privacy risks; attackers can infer membership in training data or related attributes, even without model access.

Conclusion: Release of counterfactuals should be treated with privacy concerns; developers should implement safeguards (e.g., privacy-preserving counterfactuals, auditing) to mitigate leakage.

Abstract: Counterfactuals are typically used in high-stakes decision areas to explain a machine learning model by showing how changes to the user profiles result in the desired outcome. However, explaining the model's decisions through counterfactuals can also be exploited by an adversary to conduct privacy attacks against the model or its training data. Drawing on the analogy that counterfactuals provide realistic substitutes for real training data, similar to synthetic data, we demonstrate in this paper how it is possible to successfully perform privacy attacks on counterfactuals by drawing on the attacks developed against synthetic data. More precisely, we investigate the effectiveness of the membership inference attacks designed for synthetic data on various types of counterfactuals. Additionally, while existing membership inference attacks against counterfactuals usually require to be able to query the model, we show how it is possible to perform successful membership inference attacks using only a set of counterfactuals, with no access to the model from which they are generated. Our results demonstrate that model developers should be more cautious when releasing counterfactuals to various users, as it can lead to a privacy breach.

</details>


### [371] [Bridging Domain Expertise and Generalization for Performance Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06335)
*Shuxuan Li,Zhilin Zhao,Quyu Kong,Wei-Shi Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FRAP leverages a foundation model and the base model to create a robust reference for performance estimation under distribution shift by aligning their predictions and fusing them via confidence weighting, enabling model-agreement-based performance estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge is predicting model performance on unlabeled test data when the distribution shifts, where base-model outputs alone are biased and poorly correlated with true performance. A robust surrogate reference is needed.

Method: 1) Calibrate the foundation model's prediction distribution to align with the base model's distribution using temperature-scaled calibration to minimize divergence. 2) Fuse the aligned predictions with the base model's predictions via confidence-based weighting to form a refined reference distribution. 3) Estimate performance by measuring agreement between the base model's predictions and this fused reference.

Result: FRAP achieves consistent and substantial improvements over representative performance-estimation methods across diverse datasets and architectures under distribution shift.

Conclusion: By combining the robustness of an external foundation model with domain-specific knowledge from the base model, FRAP provides a more reliable surrogate for ground-truth labels and improves performance estimation under distribution shift.

Abstract: Performance estimation under distribution shift aims to predict how a model behaves on an unlabeled test set whose distribution differs from the training data, a scenario that requires reliable indicators that can faithfully reflect model behavior without ground-truth labels. Existing approaches rely solely on the outputs of the given model whose biases are amplified once the distribution shifts, weakening the correlation with the true performance. Motivated by this limitation, we propose Fused Reference Alignment Prediction (FRAP), which leverages the complementary strengths of an external foundation model and the base model to construct a more reliable surrogate of the ground-truth labels. FRAP aligns the prediction distribution of the foundation model with that of the base model by applying temperature-scaled calibration that minimizes their divergence. The aligned predictions are fused through confidence-based weighting into a refined reference distribution that integrates robustness from the foundation model and domain-specific expertise from the base model, and performance estimation is obtained by measuring how closely the base model predictions agree with this reference. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets and architectures show that FRAP provides consistent and substantial improvements over representative performance-estimation methods under distribution shift.

</details>


### [372] [Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06344)
*Zehua Cheng,Wei Dai,Jiahao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP) uses SE(3)-equivariant Gaussian-mixture messages in a factor-graph to represent rank-2 uncertainties, enabling robust, multi-modal, SE(3)-consistent inference with fast convergence in geometry- and robotics-related tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SE(3)-equivariant networks yield only scalar/vector representations and fail to capture anisotropic uncertainty; multi-modal energy landscapes cannot be faithfully represented by single-component messages, hindering probabilistic inference over spatially embedded variables.

Method: ENBP constructs a factor-graph with SE(3)-equivariant Gaussian mixture messages. Rank-2 precision matrices are synthesized via equivariant outer products, inferred through differentiable spectral decomposition, and kept tractable by a greedy KL-based mixture reduction that provably commutes with SE(3).

Result: On GEOM-QM9 and GEOM-Drugs, ENBP achieves 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090 Å error with sub-second latency, >100x faster than diffusion baselines at higher accuracy. On multi-body robotics, ENBP converges whereas vanilla loopy BP diverges beyond 15 agents, with near-zero collision rates and machine-precision equivariance error (~1e-7) contrasted with ~1e-1 for augmented baselines.

Conclusion: ENBP provides SE(3)-consistent probabilistic inference with anisotropic uncertainty and multi-modal energy landscapes, delivering substantial accuracy and speed gains over diffusion and augmented baselines, and improved stability in multi-agent robotic settings.

Abstract: Probabilistic inference over spatially embedded variables requires beliefs that respect $SE(3)$ symmetry, yet existing equivariant networks produce only scalars and vectors -- not the rank-2 precision tensors needed for anisotropic uncertainty, and single-component messages collapse multi-modal energy landscapes to physically meaningless averages. We introduce Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP), a factor-graph framework whose messages are equivariant Gaussian mixture models with sufficient statistics that transform exactly under $SE(3)$. Rank-2 precision matrices are synthesised via equivariant outer products, ingested through differentiable spectral decomposition, and kept tractable by a greedy KL-based mixture reduction that provably commutes with $SE(3)$. On GEOM-QM9 and GEOM-Drugs, ENBP achieves 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090 $\mathring{A}$ error with sub-second latency -- over $100\times$ faster than diffusion baselines at higher accuracy. On multi-body robotic inference, vanilla loopy BP diverges at 15+ agents while ENBP converges with near-zero collision rates and machine-precision equivariance error (${\sim}10^{-7}$ vs.\ $10^{-1}$ for augmented baselines).

</details>


### [373] [Performance Evaluation of GraphCast for Medium-Range Weather Forecasting over Brazil](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06348)
*Wolfgang R. Rowell,Lucas S. Kupssinskü*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML weather predictor GraphCast shows regime-dependent performance vs ECMWF IFS HRES in Brazil: underperforms mid-range Z500 in austral winter, but exceeds in extended range; during austral summer it captures large-scale moisture but dampens convective variability; establishes baseline and guides tropicalization for regional resilience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate the regional applicability of MLWP (GraphCast) in the Global South, specifically Brazil, and establish a baseline to guide tropicalization of AI models for regional resilience.

Method: Benchmark four Brazilian sub-regions with four seasonal windows using a scalable cloud-native WeatherBench-X pipeline. Ground truth from operational IFS analysis. Evaluate tropospheric variables T850, Q850, Z500 over lead days 2–7 and beyond. Use deterministic skill metrics to compare GraphCast against ECMWF IFS HRES baseline.

Result: Skill is regime-dependent. In austral winter, GraphCast underperforms in the 2–7 day window for Z500 due to fast-propagating baroclinic systems; in the extended range it regains advantage due to smoothing of chaotic small-scale variability. In austral summer, GraphCast effectively captures large-scale moisture transport and dampens high-frequency convective variability that degrades deterministic temperature forecasts.

Conclusion: The study establishes a Brazil baseline for MLWP performance, delineates physical boundaries for tropicalization, and suggests directions to optimize AI weather models for regional resilience in tropical convection contexts.

Abstract: The paradigm of global weather forecasting is rapidly shifting with the emergence of Machine Learning Weather Prediction models (MLWP). While these data-driven architectures demonstrate remarkable global skill, regional benchmarks in the Global South remain scarce, leaving their efficacy in complex, highly convective environments largely unverified. This study evaluates the performance of GraphCast operational against the deterministic ECMWF IFS HRES as baseline across four distinct Brazilian climatic sub-regions. Utilizing a scalable, cloud-native pipeline and the WeatherBench-X framework for benchmarking weather models, we assess selected tropospheric variables ($T_{850}$, $Q_{850}$, $Z_{500}$) over four selected seasonal windows, employing the operational IFS analysis as the ground truth to calculate the statistical metrics for both models. Results reveal a regime-dependent skill profile. During the austral winter, GraphCast underperforms in the medium range (lead days 2-7) for $Z_{500}$ when resolving fast-propagating baroclinic systems over southern Brazil, but regains an advantage in the extended range, where its inherent smoothing of chaotic small-scale variability becomes beneficial under deterministic skill metrics. Conversely, during the austral summer wet season, GraphCast accurately captures large-scale moisture transport while intrinsically dampening the high-frequency convective variability that degrades deterministic NWP temperature forecasts. These findings establish a baseline for Brazil and define the specific physical boundaries that will guide future ``tropicalization'' efforts, aiming to optimize these foundational AI models for regional resilience.

</details>


### [374] [Maximising the Set-Piece Return: Optimising Football Corner Tactics with Graph Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06353)
*Sean Groom,Michael Groom,Francisco Belo,Axl Rice,Liam Anderson,Victor-Alexandru Darvariu,Shuo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-based reinforcement learning optimizes corner-kick routines to discover generalisable attacking configurations that maximize first-contact shot probability, outperforming baseline optimisers on 3k Premier League corners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of imitation-based, historical pattern analysis by seeking transferable, reward-driven tactical discoveries in set-piece play. The goal is to learn a general policy for adjusting arbitrary initial player configurations rather than solving fixed setups.

Method: A reinforcement learning architecture that operates on graph-structured data. A central policy adjusts attacking players’ positions and velocities to maximize first contact shot probability. Evaluated on 3,000 Premier League corners, comparing against baseline optimisation methods under matched inference budgets.

Result: The graph-RL approach significantly outperforms baseline optimisation under the same inference budget, indicating successful generalisation and discovery of effective corner routines beyond historical patterns.

Conclusion: Graph reinforcement learning can shift set-piece analysis from retrospective evaluation to reward-driven tactical discovery, providing a generalisable framework for optimizing football tactics on graph-structured representations.

Abstract: Machine learning is increasingly employed for the evaluation of football tactics. However, existing approaches focus on characterising historical actions or analyst-specified counterfactual scenarios. In this work, we seek to go beyond the imitation of historically observed patterns towards discovering new generalisable player configurations and strategies. To tackle this, we focus on optimising corner kick routines, and formulate a decision-making problem in which a central policy makes adjustments to attacking player positions and velocities to maximise first contact shot probability. Unlike classic optimisation that solves for isolated setups, we contribute a reinforcement learning architecture operating on graph-structured data that yields a general policy for adjusting arbitrary starting player positions. Evaluated on over 3,000 Premier League corners, our approach strongly outperforms baseline optimisation techniques under matched inference budgets. Our results suggest that graph reinforcement learning can shift set-piece analysis from historical evaluation and imitation towards reward-driven tactical discovery.

</details>


### [375] [End-to-End Subgraph Detection with GraphDETR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06364)
*Dexiong Chen,Till Hendrik Schulz,Karsten Borgwardt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphDETR reframes subgraph detection as a set-prediction problem using a GNN-encoded target graph and learnable query vectors decoded via a transformer, enabling end-to-end training with bipartite matching and support for approximate matching; demonstrates detection of diverse patterns up to 50 nodes in graphs up to 1000 nodes with high molecular AP on ChEMBL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subgraph detection is NP-complete (subgraph isomorphism); need scalable, flexible methods capable of exact and approximate matching across diverse pattern types.

Method: Encode the target graph with a graph neural network; use a fixed set of learnable query vectors; decode with a transformer decoder to jointly predict all pattern occurrences; train end-to-end using bipartite matching.

Result: Able to detect varied patterns (molecular structures, cycles, cliques, fuzzy patterns) up to 50 nodes in graphs up to 1000 nodes; AP100 = 91.2 on molecular functional group detection in ChEMBL.

Conclusion: GraphDETR provides a scalable, end-to-end deep learning approach for subgraph detection that extends to approximate matching and can handle diverse pattern types, offering an efficient alternative to combinatorial methods.

Abstract: Subgraph detection seeks to identify whether and where instances of query patterns occur within a larger graph. This problem is fundamental across scientific domains and is closely related to subgraph isomorphism, which is NP-complete, limiting combinatorial approaches to small patterns or moderately sized graphs. We introduce GraphDETR, a deep learning framework that formulates subgraph detection as a set prediction problem, analogous to DETR in object detection. GraphDETR encodes the target graph with a graph neural network, and employs a fixed set of learnable query vectors, decoded via a transformer decoder, to predict all pattern occurrences jointly in a single forward pass. This is enabled by training the model end-to-end with bipartite matching. Unlike traditional combinatorial methods that only solve exact structural matching, GraphDETR naturally extends to approximate matching, enabling detection beyond exact pattern correspondence. Empirically, we show that GraphDETR can detect diverse patterns, such as molecular structures, cycles, cliques, and fuzzy patterns of up to 50 nodes, in target graphs with up to 1000 nodes. We further evaluate on molecular functional group detection over the ChEMBL dataset, where GraphDETR predicts the complete set of functional groups per molecule, achieving a strong performance of $\text{AP}_{100} = 91.2$.

</details>


### [376] [Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator for HEC-RAS 2D Water-Surface Elevation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06385)
*Edward Holmberg,Elias Ioup,Md Meftahul Ferdaus,Mahdi Abdelguerfi,Julian Simeonov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces LRFIO, a no-forcing, increment-based surrogate for solver-consistent WSE prediction in HEC-RAS 2D, achieving substantial speedups with adaptive complexity across four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable accurate, solver-consistent WSE predictions directly on native, nonuniform cells while avoiding raster remapping errors and information-access confounding; demonstrate cross-dataset generalization with controlled model complexity.

Method: Calibrate an inertial response operator from solved HEC-RAS trajectories and deploy via closed-form native-cell rollout. Use a base response hierarchy (persistence, global calibrated inertia, segmented response-field inertia); treat segmentation, residual correction, and neuralized inertia as learnable options, retained only if validated as cost-justified. Evaluate across four benchmarks and perform a selector audit to control complexity.

Result: LRFIO reveals domain-adaptive response structures across benchmarks; maximum validation regret 4.30%; deployment rollout times 0.003–0.242 s; Beaver Bayou horizon-normalized speedup ≈ 2.75×10^4 over HEC-RAS; supports that native-cell incremental inertia is an effective predictive scaffold, with added complexity justified only by empirical gains.

Conclusion: A simple, increment-based inertia operator can deliver solver-conditioned WSE predictions with substantial speedups, and complexity should be added selectively based on empirical validation.

Abstract: This article presents a cross-dataset evaluation of learned native-cell surrogate models for solver-consistent water-surface elevation (WSE) prediction in HEC-RAS 2D. To avoid raster remapping error and information-access confounding, surrogates are evaluated directly on the original nonuniform computational cells under an explicit policy that separates static project inputs, current hydraulic state, project-input forcing, calibration-derived quantities, and future solver-output targets. We introduce the Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator (LRFIO), a no-forcing, increment-based learned surrogate that calibrates an inertial response operator from solved HEC-RAS trajectories and deploys the retained operator through closed-form native-cell rollout. LRFIO evaluates a base-case-first response hierarchy consisting of persistence, global calibrated inertia, and segmented response-field inertia. Segmentation, residual correction, and neuralized inertia are treated as learnable modeling choices, with added complexity retained only when validation evidence justifies its cost. Evaluated across four diverse HEC-RAS 2D benchmarks, LRFIO retains different response structures for different domains, demonstrating adaptive learned complexity. The selector audit shows controlled complexity with a maximum validation regret of 4.30%. During deployment, retained rollout times range from 0.003 s to 0.242 s, and the Beaver Bayou measured-solve comparison gives an estimated 2.75 x 10^4 horizon-normalized speedup over HEC-RAS. These results indicate that the current native-cell increment is a strong solver-conditioned predictive scaffold and that added response-field, neural, or spatial complexity should be retained only when empirically justified.

</details>


### [377] [In-Context Multiple Instance Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06458)
*Alexander Möllers,Marvin Sextro,Julius Hense,Gabriel Dernbach,Klaus-Robert Müller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pretraining a Perceiver-style in-context learner on synthetic bag data enables zero-shot/few-shot MIL on new tasks; a mixture of synthetic generators yields best average across 12 benchmarks, outperforming task-specific supervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MIL struggles in low-label regimes; need adaptable, label-efficient models that generalize across bag-based tasks without per-task fine-tuning.

Method: Pretrain a Perceiver-style in-context learner on synthetic, bag-structured data; explore multiple synthetic data generators; train on a mixture of generators; at inference, perform a single forward pass with no gradient updates.

Result: On 12 MIL benchmarks, the mixture-generated pretraining yields the best average performance and surpasses supervised baselines that require task-specific training.

Conclusion: Synthetic-data pretraining with a mixture of bag-generators confers transferable, task-agnostic MIL competence via in-context learning, reducing the need for task-specific labeled data and gradient-based adaptation.

Abstract: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) addresses problems where supervision is available at the level of bags of instances and has been successfully applied in fields ranging from computational pathology to satellite imagery. Nevertheless, existing algorithms struggle in the low-label regime that characterizes many real-world applications. Flexible models overfit and rigid ones fail to adapt to the task at hand. We show that pretraining an in-context learner with a Perceiver-style architecture on synthetic data yields a model that can solve new tasks from a handful of labeled bags. At inference time, classification happens in a single forward pass and requires no gradient updates. We propose and investigate different synthetic data generators for bag-structured data and find that they capture complementary inductive biases. A model pretrained on a mixture of these generators inherits their per-task strengths and achieves the best average performance across twelve MIL benchmarks, outperforming supervised baselines that require task-specific training.

</details>


### [378] [Proper Scoring Rules for Right-Censored Survival Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06393)
*Jef Jonkers,Glenn Van Wallendael,Luc Duchateau,Sofie Van Hoecke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified, censoring-aware framework for proper scoring of right-censored survival forecasts, yielding censored versions of standard scores and a new training objective (censored engression) with propriety guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Right censoring obscures event times, breaking standard scoring rules; a coherent method to score and train probabilistic survival forecasts under censoring is needed for fair evaluation and learning.

Method: Map the predictive distribution through the censoring mechanism to obtain the observed-data law; define localized scores at fixed censoring times and marginalized scores when censoring is random; derive censored versions of CRPS, pinball loss, Brier, energy score; show propriety under conditional independent censoring; extend to censored engression for multivariate survival; establish connections to right-censored likelihood and IPCW; experimental validation.

Result: The marginalized score is proper under conditional independent censoring and strictly proper on the identifiable region; the approach recovers known criteria; censored engression improves training; ranking stability across censoring regimes; ranking reversals seen with plug-in weighted scores.

Conclusion: A coherent framework for scoring and training censored survival forecasts; provides principled, robust evaluation and learning, with practical improvements over naive methods.

Abstract: Proper scoring rules provide a rigorous theoretical basis for the training and evaluation of probabilistic forecasts. However, in the presence of right censoring, the event time is only partially observed, rendering conventional scoring rules inapplicable in their standard form. We propose a framework for proper scoring of right-censored survival outcomes based on a simple idea: first, map the predictive distribution through the censoring mechanism, then apply the underlying proper score on the induced observed-data law. This yields localized scores for fixed censoring times and marginalized scores when the censoring time is random or only partially observed. The resulting construction recovers familiar right-censored likelihood and IPCW-type criteria within a coherent framework, while also yielding right-censored versions of the CRPS, pinball loss, Brier score, and energy score. We show that the marginalized score is proper under conditional independent censoring and strictly proper on the identifiable region. The same principle also leads to censored engression, a sample-based learning objective for multivariate right-censored survival modeling. In experiments, our scores correctly rank the oracle forecast across several censoring regimes, whereas forecast-dependent plug-in weighted scores can exhibit ranking reversals. Censored engression likewise substantially improves over naive training on censored outcomes.

</details>


### [379] [The Post-GCN Decade Revisited: Curvature-Stratified Evaluation of Relational Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06397)
*Shuo Wang,Xiangyu Wang,Quanxin Wang,Bailin Wu,Bokui Wang,Shunyang Huang,Boyan Deng,Haonan Liu,Ruiyi Fang,Zhenxiang Xu,Boyu Wang,Zhao Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A curvature-stratified evaluation framework reveals geometry-dependent performance in relational learning, showing that standard flat benchmarks mask regime-specific rankings. Evaluates 18 models on 14 datasets across three curvature regimes, finding geometry-aligned GNNs can outperform Graph Foundation Models in some regimes. Proposes geometry-aware evaluation protocol and releases code and splits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current flat leaderboards average across heterogeneous datasets, implicitly assuming a uniform underlying structure. The authors argue that intrinsic dataset geometry governs model effectiveness and that aggregating metrics obscures geometry-dependent trade-offs.

Method: Introduce a curvature-stratified evaluation framework that partitions datasets into positive, negative, and near-zero curvature regimes. Benchmark 18 models (GCNs, GFMs, and tabular methods) across 14 datasets. Analyze regression of performance within regimes and cross-regime shifts.

Result: Model rankings are highly stable within each curvature regime but shift significantly across regimes, indicating geometry-dependent performance. GFMs sometimes offer diminishing returns relative to geometry-aligned GNNs. Curvature stratification reveals trade-offs not visible in aggregated benchmarks.

Conclusion: Adopt a geometry-aware evaluation protocol for more reliable, interpretable comparisons in relational learning. Release code, curvature-stratified splits, and evaluation tools to support reproducible assessment.

Abstract: Current evaluation practices in relational learning rely heavily on flat leaderboards that average performance across heterogeneous datasets, implicitly assuming a uniform underlying structure. We show that this assumption introduces systematic bias: it obscures geometry-dependent performance variations and can lead to misleading conclusions about model generalization. In this work, we identify intrinsic geometry as a key latent factor governing model effectiveness. We demonstrate that conventional aggregated metrics mask critical performance trade-offs that only become visible when datasets are stratified by their geometric properties. To address this issue, we introduce a curvature-stratified evaluation framework that partitions datasets into positive, negative, and near-zero curvature regimes. Our benchmark evaluates 18 representative models including Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), Graph Foundation Models (GFMs), and tabular learning methods across 14 datasets. We find that model rankings are highly stable within each curvature regime but shift significantly across regimes, indicating that performance is fundamentally geometry-dependent rather than universally transferable. Notably, we identify regimes where GFMs offer diminishing returns compared to geometry-aligned GNNs. Based on these findings, we propose a geometry-aware evaluation protocol that yields more reliable and interpretable comparisons than standard aggregated benchmarks. We release all code, curvature-stratified dataset splits, and evaluation tools to support reproducible and rigorous assessment of future relational learning methods. Code and datasets are provided in our project homepage: https://sirbabbage.github.io/CurvBench_HOME/.

</details>


### [380] [Double Preconditioning (DoPr): Optimization for Test-Time Performance, not Validation Loss](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06418)
*Thomas T. Zhang,Alok Shah,Yifei Zhang,Vincent Zhang,Nikolai Matni,Max Simchowitz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Double-preconditioning (DoPr) combines gradient-wise and activation-wise preconditioning to mitigate test-time feedback (TTF) in one-step supervised objectives for rollout-based deployment. It yields improvements in downstream metrics across TTF settings, but validation loss does not always improve.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TTF arises when training objectives do not align with downstream metrics (task success, generation quality) as rollout length increases. Prior work focuses on data, architecture, and objective design; this work proposes optimization as a new lever to reduce error accumulation.

Method: DoPr integrates gradient-wise preconditioning (e.g., Adam, Muon) with activation-wise preconditioning (AP) (e.g., KFAC) into a unified optimization paradigm. Likely leverages layer-wise statistics and second-order information to shape updates, aiming to reduce error accumulation during rollouts.

Result: Across various TTF settings, DoPr yields improved downstream model performance when deployed along its own predictions. Gains in test-time metrics do not consistently accompany improvements in validation loss, suggesting a misalignment between training-time proxies and deployment-time objectives.

Conclusion: Optimization-based intervention (DoPr) can mitigate error accumulation at test time in one-step supervised objectives. Activation-wise preconditioning appears beneficial for downstream metrics, motivating a reevaluation of evaluation standards beyond validation loss. Further work should examine computational cost, stability, theoretical guarantees, and applicability across domains.

Abstract: Many modern applications of deep learning involve training a neural network via a one-step prediction loss (e.g., $L^2$ regression, cross-entropy), but deploy the network by rolling out along its own predictions. Key examples include autoregressive language modeling, flow-based generative modeling, and robot policy learning. It is well-documented that these settings induce a phenomenon we call test-time feedback (TTF): the mismatch between the training/validation loss and downstream metrics of interest, such as task success rate and generation quality, which grows with task length. While data curation, architecture, and objective design have been proposed to combat train-test shift in TTF settings, this paper proposes optimization as a new design axis to mitigate error accumulation. Specifically, we introduce a new optimization paradigm called double-preconditioning (DoPr) uniquely tailored to the challenges of TTF. DoPr combines gradient-wise preconditioning, as in Adam and Muon, with activation-wise preconditioning (AP), such as in KFAC. We show that the addition of AP yields a drop-in intervention for increasing downstream model performance across a range of TTF settings. Interestingly, these gains in test-time performance do not consistently accompany improvements in validation loss, opening new questions about how to properly evaluate models trained with one-step supervised objectives.

</details>


### [381] [Causal Atlases from Entropic Inference: Bayesian Networks beyond Optimal DAGs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06440)
*Hazhir Aliahmadi,Irina Babayan,Greg van Anders*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entropy-based maximum-entropy graph ensembles (an atlas) reveal multiple plausible causal graphs consistent with data; optimized DAGs may exhibit artifacts and lack stability across equally accurate topologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the incompleteness and ambiguity in causal discovery where data admit multiple causal explanations; provide a data-faithful representation by sampling a diverse set of plausible DAGs rather than a single optimized structure.

Method: Sample a maximum-entropy ensemble of DAGs conditioned on data from linear structural equation models (2- and 20-node); quantify structural ambiguity and compare with optimized DAGs.

Result: An atlas of plausible causal graphs is generated; optimized DAGs can contain causal artifacts and are not consistent across topologies that are equally accurate.

Conclusion: Entropy-based inference yields a robust, uncertainty-aware representation of causality, highlighting that single optimized DAGs may misrepresent causal structure; use ensembles to capture inherent ambiguity.

Abstract: Data-driven causal relationship identification is pertinent to advancing understanding of complex systems both within and beyond science. Bayesian networks offer a probabilistic method for modelling generic causal relationships via directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). However, typical techniques for constructing Bayesian networks rely on optimization, which can be ill-suited for learning causal relationships because the underlying data may admit multiple chains of causation. More data-faithful representations of causal relationships would provide frameworks for constructing multiple causal maps that are consistent with the variability that is inherent in underlying data. Here, we show that entropy-based inference generates atlases of plausible causal relationships that are consistent with underlying data. On simulated noisy data of 2- and 20-node linear structural equation models, we sample a maximum-entropy ensemble of graphs that allow us to quantify the inherent structural ambiguity in underlying causal relationships. Our method shows that "optimized" DAGs can contain causal artifacts are not consistent across equivalently accurate topologies.

</details>


### [382] [Event Detection for Parameter-to-KPI Dependency Learning for AI-RAN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06459)
*Christie Djidjev,Nicholas Kaminski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A machine-learning pipeline to recover latent parameter-to-KPI dependencies in AI-RAN by converting telemetry into binary event indicators via significance detection, evaluated on synthetic data with planted dependencies; threshold calibration is the key factor for event-detection quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable interpretable dependency learning in AI-RAN/O-RAN where multiple AI-driven control functions interact, producing hidden dependencies between control parameters and network performance metrics.

Method: Convert noisy continuous telemetry into binary event indicators using a significance-detection framework. Use synthetic closed-loop traffic with planted latent dependencies to provide ground-truth for evaluation. Assess how well the pipeline recovers the latent dependency structure under varying signal-to-background variation, focusing on threshold calibration as a determinant of performance.

Result: The pipeline reliably recovers the latent dependency structure from noisy traces when the signal is sufficiently separated from background variation; threshold calibration is identified as the key factor controlling event-detection quality.

Conclusion: This work provides a foundational step toward interpretable dependency learning for adaptive AI-RAN control systems and introduces a synthetic data generator with planted dependencies to facilitate evaluation.

Abstract: Next-generation wireless networks are expected to rely on multiple concurrent AI-driven control functions that optimize different network objectives simultaneously, particularly in AI-integrated and open radio access network architectures such as AI Radio Access Network (AI-RAN) and Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN). When these functions interact, they can interfere with one another in ways that are difficult to detect from raw network data alone. A key missing piece for managing such interactions is a reliable, interpretable dependency structure that captures which control parameters are actively influencing which network performance outcomes at any given time. This paper focuses on the event-detection step needed to support such dependency learning by converting noisy continuous telemetry into binary indicators of parameter activity and KPI response. The central difficulty is that not every fluctuation in the data reflects a genuine control interaction, so the method must distinguish real parameter-outcome relationships from background variation. Because real AI-RAN traffic traces with known parameter-KPI ground truth are difficult to obtain, we introduce a synthetic closed-loop traffic generator with planted latent dependencies. We use this controlled telemetry to evaluate a machine-learning-based dependency recovery pipeline that formulates the conversion of continuous traces into binary event indicators as a significance-detection problem. Experimental evaluation shows that the proposed pipeline reliably recovers the latent dependency structure from noisy continuous traces when the signal is sufficiently separated from background variation, while highlighting threshold calibration as the key factor controlling event-detection quality. These results constitute a foundational step toward interpretable dependency learning for adaptive AI-RAN control systems.

</details>


### [383] [PC Layer: Polynomial Weight Preconditioning for Improving LLM Pre-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06470)
