*Senmiao Wang,Tiantian Fang,Haoran Zhang,Yushun Zhang,Kunxiang Zhao,Alex Schwing,Ruoyu Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Polynomial preconditioning (PC) layer shapes weight spectra during LLM training to stabilize conditioning; weights are re-integrated post-training with no inference cost; shown to beat standard transformers on Llama-1B with AdamW and Muon, with a convergence theory for deep linear nets; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve optimization stability and convergence in deep transformer training by controlling layer-wise singular values; address slow convergence due to ill-conditioned weight matrices; provide a practical, mergeable training-time transformation.

Method: Introduce a PC layer that applies a low-degree polynomial preconditioner to weight matrices, reshaping their singular-value spectrum during training. After training, merge the preconditioned weights back into the original model to avoid inference overhead. Evaluate on Llama-1B pre-training with AdamW and Muon optimizers; provide theoretical justification for spectrum-control leading to geometric convergence in certain deep linear networks.

Result: Empirical demonstration of performance advantages over standard transformers during Llama-1B pre-training for both AdamW and Muon optimizers; no additional inference cost after merging; code released.

Conclusion: Uniformly bounding layer singular values via polynomial preconditioning provides a principled approach to stabilize training and ensure convergence in a class of deep networks; the method is practical due to post-training merge, and the theoretical claim is proven for specific deep linear models.

Abstract: We propose a preconditioning (PC) layer, a weight parameterization via polynomial preconditioner that ensures stable weight conditioning throughout LLM training. The PC module reshapes the singular-value spectrum of weight matrices via low-degree polynomial preconditioning. After training, the preconditioned weights can be merged back into the original architecture, incurring no inference overhead. We demonstrate the advantage of the proposed PC layer over standard transformers in Llama-1B pre-training, for both the AdamW and Muon optimizers. Theoretically, we justify this spectrum-control principle by proving that uniformly bounding each layer's singular values ensures geometric convergence of gradient descent to global minima, for certain deep linear networks. Our code is available at https://github.com/Empath-aln/PC-layer.

</details>


### [384] [RREDCoT: Segment-Level Reward Redistribution for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06475)
*Mykyta Ielanskyi,Kajetan Schweighofer,Lukas Aichberger,Sepp Hochreiter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces RREDCoT, a model-based reward redistribution method to mitigate high-variance delayed rewards in RL fine-tuning of reasoning LMs, avoiding extra generation and outperforming MC and attribution baselines in CoT tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the delayed reward problem in chain-of-thought (CoT) reinforcement learning. GRPO/Monte Carlo approaches incur high variance and rely on delayed feedback after CoT; reward redistribution to credit important CoT segments is proposed, but Monte Carlo estimation is computationally expensive for fine-grained credit assignment.

Method: RREDCoT uses the model itself to approximate the optimal reward redistribution without additional generation; investigates segmentation of CoT traces and state-value estimation; compares against MC sampling and attribution methods and analyzes construction aspects of redistribution.

Result: Shows advantages of RREDCoT over MC sampling and various attribution baselines; provides analysis on how trace segmentation and state-value estimation affect redistribution performance.

Conclusion: A practical, model-based reward redistribution method for CoT RL fine-tuning that mitigates high-variance issues, reduces reliance on costly MC sampling, and offers design insights for segmentation and value estimation.

Abstract: Recent advancements in reasoning language models have been driven by Reinforcement Learning (RL) fine-tuning. Most often, these rely on the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm or modifications thereof to steer the models to produce Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces. The final answer can only be verified, and the reward assigned, after the CoT trace is complete, making it a delayed reward problem. GRPO and its modifications correspond to Monte Carlo methods in standard RL, which are known to suffer from high variance. A possible solution to this problem is the redistribution of rewards through credit assignment, where segments of the CoT trace that are important for arriving at the desirable solution are emphasized by assigning a higher reward. While Monte Carlo sampling can be used to provide an unbiased estimate of intermediate state values, its computational overhead makes it unsuitable for train-time credit assignment in long contexts at high granularity. We introduce RREDCoT (Reward REDistribution for Chain of Thoughts), which utilizes the model itself to approximate the optimal reward redistribution without additional generation. We investigate the advantages of our method compared to MC sampling and several attribution methods. We further analyze several aspects relevant to the construction of the redistribution such as segmentation of CoT traces and state value estimation.

</details>


### [385] [Pretraining Recurrent Networks without Recurrence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06479)
*Akarsh Kumar,Phillip Isola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Supervised Memory Training (SMT) reframes RNN training as supervised learning on one-step memory transitions, enabling time-parallel training with stable gradient paths by using a Transformer encoder to generate memory labels; decouples memory content from its update.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backpropagation through time is sequential and suffers from vanishing/exploding gradients, hindering learning of long-range dependencies and limiting parallelism.

Method: Train a Transformer-based encoder to produce memory transition labels; use a predictive state objective to retain only past information needed to predict the future; train RNNs via supervised learning on (m_t, x_{t+1}) -> m_{t+1} without unrolling the RNN.

Result: SMT outperforms BPTT on pretraining RNNs for language modeling and pixel sequence modeling; enables nonlinear RNNs to capture long-range dependencies and train in parallel with a stable O(1) gradient path length between tokens.

Conclusion: SMT decouples what to remember from how to update memory, enabling scalable, parallel RNN training and potentially unlocking the scaling of temporal models.

Abstract: Training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) requires assigning credit across long sequences of computations. Standard backpropagation through time (BPTT) addresses this problem poorly: it is sequential in time, limiting parallelism, and suffers from vanishing or exploding gradients, making long-range associations difficult to learn. We propose Supervised Memory Training (SMT), a method for training nonlinear RNNs that sidesteps recurrent credit propagation entirely by reducing RNN training to supervised learning on one-step memory transition labels $(m_t, x_{t+1}) \rightarrow m_{t+1}$. SMT acquires these memory labels by training a Transformer-based encoder on a predictive state objective--retaining only information from the past necessary to predict the future. By decoupling what to remember from how to update memory, SMT enables time-parallel RNN training with a stable $O(1)$ length gradient path between any two tokens--without ever unrolling the RNN. We find that SMT outperforms BPTT when pretraining various RNN architectures on tasks like language modeling and pixel sequence modeling. SMT enables nonlinear RNNs to better capture long-range dependencies and train in parallel, potentially unlocking the scaling of models that build temporal abstractions of past experience.

</details>


### [386] [Regret Minimization with Adaptive Opponents in Repeated Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06486)
*Mingyang Liu,Asuman Ozdaglar,Tiancheng Yu,Kaiqing Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret) for repeated games with adaptive opponents, analyzes conditions for sublinear regret, proposes three minimization algorithms, and shows potential to learn subgame-perfect equilibria; experiments indicate more cooperative outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: External regret fails to capture adaptivity and counterfactual reasoning in repeated games; a native regret notion is needed to compare realized vs best-in-hindsight utilities when opponents respond to history.

Method: Define RP-Regret; derive necessary conditions for sublinear regret depending on comparator variation and memory; develop three minimization algorithms (oracle-based, convex-linearized surrogate, and slow-opponent-update); analyze non-convexity and equilibrium implications; empirical study on Stag-Hunt.

Result: RP-Regret can be minimized sublinearly under identified conditions; proposed algorithms provide practical methods for minimization; when all players minimize RP-Regret, certain subgame-perfect equilibria can be learned; experiments show more cooperative solutions with higher utility.

Conclusion: RP-Regret is a natural, robust metric for repeated interactions with adaptive opponents; with suitable optimization strategies, better equilibria can be achieved; empirical results corroborate increased cooperation in standard games like Stag-Hunt.

Abstract: In this paper, we study regret minimization in repeated games with \emph{adaptive} opponents who can respond based on histories of play. The standard metric of \emph{external regret} in online learning is known to fail to capture such adaptivity. To account for players' counterfactual reasoning, we introduce {\tt Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret)}, a game-theoretic metric that measures the difference between the \emph{realized} and the \emph{best-in-hindsight} accumulated utility when all players can \emph{respond} to the history of play. Compared to existing regret notions in this setting, ours is native to repeated game playing, enabling stronger comparators and opponents with fewer constraints, while maintaining the possibility of finding better equilibria when all players minimize it. We first identify necessary conditions for obtaining {\tt RP-Regret} sublinear in time, on the variation of the player's comparator strategies in the regret definition and on the memories of both the comparator and opponents' strategies. We then study additional conditions and provable algorithms to minimize {\tt RP-Regret}, which is by definition \emph{non-convex} in the strategy space. To address this challenge, we propose three algorithms: (i) one based on an optimization oracle, as assumed in some prior work in online non-convex learning; (ii) one that minimizes a convex and \emph{linearized} surrogate of {\tt RP-Regret} at each iteration; (iii) one that directly minimizes {\tt RP-Regret} when opponents change strategies slowly. Furthermore, when all players can run algorithms to minimize the {\tt RP-Regret} (or its linearized variant), certain subgame perfect equilibria of the repeated game can be learned. We also provide experiments showing that minimizing our regret notions can lead to more cooperative solutions with higher utility in games such as Stag-Hunt.

</details>


### [387] [TailLoR: Protecting Principal Components in Parameter-Efficient Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06494)
*Marius Dragoi,Ioana Pintilie,Alexandra Dragomir,Antonio Barbalau,Florin Brad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TailLoR uses fixed singular bases of pretrained weights to guide a low-rank update to the singular values, with a soft penalty to avoid dominating directions, enabling efficient, low-interference fine-tuning in continual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address parameter efficiency and catastrophic interference in continual learning by exploiting the spectral structure of pretrained weights to enable targeted, low-rank adaptation in a fixed reference frame.

Method: Compute the SVD of pretrained weights to obtain U, S, V; keep U and V fixed as a reference frame and learn a low-rank update to S (the singular values). Apply a soft spectral penalty that discourages updates aligned with dominant singular directions, diverting adaptation into the long-tail (less dominant) spectral coordinates.

Result: The abstract does not report numerical results; it emphasizes the methodological contribution and the claimed benefit of reduced interference and routing adaptation into flexible spectral coordinates, but no empirical numbers are provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: TailLoR proposes a parameter-efficient finetuning strategy grounded in spectral decomposition, aiming to reduce interference in continual learning by steering updates into long-tail spectral coordinates while keeping the core singular bases fixed.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient finetuning methods based on spectral decomposition have enabled progress in Continual Learning. In this paper we introduce TailLoR, which utilizes the singular bases U and V of the pre-trained weights as a fixed reference frame to learn a low-rank update applied to the singular value matrix. A soft spectral penalty discourages updates aligned with dominant singular directions, reducing interference while routing fine-grained adaptation into the highly flexible, long-tail spectral coordinates.

</details>
